Now when Jesus heard about it He left there in a boat and went <sup>[across to the east side of the lake]</sup> to a deserted place. When the crowds found out <sup>[He was leaving]</sup> they followed Him <sup>[i.e., by traveling around]</sup> on the shore from the <sup>[surrounding]</sup> towns.
When Jesus came out <sup>[of His place of seclusion]</sup> and saw a large crowd, He felt a deep compassion for them and healed their sick people.

When it became evening, the disciples came to Him and said, “This place is deserted and it is getting late. Send the crowds away so they can go into the towns and buy food <sup>[for their supper]</sup>.”
But Jesus said to them, “They do not need to go away; you men give them <sup>[something]</sup> to eat.”
And they replied, “But we have here <sup>[only]</sup> five loaves of <sup>[barley]</sup> bread <sup>[See John 6:9]</sup> and two <sup>[probably smoked]</sup> fish.”
And He said, “Bring the bread and fish here to me.”

He ordered the crowds to recline on the grass. Then He took the five loaves of bread and two fish and, looking up to heaven, asked God’s blessing on the food, broke it <sup>[in pieces]</sup>, then gave it to the disciples to distribute to the crowds.
They all ate until they were full, then gathered up twelve baskets full of the remaining broken pieces.
Those eating numbered about five thousand men, in addition to the women and children.
Then immediately Jesus ordered the disciples to enter the boat and row on ahead of Him to the other side <sup>[i.e., to the west side of Lake Galilee]</sup> until He could send the crowds away.
After He had dispersed them, He went up into the mountain by Himself to pray. When evening had come He was there alone.
<sup>[Meanwhile]</sup> the boat, still some distance from shore, was being severely battered by the waves because of the opposing winds.
Then between three and six o’clock in the morning Jesus came walking to them on the <sup>[surface of the]</sup> lake.
When the disciples saw Him walking on the water they became frightened, saying, “It is a spirit!” and cried out with fear.
Just then Jesus called out to them, “Take courage and do not be afraid; it is I.”
Peter answered Him, “Lord, if it is <sup>[really]</sup> you, ask me to come to you <sup>[walking]</sup> on the water.”
And Jesus replied, “Come <sup>[ahead]</sup>.” So, Peter climbed over the side of the boat and went to Jesus, walking on the water.
But when he saw the <sup>[furious]</sup> wind he became fearful and began to sink, shouting out, “Lord, save me!”
And immediately Jesus stretched out His hand and took hold of him and said, “You have such little faith; why did you doubt?”
And when they <sup>[i.e., both Jesus and Peter]</sup> got up into the boat, the wind <sup>[immediately]</sup> stopped.
Then the disciples in the boat worshiped Jesus, saying, “Truly you are the Son of God!”
And when they had <sup>[finally]</sup> crossed over <sup>[i.e., to the west side of the lake]</sup>, they landed at the region of Gennesaret.
When the people there recognized Jesus, they sent to all the surrounding regions and had all who were sick brought to Him.
They begged Him that they might only touch the edge of His robe. And all those who got to touch it were made well.
Then some Pharisees and experts in the Law of Moses came to Jesus from Jerusalem and said,
“Why do your disciples violate the tradition of the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders? For they do not <sup>[ceremonially]</sup> wash their hands before eating <sup>[their meals]</sup>.”
And He answered them, “Why do you also violate the commandments of God by <sup>[following]</sup> your traditions?
For God said, <sup>[Ex. 20:12]</sup>, ‘Give honor to your father and mother,’ and <sup>[Ex. 21:17]</sup>, ‘Whoever says bad things about his father or mother should surely be put to death.’
But you say, ‘Whoever says to his father or mother, I have given to God the money I could have helped you with;’
this person does not honor his father <sup>[or mother]</sup>. You have made God’s message useless by <sup>[thus enforcing]</sup> your traditions.
You hypocrites! Isaiah prophesied accurately about you, saying <sup>[Isa. 29:13]</sup>,
‘These people <sup>[claim to]</sup> honor me with their lips <sup>[i.e., by what they say]</sup>, but their heart is far from <sup>[honoring]</sup> me.
They are worshiping me for nothing <sup>[because they are]</sup> teaching principles that are <sup>[merely]</sup> the requirements of men.’”
Then Jesus called the crowds to come to Him, and He said to them, “Listen and understand.
It is not what enters the mouth of a person that <sup>[spiritually]</sup> corrupts him, but what proceeds out of his mouth that <sup>[spiritually]</sup> corrupts him.”
Then His disciples came and said to Him, “Do you know that the Pharisees were upset <sup>[with you]</sup> when they heard <sup>[you say]</sup> that?”
But He answered and said, “Every plant that my heavenly Father did not plant will be uprooted.
<sup>[So]</sup>, let these Pharisees alone; they are blind leaders. And if a blind person leads a blind person, both of them will fall into a ditch.”
And Peter said to Him, “Explain the parable to us.”
And He replied, “Are you still unable to understand?
Do you not realize that whatever enters a person’s mouth goes into his stomach and <sup>[eventually]</sup> passes out into the toilet?
But the things that come out of a person’s mouth come from his heart. These are what <sup>[spiritually]</sup> corrupt a person.
For evil thoughts, murder, extramarital affairs, sexual immorality, stealing, perjury, slander --- <sup>[all these]</sup> come from a person’s heart,
and are what corrupt him <sup>[spiritually]</sup>. But eating <sup>[a meal]</sup> with <sup>[ceremonially]</sup> unwashed hands does not <sup>[spiritually]</sup> corrupt him.”
And Jesus left there and went into the regions of Tyre and Sidon <sup>[i.e., cities on the northwest coast of Palestine]</sup>.

Just then a Canaanite woman came out of that area <sup>[Note: Mark 7:26 calls her a Syrophoenician Gentile]</sup>, crying, “O Lord, son of David, have pity on me. My daughter is seriously troubled by an evil spirit.”
But He did not say a word to her. His disciples came and begged Him; “Send her away, for she is <sup>[continually]</sup> shouting at us <sup>[as we go along]</sup>.”
He answered them, “I was sent only to the straying sheep of the Israelites.”
But she came and worshiped Him, saying, “Lord, help me.”
And He answered <sup>[her]</sup>, “It is not proper to take bread away from the children and throw it to the dogs.”
But she replied, “Yes, Lord, but even dogs eat the crumbs that fall from their owner’s table.”
Then Jesus answered her, “O, woman, how great your faith is. May what you want be done for you.” And her daughter was healed that very moment.
Jesus left there and went <sup>[over]</sup> near Lake Galilee. He went up into a mountain and sat down there.
Large crowds came to Him, bringing crippled, blind, deaf-mute, disabled and many other <sup>[sick]</sup> people and laid them down at His feet. And He healed <sup>[all of]</sup> them,

so that the crowd marveled when they saw deaf-mutes talking, injured people healed, crippled people walking and blind people seeing. And they honored the God of the Israelites <sup>[for such great miracles]</sup>.

Jesus called His disciples to Him and said, “I feel deeply moved over this crowd, because they have continued listening to me for three days now, and have nothing <sup>[left]</sup> to eat. I do not want to send them away hungry because they will become weak along the way.”
And the disciples said to Him, “Where will we get enough loaves of bread to feed a large crowd in <sup>[such]</sup> a deserted place <sup>[as this]</sup>?”
Jesus replied, “How many loaves of bread do you have?” They said, “<sup>[We have]</sup> seven <sup>[loaves]</sup> and a few small fish.”
Then He ordered the crowd to sit down on the ground,
and He took the seven loaves of bread and the fish and gave thanks to God for them. He divided the food and gave it to the disciples who, <sup>[in turn]</sup>, distributed it to the crowds.
They all ate until they were full; then gathered up seven baskets full of broken pieces which were left over.
The number who ate was four thousand men, besides women and children.
Jesus then sent the crowds away while He boarded a boat and crossed the lake to the region of Magadan <sup>[i.e., the west side of Lake Galilee]</sup>.
The Pharisees and Sadducees came <sup>[and attempted]</sup> to test Jesus <sup>[by]</sup> asking Him to perform a <sup>[supernatural]</sup> sign from heaven.

But He answered them, The rest of this verse and the next verse are omitted in most ancient manuscripts: “When the sky is reddish in the evening, you predict fair weather <sup>[for the next day]</sup>.

And when the sky is reddish and overcast in the morning, you predict <sup>[that it will be]</sup> bad weather that day. You know how to interpret <sup>[weather]</sup> conditions from the sky, but you cannot interpret the signs of the times <sup>[i.e., what will happen in the spiritual realm]</sup>”.

“It is an evil and spiritually unfaithful generation of people <sup>[that keeps on]</sup> looking for a <sup>[supernatural]</sup> sign; but there will be no <sup>[such]</sup> sign given to it except the sign of Jonah <sup>[i.e., being in the huge fish for three days]</sup>.” Then He left them and went away.
Now the disciples came to the other side <sup>[i.e., to the east side of the lake]</sup>, but had forgotten to bring food <sup>[with them]</sup>.
Jesus said to them, “Pay attention and watch out for the leavening <sup>[effect]</sup> of the Pharisees and Sadducees.”
And they began reasoning among themselves, saying, “<sup>[Why be concerned about yeast since]</sup> we did not bring <sup>[any]</sup> bread?”
Jesus, being aware of their thoughts, said, “O, you people with <sup>[such]</sup> little faith! Why are you reasoning among yourselves about not having bread?
Do you not understand yet or remember that five thousand people <sup>[were fed]</sup> with five loaves of bread and how many baskets <sup>[of pieces]</sup> were picked up <sup>[afterward]</sup>?
Or that four thousand people <sup>[were fed]</sup> with seven loaves of bread and how many baskets <sup>[of pieces]</sup> were picked up <sup>[afterward]</sup>?

How is it that you do not understand that I was not speaking to you about <sup>[physical]</sup> bread? But <sup>[rather I meant]</sup> watch out for the yeast <sup>[i.e., the influence]</sup> of the Pharisees and Sadducees.”
Then they understood that He was not telling them to watch out for the yeast in <sup>[physical]</sup> bread, but for the teaching of the Pharisees and Sadducees.
Now when Jesus came into the region of Caesarea Philippi, He asked His disciples, “Who are people saying that the Son of man is?”

They replied, “Some say you are John the Immerser; some <sup>[the prophet]</sup> Elijah; and others <sup>[the prophet]</sup> Jeremiah, or one of the <sup>[other]</sup> prophets <sup>[come back to life]</sup>.”
He <sup>[then]</sup> said to them, “But who do you say that I am?”
And Simon Peter answered, “You are the Christ <sup>[i.e., God’s specially chosen one]</sup>, the Son of the living God.”
Jesus replied, “Simon, son of Jonah, you are fortunate <sup>[indeed]</sup>, for this <sup>[truth]</sup> was not revealed to you by human beings but <sup>[rather]</sup> by my Father who is in heaven.

And I also tell you, <sup>[although]</sup> you are Peter <sup>[i.e., a stone]</sup>, it is on this rock <sup>[i.e., the truth you have just confessed]</sup> that I will build my church and <sup>[not even]</sup> the gates of the unseen place of departed spirits will win out over it <sup>[i.e., all efforts to stamp out the church by killing Christians will fail]</sup>.

<sup>[And]</sup> I will give you the keys of the kingdom of heaven <sup>[i.e., for opening up the way into it]</sup>. And whatever <sup>[truth]</sup> you <sup>[and the other apostles. See 18:18]</sup> require <sup>[people to believe and practice]</sup> here on earth will have already been required of them <sup>[by God]</sup> in heaven. And whatever <sup>[truth]</sup> you do not require <sup>[of people]</sup> on earth, will not be required <sup>[by God]</sup> in heaven.”
Then He ordered the disciples not to tell anyone that He was the Christ.

From that time onward Jesus began to explain to His disciples that He would have to go to Jerusalem and suffer many things at the hands of the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders, leading priests, and experts in the Law of Moses; <sup>[that He would then]</sup> be killed and <sup>[yet]</sup> raised up on the third day.
Peter took Him aside and began to rebuke Him, saying, “I should say not, Lord; that will never happen to you.”
But Jesus turned to Peter and said, “Get away from me, Satan; you are causing a hindrance to me because you are not thinking about God’s things but about men’s.”

Then Jesus said to His disciples, “If anyone wants to be my follower, he should deny self <sup>[i.e., of always having its own way]</sup> and lift up his cross <sup>[of responsibility]</sup> and <sup>[then he can]</sup> become my follower.

For whoever would <sup>[try to]</sup> save his life <sup>[i.e., by neglecting spiritual things]</sup> will lose it <sup>[i.e., miss out on the blessings of God]</sup>. But whoever <sup>[is willing to]</sup> lose his life <sup>[i.e., in commitment to God’s service]</sup> for my sake will find it <sup>[i.e., obtain both temporal and spiritual blessings]</sup>.

For what benefit is it to a person if he gains the whole world <sup>[of material things]</sup> but <sup>[has to]</sup> give up his life <sup>[i.e., all the spiritual blessings]</sup>? Or what should a person give in exchange for his life?
For the Son of man will return in the splendor of His Father, accompanied by His angels. Then He will reward or punish every person in proportion to what he has done <sup>[with his life]</sup>.
It is true when I tell you; some of those standing here will not die before they see the Son of man coming in His kingdom.”
And after six days <sup>[had passed]</sup>, Jesus took Peter, James, and his brother John aside and went up into a high mountain <sup>[i.e., an unidentified mountain nearby]</sup>.
There His whole appearance was <sup>[miraculously]</sup> changed in front of them. His face shone <sup>[as brilliantly]</sup> as the sun and His clothing became as bright as light.
Then suddenly Moses and Elijah appeared to them, talking with Jesus.

Peter said to Jesus, “Lord, it is so good for us to be here. If you wish, I will build three <sup>[small]</sup> shelters here, one for you, one for Moses and one for Elijah.” <sup>[Note: Perhaps Peter wanted to provide quarters for the three to stay temporarily, thus prolonging the wonderful experience. In any event, he should not have done what suggested that Moses and Elijah were equal to Jesus]</sup>.
While he was still talking, suddenly a bright cloud engulfed them. Just then, a voice spoke out of the cloud, saying, “This is my dearly loved Son, who is very pleasing to me; listen to Him.”
And when the disciples heard <sup>[the voice]</sup>, they fell <sup>[to the ground]</sup> on their faces and became very afraid.
But Jesus came and touched them, saying, “Get up, and do not be afraid.”
When they looked up, the only one they saw was Jesus.
And as they were coming down from the mountain, Jesus ordered them, saying, “Do not tell anyone about the vision <sup>[you have just seen]</sup> until the Son of man has been raised from the dead.”
His disciples asked Him, “Why then <sup>[since you are obviously the Messiah]</sup> do the experts in the Law of Moses say that Elijah must come first, <sup>[i.e., before the Messiah]</sup>?”
Jesus answered them, “Elijah truly was to come, and restore all things <sup>[i.e., to their proper perspective concerning the Messiah]</sup>.

But I tell you that Elijah has already come, but they <sup>[i.e., the Jewish leaders]</sup> did not understand who he was, but <sup>[instead]</sup> did to him what they wanted to. In the same way, the Son of man will also suffer from what the Jews will do.”
Then the disciples understood that He was talking to them about John the Immerser.
When Jesus and His disciples met the crowd <sup>[that was gathered]</sup>, a man came kneeling down to Jesus and saying to Him,

“Lord, take pity on my son, for he has violent seizures and suffers intense pain. He often falls into a fire or into water. <sup>[Note: This man was also dominated by an evil spirit. See verse 18]</sup>.
I brought him to <sup>[some of]</sup> your disciples <sup>[for help]</sup> but they were not able to heal him.”

Jesus replied <sup>[to the crowd]</sup>, “You perverse generation of faithless people! How long must I be with you <sup>[i.e., before you understand]</sup>? How long do I have to be patient with you? Bring the sick boy to me.”
Then Jesus spoke sternly to <sup>[the evil spirit in]</sup> the boy, and it left him, and he was immediately healed.
Then Jesus’ disciples came to Him privately and said, “Why could we not drive out that evil spirit?”

He answered them, “<sup>[It was]</sup> because you had such little faith. For truly I tell you, if you have faith as <sup>[small as]</sup> a mustard tree seed, you could say to this mountain, ‘Move from here to that place over there’ and it would move. And nothing will be impossible for you <sup>[to do]</sup>.”
Some manuscripts contain this verse: But this kind <sup>[of spirit]</sup> can not be driven out except by praying and fasting.
And while the disciples were staying in Galilee, Jesus said to them, “The Son of man will be handed over to <sup>[evil]</sup> men
and they will kill Him, but on the third day He will be raised up <sup>[from the dead]</sup>.” And they were very grieved <sup>[when they heard this]</sup>.

When Jesus and His disciples came to Capernaum, the collectors of the <sup>[Temple]</sup> tax <sup>[Note: This was the annual tax used for meeting the expenses of the Temple service and consisted of two twelve hour days’ worth of a farm laborer’s pay, or about $216 in 2005]</sup> came to Peter and said, “Does not your teacher <sup>[also]</sup> pay the <sup>[Temple]</sup> tax?”

He replied, “Yes, <sup>[He does]</sup>.” And when Peter entered the house, Jesus spoke to him first, <sup>[i.e., without waiting for Peter to tell Him what he had said to the tax collectors]</sup>, saying, “What do you think, Simon? From whom do earthly kings collect custom duty and taxes? From their children or from strangers?”
And when Peter said, “From strangers,” Jesus added, “Therefore, children are free. <sup>[from such taxation]</sup>

But, so that we do not become a hindrance to these <sup>[tax collectors]</sup>, go to the lake and cast in your hook <sup>[and line]</sup>. Then take the first fish <sup>[you catch]</sup>, open its mouth and you will find a coin <sup>[i.e., worth twice the amount of an individual’s Temple tax assessment]</sup>. Take it and give it <sup>[to the tax collectors]</sup> for you and me.”
At that very time His disciples came to Jesus, asking, “Who then is the most important person in the kingdom of heaven?”
So, He called to Him a little child and placed him in front of them,
and said, “Truly I tell you, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven unless you turn <sup>[your life around]</sup> and become like little children <sup>[i.e., having child-like humility]</sup>.
Therefore, the person who will humble himself, as this little child, is the most important person in the kingdom of heaven.
And the person who welcomes one little child like this, in my name <sup>[i.e., because he belongs to me]</sup>, welcomes me <sup>[also]</sup>.

But that person who causes one of these little ones <sup>[i.e., humble followers of the Lord]</sup> who believes in me to be led astray <sup>[from God]</sup>, he would have been better off to have had a huge millstone tied around his neck and been dumped into a deep ocean <sup>[Note: This “millstone” was a heavy circular stone rolled over grain to crush it and was moved by an animal walking in a circle]</sup>.

“It will be too bad for the world because of occasions that cause people to fall away <sup>[from God]</sup>! For it is inevitable that such occasions come, but it is too bad for that person who is responsible for causing it to happen.

If your hand or your foot becomes the occasion for you falling away <sup>[from God]</sup>, cut them off and throw them away. It would be better for you to enter <sup>[never ending]</sup> life disabled or crippled, rather than keeping both hands and both feet and being thrown into the never ending fire <sup>[i.e., because they caused you to fall away from God]</sup>.

And if your eye becomes the occasion for you falling away <sup>[from God]</sup>, gouge it out and throw it away, for it would be better for you to enter <sup>[never ending]</sup> life with <sup>[only]</sup> one eye, rather than keeping both eyes and being thrown into a fiery hell <sup>[i.e., because one of them caused you to fall away from God]</sup>.
Make sure you do not look down on any one of these little ones <sup>[i.e., humble followers of Christ]</sup>. For I tell you, their angels in heaven always see the face of my Father in heaven.
Some ancient manuscripts include this verse: For the Son of man came to save those who were lost.

What do you think? If anyone had a hundred sheep, and one of them wandered away, would he not leave the ninety-nine <sup>[safe ones]</sup> and go to the mountains looking for the one that wandered away?
Truly I tell you, if he happens to find it, he is happier over it than over the ninety-nine that had not wandered away.
In the same way, it is not the will of your Father in heaven that <sup>[a single]</sup> one of these little ones <sup>[i.e., humble followers of Christ]</sup> should be lost.

“And if your brother sins against you go and show him where he wronged you, <sup>[but]</sup> keep it between just the two of you. If he listens to you <sup>[i.e., accepts your reasoning and repents]</sup>, you have won your brother <sup>[back]</sup>.
But if he does not listen <sup>[to your reasoning]</sup>, take one or two other people with you, so that two or three witnesses can verify every word <sup>[that was said]</sup>.

And if he refuses to listen to them <sup>[i.e., the witnesses]</sup>, explain the <sup>[whole]</sup> matter to the church <sup>[i.e., probably a specially called meeting of mature Christians to resolve the matter]</sup>. And if he refuses to listen to the church also, <sup>[i.e., to acknowledge his sin and repent of it]</sup>, consider him like an <sup>[unconverted]</sup> Gentile or tax collector <sup>[i.e., do not have fellowship with him]</sup>.

Truly I tell you, whatever <sup>[truth]</sup> you disciples require <sup>[of people to believe and obey]</sup> on earth will have <sup>[already]</sup> been required <sup>[by God]</sup> in heaven. And whatever <sup>[truth]</sup> you do not require <sup>[of people to believe and obey]</sup> on earth, will not have <sup>[already]</sup> been required <sup>[by God]</sup> in heaven.
I tell you again, if two of you agree <sup>[here]</sup> on earth concerning anything you ask for <sup>[i.e., in prayer]</sup>, God in heaven will grant it to you.
For I am in the presence of two or three <sup>[disciples]</sup> wherever they are gathered in my name.”
Then Peter came and said to Jesus, “Lord, how often must I forgive my brother when he sins against me? As many as seven times?”
Jesus replied, “I tell you, not <sup>[just]</sup> up to seven times, but up to seventy times seven <sup>[i.e., a large indefinite number of times]</sup>.
“Therefore, the kingdom of heaven is similar to a certain king who decided to settle his account with his servants.

When he began figuring, one of his servants, who owed him a huge sum of money <sup>[Note: The amount is variously estimated to be in the multiple millions of dollars in 2005]</sup>, was brought to him.

But since he did not have enough <sup>[money]</sup> to pay <sup>[what he owed]</sup>, his master ordered that he, his wife, his children and all his possessions be sold <sup>[in order]</sup> to pay <sup>[his debt]</sup>.

The servant then fell to the ground <sup>[before his master]</sup> and humbly implored him, saying, ‘Master, <sup>[please]</sup> be patient with me and I will repay you everything <sup>[I owe]</sup>.’
And the master of that servant felt deep pity <sup>[for him]</sup>, so released him <sup>[from custody]</sup> and canceled his debt.

But that <sup>[same]</sup> servant went out and found one of his fellow-servants who owed him a small sum of money <sup>[Note: The amount was about 100 twelve hour days of a farm laborer’s pay or about $10,800 in 2005]</sup>. He grabbed him by the throat, saying, ‘Pay <sup>[me]</sup> what you owe.’
His fellow-servant fell <sup>[on the ground before him]</sup> and begged him, saying, ‘<sup>[Please]</sup> be patient with me and I will repay you <sup>[all I owe you]</sup>.’
But he would not do it. Instead, he took him and had him thrown into prison until he paid <sup>[him]</sup> all he owed.
When his fellow-servants saw what he had done, they were very upset and went and told their master everything that had happened.
Then his master called for him and said to him, ‘You wicked servant! I canceled all of your debt because you begged me to.
Should you not also have had pity on your fellow-servant just as I pitied you?’
His master then became furious and turned him over <sup>[to the authorities]</sup> to be punished until he paid all that he owed.
In the same way my heavenly Father will also deal with you <sup>[i.e., punish you]</sup> unless every one of you forgives his brother from the heart.”
And so when Jesus had finished speaking He left Galilee and came to the region of Judea, on the east side of the Jordan River.
Large crowds followed Him there and were healed by Him.
Some Pharisees came to Him, <sup>[attempting]</sup> to put Him to a test. They said, “Is it permissible by the Law of Moses for a man to divorce his wife for any reason <sup>[he chooses]</sup>?”
And He answered them, “Have you not read <sup>[Gen. 1:27; 5:2]</sup> that when God created <sup>[mankind as]</sup> male and female,

He said <sup>[Gen. 2:24]</sup>, ‘For this reason <sup>[i.e., since He made one woman for one man]</sup>, a man will leave <sup>[the home of]</sup> his father and mother and will cling to his wife, <sup>[so that]</sup> the two of them will <sup>[then]</sup> become one flesh <sup>[i.e., united in such a close relationship as to constitute one body]</sup>?’

So, <sup>[upon becoming husband and wife]</sup> they no longer function <sup>[entirely]</sup> as two persons, but <sup>[as]</sup> one body. Therefore, those whom God <sup>[so]</sup> joins together <sup>[in the marriage bond]</sup>, no person must <sup>[ever]</sup> separate.”
They replied, “Why then did Moses order <sup>[a man]</sup> to provide a legal divorce decree <sup>[Deut. 24:1]</sup>, and then divorce his wife?”

Jesus said to them, “Moses permitted you to divorce your wives <sup>[only]</sup> because of the rebellious spirits you <sup>[Jews]</sup> had. But this was not how it was <sup>[intended to be]</sup> from the beginning <sup>[i.e., when God created one woman for one man]</sup>.

And I tell you, whoever divorces his wife and marries another woman commits sexual unfaithfulness <sup>[toward her]</sup>, unless the reason for the divorce was sexual unfaithfulness <sup>[on his wife’s part]</sup>. And the person who marries a woman who has been divorced commits sexual sin with her <sup>[i.e., because she is still rightfully married to her first husband]</sup>.”
His disciples <sup>[then]</sup> said to Jesus, “If this is the way it has to be between a man and his wife, it is not worth getting married.”
But He replied, “Not all men can accept such an arrangement <sup>[i.e., not marrying]</sup>, but it is <sup>[only]</sup> for those who have the capacity for accepting it.

For there are eunuchs <sup>[i.e., men who do not have normal sexual activity]</sup> who are born that way; then there are eunuchs who were made that way by undergoing a surgical operation; then there are <sup>[also]</sup> eunuchs who purposely chose to avoid normal sexual activity for the sake of <sup>[ministering in]</sup> the kingdom of heaven. Whoever can accept such an arrangement <sup>[i.e., of not marrying]</sup>, should do so.”

Then some little children were brought to Jesus so he could pray and place His hands on them <sup>[i.e., to bestow a blessing on them]</sup>. But His disciples spoke harshly to the people <sup>[for doing this]</sup>.
But Jesus said, “Allow these little children to come to me and stop trying to prevent them. For the kingdom of heaven belongs to such <sup>[humble ones]</sup> as these.” <sup>[See 18:4]</sup>.
So, He placed His hands on them <sup>[i.e., to bestow a blessing]</sup> and <sup>[then]</sup> left that place.
Then a rich, young man <sup>[See verse 22]</sup> came to Him and said, “Teacher, what good deed should I do in order to have never ending life?”

Jesus answered him, “Why do you ask me about what is good? There is <sup>[only]</sup> One who is good <sup>[i.e., God]</sup>. But if you want to enter <sup>[never ending]</sup> life, <sup>[then]</sup> obey His commandments.”
The young man asked Him, “Which <sup>[ones]</sup>?”Jesus answered, “You must not murder. You must not be sexually unfaithful to your mate. You must not steal. You must not give false testimony.
Show honor to your father and mother. And you must love your neighbor the same as you love yourself.”
The young man said to Him, “I have <sup>[already]</sup> been observing all of these commandments. What do I <sup>[still]</sup> lack?”
Jesus replied, “If you want to be complete, go and sell your possessions and give <sup>[the money]</sup> to poor people; then become my follower and you will have treasure in heaven.”
But when the young man heard these words, he went away saddened, for he had many possessions.
Jesus said to His disciples, “Truly I tell you, it is difficult for a rich person to enter the kingdom of heaven.
And again I tell you, it is <sup>[actually]</sup> easier for a camel to pass through the eye of a needle, than for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God.”
When the disciples heard this, they were utterly amazed, saying, “Who then can <sup>[possibly]</sup> be saved?”
Jesus looked at them and said, “This would be impossible with men, but everything is possible with God.”
Peter then answered Him, “Look, we have left everything <sup>[i.e., homes, jobs, family, etc.]</sup>, to follow you. What will we get for it?”

Jesus said to them, “Truly I tell you, <sup>[since]</sup> you have become my followers, in the time of new beginnings <sup>[i.e., the church age]</sup>, when the Son of man sits on His glorious throne, you apostles will also sit on twelve thrones, judging <sup>[i.e., through their writings]</sup> the twelve tribes of Israel <sup>[i.e., God’s people]</sup>.

And every person who has left his house, or brothers, or sisters, or father, or mother, or children or property, for my name’s sake <sup>[i.e., to become a servant of the Lord]</sup>, will receive <sup>[back]</sup> a hundred times as much <sup>[in this life]</sup> and will <sup>[also]</sup> inherit never ending life.
But many will be last <sup>[i.e., in importance and blessings]</sup> who <sup>[seem to]</sup> be first, and those <sup>[who seem to be]</sup> first will <sup>[end up]</sup> being last.”
“For the kingdom of heaven is similar to a farmer who went out early one morning to hire laborers for his vineyard.

When he had agreed with the workers <sup>[on wages]</sup> for the usual farm laborer’s pay for a day’s work, he <sup>[then]</sup> sent them to work in his vineyard. <sup>[Note: The coin mentioned here would amount to about $9.00, based on the average farm laborer’s hourly wage in 2005. <sup>[National Agricultural Statistics Service, U.S. Dept. of Agriculture, Feb. 2005]</sup>. This coin also forms the basis for calculating all other monetary references in the New Testament]</sup>.
He went out about nine o’clock in the morning and saw other unemployed laborers standing <sup>[around]</sup> in the open shopping market.
He said to them, ‘You men also go and work in my vineyard, and I will pay you whatever is right.’ So, they went to work.
<sup>[Then]</sup> he went out again about noon and again at three o’clock in the afternoon and made the same offer.

<sup>[Finally]</sup> he went out <sup>[once more]</sup> at about five o’clock in the afternoon and found <sup>[still]</sup> other men standing around. He said, ‘Why have you been standing around all day and not working?’
They replied, ‘Because no one has hired us.’ He said to them, ‘You men also may go to work in my vineyard.’
And when evening came the owner of the vineyard said to his foreman, ‘Call the workers <sup>[in]</sup> and pay them their wages, beginning with the last <sup>[one hired, then]</sup> to the first.’
So, when those hired about five o’clock in the afternoon came in, each one received the usual pay for one full day’s work. <sup>[See note on verse 2]</sup>.

Then when those who were hired first came <sup>[to get paid]</sup>, they expected to receive more money <sup>[than those hired later]</sup>, but each one received the usual pay for one full day’s work also.
And when they received it, they complained to the farmer,

saying, ‘These men <sup>[you hired]</sup> last have worked only one hour and you gave them wages equal to ours when we carried the heaviest load of the day’s work and <sup>[put up with]</sup> the scorching heat <sup>[all day long]</sup>.’
But he answered one of them, saying, ‘Friend, I have not done anything wrong to you. Did you not agree with me to accept the usual pay for one full day’s work?
Take what belongs to you and go on your way. I want to give this last person <sup>[hired]</sup> the same wages I gave you.
Is it against the law for me to do what I want with my own money? Or, is it that your eye is envious because I choose to be so generous?’
So, the <sup>[person who seems to be]</sup> last will be first <sup>[i.e., in importance and blessings]</sup>, but the <sup>[person who seems to be]</sup> first will <sup>[end up]</sup> being last.”
As Jesus was going up to Jerusalem He took the twelve apostles aside. As they traveled along He said to them,
“Now look, we are going up to Jerusalem and <sup>[while there]</sup> the Son of man will be turned over to the leading priests and experts in the Law of Moses. They will condemn Him to death,
and turn Him over to the <sup>[unconverted]</sup> Gentiles, who will mock, whip and crucify Him; then on the third day He will be raised up.”
Then the mother of Zebedee’s sons <sup>[i.e., James and John]</sup> came to Jesus with her sons and, bowing down in reverence before Him, made a request of Him.
He responded to her, “What would you like me to do for you?” She answered; “Appoint my two sons to sit at your right side and at your left in your <sup>[coming]</sup> kingdom.”

But Jesus replied to her, “You <sup>[really]</sup> do not know what you are asking for. Are you able to drink the cup <sup>[i.e., of suffering]</sup> that I am about to drink?” They <sup>[i.e., James, John and their mother. See Mark 10:35ff]</sup> answered, “<sup>[Yes]</sup> we are able to.”

He said to them, “Indeed you will drink my cup <sup>[of suffering]</sup>. But it is not my prerogative to appoint who sits at my right side and at my left, but rather these places are for those to whom it has been assigned by my Father.”
When the other ten apostles heard this, they became very upset with the two brothers.

Then Jesus called all the apostles to Him and said, “You are aware that rulers among the <sup>[unconverted]</sup> Gentiles lord it over their own people and their important men domineer over them <sup>[as well]</sup>.
But it will not be this way among you, for whoever would like to become important among you will become your servant.
And whoever would like to be first <sup>[in prominence]</sup> among you will be your slave.

Even so, the Son of man did not come to be served <sup>[by others]</sup> but to be the servant <sup>[of others]</sup>, and to sacrifice His life as a ransom price to purchase many people <sup>[i.e., back from Satan]</sup>.”
As they <sup>[i.e., the twelve apostles and Jesus]</sup> were leaving Jericho <sup>[Note: This was a town about eighteen miles northeast of Jerusalem]</sup>, a huge crowd followed them.
Just then, two blind men sitting along side of the road heard that Jesus was passing by that way. <sup>[So]</sup>, they shouted out, “Lord, take pity on us, son of David.”
The crowd spoke harshly to them, telling them to be quiet, but they shouted all the more, “Lord, have pity on us, son of David.”
Then Jesus stopped, and calling to them, said, “What do you want me to do for you?”
They answered, “Lord, we want to have our sight back.”
Jesus felt deep pity for them, so He touched their eyes and immediately their sight was restored, and they became <sup>[His]</sup> followers.

And when they <sup>[i.e., Jesus and the twelve apostles]</sup> approached Jerusalem and came close to Bethphage, near the Mount of Olives <sup>[Note: This was about three-fourths of a mile east of Jerusalem]</sup>, Jesus sent two disciples on ahead,
saying to them, “Go into the village there and immediately you will find a donkey tied up with her colt. Untie them and bring them to me.
If anyone says something to you, tell them, ‘The Lord needs these animals,’ and he will immediately send them.
Now this will happen in order to fulfill what was spoken by the prophet, saying,

<sup>[Zech. 9:9]</sup>, ‘You should tell the daughter of Zion <sup>[i.e., the people of Jerusalem]</sup>, look, your King is coming to you. He is gentle and <sup>[will be]</sup> riding on a donkey, <sup>[even]</sup> on a colt, the offspring of the donkey.’”
And the disciples went and did exactly what Jesus had ordered them to do.
They brought the donkey and its colt and spread their clothing on them, and Jesus mounted the colt.
A large part of the crowd spread their clothing on the roadway; others cut branches from trees and spread them <sup>[also]</sup> on the roadway.

And the crowds that walked ahead and followed behind Him shouted, “Hosanna to the son of David. <sup>[Note: The Syriac word ‘Hosanna’ originally meant ‘save now’ but came to be used as an expression of welcome, praise, blessing or acclamation]</sup>. May He, who comes in the name <sup>[i.e., by the authority]</sup> of the Lord, be blessed. Hosanna in the highest <sup>[i.e., may this blessing reach to the highest heaven]</sup>.”
And when He had come to Jerusalem, the entire city was stirred up; they were asking, “Who is this man?”
The crowds answered, “He is the prophet Jesus, from Nazareth in Galilee.”

Then Jesus entered the Temple of God and drove out all those who bought and sold things there. He upset the tables of the cashiers <sup>[i.e., those who exchanged foreign coins]</sup> and the benches of those who sold pigeons <sup>[for sacrifices]</sup>.

And He said to them, “It is written <sup>[Isa. 56:7]</sup>, ‘My house <sup>[i.e., the Temple]</sup> will be called a house for prayer’, but <sup>[Jer. 7:11 says]</sup>, ‘You have made it a hideout for thieves.’”
<sup>[Then]</sup> blind and crippled people came to Him in the Temple and He healed them.

But when the leading priests and the experts in the Law of Moses saw the wonderful things <sup>[i.e., miracles]</sup> He was performing and <sup>[heard]</sup> the children shouting in the Temple, “Hosanna to the son of David,” <sup>[See note at verse 9]</sup>, they were filled with anger <sup>[i.e., over the acclaim being given to Jesus]</sup>.

They said to Jesus, “Do you hear what these [children] are saying [about you]?” Jesus answered them, “Yes, <sup>[I do]</sup>. Have you not read <sup>[in Psa. 8:2]</sup> ‘You have ordained that praise would come from the mouths of infants and small children?’”

He then left them and went out of the city <sup>[of Jerusalem]</sup> to Bethany <sup>[Note: This was a small village near Bethphage]</sup> and stayed there <sup>[i.e., probably at the home of Mary, Martha and Lazarus. See John 11:1]</sup>.
Now in the morning, as Jesus returned to the city <sup>[of Jerusalem]</sup>, He became hungry.

Upon seeing a fig tree along side of the road, He approached it <sup>[i.e., expecting to find fruit on it]</sup>, but found nothing but leaves. He said to the tree, “There will not be fruit on you ever again.” Immediately the fig tree withered up.
When His disciples saw this, they marveled, saying, “How could the fig tree wither up so suddenly?”

Jesus answered, “Truly I tell you, if you have faith, without doubting, you will not only <sup>[be able to]</sup> do what was done to this fig tree, but even if you tell this mountain, ‘Be lifted up and thrown into the ocean,’ it will happen.
And everything you ask for in prayer you will receive, if you have <sup>[enough]</sup> faith.”

When He had come into the Temple, the leading priests and elders of the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> people came to Him while He was teaching, and said, “By what authority are you doing these things <sup>[i.e., miracles and teaching]</sup>? And who gave you this authority?”
Jesus answered them; “Let me also ask you a question. If you answer it, then I will tell you by what authority I am doing these things.

Where did the immersion of John come from? From heaven or from men?” And they discussed this among themselves, saying, “If we say, ‘from heaven,’ He will say to us, ‘Why then did you not believe his message?’
But if we say, ‘from men,’ we are afraid the crowd will oppose us because they all consider John to be a prophet.”

So, they answered Jesus, “We do not know <sup>[where John got the authority for his immersion]</sup>.” Jesus then replied to them, “<sup>[Then]</sup> neither will I tell you by what authority I am doing these things <sup>[i.e., miracles and teaching]</sup>.
But what do you think <sup>[about this]</sup>? A man had two sons; He went to the first one and said, ‘Son, go to work in my vineyard today.’
The son answered, ‘No, I will not,’ but later changed his mind and went.
He went to the second son and said the same thing. That son replied, ‘<sup>[Yes]</sup>, sir, I will,’ <sup>[but]</sup> then did not go.

Which of these two sons did what his father wanted?” They answered, “The first one.” Jesus said to them, “Truly I tell you, tax collectors and prostitutes <sup>[will]</sup> enter the kingdom of God ahead of you.

For John came to show you the way to be right with God, but you would not believe his message. Yet the tax collectors and prostitutes believed it, and when you saw this, even then you did not change your minds so as to believe his message.

“Listen to another parable <sup>[i.e., a brief story used to illustrate His teaching]</sup>: There was the owner of a farm who planted a vineyard and built a fence around it. He constructed a grape squeezing device, built a <sup>[lookout]</sup> tower <sup>[near it]</sup>, leased it to tenant farmers and then went to another country.
And when the season for harvesting the grapes approached, the owner of the farm sent his slaves to the tenant farmers to arrange for delivery of his crop.
But the tenant farmers took the farm owner’s slaves and beat one, killed another and stoned another.
The owner of the farm then sent additional slaves and they were treated the same way.
Then later on he sent his own son to the tenant farmers, saying, ‘<sup>[Surely]</sup> they will treat my son with respect.’
But when the tenant farmers saw the son, they said to one another, ‘This is the heir <sup>[to the vineyard]</sup>. Come on; let us kill him so we can take over his inheritance.’
So, they took him and threw him out of the vineyard and killed him.
When the owner of the vineyard returns, what <sup>[do you think]</sup> he will do to those tenant farmers?
They answered him; “He will bring terrible destruction on those evil men and will lease the vineyard to other tenant farmers who will deliver the crops to him each harvest time.”

Jesus <sup>[then]</sup> said to the leading priests and elders of the Jewish people, “Did you not ever read in the Scriptures <sup>[Psa. 118:22-23]</sup>, ‘The building block rejected by the builders is the same one that was made the principal stone by which the entire building was aligned. This was planned by the Lord and is a marvelous thing to us’?
Therefore I tell you, the kingdom of God will be taken from you <sup>[Jews]</sup> and will be given to a nation <sup>[i.e., the Gentiles]</sup> that will produce the fruit of the kingdom.
And the person who falls on this stone will be broken to pieces, but whoever it falls on will be scattered like dust.”
And when the leading priests and Pharisees heard Jesus’ parables, they realized He was speaking about them.
When they attempted to arrest Him, <sup>[they hesitated because]</sup> they feared <sup>[what]</sup> the crowds <sup>[might do]</sup>, since they considered Jesus a prophet.
Jesus again spoke in parables to them <sup>[i.e., the Jewish leaders]</sup>, saying,
“The kingdom of heaven is similar to a certain king who held a wedding reception for his son.
He sent his servants out to call those who had been invited to the wedding reception, but they refused to come.

Again he sent out other servants saying, ‘Tell those who were invited, look, I have already prepared the dinner; my cattle and <sup>[other]</sup> well-fattened animals have been butchered and everything is ready. <sup>[Now]</sup> come to the wedding reception dinner.’
But they made fun of it, and went on about their business, one to his farm, another to his store.
But the rest of them grabbed his servants, physically abused them and <sup>[finally]</sup> killed them.
The king became furious and sent his army to destroy these murderers and burn their city.
Then he told his servants, ‘The wedding is ready, but those who were invited to it <sup>[proved they]</sup> were not worthy <sup>[to attend]</sup>.
Now go to the main crossroads of the highways and invite everyone you find there to the wedding reception.’
So, his servants went out to the highways and gathered in all those who could be found, both bad and good, so that the wedding <sup>[reception]</sup> was full of guests.
But when the king came in <sup>[to the dining hall]</sup> to meet the guests, he noticed a man who was not wearing a <sup>[proper]</sup> wedding outfit.
He said to the man, ‘Friend, how did you get in here without wearing the proper wedding outfit?’ And he could not say a word.
Then the king said to his servants, ‘Tie him up, hand and foot, and throw him into <sup>[a place of]</sup> outer darkness where there will be crying and excruciating pain.’
For many people are called <sup>[by God]</sup> but <sup>[only those]</sup> few <sup>[who respond]</sup> are chosen <sup>[by Him]</sup>.”
Then the Pharisees conspired as to how they could trap Jesus by what He said.

So, they sent their disciples, <sup>[along]</sup> with the Herodians to Him <sup>[Note: These were members of a political party favoring King Herod]</sup>, saying, “Teacher, we know that you are sincere, and that you teach the truth about God’s way. You do not care for <sup>[i.e., do not fear or give in to]</sup> anyone or allow the position of any person to influence you.
So, tell us what you think. Is it lawful to pay taxes to Caesar, or not?”
But Jesus perceived their wicked intention and said, “Why are you putting me to a test, you hypocrites?
Show me the coin used for paying taxes.” And they brought to Him a coin <sup>[Note: This coin was equivalent to one twelve hour day’s wages of a farm laborer, or about $108 in 2005]</sup>.
He said to them, “Whose image and inscription are on this coin?”
They answered Him, “Caesar’s.” Then He replied, “Pay to Caesar whatever belongs to Caesar and pay to God whatever belongs to God.”
And when they heard this, they marveled; then left Him and went away.
Some Sadducees <sup>[Note: These were the Jewish party that denied there was a resurrection]</sup> came to Jesus that day and asked Him,

“Teacher, Moses said <sup>[in Deut. 25:5]</sup> that when a man dies without having had any children, his brother must marry his widow and father children by her, who would <sup>[then]</sup> be considered his <sup>[dead]</sup> brother’s.
Now among our people there were <sup>[these]</sup> seven brothers; the first one married and <sup>[later]</sup> died without having any children, leaving his widow to <sup>[marry]</sup> his brother.
The same thing happened to the second and the third and <sup>[on through to]</sup> the seventh <sup>[brother]</sup>.
And after they all died, the woman <sup>[also]</sup> passed away.
Now whose wife will she be in the resurrected state, since all seven of them were married to her?”
But Jesus answered them, “You are mistaken <sup>[in your reasoning]</sup>, not knowing <sup>[what]</sup> the Scriptures <sup>[teach]</sup> or <sup>[how much]</sup> power God has.
For in the resurrected state men do not get married, nor are women given away in marriage, but <sup>[all]</sup> are like angels in heaven.
But concerning the resurrection of the dead, have you not read what God spoke to you, saying, <sup>[Ex. 3:6]</sup>

‘I am Abraham’s God, and Isaac’s God and Jacob’s God.’ <sup>[So]</sup>, He is not God to those who are <sup>[physically]</sup> dead, but <sup>[He is]</sup> God to those who are <sup>[still]</sup> alive.”
And when the crowds heard this they were astounded at His teaching.
But when the Pharisees heard that Jesus had silenced <sup>[the objections of]</sup> the Sadducees, they gathered together <sup>[i.e., to continue plotting against Jesus. See verse 15]</sup>.
One of them, a teacher of the Law of Moses, asked Him a question <sup>[in an attempt]</sup> to test Him.
“Teacher, which one is the greatest commandment in the Law of Moses?” he asked.
Jesus replied, <sup>[Deut. 6:5]</sup> “‘You must love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul and with all your mind.’
This is the foremost and greatest commandment.
A second one, similar to it is <sup>[Lev. 19:18]</sup>, ‘You must love your neighbor the same way that you love yourself.’
The entire Law of Moses and the prophets hinge on these two commandments.”
Now while the Pharisees were assembled together, Jesus asked them this question,
“What do you think about the Christ <sup>[i.e., God’s specially chosen one]</sup>? Whose son do you think He is?” They replied, “<sup>[He is]</sup> the son of David.”
He said to them, “How is it <sup>[then]</sup> that David, by <sup>[inspiration of]</sup> the Holy Spirit, calls Him Lord? saying,

<sup>[Psa. 110:1]</sup> ‘The Lord <sup>[i.e., God]</sup> said to my <sup>[i.e., David’s]</sup> Lord <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup>, sit at my right side until I put your enemies <sup>[in full subjection]</sup> beneath your feet.’
If David then could call Him <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup> Lord, how could He be his son?”
No one was able to answer <sup>[Jesus’ reasoning]</sup> with <sup>[so much as]</sup> a word, and no one dared to ask Him any more questions from that day onward.
Then Jesus spoke to the crowds and to His disciples,
saying, “The experts in the Law of Moses and the Pharisees teach by the authority of Moses.
Therefore, observe and practice everything they urge upon you, but do not follow their example <sup>[of living]</sup> because they say <sup>[the right things]</sup> but do not do them.
Yes, they require people to bear heavy and difficult responsibilities, and place them on people’s shoulders <sup>[to carry out]</sup>, but they themselves will not lift a finger to do them.
But they do all their deeds just to be seen by people. They wear elaborate Scripture texts displayed on their clothing and enlarge <sup>[conspicuously]</sup> the fringes of their robes.

They love to recline at the head place at dinner tables <sup>[Note: This referred to the practice of lying down on their left side on a couch next to the dinner table, and leaning on their left elbow, while eating with their right hand]</sup>, and to occupy the principal seats in the synagogues.
They love the <sup>[special]</sup> greetings they receive at the open shopping markets and to be addressed as ‘Rabbi’ <sup>[Note: This word signified ‘great’ or ‘master’]</sup>.
But you should not assume the title ‘Rabbi,’ for <sup>[only]</sup> one is your teacher <sup>[i.e., Jesus Himself]</sup> and all of you are <sup>[simply]</sup> brothers.
And do not call any man on earth ‘father,’ <sup>[i.e., in a religious sense]</sup>, for the <sup>[only]</sup> one who is your Father is in heaven.
Neither assume the title ‘Master’ <sup>[i.e., teacher]</sup>, for <sup>[only]</sup> one is your Master, the Christ <sup>[i.e., God’s specially chosen one]</sup>.
But the greatest person among you will be your servant.
And whoever exalts himself <sup>[as important]</sup> will be humbled, and whoever humbles himself will be exalted <sup>[as important]</sup>.

“But it is too bad for you hypocritical experts in the Law of Moses and <sup>[you]</sup> Pharisees! <sup>[It is]</sup> because you withhold the kingdom of heaven from people, for you do not enter it yourselves, nor <sup>[do you]</sup> allow those who are trying to enter it to get in.
This verse is absent from the oldest manuscripts

“It is too bad for you hypocritical experts in the Law of Moses and <sup>[you]</sup> Pharisees! For you travel over land and sea <sup>[in hope of]</sup> making one person a proselyte <sup>[i.e., a convert to the Jewish religion]</sup>. And when you do convert one, you make him twice as deserving of hell as yourselves.

“It is too bad for you blind leaders who say that whoever takes an oath ‘by the Temple,’ it does not <sup>[really]</sup> mean anything, but whoever takes an oath ‘by the gold of the Temple,’ he is obligated to <sup>[fulfill]</sup> the oath.
You foolish, blind people! Which is greater, the gold or the Temple that makes its gold sacred?

And <sup>[you say]</sup>, whoever takes an oath ‘by the Altar,’ it does not <sup>[really]</sup> mean anything, but whoever takes an oath ‘by the gift on the Altar,’ he is obligated to <sup>[fulfill]</sup> the oath.
You blind people! Which is more important, the gift or the Altar that makes the gift sacred?
The person, therefore, who takes an oath ‘by the Altar,’ <sup>[not only]</sup> swears by it, but <sup>[also]</sup> by everything on it.
And the person who takes an oath ‘by the Temple,’ <sup>[not only]</sup> swears by it, but <sup>[also]</sup> by Him <sup>[i.e., God]</sup> who lives there.
And the person who takes an oath ‘by heaven,’ swears ‘by the throne of God,’ and <sup>[also]</sup> by Him <sup>[i.e., God]</sup> who sits on it.

'It is too bad for you hypocritical experts in the Law of Moses and <sup>[you]</sup> Pharisees! For you give a tenth of your mint, dill and cumin <sup>[i.e., small garden herbs used for seasoning or fragrance]</sup>, and <sup>[yet]</sup> neglect the heavier responsibilities required by the law, <sup>[such as]</sup> justice, mercy and faith. You should have done these <sup>[“lighter”]</sup> things and not neglected to do the other <sup>[“heavier”]</sup> things <sup>[as well]</sup>.
You blind leaders, who strain out a <sup>[tiny]</sup> gnat <sup>[i.e., from your drinking water]</sup> and <sup>[yet]</sup> swallow a <sup>[whole]</sup> camel.

'It is too bad for you hypocritical experts in the Law of Moses and <sup>[you]</sup> Pharisees! For you <sup>[carefully]</sup> wash the outside of the cup and dish, but inside they <sup>[i.e., such evil people]</sup> are full of greed and uncontrolled desire.
You blind Pharisees! First wash the inside of the cup and dish, so <sup>[then]</sup> the outside will be clean also.

'It is too bad for you hypocritical experts in the Law of Moses and <sup>[you]</sup> Pharisees! For you are like white­washed tombs, used for burial <sup>[sites]</sup>, which appear beautiful on the outside, but on the inside are full of the bones and decaying flesh of dead people.
Even so, you also appear on the outside to be doing right in front of people, but on the inside you are full of hypocrisy and sin.
'It is too bad for you hypocritical experts in the Law of Moses and <sup>[you]</sup> Pharisees! For you construct burial sites for the prophets and decorate the tombs of people who were righteous.
And you say, ‘If <sup>[only]</sup> we had lived in the days of our forefathers, we <sup>[certainly]</sup> would not have participated with them in <sup>[shedding]</sup> the blood of the prophets.’

<sup>[But by saying this]</sup> you are witnesses <sup>[against]</sup> yourselves, that you are the sons of those who murdered the prophets <sup>[i.e., indicating your approval of their actions]</sup>.
So <sup>[then]</sup>, fulfill the pattern of behavior begun by your forefathers.
You snakes! You children of snakes! How do you expect to escape being judged <sup>[and condemned]</sup> in hell?

Therefore look, I am sending to you prophets, wise men and scholarly teachers. You will murder and crucify some of them; you will whip some of them in your synagogues and pursue them from town to town.

<sup>[This will be done]</sup> so that on you may fall the guilt for shedding on the ground the blood of all those innocent people, from the blood of righteous Abel to that of Barachiah’s son Zachariah, whom you murdered between the Sanctuary and the Altar <sup>[i.e., in the Temple area]</sup>.
Truly I tell you, this generation will bear <sup>[the guilt for]</sup> all these things.

“O, Jerusalem, Jerusalem, <sup>[you people]</sup> who murdered the prophets and stoned those who were sent to you! How often I have wanted to gather you people together, even as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, but you were not willing!
Look, your house <sup>[i.e., the Jewish Temple]</sup> is being abandoned <sup>[by me (?)]</sup>.
For I tell you, you will not see me from now on until <sup>[the time when]</sup> you will be saying, ‘Praise the one who is coming in the name of the Lord!’”
As Jesus left the Temple, His disciples approached Him as He was walking along the road and pointed out the Temple buildings.
He responded to them by saying, “Do you see all these structures? Truly I tell you, there will not be one stone left on another here that will not be thrown down.”

And as He was sitting on the Mount of Olives, His disciples came to Him privately, saying, “Tell us, when will these things <sup>[you just spoke of]</sup> happen? And what will be the sign that indicates your presence and the end of the <sup>[present]</sup> age?”
And Jesus answered them, “Pay attention so that no one leads you astray <sup>[from the truth]</sup>.

For many <sup>[false teachers]</sup> will come, claiming to be me, and saying, ‘I am the Christ <sup>[i.e., God’s specially chosen one]</sup>’ and they will lead many people away <sup>[from the truth]</sup>.

And you will hear of wars <sup>[going on]</sup> and rumors of <sup>[other]</sup> wars <sup>[pending]</sup>. Do not worry, for such things must necessarily happen. But the end has not yet come. <sup>[Note: By “the end” here Jesus probably alludes to the downfall of Jerusalem, in AD 70, with its attending destruction of the Temple buildings]</sup>.
For nations will wage war against one another, and kingdoms will attack one another. And famines and earthquakes will occur at various places <sup>[in the world]</sup>.
But all these things are <sup>[only]</sup> the beginning of terrible times.
At that time people will turn you over <sup>[to the authorities]</sup> for persecution and will <sup>[even]</sup> kill you. And people from all countries will hate you for being loyal to me.
And then many people will fall away <sup>[from God]</sup> and they will turn one another over <sup>[i.e., to the authorities for punishment]</sup>, and they will hate one another.
And many false prophets will appear and will lead many people away <sup>[from the truth]</sup>.
And because sin will abound, many people will grow cold in their love <sup>[for one another]</sup>.
But the person who holds out <sup>[i.e., remains faithful to God]</sup> until the end <sup>[i.e., of this time of severe persecution]</sup> will be saved <sup>[from destruction]</sup>.

And this good news concerning the kingdom <sup>[of heaven]</sup> will be preached to people throughout the world as a testimony <sup>[of God’s message]</sup> to all nations. Then, <sup>[after that]</sup> the end <sup>[i.e., of Jerusalem, the Temple, etc.]</sup> will come.

“Therefore, when you see that disgusting thing which causes total destruction <sup>[i.e., the Roman armies. See Luke 21:20]</sup>, which Daniel the prophet spoke about <sup>[Dan. 9:27; 11:31; 12:11]</sup>, standing in the sacred place <sup>[i.e., Jerusalem and especially the Temple area]</sup>, (let the reader understand <sup>[what is meant by this]</sup>),
then those <sup>[of you]</sup> in Judea are to run away into the <sup>[nearby]</sup> mountains.

<sup>[Also]</sup>, the person who is on a housetop <sup>[Note: This was a flat area where people retired for rest, prayer, etc., with its stairway on the outside]</sup>, is not to take any of the belongings from his house as he comes down <sup>[to flee]</sup>.
The person who is in a field <sup>[i.e., doing farm work]</sup> is not <sup>[even]</sup> to return <sup>[to his house]</sup> to get his coat.
It will be too bad for those who are pregnant and who are nursing babies in those days.

And pray that it will not be in the winter or on a Sabbath day when you will have to flee <sup>[from the city]</sup>. <sup>[Note: Jewish Sabbath day restrictions on travel (only three-fifths of a mile) and city gate closures would make leaving the city nearly impossible]</sup>.
There will be great trouble <sup>[during these times]</sup>, such as had never been from the beginning of time up to the present, or ever will be <sup>[in the days to come]</sup>.

And if those days had not been as short as they were no one would have been saved <sup>[i.e., from the devastating destruction]</sup>. But for the sake of the elect <sup>[i.e., God’s people]</sup>, those days will be short.
Then if anyone says to you, ‘Look, here is Christ,’ or ‘There <sup>[he is]</sup>,’ do not believe him.

For false christs and false prophets will appear and they will perform great <sup>[miraculous]</sup> signs and <sup>[supernatural]</sup> wonders in order to lead astray even the elect <sup>[i.e., God’s people]</sup>, if possible.
You see, I have told you <sup>[all this]</sup> ahead of time.

Therefore, if people say to you, ‘Look, he <sup>[i.e., Christ. See verse 23]</sup> is in the desert,’ do not go out there. <sup>[Or if they say]</sup>, ‘Look, he is in an inside room,’ do not believe him.
For just like lightning that appears in the east can be seen even in the west, so it will be with the presence <sup>[i.e., coming]</sup> of the Son of man.

Wherever there is a dead body, <sup>[you can expect to see]</sup> vultures gathered there. <sup>[Note: This may mean that such a significant event as the coming of Christ would surely not go unnoticed]</sup>.

“But immediately after the terrible trouble of those days, <sup>[i.e., the next major event on God’s calendar]</sup>, the sun will become dark, and <sup>[therefore]</sup> the moon will not <sup>[be able to]</sup> shed its <sup>[reflected]</sup> light. The stars will fall from the sky and the forces of the heavens will be shaken <sup>[Note: From the description of these same events in Luke 21:25-26, it is possible that this is figurative language for great calamities happening on the earth]</sup>.

Then the sign <sup>[i.e., signifying the coming]</sup> of the Son of man will appear in the sky, and all the nations of the earth will be stricken with grief as they see the Son of man coming in the clouds of the sky <sup>[See Acts 1:9-11]</sup> with power and great splendor.

And He will send out His angels <sup>[i.e., as messengers]</sup> with the sound of a great trumpet <sup>[See I Thess. 4:16]</sup> to gather His elect <sup>[i.e., God’s people]</sup> from all directions, <sup>[even]</sup> from throughout the whole world. <sup>[Note: See Mark 13:27, which suggests this thought by its use of ‘earth’ and ‘sky’]</sup>
“Now learn this parable <sup>[i.e., a brief analogy to illustrate a lesson]</sup> from the fig tree: When its tender sprouts appear and its leaves begin to develop, you know that summer is near.
In the same way also, when you see all these things happening, you <sup>[should]</sup> know that He <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup> is as near as your <sup>[front]</sup> door.
Truly I tell you, the people of this generation will not <sup>[all]</sup> die off before all these things <sup>[i.e., mentioned above]</sup> happen.
<sup>[Although]</sup> the sky and the earth will pass away <sup>[See II Pet. 3:10]</sup>, my words will not pass away <sup>[i.e., they will certainly come true]</sup>.
“But no one knows when that day or hour will come. Not even the angels in heaven or the Son <sup>[of God]</sup> know <sup>[when it will happen]</sup>, but only the Father <sup>[does]</sup>.
As it was during the days of Noah, so it will be when the presence of the Son of man <sup>[arrives]</sup>.

For in those days before the Flood, people were eating and drinking <sup>[i.e., partaking of ordinary meals]</sup>, men were getting married and women were being given away in marriage <sup>[right up]</sup> until the day Noah entered the ship.

And the people <sup>[i.e., of Noah’s day]</sup> did not know <sup>[when it would happen]</sup> until the Flood came and swept them all away. It will be the same way when the presence of the Son of man <sup>[arrives]</sup>.

At that time there will be two men <sup>[working]</sup> in a field; one will be taken <sup>[i.e., to be with the Lord. See I Thess. 4:17]</sup>, while the other one will be left <sup>[i.e., to be punished by the Lord. See II Thess. 1:7-9]</sup>.

Two women will be grinding grain at a millstone <sup>[Note: This was a hand-operated device where two persons sat across from each other and rotated a circular stone over kernels of grain]</sup>; one will be taken, while the other one will be left.
Therefore be alert, for you do not know on what day the Lord will come.
But you should know this: if the owner of a house had known when a thief was coming, he would have been watching so as not to allow his house to be broken into.
Therefore, you also should be ready, for the Son of man will come at a time when you least expect Him.
“Who then, is the trustworthy and sensible slave, to whom his master has assigned the management of his household to provide timely meals for its members?
That slave will be happy when his master returns to find him doing his job <sup>[well]</sup>.
Truly I tell you, he will put that slave in charge of his entire estate.
But if that slave, with evil intent, should think to himself, ‘My master will not be back very soon,’
and then becomes physically abusive to his fellow-slaves, and eats and drinks <sup>[at parties]</sup> with drunkards,
that slave’s master will return on a day that he least expects, and at an unknown hour.
And <sup>[that master]</sup> will whip him to shreds and will turn him over to suffer the same fate as hypocrites do. That fate will involve crying and excruciating pain.”

“Then the kingdom of heaven will be similar to ten bridesmaids who took their lamps and went out to meet the <sup>[bride and]</sup> groom. <sup>[Note: The picture here is that of a newly married couple returning from a festive reception to the groom’s home where they continued celebrating and were welcomed by a late evening wedding party]</sup>
Five of the bridesmaids were foolish and five were sensible.
The foolish ones did not take enough olive oil for their lamps with them,
but the sensible ones took <sup>[extra]</sup> oil in containers, along with their lamps.
Now when the <sup>[bride and]</sup> groom were late <sup>[in arriving]</sup>, the bridesmaids all got drowsy and fell asleep.
Then at midnight someone shouted, ‘Look, the <sup>[bride and]</sup> groom are here. Come on, let us go out and meet them.’
Then all the bridesmaids woke up and trimmed their lamps <sup>[i.e., removed the burnt wicks and replenished their lamps with oil]</sup>.
The foolish ones said to the sensible ones, ‘Give us some of your oil, because our lamps are going out.’
But the sensible ones answered, ‘There might not be enough for us and you, <sup>[so]</sup> instead, you should go to the store and buy some for yourselves.’

And while they were away <sup>[trying]</sup> to buy olive oil, the <sup>[bride and]</sup> groom arrived, and those who were ready <sup>[to welcome them]</sup> entered the <sup>[house where the]</sup> wedding reception <sup>[was being held]</sup> and the door was shut.
After that, the other bridesmaids also came asking, ‘Sir, Sir, <sup>[please]</sup> open the door for us!’
But he answered them, ‘Truly I tell you, I do not know you.’
Therefore, you should be watchful, for you do not know the day or hour <sup>[i.e., when the Son of man will return]</sup>.

“For the kingdom of heaven is similar to a man who planned to go on a trip to a distant country, so called his servants and entrusted to each of them a large sum of money <sup>[Note: The narrative that follows suggests that the man was turning over some of his assets to financial managers to invest for him]</sup>.

He gave one servant five talents <sup>[Note: This ‘talent’ was a large sum of money in the form of a certain weight of silver. It was the equivalent of sixty years of a farm laborer’s wages, or about $1,400,000 in 2005 income]</sup>. He gave two talents to another servant and one talent to another. He gave to each one an amount in keeping with his ability <sup>[to invest it wisely]</sup>, and then left on his trip.
The one with the five talents soon invested the money in business transactions which netted him five more.
In the same way the one who was given two talents made two more.
But the one who was given one talent went and dug a hole in the ground and buried his master’s money.
Now after a long time the master of these servants returned <sup>[from his trip]</sup> and proceeded to go over his accounts with each of them.
The one who had been given five talents came bringing another five and reported, ‘Master, you entrusted me with five talents. Look, I have earned five more with them.’

His master replied, ‘Well done, you are a good and trustworthy servant. You have been trustworthy over a <sup>[relatively]</sup> few things, so I will place you over many things. Come and share in your master’s joy.’
And the one who was given two talents came and reported, ‘Master, you entrusted me with two talents. Look, I have earned two more with them.’

His master said to him, ‘Well done, you are a good and trustworthy servant. You have been trustworthy over a <sup>[relatively]</sup> few things, <sup>[so]</sup> I will place you over many things. Come and share in your master’s joy.’

Then the one who was given <sup>[only]</sup> one talent came and reported, ‘Master, I knew you were a difficult man who reaped where you had not sown, and gathered a harvest where you had not scattered seed,
and I was afraid, so I went away and hid your talent in the ground. Look, you <sup>[can]</sup> have your own talent <sup>[back]</sup>.’
But his master said to him, ‘You wicked, lazy servant. <sup>[Since]</sup> you knew that I reaped where I had not sown and gathered where I had not scattered seed,
you should have therefore deposited my money in the bank <sup>[so]</sup> when I returned, <sup>[at least]</sup> I would have received my own money back, with interest.
Take the one talent away from him, therefore, and give it to the servant who had the ten talents.

For to every person who has <sup>[something]</sup>, more will be given, so he will have plenty. But from the person who has <sup>[virtually]</sup> nothing, even that <sup>[little bit]</sup> he has will be taken away <sup>[from him]</sup>.
Throw out that worthless servant into the <sup>[place of]</sup> outer darkness, where there will be crying and excruciating pain.
“But when the Son of man comes <sup>[back]</sup> in <sup>[all]</sup> His splendor, accompanied by all the angels, then He will sit on His throne of splendor.
And people from all the nations will be assembled in front of Him and He will separate them from one another, like a shepherd separating his sheep from the goats.
He will place the sheep at His right side, but the goats at His left.
Then the King will say to those at His right side, ‘You who are blessed by my Father, come and receive the kingdom which has been prepared for you since the creation of the world,

for <sup>[when]</sup> I was hungry, you fed me; <sup>[when]</sup> I was thirsty, you gave me <sup>[something]</sup> to drink; <sup>[when]</sup> I came <sup>[to you as]</sup> a stranger, you gave me a place to stay;
when I was without adequate clothing, you gave me something to wear; <sup>[when]</sup> I was sick, you came to visit me; <sup>[when]</sup> I was in prison, you came to see me.’

Then those who had done what was right <sup>[i.e., the ones at His right side]</sup> replied, ‘Lord, when did we see you hungry, and feed you? Or thirsty, and give you <sup>[something]</sup> to drink?
And when <sup>[did you come as]</sup> a stranger, and we gave you a place to stay? Or <sup>[when were you]</sup> without adequate clothing, and we gave you something to wear?
And when did we see you sick or in prison and come to visit you?’
And the King will answer them and say, ‘Truly I tell you, since you did all this for one of my least <sup>[significant]</sup> brothers, you did it for me.’

Then He <sup>[i.e., the King]</sup> will say to those at His left side, ‘Go away from me, you who are cursed <sup>[by God]</sup>, into the never ending fire which is prepared for the devil and his angels.
For <sup>[when]</sup> I was hungry, you did not feed me; <sup>[when]</sup> I was thirsty, you did not give me anything to drink;

<sup>[when]</sup> I came <sup>[to you as]</sup> a stranger, you did not give me a place to stay; <sup>[when I]</sup> did not have adequate clothing, you did not give me anything to wear; when I was sick, or in prison, you did not visit me.’
They will reply, ‘Lord, when did we <sup>[ever]</sup> see you hungry, or thirsty, or a stranger, or inadequately dressed, or sick, or in prison, and not provide for your needs?’
Then the Lord will answer them, ‘Truly I tell you, since you did not do it for one of these least <sup>[significant brothers of mine]</sup>, you did not do it for me.’
And these <sup>[i.e., the ones at His left side]</sup> will go away to never ending punishment, but those who did what was right will go to never ending life.”
And it happened when Jesus had finished all this teaching, He said to His disciples,

“You know that the Passover Festival will be celebrated in two more days <sup>[Note: This was the annual Jewish festival commemorating Israel’s deliverance from Egyptian bondage under Moses’ leadership]</sup>, and the Son of man will be turned over to be crucified.”
Then the leading priests and elders of the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> people were gathered in the courtroom of the head priest, named Caiaphas.
They were taking counsel together as to how they might take Jesus by trickery and kill Him.
But they reasoned, “We will not do it during the Festival because it might start a riot among the people.”

Now when Jesus was at Bethany <sup>[Note: This was a small village less than two miles east of Jerusalem]</sup>, in the house of Simon, the man <sup>[who probably had been healed]</sup> of an infectious skin disease,
a woman with an alabaster <sup>[i.e., stone]</sup> jar of very expensive perfume came to Him and poured it on His head as He reclined at the dinner table. <sup>[Note: See Matt. 23:6]</sup>
But when His disciples saw this they were very upset and said, “What is the reason for such waste?
For this perfume could have been sold for a large sum of money and given to poor people.”
But Jesus was aware <sup>[of what they were up to]</sup>, so said, “Why are you bothering this woman? For she has done a kind deed to me.
You will always have poor people with you, but you will not always have me.
For in the act of pouring this perfume on my body she was preparing it for my burial.

Truly I tell you, wherever this Gospel <sup>[message]</sup> is preached in the whole world, what this woman has done <sup>[for me]</sup> will <sup>[also]</sup> be told about, as a memorial of her <sup>[kindness to me]</sup>.”
Then one of the twelve apostles, named Judas Iscariot, went to the leading priests

and said, “What would you give me if I turned Jesus over to you?” And they weighed out thirty silver coins <sup>[Note: This amount was equivalent to about five months of a farm laborer’s wages, or approximately $13,500 in 2005]</sup>, and promised <sup>[See Mark 14:11]</sup> to give him that much money.
So, from then on Judas looked for a <sup>[good]</sup> opportunity to turn Jesus over <sup>[to the Jewish leaders]</sup>.

Now on the first day of the Festival of Unleavened Bread <sup>[i.e., the first day of Passover week]</sup>, Jesus’ disciples came to Him, saying, “Where do you want us to make preparations for you to eat the Passover meal?”

He answered, “Go into the city <sup>[i.e., Jerusalem]</sup> to a certain man and say to him, ‘The Teacher says, My time is near <sup>[i.e., to be turned over to the Jewish leaders]</sup>; I plan to observe the Passover Festival at your house with my disciples.’”
And the disciples did <sup>[just]</sup> as Jesus had asked them, and they made preparations for the Passover meal.
Now when evening came, Jesus was reclining at the dinner table with His twelve apostles.
As they were eating, He said, “Truly I tell you, one of you will turn me over <sup>[to the Jewish leaders]</sup>.”
And they were extremely grieved, and each one of them began saying to Him, “Am I the one, Lord?”
He answered, “The one who dipped his hand with me into the <sup>[sauce]</sup> bowl is the one who will turn me over <sup>[to the Jewish leaders]</sup>.

The Son of man is going <sup>[to die]</sup>, but it is too bad for that person through whom the Son of man is turned over <sup>[to the Jewish leaders]</sup>! It would be better for that man if he had not been born.”
Then Judas, the one who betrayed Him asked, “Am I the one, Rabbi?” Jesus answered Him, “You have said so.”

And as they were eating, Jesus took a <sup>[small]</sup> loaf of bread, asked God’s blessing on it, then broke it and gave pieces to His disciples and said, “Take some and eat it; this is <sup>[i.e., represents]</sup> my <sup>[physical]</sup> body.”
And He took a cup <sup>[i.e., probably wine made from fresh or possibly preserved grape juice]</sup>, gave thanks to God, then passed it to them, saying, “All of you, drink from it;

for this is <sup>[i.e., represents]</sup> my blood of the Agreement <sup>[i.e., between God and mankind]</sup>, which is <sup>[to be]</sup> poured out for many people in order for <sup>[their]</sup> sins to be forgiven.

But I tell you, I will not drink of this fruit of the vine <sup>[i.e., grape juice]</sup> from now on until that day when I will drink it again with you <sup>[i.e., figuratively]</sup> in my Father’s kingdom.”
And after they had sung a hymn, they went up to the Mount of Olives. <sup>[Note: This was a small hill just east of Jerusalem]</sup>
