Then Jesus said <sup>[to His disciples]</sup>, “All of you will have doubts about me tonight, for it is written <sup>[Zech. 13:7]</sup>, ‘I will strike down the shepherd and the sheep of the flock will be scattered everywhere.’
But after I am raised up, I will go on ahead of you to Galilee.”
Peter answered Him, “<sup>[Even]</sup> if everyone <sup>[else]</sup> has doubts about you, I never will.”
Jesus said to him, “Truly I tell you, this <sup>[very]</sup> night, before the rooster crows, you will deny <sup>[knowing]</sup> me three times.”
Peter replied to Him, “Even if I have to die with you, still I will not deny <sup>[knowing]</sup> you.” And all <sup>[the rest of]</sup> the disciples said the same thing.

Then Jesus came with His disciples to a place called Gethsemene. <sup>[Note: This was an olive orchard on a hillside just east of Jerusalem]</sup>. He said to them, “You sit here while I go over there and pray.”
He took Peter and the two sons of Zebedee <sup>[i.e., James and John]</sup> with Him, and <sup>[soon]</sup> began to be grieved and very distressed.
Then He said to them, “My soul is extremely grieved, even to <sup>[the point where I could]</sup> die. Stay here and be alert with me <sup>[i.e., in prayer]</sup>.”

And He went on ahead a short distance and fell face-down and prayed, “My Father, allow this cup <sup>[i.e., of suffering]</sup> to pass away from me if it is <sup>[at all]</sup> possible; nevertheless, let it not be what I want, but what You want <sup>[for me]</sup>.”

And <sup>[when]</sup> He came <sup>[back]</sup> to where His disciples were and found them asleep, He said to Peter, “What <sup>[is this]</sup>, could you not remain alert with me <sup>[in prayer]</sup> for one hour?

Be alert and pray, so that you do not give in to temptation; the spirit <sup>[of a person]</sup> is truly willing <sup>[i.e., to do a certain thing]</sup>, but his physical nature is <sup>[often too]</sup> weak <sup>[to carry it out]</sup>.”

He <sup>[then]</sup> went away a second time and prayed, “My Father, if this <sup>[cup of suffering]</sup> cannot pass away unless I drink it <sup>[i.e., experience the suffering]</sup>, <sup>[then]</sup> let what You want be done.”
And He came <sup>[back]</sup> again and found them asleep, because they had not been able to keep their eyes open.
He left and went away from them again and prayed a third time, saying the same words again.
Then He came to His disciples and said to them, “Go ahead and sleep now, and get your rest. See, the time is near and the Son of man is <sup>[about to be]</sup> turned over into the hands of sinners.
Get up, let us be going. Look, the one who will turn me over <sup>[to the Jewish leaders]</sup> is nearby.”

And while He was still speaking, Judas, one of the twelve apostles, came with a large crowd who were carrying swords and clubs. They had come from the leading priests and elders of the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> people.
Now the one who betrayed Him had given them a signal, saying, “Whoever I give a kiss <sup>[of greeting to]</sup>, he is the one, take hold of him.”
So, immediately he went to Jesus and said, “Greetings, Rabbi,” and <sup>[then]</sup> kissed Him <sup>[enthusiastically]</sup>.
And Jesus said to him, “<sup>[My]</sup> friend, <sup>[go ahead]</sup>, do what you came to do.” Then the Jewish officers came and arrested Jesus and began taking Him away.
Just then, one of those who were with Jesus <sup>[i.e., Peter. See John 18:10]</sup>, reached out with his sword and struck the head priest’s servant <sup>[with it]</sup>, shearing off his ear.
Then Jesus said to him, “Put your sword back in your belt again, for all those who use the sword will die by the sword.

Or, do you think that I cannot appeal to my Father and He will immediately send me more than twelve legions of angels <sup>[i.e., Jesus could have called about 72,000 angels to minister to Him. See Matt. 4:11]</sup>?
How <sup>[else]</sup> then should the Scriptures be fulfilled that this must happen <sup>[than what is taking place]</sup>?”

<sup>[Then]</sup> Jesus said to the crowds at that very time, “Have you come out to arrest me with swords and clubs as <sup>[you would apprehend]</sup> a thief? I sat everyday teaching in the Temple, and you did not <sup>[come to]</sup> take me.
But all this has happened so that the Scriptures of the prophets would be fulfilled.” Then all of His disciples left Him and ran away.
And those who had arrested Jesus led Him away to the house of Caiaphas, the head priest, where the experts in the Law of Moses and the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders were gathered.

But Peter followed Him from a distance <sup>[as they were going]</sup> to the courtyard of the head priest. He went inside <sup>[i.e., eventually. See John 18:15-16]</sup> and sat down with the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> officers to see how things would turn out <sup>[i.e., with Jesus’ trial]</sup>.
Now the leading priests and the entire Council <sup>[called “the Sanhedrin”]</sup> looked for false witnesses <sup>[to testify]</sup> against Jesus, in order to put Him to death.

But they did not find any <sup>[i.e., whose stories harmonized. See Mark 14:56]</sup>, even though many false witnesses came <sup>[forward to testify]</sup>. Finally, two <sup>[false witnesses]</sup> came forward
and said, “This man <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup> said, ‘I can destroy the Temple of God, and <sup>[then]</sup> rebuild it within three days.’”
The head priest stood up and said to Him, “Have you nothing to answer? What about this charge being made against you by these people?”

But Jesus did not say anything. So, the head priest said to Him, “I command you <sup>[to swear]</sup> by the living God, <sup>[and]</sup> to tell us if you are the Christ <sup>[i.e., God’s specially chosen one]</sup>, the Son of God!”

Jesus replied to him, “You have said so; nevertheless I tell you, from now on you will see the Son of man sitting at the right side of Power <sup>[i.e., God Himself]</sup>, and coming on the clouds of the sky.”

Then the head priest tore at his clothing <sup>[i.e., as an expression of frustration]</sup>, saying, “He has spoken against God <sup>[i.e., by claiming Deity for Himself]</sup>. What additional need do we have for witnesses? Look, you <sup>[yourselves]</sup> have now heard him speaking against God.
What do you people think <sup>[i.e., about such statements]</sup>?” They answered, “He deserves to die.”
Then they spit in His face and hit Him <sup>[with their fists]</sup>, while others slapped Him <sup>[in the face]</sup>,
saying, “Prophesy to us, you ‘Christ.’ Tell us who hit you?” <sup>[Note: By this time Jesus had been blindfolded. See Mark 14:65]</sup>.
Now Peter was sitting outside in a courtyard and a servant-girl approached him and said, “You also were with Jesus, the Galilean.”
But he denied <sup>[it]</sup> in front of all of them, saying, “I do not know what you are talking about.”
And when he had gone out by the gate, another servant-girl saw him and said to those nearby, “This man also was with Jesus of Nazareth.”
And he denied <sup>[it]</sup> again, <sup>[this time]</sup> with an oath, saying, “I do not <sup>[even]</sup> know the man.”
And a little later, those standing nearby came to Peter and said, “<sup>[We know]</sup> for sure, you also are one of them <sup>[i.e., Jesus’ disciples]</sup>, because your accent gives you away.”
Then Peter began to curse and swear, “<sup>[I tell you]</sup>, I do not know the man.” And immediately a rooster crowed.
Then Peter remembered the words Jesus had said, “Before the rooster crows, you will deny <sup>[knowing]</sup> me three times.” And he went out and cried bitterly.
Now when morning came, all the leading priests and the elders of the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> people conferred together against Jesus as to how they might put Him to death.
So, they chained Him, led Him away and turned Him over to Pilate, the <sup>[Roman]</sup> governor.

Then Judas, who had betrayed Him <sup>[to the Jewish leaders]</sup>, when he saw that Jesus had been condemned to die, changed his mind <sup>[i.e., about betraying Him]</sup> and brought back the thirty silver coins <sup>[Note: See Matt. 26:15 for the amount of money involved]</sup> to the leading priests and <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders,
saying, “I have sinned by delivering up an innocent man to die.” But they replied, “What is that to us? It is your affair!”
And he threw down the thirty silver coins in the Sanctuary <sup>[i.e., probably the outer area of the Temple]</sup>, then left and went out and hanged himself.

The leading priests took the silver coins and said, “It is not permissible by the Law of Moses to put this money in the Temple treasury, since it is the price <sup>[paid]</sup> for <sup>[taking]</sup> a life.

So, they conferred together <sup>[and decided]</sup> to buy ‘The Potter’s Field’ with the money <sup>[Note: This was probably an abandoned field where clay had been obtained for making pottery]</sup>, for use in burying people who were not from the local area.
So, that field is called, ‘The Field of Blood’ to this day.

<sup>[Thus the prophecy]</sup> spoken by Jeremiah, the prophet <sup>[Note: The passage actually appears to be Zech. 11:12- 13]</sup>, was fulfilled which said, ‘And they took the thirty silver coins, the amount charged for Him on whom certain Israelites had set a price,
and gave the money for ‘The Potter’s Field,’ as the Lord directed me.”
Now Jesus stood in front of the governor <sup>[i.e., Pilate]</sup>, who asked Him, “Are you the king of the Jews?” Jesus answered him, “You have said so.”
When He was accused by the leading priests and the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders, He made no reply.
Pilate then said to Him, “Do you not hear how many charges the witnesses are bringing against you?”
But He <sup>[again]</sup> made no reply, not even to a single charge, which caused the governor to be greatly amazed.
Now at the <sup>[Passover]</sup> Festival, the governor was accustomed to releasing whatever prisoner the crowd wanted.
There was at that time in custody a notorious prisoner named Barabbas.
Therefore, when the people had gathered together, Pilate asked them, “Whom do you want me to release, Barabbas or Jesus, who is called ‘Christ’?”
For he knew that the reason they had turned Jesus over to him was out of envy.

Then while Pilate was seated in his courtroom of justice, his wife sent <sup>[word]</sup> to him, saying, “You should have nothing to do with that innocent man. For I was very troubled in a dream over him today.” <sup>[Note: That is, she was troubled over a dream she had before waking up that morning]</sup>.
Now the leading priests and <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders persuaded the crowds to ask for Barabbas <sup>[to be released]</sup> and for Jesus to be killed.
But the governor asked them, “Which one of these two men do you want me to release to you?” And they answered, “Barabbas.”
Pilate replied, “Then what should I do to Jesus, who is called ‘Christ’?” They all shouted, “Let him be crucified!”
And he said, “Why <sup>[should he be]</sup>? What wrong has he done?” But they shouted out even louder, “Let him be crucified!”

So, when Pilate saw that he was not convincing anyone, but that a riot was brewing instead, he took water and washed his hands in front of the crowd saying, “I am not responsible for this man’s blood. <sup>[Note: Some ancient manuscripts say “righteous man’s blood”]</sup>. You tend to the matter.”
And all the people answered, “Let his blood be on our hands, and on our children’s <sup>[as well]</sup>.”
Then he released Barabbas to them, but he had Jesus flogged and then turned over to be crucified.
Then the governor’s soldiers took Jesus to Pilate’s headquarters and assembled the entire company <sup>[i.e., about 500 men]</sup> in front of Him.

They stripped off His clothing and placed a scarlet robe on Him <sup>[Note: Mark 15:17 calls this a “purple” robe because in that day, any color with a mixture of “red” in it was often called “purple,” so the use of “scarlet” was appropriate]</sup>.
Then they made a wreath out of thorns and placed it on His head and put a stick in His right hand. They knelt down in front of Him and mocked Him, saying, “Hey, king of the Jews!”
And they spat on Him and took the stick and beat Him on the head with it.
And after mocking Him <sup>[some more]</sup>, they removed the <sup>[scarlet]</sup> robe and put His own clothes back on Him. Then they led Him away to crucify Him.

As they were leaving <sup>[i.e., probably the city of Jerusalem]</sup>, they met a man from Cyrene <sup>[Note: This was a city in the northern African country of Libya]</sup>. His name was Simon and they forced him to go with them so he could carry Jesus’ cross <sup>[i.e., probably only the cross-beam portion]</sup>.
And when they had come to the place called Golgotha, which means “the place of a skull” <sup>[i.e., probably because it was a skull-shaped knoll in appearance]</sup>,
they offered Jesus wine mixed with a bitter drug <sup>[i.e., for the purpose of dulling the pain of crucifixion. Mark 15:23 calls it “myrrh”]</sup>, but when He tasted it He refused to drink it.
And after they had crucified Him, the soldiers divided His clothing among themselves by gambling for them.
Then they just sat there and watched Him <sup>[die]</sup>.
They attached over His head <sup>[i.e., to the upright portion of the cross]</sup> the charge made against Him, which read, “This is Jesus, the king of the Jews.”
They <sup>[also]</sup> crucified two thieves with Him, one at His right side and one at His left.
And those who passed by <sup>[the cross]</sup> shouted abuse at Him and shook their heads <sup>[in derision]</sup>,

saying, “You who <sup>[said you]</sup> would destroy the Temple and rebuild it in three days, save yourself <sup>[from dying]</sup>; if you <sup>[really]</sup> are the Son of God, come down from the cross.”
In the same way the leading priests, experts in the Law of Moses and <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders also mocked Him, saying,

“He saved other people, <sup>[but]</sup> he cannot <sup>[even]</sup> save himself <sup>[i.e., from dying]</sup>. He is ‘the king of Israel;’ let him now come down from the cross and then we will believe in him.
He trusted in God; now let God deliver him, if He wants <sup>[to save him from dying]</sup>, for he said, ‘I am the Son of God.’”
And the thieves who were crucified with Him also heaped the same abuse on Him.
Now from noon until three o’clock in the afternoon there was darkness over all the land.

Then about three in the afternoon Jesus called out in a loud voice, saying, Eli, Eli, lama, sabachthani <sup>[Note: These words were spoken in the commonly used Aramaic language]</sup>, which mean, My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?
And when some of those standing there heard this, they said, “This man is calling for Elijah.”
And immediately one of them ran and got a sponge, soaked it in sour wine and, attaching it to a stick, <sup>[lifted it up]</sup> and offered Jesus a drink.
But the rest of them said, “Let him alone; let us see if Elijah <sup>[really]</sup> comes to save him.”
Then Jesus called out again with a loud voice and <sup>[finally]</sup> gave up His spirit <sup>[to God. See Luke 23:46]</sup>.
Just then the Temple curtain was torn in two from top to bottom, the ground shook, and rocks were split open.
Graves opened up and the bodies of many dead saints <sup>[i.e., God’s holy people]</sup> were raised up <sup>[from the dead]</sup>.
They came out of their graves after Jesus’ resurrection and entered the holy city <sup>[i.e., Jerusalem]</sup> where they appeared to many people.

Now when the military man in charge, and those with him who were watching Jesus, saw <sup>[and felt]</sup> the earthquake and the <sup>[other]</sup> things that happened, they became extremely fearful, saying, “Truly this was the Son of God.”
And many women, who had followed Jesus from Galilee and had ministered to His needs, were watching <sup>[all this]</sup> from a distance.

Among them were Mary from Magdala; Mary, the mother of James and Joseph <sup>[i.e., probably the wife of Clopas. See John 19:25]</sup>; and the mother of James and John, the sons of Zebedee <sup>[i.e., Salome. Mark 15:40]</sup>.
And when evening had come, a rich man from Arimathea named Joseph, who was also one of Jesus’ disciples,
went to <sup>[governor]</sup> Pilate and asked for the body of Jesus. So, Pilate ordered that it be given to him.
Joseph took the body and wrapped it in a clean linen cloth
and placed it in his own newly prepared grave, which he had cut out of <sup>[a ledge of]</sup> rock. Then he <sup>[had]</sup> a huge stone rolled across the entrance of the grave and went away.
Mary from Magdala and the other Mary <sup>[i.e., probably Jesus’ mother. See verse 56]</sup>, stood near the grave site.
Now on the following day, which was the day after the Preparation <sup>[for the Passover Festival]</sup>, the leading priests and Pharisees assembled in front of Pilate
and said, “Sir, we remember what that deceiver said while he was still alive: ‘I will rise <sup>[from the dead]</sup> after three days.’

Therefore, give the order to secure the grave site until the third day, so his disciples will not steal his body and then tell the people ‘He has risen from the dead.’ This last deception would be worse than the first one <sup>[i.e., the claim that Jesus rose from the dead would be worse than the claim that he was the Messiah]</sup>.”
Pilate said to them, “Take <sup>[a group of]</sup> guards <sup>[with you]</sup>; go and make the grave site as secure as you can.”
So, they went and secured the grave site, placing a <sup>[wax]</sup> seal on the stone, while the guards watched.

Now as the Sabbath day ended and the first day of the week <sup>[i.e., Sunday]</sup> approached <sup>[Note: See Mark 16:1-2 for the exact timing]</sup>, Mary from Magdala and the other Mary <sup>[i.e., Jesus’ mother. See Matt. 27:56]</sup> went to look at the grave site.
Now there had been a violent earthquake, for an angel of the Lord had descended from heaven and, <sup>[having]</sup> come to roll away the stone, was <sup>[now]</sup> sitting on it.
He appeared as <sup>[brilliant]</sup> lightning and his clothing was white as snow.
And the guards shook with fear at seeing him, and became as dead men.
The angel said to the women; “Do not be afraid, for I know you are looking for Jesus, who has been crucified.
He is not here, for He has risen just as He said <sup>[He would]</sup>. Come and see the place where the Lord had been lying,

then go quickly and tell His disciples, ‘He has risen from the dead and is <sup>[now]</sup> going on ahead of you into Galilee. You will see Him there.’ Now <sup>[that is all]</sup> I have to tell you.”
So, they left the grave site quickly, with fear and great joy, and ran to give the message to His disciples.
Suddenly Jesus met them and gave them greetings. They approached Him and took hold of His feet <sup>[and knelt]</sup> to worship Him.
Then Jesus said to them, “Do not be afraid. Go and tell my brothers <sup>[i.e., the disciples, that you have seen me]</sup>, so they can head for Galilee where they will see me.”
Now while these women were on their way, some of the guards entered the city and began telling the leading priests everything that had happened.
And when they had assembled with the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders and conferred with them, they offered large sums of money to the soldiers,
saying, “We want you to say, ‘Jesus’ disciples came at night and stole his body while we were asleep.’
And if the governor hears about this <sup>[plot]</sup>, we will persuade him <sup>[i.e., not to punish you for negligence]</sup>, so you will not have to worry about that.”

So, they took the money and did as they were instructed. And this story <sup>[i.e., that Jesus’ body had been stolen by His disciples]</sup> was told widely among the Jews, and continues to be told to this day.
But the eleven disciples <sup>[i.e., apostles]</sup> went to the mountain in Galilee where Jesus had ordered them to go.
And when they met Him <sup>[there]</sup>, they <sup>[knelt down]</sup> to worship Him; but some <sup>[still]</sup> had doubts <sup>[i.e., about His resurrection. See John 20:25]</sup>.
Then Jesus came and spoke to them, saying, “I have been given all authority in heaven and on earth.

So, as you go, make disciples out of people from all the nations, then immerse believers <sup>[See Mark 16:15-16]</sup> into the name of <sup>[i.e., to enter a relationship with]</sup> the Father, and the Son and the Holy Spirit.
<sup>[Then]</sup> go on teaching them <sup>[i.e., these new converts]</sup> to obey everything I have commanded you and my presence will be with you always, even to the end of the age.”
This is <sup>[how]</sup> the good news about Jesus Christ, the Son of God, began.

It was even written in Isaiah, the prophet <sup>[Note: Verse 2 is quoted from Mal. 3:1; verse 3 from Isa. 40:3]</sup>, “Look, I am sending my messenger on ahead of you. He will prepare the way for you.
He will be the voice of a man shouting out in the desert, <sup>[urging people]</sup> to make the road for the Lord ready <sup>[to travel on]</sup>; to make a straight pathway for him.”
John <sup>[the Immerser]</sup> came immersing people in the desert and proclaiming that those immersed on the basis of their repentance would receive the forgiveness of sins <sup>[from God]</sup>.
And people from throughout all the regions of Judea and all of Jerusalem went out to John <sup>[in the desert]</sup>. And he immersed them in the Jordan River as they confessed their sins.
John wore clothing made of camel’s hair and he had a leather belt around his waist. He ate grasshoppers and wild honey.

In his preaching John said, “One who is more powerful than I am will follow my ministry. I am not <sup>[even]</sup> worthy to stoop down and loosen the straps of His sandals <sup>[i.e., to serve as His slave]</sup>.
I am immersing you in water, but He will immerse you in the Holy Spirit.”
And it happened at that time that Jesus went from Nazareth, in Galilee <sup>[i.e., south to Judea]</sup>, where He was immersed by John in the Jordan River.
And immediately upon emerging from the water, He saw the sky split open and the Holy Spirit descending on Him as a dove.
And a voice called out from heaven, saying, “You are my dearly loved Son. I am very pleased with you.”
Then right after that the Holy Spirit drove Jesus out into the desert.
He spent forty days in this desert where He was put to the test by Satan. He was with wild animals, and angels ministered to His needs <sup>[while there]</sup>.
Now after John was turned over <sup>[i.e., to the authorities and imprisoned]</sup> Jesus went <sup>[back]</sup> to Galilee preaching the good news from God

and saying, “The appointed time has arrived and the kingdom of God is near. So, you people should repent <sup>[i.e., change your hearts and lives]</sup> and believe the good news <sup>[about the kingdom]</sup>.”
And while walking along the shore of Lake Galilee, Jesus saw Simon <sup>[i.e., Peter]</sup> and his brother Andrew casting their net in the lake, for they were fishermen.
And Jesus said to them, “Come, follow me and I will make you fishermen for people.”
So, they left their nets immediately and became His followers.
Then, as He walked on a little farther, He saw James, the son of Zebedee, and his brother John, who were in their boat repairing nets.
Immediately He called them <sup>[to become His followers]</sup> and they left their father, Zebedee, and went along with Him.

So, they went to Capernaum <sup>[Note: This was a city in Galilee that later became Jesus’ headquarters while in Galilee. See verse 29 and 2:1]</sup>, and on the <sup>[first]</sup> Sabbath day Jesus entered the synagogue and taught people.
They were amazed at His teaching for He taught them as one who had <sup>[real]</sup> authority, and not as the experts in the Law of Moses.
And just then there appeared in the synagogue a man dominated by an evil spirit. He shouted out,
“What business do we have with you, Jesus from Nazareth? Have you come to destroy us? I know who you are; <sup>[you are]</sup> God’s Holy One.”
Jesus spoke sternly to him, saying, “Be quiet, and come out of him.”
And the evil spirit caused the man to have a convulsion, then shouted with a loud voice and came out of him.

And all the people <sup>[in the synagogue]</sup> were so amazed they began asking one another, “What is this, some new teaching? And it is spoken with such authority! He even orders evil spirits, and they obey him.”
And news about Him quickly spread everywhere, <sup>[even]</sup> into all of Galilee and the surrounding regions.
And as soon as they left the synagogue, they went into the house of Simon <sup>[i.e., Peter]</sup> and Andrew, taking James and John with them.
Now Simon’s mother-in-law was sick <sup>[in bed]</sup> with a fever. Immediately, Jesus was told about her <sup>[condition]</sup>
so He went to her, took her by the hand and raised her up <sup>[from bed]</sup>. The fever left her and she began serving them.
When evening came and the sun had set, they brought to Jesus all those who were sick and those dominated by evil spirits.
And people from all over the city <sup>[of Capernaum]</sup> gathered at the door of the house,

and He healed many of those who were sick with various diseases and drove out many evil spirits. But He did not permit the evil spirits to tell people <sup>[who He was]</sup>, because they knew who He was <sup>[i.e., the Christ]</sup>.
Very early in the morning, <sup>[even]</sup> before daylight, Jesus woke up, left Simon’s house <sup>[verse 29]</sup> and went out to a deserted place where He prayed.
And Simon and those who were with him <sup>[i.e., probably Andrew, James and John. See verse 29]</sup> followed Him.
When they found Him, they said to Him, “Everyone is looking for you.”

He said to them, “Let us go on to the next towns so that I can preach there also, for this was why I came out <sup>[i.e., Jesus was sent by God to preach to other towns as well as Capernaum. See Luke 4:43]</sup>.”
So, Jesus went into the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> synagogues throughout all Galilee, preaching <sup>[the good news about the coming kingdom, verses 14- 15]</sup> and driving out evil spirits.

Then a person with an infectious skin disease came to Jesus and, kneeling down before Him, begged Him, saying, “If you want to, you can heal me.” <sup>[Note: Throughout this narrative the terms “heal/healing” are originally “clean/cleansing” because the Jews viewed a person with such a disease as ceremonially “unclean.” See Lev. 13:1-3]</sup>.
And being moved with deep pity, Jesus reached out His hand, touched him and said, “I do want to; be healed.”
And immediately the infectious skin disease left him and he was healed.
Jesus sternly warned him, sending him directly out,

and saying to him, “Make sure you do not say anything to anyone. But go and show yourself to the priest and make an offering for your healing with whatever Moses ordered <sup>[Lev. 14:1-9]</sup>, as evidence to people <sup>[that you were completely healed]</sup>.”

But <sup>[instead]</sup> the man went out telling everyone about the miracle of healing and spreading the story widely, so that Jesus was not able to enter a town publicly but had to remain outside in deserted places. <sup>[So]</sup>, people went to Him there from all over the region.

After Jesus returned to Capernaum several days later, it became known that He was <sup>[back]</sup> at home <sup>[i.e., possibly at the house of Peter and Andrew, where He was staying. See 1:29]</sup>.
And there were so many people gathered to listen to His message that there was no room for any more <sup>[in the courtyard]</sup> around the doorway.
Then some people came, bringing a man afflicted with paralysis, who was being carried by four friends.

And when they were unable to get near Jesus, because of the <sup>[large]</sup> crowd, they removed <sup>[a section of]</sup> the roof <sup>[directly above Him]</sup>. <sup>[Note: The roof was a flat area, where people retired for rest, prayer, etc., and easily accessible from an outside stairway]</sup>. And when they had broken open <sup>[a sizable hole]</sup>, they lowered the cot on which the paralyzed man was lying.
When Jesus saw <sup>[the evidence of]</sup> their faith, He said to the paralyzed man, “Son, your sins are forgiven.”
But certain experts in the Law of Moses, who were sitting there, began reasoning in their minds,
“Why does this man talk this way? He is speaking against God. Who can forgive sins except one, that is, God?”
And immediately Jesus perceived in His spirit what they were reasoning in their minds and said to them, “Why are you reasoning like this in your minds?
Which is easier, to say to the paralyzed man, ‘your sins are forgiven,’ or to say ‘get up, pick up your cot and walk’?
But, so you will know that the Son of man has authority on earth to forgive sins (He then said to the paralyzed man),
‘I say to you, get up, pick up your cot and go home.’”
So, he got up and immediately picked up his cot and walked out in front of them all, so that everyone was amazed and gave honor to God, saying, “We have never seen anything like this before.”
Then Jesus went out again, walking along the shore of the lake, and <sup>[when]</sup> the entire crowd came out to Him, He taught them.

And as He walked along He saw Levi <sup>[i.e., the same person as Matthew. See Matt. 9:9]</sup>, the son of Alphaeus, sitting at the toll booth <sup>[i.e., collecting taxes]</sup>. He said to him, “Become my follower.” And he got up and followed Him.

And it happened that Jesus was having a meal at Levi’s house and many tax collectors and worldly people sat down with Him and His disciples, for many <sup>[of these kinds of people]</sup> became His followers.

And when the experts in the Law of Moses, being Pharisees <sup>[i.e., a strict sect of the Jewish religion]</sup>, saw that He was eating with tax collectors and worldly people, they said to His disciples, “Why does Jesus eat and drink with tax collectors and worldly people?”

When Jesus heard <sup>[about]</sup> this, He said to them, “People who are healthy do not need a doctor, but <sup>[only]</sup> sick people do. I did not come to call those who do right, but sinners <sup>[instead]</sup>.”

Now John’s disciples and the Pharisees were fasting, <sup>[i.e., going without food and/or drink for religious reasons]</sup>. Some people came and asked Jesus, “Why do John’s disciples and the Pharisees’ disciples fast, but your disciples do not?”
Jesus answered them, “Can the companions of the groom fast while the groom is still with them? As long as they have the groom with them they cannot fast.
But the time will come when the groom will be taken away from them. Then they will fast at that time.

“No one sows a patch of unshrunk cloth on an old garment, because what was supposed to cover the hole <sup>[actually]</sup> makes it larger. The new <sup>[piece of cloth]</sup> tears away from the old one and the hole is made worse.

And no one puts freshly squeezed grape juice into bottles made of previously used animal skins. If he does, the grape juice will <sup>[cause cracks and]</sup> burst the <sup>[dried-out]</sup> animal skins and the grape juice will <sup>[all leak out and]</sup> be wasted, and the animal skin bottles will become useless. But people put freshly squeezed grape juice into newly prepared animal skin bottles.”

And it happened that Jesus was walking through the grain fields on the Sabbath day and His disciples began picking off heads of grain <sup>[to eat]</sup> as they traveled along. <sup>[Note: It was an accepted custom for people in that day to be permitted to do this as they walked long the paths bordering a grain field]</sup>.
And the Pharisees asked Him, “Look, why are they <sup>[i.e., Jesus’ disciples]</sup> doing what is against the Law of Moses on the Sabbath day?”
And He replied to them, “Did you never read what King David did when he and his men were hungry and needed something to eat? <sup>[See I Sam. 21:6]</sup>.

Or how he entered the house of God <sup>[i.e., the Temple]</sup> when Abiathar was head priest, and ate the ‘Bread of Presence’ which was not permissible, according to the Law of Moses, for anyone but priests? He even gave <sup>[some of it]</sup> to those who were with him.”
And Jesus said to them, “The Sabbath day was made for man’s <sup>[benefit]</sup>, and not man for the Sabbath <sup>[day’s benefit]</sup>.
So, the Son of man is lord <sup>[i.e., has authority]</sup> even over the Sabbath day.”
And Jesus entered the synagogue again and there He met a man with a deformed hand.
And the Pharisees were watching Him to see if He would heal the man on the Sabbath day, so that they could <sup>[find a reason to]</sup> accuse Him.
And He said to the man whose hand was deformed, “Step forward.”
Then He said to the Pharisees, “Is it permissible by the Law of Moses to do something good, or something harmful on the Sabbath day? To save a life or to kill it?” But they did not reply.

And when He had looked around at them with righteous indignation, being grieved over their stubbornness, He said to the man, “Reach out your hand.” And when he reached it out, his hand was restored <sup>[to normal use]</sup>.

And the Pharisees immediately went out and conferred with the Herodians against Jesus, plotting how they could kill Him. <sup>[Note: These Herodians were members of a political party favoring King Herod]</sup>.

<sup>[Then]</sup> Jesus withdrew <sup>[from there]</sup> with His disciples and went to the lake <sup>[i.e., Lake of Galilee]</sup>, being followed by a large crowd from Galilee. Also, a large crowd from Judea,

Jerusalem, Idumaea, the east side of the Jordan River, and from around Tyre and Sidon <sup>[i.e., cities on the northwest coast of Palestine]</sup> came to Him, having heard about the great things <sup>[i.e., miracles]</sup> He had been performing.
He told His disciples to have a small boat waiting for Him <sup>[i.e., from which He could address the people. See 4:1]</sup>, to prevent His being thronged by the large crowd.
For He had healed so many people that large numbers of them who were plagued with serious illnesses were crowding around Him in hope of getting to touch Him.
And whenever they saw Him, <sup>[people possessed by]</sup> evil spirits fell to the ground in front of Him, shouting, “You are the Son of God.”
But He sternly ordered them not to reveal His identity.
He then went up into the mountain and summoned those He wanted to come to Him and they came.
He appointed twelve apostles to travel with Him, whom He could send out to preach
and have authority to drive out evil spirits.
These apostles were: Simon, to whom He gave the added name Peter,
James and his brother John, the sons of Zebedee, to whom He gave the added name Boanerges, which means “Sons of Thunder,”

Andrew, Philip, Bartholomew <sup>[i.e., the same person as Nathaniel. See John 1:45]</sup>, Matthew <sup>[i.e., the same person as Levi. See Mark 2:14]</sup>, Thomas <sup>[i.e., the same person as Didymus. See John 11:16]</sup>, James, the son of Alphaeus, Thaddaeus <sup>[i.e., the same person as Judas, the son of James. See Luke 6:16]</sup>, Simon the Cananaean <sup>[i.e., the same person as the Zealot. See Luke 6:15]</sup>,
and Judas Iscariot, who also betrayed Him. Then Jesus went into a house.
Soon a crowd assembled again so that they could not even eat a meal <sup>[i.e., without people crowding all around them]</sup>.

When His relatives <sup>[or friends]</sup> heard about this <sup>[i.e., all that He was doing]</sup>, they went out to take custody of Him <sup>[for safety reasons (?)]</sup>, for people were saying, “He has lost his mind.”

And the experts in the Law of Moses who had come down from Jerusalem were saying <sup>[Note: This followed the incident of Jesus healing a man with an evil spirit. See Matt. 12:22-28]</sup>, “He has Beelzebub <sup>[in him]</sup>,” and “He is driving out evil spirits by <sup>[the power of]</sup> the chief of evil spirits.”
And He called them to Him and began speaking to them in parables <sup>[i.e., brief stories to illustrate His teaching]</sup>, saying, “How can Satan drive out Satan?
And if a kingdom is plagued by internal division, it will never survive.
And if a household is divided <sup>[by strife]</sup> within, that household will never last.
And if Satan has risen up against himself, and is divided <sup>[i.e., in aim and purpose]</sup> he cannot survive, and his end has come.
But no one can enter a strong man’s house and steal his belongings unless he first ties up the strong man. Only then will he be able to steal from his house.
Truly I tell you, all of mankind’s sins and the abusive language that they use in speaking against God <sup>[and men]</sup> can be forgiven.
But the person who speaks against the Holy Spirit can never be forgiven, for he is guilty of a never ending sin.”
Jesus said all this because these experts in the Law of Moses had been saying; “He has an evil spirit in him.”
Then Jesus’ mother and <sup>[half-]</sup> brothers came and stood outside <sup>[i.e., of a large house. See Matt. 12:46-13:1]</sup>. They sent a message to Him, asking Him to come out to them.
Someone in the large crowd that was sitting around Him said to Him, “Look, your mother and your brothers are outside looking for you.”
He answered them, “Who is my mother and who are my brothers?”
Then He looked out over those gathered around Him and said, “Look, <sup>[here is]</sup> my mother and my brothers!
For whoever will do what God wants, that person is my brother, and sister and mother.”

Jesus again began teaching along the shore of the lake <sup>[of Galilee]</sup>. A huge crowd gathered around Him so that He had to sit in a boat out in the lake <sup>[to speak to them]</sup>, while the entire crowd stood on the shore.
He taught the people many things using parables <sup>[to illustrate His lessons]</sup>. In His teaching He said to them,
“Listen carefully! A farmer went out to plant seed,
and it happened, as he sowed <sup>[i.e., scattering the seed by hand]</sup>, some of it fell by the side of the road and birds came and ate it.
And some seed fell on rocky ground where there was not much soil, and immediately it sprouted up because the soil was so shallow.
But when the sun came up the newly sprouted plants were scorched, and because they had no roots, <sup>[soon]</sup> withered away.
And other seeds fell among thorns which grew up and choked them so that they yielded no crop.
Then other seeds fell into fertile soil, and growing up to maturity, they yielded an ever increasing crop. They produced thirty, sixty and even a hundred times <sup>[as much as was planted]</sup>.”
And Jesus said, “Whoever has ears to hear with ought to listen <sup>[to this]</sup> carefully.”
And when He was alone those people who gathered around Him, along with the twelve apostles, asked Him what the parables meant.
He said to them, “You disciples are being given an understanding of the secret of the kingdom of God, but to outsiders everything will be told in parables.

This is so that even though they see, they will see and yet not perceive. And even though they hear, they will hear and yet not understand. For if they did, they would turn <sup>[back to God]</sup> again and receive forgiveness.”
And Jesus continued to speak to them; “Do you not know what this parable means? Then how can you understand any of the other parables?
A farmer planted ‘the word’ <sup>[i.e., the message of God]</sup>.

Some of the seed that fell along the roadside represents where ‘the word’ was sown <sup>[i.e., preached]</sup>. When these people have heard, immediately Satan comes and takes away ‘the word’ which had been sown in them.
And in the same way, some <sup>[of the seed]</sup> that was sown on rocky soil represented those who heard ‘the word’ and immediately accepted it gladly,

but <sup>[since]</sup> they had such shallow roots, they continued on for <sup>[only]</sup> a little while. Then, when trouble and persecution arose over <sup>[obeying]</sup> ‘the word,’ immediately they stumbled <sup>[i.e., and fell away from God]</sup>.
And other seeds represent those who are sown among thorns. These are people who heard ‘the word,’

but worry <sup>[over things]</sup> of the world, and the deceitfulness of material wealth, and the evil desires for other things entered their hearts and choked <sup>[the effectiveness of]</sup> ‘the word,’ so that it did not yield a crop <sup>[in their lives]</sup>.

Then there were those who were sown on fertile soil. These represent people who heard ‘the word,’ accepted it and yielded a crop of thirty, sixty and even a hundred times <sup>[as much as was planted]</sup>.”
Then He said to them, “Is a lamp brought in to be put under a bushel-sized container, or under the bed, instead of on its stand?
For there is nothing that is hidden that should not be exposed. Neither is anything covered up that should not be revealed.
If anyone has ears to hear with, he ought to listen to this carefully.”
And He said to them, “Pay attention to what you are hearing from me. The standard you use in giving <sup>[to others]</sup> is the standard they will use on you, with even more added.

For to the person who <sup>[already]</sup> has something, <sup>[more]</sup> will be given, and from the person who has <sup>[almost]</sup> nothing, even what <sup>[little]</sup> he has will be taken away from him.”
And Jesus said, “The kingdom of God is like a farmer who scattered seed on the ground,
then went to bed and got up the next day to find that the seed had sprouted and grown, yet he did not know how it happened.
The soil yields its crop, first the green sprout, then the ears of grain, and then the kernels in the ears.
But when the grain ripens the farmer wields his sickle because harvest time has come.”
And He continued to speak, “What shall I compare the kingdom of God to? Or, what parable will <sup>[best]</sup> illustrate it?
It is similar to a seed from the mustard tree which, when planted in the ground,
even though it is the least <sup>[significant]</sup> of all seeds on earth, becomes larger than all other plants. It develops huge branches so that the birds of the sky can roost in their shade.”
And He spoke “the word” to them <sup>[i.e., the crowds]</sup> with as many such parables as they were able to receive.
And He did not speak to them <sup>[i.e., concerning the kingdom]</sup> without using a parable, but He explained everything to His own disciples privately.
When evening came that day He said to the disciples, “Let us go over to the other side <sup>[i.e., the east side of Lake Galilee]</sup>.”

And leaving the crowd, the disciples took Him in their boat just the way He was <sup>[i.e., this probably means without His making provision for the trip]</sup>. They were accompanied by other boats also.
<sup>[Then]</sup> a violent windstorm arose <sup>[on the lake]</sup> and huge waves began splashing into the boat until it began to fill with water.
Jesus was sleeping on a pillow in the stern of the boat. They awakened Him, shouting, “Teacher, do you not care that we are sinking?”

He <sup>[immediately]</sup> awoke and spoke sternly to the wind and called out to the water, “Be peaceful and still.” <sup>[Immediately]</sup> the wind stopped blowing and a great calm came over the water.

And He said to His disciples, “Why are you so afraid? Do you not still have <sup>[any]</sup> faith?” And they were extremely afraid, and said to one another, “Who can this man be that even the wind and water obey him?”

Then Jesus and His disciples <sup>[finally]</sup> arrived on the other side of the lake <sup>[i.e., the east side]</sup> in the district of Geresa <sup>[Note: Matt. 8:28 says ‘Gadara.’ Geresa and Gadara were two towns 12 miles apart in the same region east of Lake Galilee]</sup>.
When Jesus left the boat, immediately a man with an evil spirit came from the graveyard to meet Him.
This man had been living in the graveyard and no one had been able to keep him tied up, not even with a chain.
He had often been bound with ropes and chains but had broken the chains off and torn the ropes to pieces. And no one had enough strength to subdue him.
And night and day, in the graveyard and in the mountains, he was always shouting out and cutting himself with <sup>[sharp]</sup> rocks.
When he saw Jesus from far away he ran to Him and worshiped Him.
And shouting with a loud voice, he said, “What do you want with me, Jesus, Son of the Most High God? I beg you, in God’s name, do not torture me.”
For Jesus was saying to <sup>[the evil spirit in]</sup> him, “Come out of this man, you evil spirit.”

Then He asked <sup>[the evil spirit]</sup>, “What is your name?” And he answered Him, “My name is ‘Legion,’ <sup>[Note: This word means a number consisting of between 5,000 and 6,000]</sup>, for there are many of us <sup>[i.e., evil spirits]</sup>.
Then the spirits begged Jesus earnestly not to send them away out of the region.
On the side of a <sup>[nearby]</sup> mountain, a large herd of <sup>[wild (?)]</sup> hogs was grazing.
The evil spirits begged Him, saying; “Send us into those hogs, so we can enter their bodies.”

And He gave them permission to do it. So, the evil spirits went out of the man’s body and entered the bodies of the hogs. The herd then rushed down the cliff into the lake. There were about two thousand that drowned in the lake.
Those who had been feeding them ran and told what all occurred in the town and around the countryside and <sup>[so]</sup> people came to find out what had happened.
And when people came to Jesus, they saw the man who had been dominated by 5,000 to 6,000 evil spirits sitting down with his clothes on and perfectly sane, and they were afraid.
Those who saw this told the people what had happened to the man dominated by the evil spirits and about the hogs.
So, they began begging Jesus to leave that region.
As He entered the boat the man who had been dominated by evil spirits begged for permission to be allowed to go with Him.
But Jesus would not allow him, saying to the man, “Go home to your friends <sup>[and family (?)]</sup> and tell them about the great things the Lord has done for you and how He had pity on you.”

But the man went away and began telling people throughout Decapolis about all of the great things Jesus had done for him. <sup>[Note: “Decapolis” means “ten cities” and was a region located east of the Jordan River]</sup>.

When Jesus had crossed over again in a boat to the other side <sup>[i.e., to the northwest shore of the lake, probably in the vicinity of Capernaum]</sup>, a large crowd gathered around Him along the shore of the lake.
About then a man named Jairus, one of the officials of the synagogue, came to Jesus and, upon seeing Him, fell to the ground at His feet.
He begged Him earnestly, saying, “My little daughter is about to die; <sup>[please]</sup> come and place your hands on her so she may be healed and live.”
So, Jesus went along with the man, and was being thronged by a large crowd that followed Him.
Then a woman, who had been bleeding for twelve years, came to Him.

She had suffered much at the hands of many doctors <sup>[i.e., through treatments and medication that only increased her discomfort]</sup> and had spent all her money <sup>[on medical bills]</sup> and yet got worse instead of better.
She had heard what Jesus was doing so came from the crowd behind Jesus and touched His robe.
For she had told herself, “If only I can touch his clothing, I will be healed.”
And immediately her flow of blood stopped and she felt healed in her body from the affliction.
Just then Jesus perceived in Himself that <sup>[healing]</sup> power had gone out from Him so turned to the crowd and asked, “Who touched my clothing?”
And His disciples said to Him, “You see the large crowd thronging you and yet you ask ‘Who touched me’?”
Then He looked around to see who had done this <sup>[i.e., touched Him and received healing]</sup>.
But the woman became afraid and trembled with fear, for she knew what had happened to her <sup>[i.e., the healing]</sup>, so she came and fell down in front of Jesus and told Him the whole truth.
And He said to her, “Daughter, your faith has made you well. Go in peace and be <sup>[i.e., remain]</sup> healed from your affliction.”
While Jesus was still speaking, someone came from the synagogue official’s house and said <sup>[to the official]</sup>, “Your daughter is dead; why are you bothering the Teacher any more?”
But Jesus disregarded what the person said and told the official, “Do not be afraid; just believe <sup>[i.e., in my power to restore your daughter]</sup>.”
He did not allow anyone to follow Him <sup>[i.e., to the official’s house]</sup> except Peter, James and his brother John.
And when they arrived at the synagogue official’s house He saw a commotion there, with many people crying and wailing loudly.
And when He entered <sup>[the house]</sup> He said to them, “Why are you making such a commotion and crying? The child is not <sup>[really]</sup> dead, but only sleeping.”

They laughed at Him scornfully. But after having all the people wait outside He took the child’s father and mother, along with those who came with Him <sup>[i.e., Peter, James and John]</sup> and went in to where the <sup>[dead]</sup> child lay.
And taking the child by the hand He said to her, “Talitha cumi,” which means “Little girl,” (I tell you), “get up.”
And immediately the little girl arose and walked, for she was twelve years old. <sup>[Upon seeing this]</sup> the people were completely amazed.
But He strictly ordered them not to tell anyone about this; then He told them to give the girl something to eat.
Then Jesus went out from there and came to His home town <sup>[i.e., Nazareth]</sup> and His disciples went with Him.

And when the Sabbath day came, He began teaching in the synagogue and many who heard Him were amazed, and asked <sup>[such questions as]</sup>, “Where did this man get these things <sup>[i.e., knowledge, miracles, etc.]</sup>?” and “What kind of wisdom has been given to him?” and “What do these supernatural powers he performs mean?
Is not this the carpenter, the son of Mary and brother of James, Joseph, Judas and Simon? And are not his sisters living here among us?” And they were led to doubt His true identity.
Then Jesus said to them, “A prophet does not go without receiving honor, except in his own home town, and among his own relatives, and among his own family members.”

And He could not perform any supernatural deed there <sup>[i.e., because of their lack of faith in Him]</sup>, except He placed hands on a few sick people <sup>[i.e., accompanied by prayer (?)]</sup> and healed them.
<sup>[But]</sup> He marveled at the people’s lack of faith. So, He traveled and taught throughout the <sup>[surrounding]</sup> villages.
Then Jesus called to Him the twelve apostles and sent them out two by two. He gave them authority over evil spirits

and ordered them not to take anything with them during their travels, except a walking stick. They were to take no food, no traveling bag <sup>[for personal belongings]</sup>, and no money in their money belts,
but were to wear sandals and take only one coat.
And He said to them; “Wherever you enter a house, stay there until you leave that place.

And the people from whatever place that does not welcome you or listen to you, when you leave there, shake the dust off from under your shoes as evidence against them.” <sup>[Note: This was a Jewish custom showing disdain and intended to suggest their unworthiness]</sup>.
Then they went out and preached that people should repent <sup>[i.e., change their hearts and lives]</sup>.
And they drove out many evil spirits and applied <sup>[olive]</sup> oil to <sup>[the heads of]</sup> many sick people and healed them.

Now King Herod heard about this <sup>[i.e., what Jesus was doing]</sup> because His name had become well known. He said, “John the Immerser has risen from the dead and it is his powers that are at work in Jesus.”
But others said, “He is <sup>[the prophet]</sup> Elijah” <sup>[while]</sup> others said, “He is a prophet like one of the prophets <sup>[of old]</sup>.”
But when King Herod heard <sup>[about Jesus]</sup>, he said, “John, whom I <sup>[had]</sup> decapitated, has risen.”

For it was Herod himself who had sent for John and had him arrested and chained up in prison. He did this to please Herodias, who was <sup>[i.e., had been]</sup> his brother Philip’s wife, but whom Herod had married.
For John had said to Herod, “It is unlawful for you to marry your brother’s wife.”
Now <sup>[because of this]</sup> Herodias had a grudge against John and wanted to kill him, but had not been able to.
It was because Herod was afraid of John, knowing he was a righteous and holy man, so he protected him. And Herod became very disturbed whenever he listened to John, yet he heard him gladly.

Then an ideal opportunity arose <sup>[i.e., for Herodias to do away with John]</sup> when Herod gave a dinner on his birthday for his influential friends, military commanders and the prominent officials of Galilee.

And when Herodias’ daughter <sup>[Note: According to Josephus her name was Salome]</sup> came in and danced <sup>[in front of them, sensually]</sup>, she pleased Herod and his party guests so much that the king said to the young woman, “Ask me for whatever you want and I will give it to you.”
And he vowed to her, “I will give you whatever you ask me for, up to one half <sup>[the wealth]</sup> of my kingdom.”
So, she went out and said to her mother, “What shall I ask for?” And her mother said, “<sup>[Ask for]</sup> the head of John the Immerser.”
At once she hurried in to the king and said, “I would like you to give me the head of John the Immerser on a <sup>[large]</sup> platter right away.”

And the king became very distressed <sup>[over such a gruesome request]</sup>, but because he had promised her with oaths, and <sup>[to keep from looking bad]</sup> in front of his party guests, he did not refuse her <sup>[request]</sup>.
And immediately the king sent a soldier who served as his guard and ordered him to bring John’s head to him. So, he went and decapitated him in the prison,
and brought his head on a <sup>[large]</sup> platter and gave it to the young woman. She <sup>[in turn]</sup> gave it to her mother <sup>[Herodias]</sup>.
And when John’s disciples heard <sup>[what had happened]</sup> they took his body and buried it in a grave.
Then the apostles got together with Jesus and told Him everything they had done and taught <sup>[on their mission]</sup>.

He said to them, “You men, leave here and go to a deserted place and rest for awhile.” For there were so many people coming and going <sup>[i.e., to listen to preaching, receive healing, etc.]</sup>, that they had no opportunity even to eat.
So, they <sup>[i.e., Jesus and His apostles]</sup> left by boat and went to a separate place <sup>[where they could be]</sup> alone.
But the people saw them going and, since many recognized them, these people ran on ahead of them from all the <sup>[surrounding]</sup> towns.

Then Jesus left <sup>[His place of seclusion]</sup> and, seeing the large crowd, He felt deep compassion for them because they were <sup>[as disoriented]</sup> as sheep without a shepherd, so He began teaching them.
And when the day was nearly over His disciples came to Him and said, “This place is deserted and the day is almost over;
send the crowd away so they can go into the surrounding countryside and towns to buy themselves something to eat.”

But He answered them, “You <sup>[men]</sup> give them something to eat.” And they replied, “Should we go and buy a supply of bread to give them to eat?” <sup>[Note: The amount mentioned here indicates that it was two hundred twelve-hour days of a farm laborer’s pay, or about $21,600 in 2005]</sup>.

And He said to them, “How many loaves <sup>[of bread]</sup> do you have? Go and see.” And when they had found out, they said, “Five <sup>[barley]</sup> loaves <sup>[See John 6:9]</sup> and two <sup>[probably smoked]</sup> fish.”
And He ordered all of them to recline in groups on the green grass.
So, they reclined in groups of fifty and one hundred.

Then He took the five loaves of bread and the two fish, and looking up to heaven, He asked God’s blessing on them. Then He broke the loaves and gave them to His disciples to set in front of the people <sup>[to eat]</sup>. He divided the two fish among them also
and they all ate until they were full.
Then they gathered up twelve baskets full of broken pieces <sup>[of bread]</sup> and also some fish.
The number eating the loaves of bread was five thousand men. <sup>[Note: This was besides women and children. See Matt. 14:21]</sup>.

And about then Jesus compelled His disciples to get into a boat and go on ahead of Him to the other side <sup>[i.e., to the west side of Lake Galilee]</sup> to Bethsaida <sup>[Note: This was apparently a different “Bethsaida” from the one mentioned in Luke 9:10, which was on the east side of Lake Galilee]</sup>, while He Himself sent the crowd away.
And after He had left them He went up to a mountain to pray.
When evening came the boat <sup>[containing His disciples]</sup> was in the middle of the lake while He remained alone on the shore.
When He saw how distressed His disciples were, rowing against an opposing wind, Jesus came to them between three and six o’clock in the morning, walking on the water, and almost walked past them.
But when they saw Him walking on the water they thought it was a spirit, so shouted out,
becoming <sup>[very]</sup> frightened when they saw Him. But He immediately spoke to them and said, “Take courage, and do not be afraid, it is I.”
Then He got up into the boat with them and the wind <sup>[immediately]</sup> stopped. And His disciples were greatly amazed <sup>[at this]</sup>,
but they did not understand <sup>[the miracle]</sup> of the loaves because their minds were insensitive <sup>[i.e., to the nature and power of Jesus]</sup>.
And when they had <sup>[finally]</sup> crossed over <sup>[i.e., to the west side of Lake Galilee]</sup>, they arrived in the district of Gennesaret and moored the boat on shore.
As soon as they got out of the boat the people recognized Jesus
and rushed around the whole district to bring sick people on cots to where He was.

And wherever He entered towns, cities or the countryside, they placed sick people at the open shopping markets and begged Him to allow them to touch even the edge of His robe. And all those who touched Him were made well.
The Pharisees and some experts in the Law of Moses, who had come from Jerusalem, gathered around Jesus.
They had observed some of His disciples eating their food with contaminated, that is, <sup>[ceremonially]</sup> unwashed hands.

(For the Pharisees and all the Jews would not eat anything unless they first scrubbed their hands <sup>[i.e., up to the wrists]</sup> in accordance with the <sup>[long-established]</sup> tradition of the Jewish elders).

And when they returned from the open shopping markets they refused to eat <sup>[anything]</sup> until they washed themselves <sup>[or “it,” that is, the food, thoroughly]</sup>. There were also many other traditions which they <sup>[strictly]</sup> observed, like washing cups, pots and copper kettles <sup>[thoroughly]</sup>.) <sup>[Note: The most accurate Greek manuscripts do not add “and beds” in this verse]</sup>.

The Pharisees and experts in the Law of Moses asked Jesus, “Why do your disciples not live according to the traditions of the Jewish elders, but <sup>[instead]</sup> eat their food with contaminated <sup>[i.e., ceremonially unwashed]</sup> hands?”

And He answered them, “Isaiah prophesied about you hypocrites very well when he wrote <sup>[Isa. 29:13]</sup>, ‘These people honor me with their lips <sup>[i.e., by what they say]</sup>, but their heart is far from <sup>[honoring]</sup> me.
They are worshiping me for nothing because they are teaching principles which are <sup>[merely]</sup> the requirements of men.’
You disregard the commandment of God and <sup>[yet]</sup> hang onto the traditions of men.”
And He said to them, “You are good at rejecting the commandment of God so you can keep your traditions.
For Moses said, <sup>[Ex. 20:12]</sup> ‘Give honor to your father and mother,’ and <sup>[Ex. 21:17 says]</sup>, ‘Whoever says bad things about his father or mother should surely be put to death.’
But when you people say, ‘If someone says to his father or mother, <sup>[the money]</sup> I could have helped you with is Corban; in other words, <sup>[it is]</sup> given to God,’
you are not allowing him to do anything for his father or mother anymore.
You are making God’s message useless by <sup>[enforcing]</sup> the tradition you have been following. And you do many things like that.”
Then He called the crowd to Him again and said to them, “Listen to me, all of you, and understand <sup>[what I am saying]</sup>.

There is nothing that enters a person’s body from the outside that can <sup>[spiritually]</sup> corrupt him, but it is what proceeds out of a person’s life that <sup>[spiritually]</sup> corrupts him.”
This verse is absent from most ancient manuscripts.
And when He had gone into the house away from the crowd His disciples asked Him about this parable.

He said to them, ‘Are you also as lacking in understanding <sup>[as the others]</sup>? Do you not understand that whatever enters a person’s body from the outside cannot <sup>[spiritually]</sup> corrupt him?
It is because it does not go into his heart, but into his stomach, and <sup>[eventually]</sup> passes out into the toilet.” By saying this He made all foods <sup>[ceremonially]</sup> acceptable.
And He said, “Whatever proceeds out of a man’s life is what <sup>[spiritually]</sup> corrupts him.
For from within, out of people’s hearts, proceed evil thoughts, sexual immorality, stealing, murder, extramarital affairs,
greedy desires, wickedness, deceit, unrestrained indecency, envy, slander, arrogance and foolishness.
All these evil things proceed from within <sup>[man’s heart]</sup> and <sup>[spiritually]</sup> corrupt him.”
And from there Jesus got up and went away into the region of Tyre and Sidon. He entered a house but did not want anyone to know it. However, He could not keep it secret.
But just then a woman whose little daughter was dominated by an evil spirit heard about Jesus and came and fell down at His feet.

Now the woman was a Greek <sup>[i.e., a Gentile]</sup>, a Syrophoenician by nationality. <sup>[Note: This was a region just north of Galilee and consisted of Syria and Phoenicia]</sup>. She begged Him to drive out the evil spirit from her daughter.
So, He said to her, “Children should be the first ones to eat until they are full, for it is not proper to take the children’s food and throw it to the dogs.”
But she answered Him, “Yes Lord, but even the dogs under the table eat the crumbs dropped by the children.”
And He said to her, “Because you have said this, go on your way; the evil spirit has left your daughter.”
And she went away to her house and found her child lying on her bed with the evil spirit gone from her.
Again He left the region of Tyre and traveled <sup>[north]</sup> through Sidon, <sup>[then back south]</sup> to Lake Galilee, and through the middle of Decapolis.
And they brought to Him a deaf man who had a speech impediment and they begged Him to place His hand on him <sup>[for healing]</sup>.
So, Jesus took him away from the crowd by himself and put His fingers into the man’s ears. Then He spit and touched the man’s tongue <sup>[i.e., with His saliva]</sup>
and, looking up to heaven, He sighed and said, “Ephphatha,” which means, “Be opened.”
And <sup>[immediately]</sup> the man’s hearing was restored, his speech impediment was removed and he began speaking clearly.
Then Jesus ordered the people <sup>[who witnessed the miracle]</sup> not to tell anyone <sup>[about it]</sup>, but the more He urged them not to, the more widely they publicized it.
And they were utterly amazed, saying, “He has done everything just right; He makes even the deaf to hear and the mute to speak.”
In those days, when a large crowd again assembled <sup>[i.e., to listen to Jesus’ teaching]</sup> and did not have anything to eat, Jesus called His disciples and said to them,
“I feel deep pity for this crowd because they have continued listening to me for three days now and have nothing <sup>[left]</sup> to eat.
And if I send them home hungry they will become weak on the way, because some of them have come a long distance.”
And His disciples replied to Him, “Where will a person get enough bread to feed these people in such a deserted place like this?”
And He asked them, “How many loaves of bread do you have?” And they answered, “We have seven loaves.”

Then He ordered the crowd to sit down on the ground; he took the seven loaves, and after giving thanks to God <sup>[for them]</sup>, He divided the food and gave it to His disciples to set in front of them. They <sup>[in turn]</sup> set it in front of the crowd.
They <sup>[also]</sup> had a few small fish and after asking God’s blessing on them, He ordered these also to be set in front of them.
And they <sup>[all]</sup> ate until they were full, then gathered up seven baskets of broken pieces which were left over.
The number <sup>[who ate]</sup> was about four thousand men <sup>[besides women and children. See Matt. 15:38]</sup>. Then Jesus sent them away.

Immediately He boarded a boat with His disciples and crossed <sup>[to the west side of the lake]</sup>, to the region of Dalmanutha. <sup>[Note: Matt. 15:39 says “Magadan,” which was probably in the same vicinity as Dalmanutha]</sup>.

The Pharisees and Sadducees <sup>[i.e., these were strict sects of the Jewish religion]</sup>, came out and began disputing with Jesus, asking for a <sup>[supernatural]</sup> sign from Him, <sup>[in an attempt]</sup> to test Him.

Then He sighed deeply in His spirit and said, “Why does this generation of people look for a <sup>[supernatural]</sup> sign? Truly I tell you, there will not be any <sup>[supernatural]</sup> sign given to this generation.”
Then He left them and boarded a boat again and sailed to the other side <sup>[i.e., the east side of Lake Galilee]</sup>.
Now Jesus’ disciples had forgotten to take <sup>[any]</sup> food <sup>[with them]</sup>, and had only one loaf of bread in the boat.

Then Jesus ordered them, saying, “Pay attention and watch out for the leavening <sup>[effect]</sup> of the Pharisees and Herod.” <sup>[Note: By “Herod” is probably meant the Herodians, who were a party favorable to King Herod and, along with the Pharisees and Sadducees, were out to get Jesus. See Matt. 16:6]</sup>.
And they began reasoning with one another, saying, “<sup>[Why be concerned about yeast since]</sup> we do not have <sup>[any]</sup> bread?”
Jesus, being aware of what they were thinking, said, “Why are you reasoning about not having any bread? Have you not yet perceived or understood? Have your hearts become insensitive?
Even though you have eyes, do you not see? And even though you have ears, do you not hear? And do you not remember?
When I divided the five loaves of bread among the five thousand persons, how many baskets full of broken pieces did you gather up?” They said to Him, “Twelve baskets full.”
He again asked, “And when the seven loaves were divided among the four thousand persons, how many large basketfuls of broken pieces did you gather up?” And they said to Him, “Seven large basketfuls.”
Then He said to them, “Do you not understand yet?”
And when they came to Bethsaida some people brought a blind man to Jesus and begged Him to touch him <sup>[i.e., for healing]</sup>.

<sup>[So]</sup>, He took hold of the blind man by his hand and brought him out of the village. When Jesus spit <sup>[and put His saliva]</sup> on his eyes <sup>[See 7:33 for a similar use of His saliva in healing]</sup> and placed His hands on him <sup>[for healing]</sup>, He asked him, “Can you see anything?”
The man looked up and said, “<sup>[Yes]</sup>, I see people, but they look like trees walking around.”
Then Jesus again placed His hands on his eyes; the man stared intently, then his sight was <sup>[completely]</sup> restored and he was able to see everything clearly.
Then Jesus sent him home, saying, “Do not even go through the village <sup>[on your way]</sup>.”

Now Jesus and His disciples went out to the villages <sup>[in the vicinity]</sup> of Caesarea Philippi <sup>[Note: This was a city about 25 miles north of Lake Galilee]</sup>. On their way, He asked His disciples, “Who are people saying that I am?”
And they answered Him, “<sup>[Some say]</sup> you are John the Immerser; others say <sup>[the prophet]</sup> Elijah, but others say <sup>[you are]</sup> one of the prophets.”
Then He asked them, “But who do you say that I am?” Peter answered Him, “You are the Christ <sup>[i.e., God’s specially chosen one]</sup>.”
Then He ordered them not to tell anyone about His identity.

Jesus then began teaching them that the Son of man must suffer very much, be rejected by the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders, leading priests, and experts in the Law of Moses and be killed, but rise again <sup>[from the dead]</sup> after three days.
And He spoke about this freely. But Peter took Jesus aside and began rebuking Him <sup>[i.e., for saying such things]</sup>.
But He turned around, and looking at His disciples, He rebuked Peter, saying to him, “Get away from me, Satan; for you are not thinking about God’s things but about men’s. ”

And He called the crowd to Him, along with His disciples, and said to them, “If anyone wants to be my follower, he should deny himself <sup>[i.e., of always having his own way]</sup> and accept his cross <sup>[i.e., his responsibilities, with all their difficulties]</sup> and then he can become my follower.

For whoever would <sup>[try to]</sup> save his life <sup>[i.e., by neglecting spiritual things]</sup> will lose it <sup>[i.e., miss out on the blessings of God]</sup>. But whoever <sup>[is willing]</sup> to lose his life <sup>[i.e., in commitment to God’s service]</sup> for my sake and <sup>[the work of]</sup> the Gospel’s sake, will save it <sup>[i.e., obtain both temporal and spiritual blessings]</sup>.
For what would it benefit a person to gain the whole world <sup>[i.e., of material things]</sup> and give up his life <sup>[i.e., of spiritual blessings]</sup>?
Or what should a person give in exchange for his life?

For whoever will be ashamed of me and of my words in this <sup>[spiritually]</sup> unfaithful and sinful generation, the Son of man will also be ashamed of him when He returns in the splendor of His Father, accompanied by the holy angels.”

And Jesus said to them, “Truly I tell you, there are some people standing here who will surely not experience death until they see that God’s kingdom has come in <sup>[a demonstration of]</sup> power.”

And after six days had passed, Jesus took Peter, James and John aside alone and went up to a high mountain <sup>[i.e., probably Mt. Tabor, which was nearby]</sup>. <sup>[There]</sup> His whole appearance was <sup>[miraculously]</sup> changed in front of them.
His clothing became dazzling and extremely white; whiter than anyone in the world could <sup>[possibly]</sup> launder them.
Then Elijah, along with Moses, appeared to them and they were talking with Jesus.

And Peter said to Jesus, “Rabbi <sup>[i.e., Teacher]</sup>, it is good for us to be here. Let us build three small shelters, one for you, one for Moses and one for Elijah.” <sup>[See note at Matt. 17:4]</sup>
For he did not know what to say, because they <sup>[all]</sup> became very afraid.
<sup>[Just then]</sup> a cloud engulfed them and a voice spoke out of the cloud saying, “This is my dearly loved Son, listen to Him.”
And suddenly they looked all around them, but the only one they saw with them was Jesus.
And as they were coming down from the mountain Jesus ordered His disciples not to tell anyone what they had seen until <sup>[after]</sup> the Son of man was raised again from the dead.
So, they kept what He told them to themselves, discussing <sup>[only]</sup> among themselves what “rising again from the dead” meant.
Then His disciples asked Him, “Why is it, <sup>[since you are obviously the Messiah]</sup>, that the experts in the Law of Moses say that Elijah must come first?”

Jesus answered them, “Elijah truly will come first and restore all things <sup>[i.e., to their proper perspective concerning the Messiah]</sup>. And it is <sup>[also]</sup> written <sup>[in the Scriptures]</sup> about the Son of man, that He would suffer very much and be rejected.
But I tell you, Elijah has <sup>[already]</sup> come, and they did to him what they wanted to, just like it was written about him <sup>[in the Scriptures]</sup>.”

And when Peter, James and John <sup>[See verse 2]</sup> returned to <sup>[the rest of]</sup> the disciples, they saw a large crowd gathered around them, and the experts in the Law of Moses disputing with them.
And immediately, upon seeing Jesus, the entire crowd ran to greet Him in great amazement <sup>[i.e., possibly over His sudden appearance]</sup>.
And He asked the crowd, “What are you arguing about with my disciples?”
And someone in the crowd answered Him, “Teacher, I brought to you my son, who is dominated by a spirit which causes him to be a mute.
Wherever it seizes him, it throws him down and he foams at the mouth, grinds his teeth and stiffens out. I spoke to your disciples, asking them to drive it out, but they were not able to.”
Jesus said to them, “You generation of faithless people! How long must I be with you <sup>[i.e., before you understand]</sup>? How long do I have to be patient with you? Bring the sick boy to me.”

Then they <sup>[i.e., some people from the crowd]</sup> brought the sick boy to Jesus, and when the spirit saw Him, it immediately caused the boy to have convulsions and to fall to the ground and roll around, foaming at the mouth.
Jesus asked the boy’s father, “How long has this been happening to him?” And he answered, “Since he was a small child.
And frequently the evil spirit has tried to kill him, throwing him into a fire or into the water. But if you can do anything <sup>[for him]</sup>, have pity on us and help us.”

And Jesus said to him, “’If you can.’ <sup>[Note: By repeating the man’s words Jesus seems to be saying, “You mean, if you can believe”]</sup>. Everything is possible to the person who believes <sup>[it can happen]</sup>.”
Immediately the father of the boy cried out, “I do believe; help me to have more faith.”

When Jesus saw a crowd running together <sup>[toward Him]</sup>, He spoke sternly to the evil spirit <sup>[in the boy]</sup>, saying to it, “You deaf-mute spirit, I order you to come out of this boy and to never return to him again.”
The spirit shrieked and caused violent convulsions <sup>[in the boy]</sup>, then came out of him. The boy appeared to be dead, so that most of the people were saying, “He is dead.”
But Jesus grasped his hand and lifted him up, and he arose <sup>[fully healed]</sup>.
And when Jesus had come into a house, His disciples asked Him privately, “Why could we not drive out the evil spirit <sup>[from that boy]</sup>?”
He answered them; “This kind <sup>[of spirit]</sup> cannot be driven out except by praying.”
So, they left there and traveled through Galilee, but Jesus did not want anyone to know about it.
For He taught His disciples, saying to them, “The Son of man will be handed over to <sup>[evil]</sup> men and they will kill Him, and three days after He dies He will rise up again.”
But His disciples did not understand what He meant and were afraid to ask Him.

Then Jesus and His disciples came to Capernaum <sup>[Note: This city on the northwest shore of Lake Galilee had become sort of a headquarters for Jesus when He was in this region]</sup>. When He <sup>[and His disciples]</sup> entered a house He asked them, “What were you discussing on the way here?”
But they kept quiet for they had been arguing with one another over which one of them was the greatest.

So, He sat down and called the twelve apostles and said to them, “If anyone <sup>[of you]</sup> should <sup>[try to]</sup> be first <sup>[in importance]</sup> he will <sup>[end up]</sup> being last, and become the servant of all <sup>[the others]</sup>.”
And He took a little child and placed it in front of them, and lifting the child up into His arms, He said to them,
“Whoever welcomes one such little child <sup>[as this]</sup> for my sake, welcomes me; and whoever welcomes me, does not welcome me <sup>[only]</sup>, but <sup>[also]</sup> the One who sent me.”

<sup>[Then]</sup> John <sup>[the apostle]</sup> said to Him, “Teacher, we saw someone driving out evil spirits by using your name, so we told him to stop doing it, because he was not following <sup>[You]</sup> with us.”
But Jesus said, “Do not try to stop him, for there is not anyone who performs a supernatural deed using my name that will be able to quickly say something bad about me.
For the person who is not against us is for us.
Truly I tell you, whoever will give you <sup>[even]</sup> a cup of water to drink because you belong to Christ will certainly not lose his <sup>[temporal or eternal]</sup> reward.

And whoever causes one of these little ones <sup>[i.e., humble followers of the Lord. See Matt. 18:6]</sup> who believes in me to be led astray <sup>[from God]</sup>, he would have been better off to have had a huge millstone tied around his neck and thrown into the ocean <sup>[Note: This was a heavy, circular stone rolled over grain to crush it, and moved by an animal walking in a circle]</sup>.

And if your hand becomes the occasion for falling away <sup>[from God]</sup>, cut it off; it would be better for you to enter <sup>[never ending]</sup> life disabled, rather than keeping both hands and going to hell, into the fire that cannot be put out <sup>[i.e., all because it caused you to fall away from God]</sup>.
This verse is absent from many manuscripts.

And if your foot becomes the occasion for falling away <sup>[from God]</sup>, cut it off; it would be better for you to enter <sup>[never ending]</sup> life crippled, rather than keeping both feet and being thrown into hell.
This verse is absent from many manuscripts.

And if your eye becomes the occasion for falling away <sup>[from God]</sup> gouge it out; it would be better for you to enter the kingdom of God with <sup>[only]</sup> one eye, rather than keeping both eyes and being thrown into hell <sup>[i.e., all because one of them caused you to fall away from God]</sup>.
For in hell the worm of those who are there will never die <sup>[i.e., their gnawing punishment will never cease]</sup> and the fire <sup>[there]</sup> will never go out.

For everyone will be salted with fire. <sup>[Note: This meant either the suffering “salt” of fiery trials (the sacrifices of verses 43-47) or the punishing “salt” of being thrown into a fiery hell (verse 45-48)]</sup>..

“Salt is good, but if it loses its salty flavor, what will you use to restore it? <sup>[i.e., it is difficult to restore the “salt” of sacrificial commitment to God once it is lost]</sup>. You should have salt in yourselves <sup>[i.e., develop the qualities of preserving, purifying commitment to God]</sup>, and live peacefully with one another <sup>[i.e., instead of in rivalry. See 9:33-34]</sup>.”
