And Jesus left there and went to the region of Judea and east of the Jordan River. Again crowds gathered around Him and He continued teaching them, as was His custom.

Some Pharisees <sup>[i.e., a strict sect of the Jewish religion]</sup> came to Him <sup>[and attempted]</sup> to test Him by asking, “Is it permissible by the Law of Moses for a man to divorce his wife?”
And He answered them, “What did Moses command you <sup>[to do]</sup>?”
They replied, “Moses allowed us to provide a legal divorce decree and <sup>[then]</sup> to divorce her.”
But Jesus said to them, “He wrote you this commandment <sup>[only]</sup> because of the rebellious spirit of you <sup>[Jews]</sup>.
But from the beginning of creation God made mankind male and female.
For this reason <sup>[i.e., since he made one woman for one man]</sup> a man will leave <sup>[the home of]</sup> his father and mother and will cling to his wife

and the two of them will <sup>[then]</sup> become one flesh <sup>[i.e., united in such a close relationship as to constitute one body]</sup>, so that <sup>[upon becoming husband and wife]</sup> they no longer function as two persons but as one body.
Therefore, those whom God so joins together <sup>[in the marriage bond]</sup>, no person must ever separate.”
And when they were in the house, His disciples asked Him about this matter again.
And He said to them, “Whoever divorces his wife and marries another <sup>[woman]</sup> commits sexual unfaithfulness <sup>[toward her]</sup>.
And if she herself divorces her husband and marries another <sup>[man]</sup>, she commits sexual unfaithfulness <sup>[toward him]</sup>.”

Then some <sup>[parents]</sup> were bringing <sup>[their]</sup> little children to Jesus so He could touch them <sup>[i.e., to bestow a blessing on them]</sup>, but His disciples spoke harshly to these parents <sup>[for doing this]</sup>.

But when Jesus saw it, He was moved with <sup>[righteous]</sup> indignation, and said to them, “Allow these little children to come to me and stop trying to prevent them, for the kingdom of God belongs to such <sup>[humble ones. See Matt. 18:4]</sup>.
Truly I tell you, whoever does not welcome the kingdom of God as a little child does, that person will by no means enter it.”
Then He lifted up the children into His arms, and placing His hands on them, He asked God’s blessing on them.
As Jesus was leaving to go on His way, a man ran to Him and kneeled down in front of Him and asked, “Good Teacher, what should I do in order to possess never ending life?”
Jesus answered him, “Why are you calling me good? Nobody is good except One, that is, God.
You know the commandments: Do not murder. Do not be sexually unfaithful to your mate. Do not steal. Do not give false testimony. Do not cheat. Show honor to your father and mother.”
And he replied, “Teacher, I have <sup>[already]</sup> been observing all these commandments since I was a young lad.”

And Jesus looked at him, and filled with love, said to him, “There is one thing you <sup>[still]</sup> lack. Go and sell your possessions and give <sup>[the money]</sup> to poor people; become my follower, then you will have treasure in heaven.”
But the man had a sad look on his face when he heard these words, and went away sorrowful, for he had many possessions.
Then Jesus looked around Him and said to His disciples, “How difficult it is for rich people to enter the kingdom of God!”
And His disciples were amazed at what He said. But Jesus spoke to them again and said, “How difficult it is <sup>[for anyone]</sup> to enter the kingdom of God!
It is <sup>[actually]</sup> easier for a camel to pass through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God.”
And they were utterly amazed and said to Him, “Then who can <sup>[possibly]</sup> be saved?”
Jesus looked at them and said, “This would be impossible with men, but not with God, for everything is possible with God.”
Peter began saying to Him, “Look, we have left everything <sup>[i.e., homes, jobs, family, etc.]</sup> to follow you.”

Jesus said, “Truly I tell you, no one who has left his house, or brothers, or sisters, or mother, or father, or children or property for my sake and the Gospel’s sake <sup>[i.e., to become a servant of the Lord]</sup>,

but what he will receive <sup>[back]</sup> a hundred times as much now in this life --- houses, and brothers, and sisters, and mothers, and children and property --- <sup>[yet]</sup> with persecutions. And in the coming age <sup>[he will receive]</sup> never ending life.

But many that <sup>[appear to]</sup> be first <sup>[i.e., in importance and blessings]</sup> will <sup>[end up]</sup> being last. And <sup>[those appearing to]</sup> be last will <sup>[end up]</sup> being first.”

Now Jesus and His disciples were <sup>[traveling]</sup> along the road, going up to Jerusalem, with Jesus moving on ahead of the others. The disciples were amazed and they followed <sup>[behind Him]</sup> fearfully. <sup>[Note: From the context, it appears their amazement was due to His eagerness to enter Jerusalem in spite of the imminent danger they felt awaited Him there]</sup>. He took the twelve apostles aside and began telling them about the things that were going to happen to Him.

He said, “Look, we are going up to Jerusalem and the Son of man will be turned over to the leading priests and experts in the Law of Moses. They will condemn Him to death and turn Him over to the <sup>[unconverted]</sup> Gentiles.
They will mock Him, and spit on Him, and whip Him and <sup>[finally]</sup> kill Him, but after three days He will rise again <sup>[from the dead]</sup>.”
Now James and John, the sons of Zebedee, came over to Jesus and said to Him, “We would like you to do for us whatever we ask you.”
And He said to them, “What do you want me to do for you?”
They answered Him, “Appoint one of us to sit at your right side and the other at your left side <sup>[when you come]</sup> in splendor.”

But Jesus said to them, “You <sup>[really]</sup> do not know what you are asking for. Are you able to drink the cup <sup>[i.e., of suffering]</sup> that I will drink? Or <sup>[are you able]</sup> to be immersed with the immersion that I am <sup>[to be]</sup> immersed with? <sup>[i.e., to be overwhelmed with agony]</sup>.”

And they said to Him, “<sup>[Yes]</sup>, we are able to.” And Jesus said to them, “The cup <sup>[of suffering]</sup> that I <sup>[will]</sup> drink, you <sup>[also]</sup> will drink. And the immersion <sup>[with agony]</sup> with which I am <sup>[to be]</sup> immersed, you <sup>[also]</sup> will be immersed.
But it is not my prerogative to appoint who sits at my right side and at my left, but <sup>[those places]</sup> are for people to whom it has been assigned <sup>[by the Father]</sup>.”
When the ten other apostles heard this, they became very upset with James and John.

Jesus called them <sup>[i.e., probably all twelve apostles]</sup> to Him and said, “You are aware that those who are regarded as rulers among the <sup>[unconverted]</sup> Gentiles lord it over their own people, and their important men domineer over them <sup>[as well]</sup>.
But it is not <sup>[to be]</sup> this way among you, for whoever would like to become important among you will be your servant.
And whoever would like to be first <sup>[in prominence]</sup> among you, will be your slave.

For the Son of man did not come to be served <sup>[by others]</sup> but to be the servant <sup>[of others]</sup>, and to sacrifice His life as a ransom price for many people <sup>[i.e., to purchase them back from Satan]</sup>.”

Then they <sup>[i.e., Jesus and the twelve apostles]</sup> came to Jericho <sup>[Note: This was a town about 18 miles northeast of Jerusalem]</sup>. As He left there with His disciples and a large crowd, they met a blind beggar named Bartimaeus, the son of Timaeus, sitting along side the road.
When he heard that it was Jesus from Nazareth <sup>[passing by]</sup>, he began shouting, “Jesus, son of David, have pity on me.”
Many people spoke harshly to the blind man, telling him to be quiet. But he shouted all the more <sup>[loudly]</sup>, “Son of David, have pity on me.”
So, Jesus stopped and said, “Call that man <sup>[to me]</sup>.” Then the crowd called the blind man, saying to him, “Cheer up, Jesus is calling you.”
So, he threw off his robe, jumped up, and <sup>[immediately]</sup> went to Jesus.
Jesus said to him, “What do you want me to do for you?” And the blind man answered, “Rabboni <sup>[i.e., Teacher]</sup>, I want to have my sight restored.”
And Jesus said to him, “Go on your way, your faith <sup>[in me]</sup> has made you well.” And immediately his sight was restored, and he began following Jesus along the road.

And when they <sup>[all]</sup> approached Jerusalem, and came close to Bethphage and Bethany, near the Mount of Olives <sup>[Note: These small villages were about two miles east of Jerusalem]</sup>, He sent two of His disciples on ahead,
saying to them, “Go into the village just ahead of you and, upon entering it, you will find tied up <sup>[there]</sup> a colt that no one has ever ridden; untie it and bring it here.
And if anyone says to you, ‘Why are you doing this?’ you should say, ‘The Lord needs it,’ and immediately he will send it back here.”
So, they went away and found a <sup>[donkey’s]</sup> colt tied up at the gate, outside in the street, and untied it.
Some bystanders said to them, “What are you doing, untying that colt?”
But they answered him exactly what Jesus had told them to say, so the people let them go.
Then they brought the colt to Jesus and spread their clothing on it and He mounted it.
Many people spread their clothing on the roadway, while others spread leafy branches, which they had cut from the fields.

And the people who walked ahead of Him and followed behind shouted, “Hosanna <sup>[Note: The Syriac word ‘Hosanna’ originally meant ‘save now’ but came to be used as an expression of welcome, praise, blessing or acclamation]</sup>, may He who comes in the name <sup>[i.e., by the authority]</sup> of the Lord, be blessed.
May the coming kingdom of our forefather David be blessed. Hosanna in the highest <sup>[i.e., may this blessing reach to the highest heaven]</sup>.”

Then Jesus entered the Temple in Jerusalem, and after looking around at everything <sup>[there]</sup>, He left for Bethany with the twelve apostles, since it was evening by then. <sup>[Note: Jesus stayed overnight at Bethany during the week prior to His crucifixion. See verse 19]</sup>.
On the next day, when they were going from Bethany <sup>[to Jerusalem]</sup>, Jesus became hungry.

Upon seeing in the distance a leafy fig tree, He approached it in hope of finding something on it <sup>[to eat]</sup>. But when He got there He found nothing but leaves on it, for it was not the season for figs <sup>[yet]</sup>.
Therefore, He said to the tree, “From now on no one will ever eat fruit from you.” Now His disciples heard Him say this.

Then they entered Jerusalem and Jesus went into the Temple and began to drive out those who bought and sold <sup>[animals for sacrifice]</sup>. He upset the tables of the cashiers <sup>[i.e., those who exchanged foreign coins]</sup> and the benches of those who sold pigeons <sup>[for sacrifices]</sup>.
And He would not <sup>[even]</sup> allow anyone to carry a container <sup>[of goods]</sup> through the Temple <sup>[area]</sup>.

Then He taught them, saying, “Is it not written <sup>[Isa. 56:7]</sup>, ‘My house <sup>[i.e., the Temple]</sup> will be called a house of prayer for people of all nations’? But <sup>[Jer. 7:11 says]</sup>, ‘You have made it a hideout for thieves.’”
The leading priests and experts in the Law of Moses heard this and began looking for some way to kill Jesus, because they were afraid of Him, since the whole crowd was amazed at His teaching.
And each evening He left the city <sup>[of Jerusalem, and went to Bethany for the night]</sup>.
The next morning, <sup>[as they returned to the city]</sup>, they passed by the fig tree and saw that it had withered, clear down to its roots.

Then Peter remembered <sup>[what Jesus had done to the fig tree on a recent occasion]</sup>, and said to Him, “Rabbi <sup>[i.e., Teacher]</sup>, look, the fig tree you cursed <sup>[the other day]</sup> has withered up.”
Jesus replied to him, “You should have faith in God.

Truly I tell you, whoever will say to this mountain, ‘Be lifted up and thrown into the ocean’ and does not doubt in his heart, but believes that what he said will happen, he will have his request granted.
Therefore I tell you, whatever things you ask for in prayer, believe that you have received them, and you will have every one of them.
And whenever you stand praying, forgive whatever <sup>[grievance]</sup> you might have against anyone, so that your Father in heaven will also forgive you of your sins.”
This verse is absent from many ancient manuscripts.
Jesus and His disciples again came to Jerusalem. As He was walking in the Temple <sup>[area]</sup> the leading priests, experts in the Law of Moses and <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders came to Him
and said, “By what authority are you doing these things <sup>[i.e., miracles and teaching]</sup>? Or who gave you the authority to do these things?”
Jesus answered them; “Let me ask you a question. And if you answer it, then I will tell you by what authority I am doing these things.
Was the immersion of John <sup>[authorized]</sup> from heaven or from men? Give me an answer.”
So, they discussed this among themselves, saying, “If we say ‘from heaven’ He will say to us, ‘Then why did you not believe his message?’
But, should we say ‘from men’ <sup>[instead]</sup>?” Now they feared the people, for all of them considered John to be a true prophet.

So, they answered Jesus and said, “We do not know <sup>[where John got his authority to immerse]</sup>.” Jesus replied to them, “<sup>[Then]</sup> neither will I tell you by what authority I am doing these things <sup>[i.e., miracles and teaching]</sup>.”

Jesus began speaking to them <sup>[i.e., to the leading priests and experts in the Law of Moses (See 11:18) or, to the people (See Luke 20:19)]</sup> by using parables. He said, “A man planted a vineyard and built a fence around it. He dug a place for constructing a grape squeezing device, built a <sup>[lookout]</sup> tower <sup>[near it]</sup>, then leased it out to tenant farmers and went to another country.
And when the <sup>[grape harvest]</sup> season came, the owner of the farm sent a slave to the tenant farmers asking them to deliver the grape crop <sup>[to him]</sup>.
But they took the slave and beat him and sent him away empty handed.
Again the owner of the farm sent another slave, whom they wounded in the head and shamefully abused.
He sent <sup>[still]</sup> another slave, whom they killed. He continued sending many more slaves; they beat some and killed others.

The owner had one more person <sup>[left to send]</sup>, his dearly loved son. <sup>[So]</sup> he sent him to them last of all, reasoning to himself, ‘<sup>[Surely]</sup> they will treat my son with respect.’
But the tenant farmers said to one another, ‘This is the heir <sup>[to the vineyard]</sup>. Come on, let us kill him and then the inheritance will be ours.’
So they took him and killed him and threw his body out of the vineyard.

What do you think the owner of the vineyard will do <sup>[when he gets back]</sup>?” Jesus asked. <sup>[Then He continued]</sup>, “He will come and kill those tenant farmers and give the vineyard to other people.

Have you never read this Scripture <sup>[Psa. 118:22- 23]</sup>, ‘The building block rejected by the builders is the same <sup>[one]</sup> that was made the principal stone by which the entire building was aligned.
This was planned by the Lord and is a marvelous thing to us?’”

And they <sup>[i.e., the leading priests and experts in the Law of Moses]</sup> attempted to arrest Jesus, but <sup>[hesitated because]</sup> they feared what the crowd might do, for they understood that He was speaking the parable against them. So, they left Him and went away.

Then the Pharisees sent certain ones of their number <sup>[See Matt. 22:15-16]</sup> along with the Herodians <sup>[Note: This refers to members of a political party favoring King Herod]</sup> to attempt to trap Him by what He said.

And when they arrived, they said to Him, “Teacher, we know that you are sincere, and <sup>[that you]</sup> do not care for <sup>[i.e., do not fear or give in to]</sup> anyone. For you do not allow <sup>[the position of]</sup> any person to influence you, but teach the truth about God’s way. Is it lawful to pay taxes to Caesar, or not?

Should we pay it, or should we not pay it?” But Jesus was aware of their hypocrisy and said to them, “Bring me the coin so I can see it.” <sup>[Note: The amount of this coin was equivalent to one twelve-hour day of a farm laborer’s pay, or about $108 in 2005]</sup>.
So, they brought it to Him and He said to them, “Whose image and inscription <sup>[are on this coin]</sup>?” And they answered Him, “Caesar’s.”
Then Jesus replied to them, “Pay to Caesar whatever belongs to Caesar and pay to God whatever belongs to God.” And they greatly marveled at Him.
Then some Sadducees, <sup>[the ones]</sup> who say there is no resurrection <sup>[of the dead]</sup>, came and asked Him,

“Teacher, Moses wrote to us <sup>[Deut. 25:5]</sup> that if a man’s brother dies, leaving his widow behind without having had any children, his brother should marry his widow and father children by her. These children would then be considered his <sup>[dead]</sup> brother’s.
<sup>[Now]</sup> there were <sup>[these]</sup> seven brothers; the first one got married, but when he died he left no children behind.
So, the second one married his widow, and at his death he too left no children behind. Then the third one did the same thing.
All seven left no children <sup>[behind when they died]</sup>. Finally, the woman also passed away.
<sup>[Now]</sup> whose wife will she be in the resurrected state, for all seven <sup>[brothers]</sup> were married to her?”
Jesus said to them, “Is not this the reason that you are <sup>[so]</sup> mistaken, that you do not know <sup>[what]</sup> the Scriptures <sup>[teach]</sup> or <sup>[how much]</sup> power God has?
For when people rise from the dead, men do not get married, and women are not given away in marriage, but <sup>[all]</sup> are like angels in heaven.

But concerning those who are raised from the dead, have you never read in the book of Moses, in the incident about the bush, how God spoke to him, saying <sup>[Ex. 3:6]</sup>, ‘I am Abraham’s God, and Isaac’s God, and Jacob’s God?’
<sup>[So]</sup>, He is not God to those who are dead, but <sup>[He is]</sup> God to those who are alive. You are seriously mistaken.”

Then one of the experts in the Law of Moses <sup>[Note: Matt. 22:34 identifies him as being a Pharisee also]</sup>, came and heard Jesus and the Sadducees discussing together <sup>[i.e., the subject of the resurrection]</sup>, and knowing that Jesus had refuted the Sadducees successfully, asked Him, “Which commandment is foremost, above all the others?”
And Jesus answered, “The foremost one is this <sup>[Deut. 6:4-5]</sup>, ‘Hear this, you Israelites, the Lord our God is the only Lord,
and you must love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, with all your mind and with all your strength.’
The second one is this; ‘You must love your neighbor the same way that you love yourself.’ There is no other commandment greater than these <sup>[i.e., love for God and man]</sup>.”
The expert in the Law of Moses said to Jesus, “Truly, Teacher, you are right in saying that God is <sup>[the only]</sup> one and that there is no other God but Him.

And that to love Him with all one’s heart, with all one’s understanding, and with all one’s strength, and to love one’s neighbor the same way he loves himself, are far more <sup>[important]</sup> than all the burnt offerings and sacrifices <sup>[in the world]</sup>.”
When Jesus saw that this man answered wisely, He said to him, “You are not <sup>[very]</sup> far from the kingdom of God.” And after that, no one dared to ask Him any more questions.

As Jesus was teaching in the Temple, He responded <sup>[to some Pharisees. See Matt. 22:41]</sup> by asking, “How is it that the experts in the Law of Moses can say that the Christ is the son of David?

<sup>[It was]</sup> David himself <sup>[who]</sup> said by <sup>[inspiration of]</sup> the Holy Spirit <sup>[Psa. 110:1]</sup>, ‘The Lord <sup>[i.e., God]</sup> said to my <sup>[i.e., David’s]</sup> Lord <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup>, sit at my right side until I make your enemies the footrest <sup>[i.e., in subjection]</sup> under your feet.’
<sup>[Since]</sup> David himself called Him <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup> Lord, how could He be his son?” And the large crowd listened to Him with pleasure.
In His teaching Jesus said, “Look out for the experts in the Law of Moses, who like to walk around in long <sup>[flowing]</sup> robes and to receive special greetings at the open shopping markets
and to occupy the principal seats in the synagogues and the head places at dinner tables.
They consume widows’ houses <sup>[i.e., by foreclosing on them]</sup> and <sup>[then]</sup> cover it up by offering lengthy prayers. These people will receive a more severe judgment.”

Then Jesus sat down over near the <sup>[Temple]</sup> treasury and watched how the crowd was throwing money into the treasury <sup>[Note: This “treasury” was a large open-mouthed container used for receiving offerings for meeting expenses of Temple upkeep]</sup>. Many rich people were throwing in lots of money.

A poor widow came and threw in two small copper coins <sup>[Note: These two coins each amounted to about ten minutes’ worth of a farm laborer’s pay, or about $1.50 in 2005]</sup>. The total she gave was equivalent to a larger coin <sup>[i.e., worth twice as much]</sup>.
Jesus called His disciples to Him and said to them, “Truly I tell you, this poor widow has thrown in more than all the rest who are throwing <sup>[money]</sup> into the treasury.
For all of them threw in from their abundance, but being very poor, she threw in everything she had, even all she had to live on.”
As Jesus left the Temple, one of His disciples said to Him, “Teacher, look at the kind of stones these buildings are made of!”
And Jesus responded to him, “Do you see these huge buildings? There will not be one stone left on another here that will not be thrown down.”
As Jesus was sitting on the Mount of Olives, over near the Temple, Peter, James, John and Andrew asked Him privately,
“Tell us, when will these things <sup>[you just spoke of]</sup> happen? And what will be the sign <sup>[that indicates]</sup> when all these things are about to be fulfilled?”
Then Jesus began saying to them, “Pay attention so that no one leads you astray <sup>[i.e., from the truth]</sup>.
<sup>[For]</sup> many <sup>[false teachers]</sup> will come, claiming to be me, and saying, ‘I am <sup>[the Christ]</sup> and they will lead many people astray.

And when you hear of wars <sup>[going on]</sup> and rumors of <sup>[other]</sup> wars <sup>[pending]</sup>, do not worry, for such things must necessarily happen. But the end has not yet come. <sup>[Note: By “the end” here Jesus probably alludes to the downfall of Jerusalem in AD 70, with its attending destruction of the Temple buildings]</sup>.

For nations will wage war against one another, and kingdoms will attack one another. And earthquakes will happen at various places <sup>[in the world]</sup>, and there will be famines. These things are <sup>[only]</sup> the beginning of terrible times.

“But pay attention to yourselves, for people will turn you over to <sup>[Jewish]</sup> councils <sup>[i.e., for judgment]</sup> and you will be beaten in synagogues. You will stand trial before governors and kings for being loyal to me. <sup>[This will afford you an opportunity]</sup> for witnessing to them.
And the good news must first be preached to people of all nations.

And when they lead you into court, and hand you over <sup>[to be judged]</sup>, do not worry ahead of time about what you will say, but <sup>[just]</sup> say whatever you are told <sup>[i.e., by God]</sup> when the time comes.

And <sup>[at that time]</sup> a person will turn his brother over to be killed, and the father will do the same to his child. And children will rebel against their parents and will turn them over to be killed.

You will be hated by all people because you belong to me, but the person who holds out <sup>[i.e., remains faithful to God]</sup> until the end <sup>[i.e., the end of this time of severe persecution]</sup> will be saved <sup>[from destruction]</sup>.

`But when you see that disgusting thing that causes total destruction <sup>[i.e., the Roman army. See Luke 21:20]</sup> standing where it ought not to be <sup>[i.e., surrounding the besieged city of Jerusalem]</sup>, (let the reader understand <sup>[what is meant by this]</sup>), then those of you in Judea are to run away into the <sup>[nearby]</sup> mountains.

Also the person who is on a housetop <sup>[Note: This was a flat area where people retired for rest, prayer, etc., with its stairway on the outside]</sup> should not go down into his house to take anything out <sup>[when he flees]</sup>.
The person who is in a field <sup>[i.e., doing farm work]</sup> should not return <sup>[to his house]</sup> to get his coat <sup>[even]</sup>.
<sup>[It will be]</sup> too bad for those who are pregnant and those who are nursing babies in those days.
And pray that <sup>[your flight]</sup> will not be in the winter.
There will be great trouble during those days, such as had never been from the beginning of God’s creation until the present, or even will be <sup>[in the days to come]</sup>.

And if the Lord had not kept those days as short as they were, no one would have been saved <sup>[i.e., from the devastating destruction]</sup>. But for the sake of the elect <sup>[i.e., God’s people]</sup>, whom He chose, He kept those days short.
And then if anyone says to you, ‘Look, here is Christ,’ or ‘Look, there He is,’ do not believe him.

For false christs and false prophets will appear and they will perform <sup>[miraculous]</sup> signs and <sup>[supernatural]</sup> wonders in order to lead astray <sup>[even]</sup> the elect, if possible.
But pay attention, for I have told you <sup>[all this]</sup> ahead of time.

`But after those days of terrible trouble <sup>[i.e., the next major event on God’s calendar will be]</sup>, the sun will become dark, and <sup>[therefore]</sup> the moon will not <sup>[be able to]</sup> shed its <sup>[reflected]</sup> light,

and the stars will be falling from the sky and the forces of the heavens will be shaken. <sup>[Note: From the description of these same events in Luke 21:25-26, it is possible that this is figurative language for great calamities happening on earth]</sup>.
And then they will see the Son of man coming in the clouds with great power and splendor.

And He will send out His angels and gather His elect <sup>[i.e., God’s people]</sup> from all directions, from the farthest part of the earth to the farthest part of the sky <sup>[i.e., from throughout the whole world]</sup>.
“Now learn this parable from the fig tree. When its tender sprouts appear and its leaves begin to develop, you know that summer is near.
In the same way also, when you see these things happening, you <sup>[should]</sup> know that He <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup> is as near as your <sup>[front]</sup> door.
Truly I tell you, the people of this generation will not <sup>[all]</sup> die off before all these things <sup>[i.e., mentioned above]</sup> happen.
Although the sky and the earth will pass away <sup>[See II Pet. 3:10]</sup>, my words will not pass away <sup>[i.e., they will certainly come true]</sup>.
But no one knows when that day or that hour will come; not even the angels in heaven or the Son of God know, but only the Father does.
“You should pay attention; be alert and pray, for you do not know when the time will come <sup>[i.e., for the Lord to return]</sup>.

It is like a man who left his house on a trip to another country. <sup>[Before leaving]</sup> he put his servants in charge of his affairs, giving each one a <sup>[particular]</sup> job to do, then he ordered the gatekeeper to stay alert <sup>[i.e., while he was gone]</sup>.
So, be alert, because you do not know when the owner of the house will return, whether <sup>[some]</sup> evening, at midnight, at dawn, or at <sup>[mid-]</sup> morning.
<sup>[Be alert]</sup>, for he might come unexpectedly and find you asleep.
And what I am telling you, I am telling everyone. Be alert!”

Now the Passover Festival and the Festival of Unleavened Bread were held two days later. <sup>[Note: This was the annual Jewish Festival week commemorating Israel’s deliverance from Egyptian bondage under Moses’ leadership. The unleavened bread was specially baked to contain no yeast, and was eaten for seven days as part of the celebration]</sup>. And the leading priests and experts in the Law of Moses were looking for a way to take Jesus by trickery and kill Him.
But they reasoned, “We will not <sup>[take him]</sup> during the Festival, because it might start a riot among the people.”

And while Jesus was in Bethany <sup>[Note: This was a small village fewer than two miles east of Jerusalem]</sup>, sitting at the dinner table in the house of Simon, the man with an infectious skin disease <sup>[Note: This man had probably been healed by now]</sup>, a woman with an alabaster <sup>[i.e., stone]</sup> jar of very expensive perfume came to Him, broke the jar and poured the perfume on His head.
But some <sup>[of the disciples. See Matt. 26:8]</sup> were upset among themselves, saying, “What is the reason for wasting this perfume?

It might have been sold for a large sum of money and the money given to poor people.” <sup>[Note: The amount indicated here was equivalent to 300 twelve-hour days of a farm laborer’s pay, or over $32,000 in 2005]</sup>. And they complained about her <sup>[doing this]</sup>.
But Jesus said, “Let her alone. Why are you bothering this woman? For she has done a kind deed to me.
You will always have poor people with you, and you can do something good for them whenever you want to, but you will not always have me with you.
She has done what she could. She has applied this aromatic oil to my body in anticipation of my burial.

For truly I tell you, wherever the Gospel message will be preached throughout the whole world, what this woman has done <sup>[for me]</sup> will <sup>[also]</sup> be told about as a memorial of her <sup>[kindness to me]</sup>.”
Now Judas Iscariot, who was one of the twelve apostles, left and went to the leading priests in order to make arrangements for turning Jesus over to them.

And when they <sup>[i.e., the leading priests]</sup> realized this, they were very pleased and promised to give him money. So, Judas began looking for a convenient way to turn Jesus over to these Jewish leaders.

Now on the first day of the Festival of Unleavened Bread <sup>[i.e., the beginning of the Passover Festival week]</sup>, when they sacrificed the Passover <sup>[lamb]</sup>, Jesus’ disciples said to Him, “Where do you want us to go to make preparations for you to eat the Passover meal?”
So, He sent two of His disciples, saying to them, “Go into the city <sup>[of Jerusalem]</sup> and there a man carrying a pitcher of water will meet you; follow him.
And wherever he enters a house <sup>[follow him inside and]</sup> say to the owner, ‘The Teacher says, where is my guest room where I can eat the Passover meal with my disciples?’
And the owner himself will show you a large upstairs room all furnished and ready. Make preparations for us there <sup>[i.e., to observe the Passover meal]</sup>.”
So, the disciples left and entered the city <sup>[of Jerusalem]</sup>. They found everything just as Jesus told them <sup>[it would be]</sup>, and they made preparations for the Passover meal.
When evening came, Jesus arrived <sup>[at the upstairs room]</sup> with the twelve apostles.
As they reclined at the table to eat, <sup>[Note: See Matt. 23:6]</sup> Jesus said, “Truly I tell you, one of you who is eating with me will turn me over <sup>[i.e., to the Jewish leaders]</sup>.”
And they <sup>[all]</sup> began to be grieved and said to Him, one by one, “Am I the one?”
And He answered them, “It is one of you twelve, <sup>[the one]</sup> who is dipping <sup>[his bread]</sup> with me in the <sup>[sauce]</sup> bowl.
For the Son of man is going <sup>[to die]</sup>, but it is too bad for that person who will turn me over to the Jewish leaders! It would be better for that man if he had not been born.”

And as they were eating He took a <sup>[small]</sup> loaf of bread, and when He had asked God’s blessing on it, He broke it and gave <sup>[pieces]</sup> to His disciples and said, “Take this, it is <sup>[i.e., represents]</sup> my <sup>[physical]</sup> body.”
Then He took a cup <sup>[i.e., possibly containing wine made from fresh or preserved grape juice]</sup>, and when He had given thanks to God, He passed it to them and they all drank from it.
And He said to them, “This is <sup>[i.e., represents]</sup> my blood of the Agreement <sup>[i.e., between God and mankind]</sup> which is <sup>[to be]</sup> poured out for many people.
Truly I tell you, I will not drink of this fruit of the vine <sup>[i.e., grape juice]</sup> any more until that day when I will drink it again <sup>[i.e., figuratively]</sup> in the kingdom of God.”
And after they had sung a hymn, they went up to the Mount of Olives.
Then Jesus said to His disciples, “All of you will have doubts about me, for it is written <sup>[Zech. 13:7]</sup>, ‘I will strike down the shepherd and the sheep will be scattered everywhere.’
However, after I am raised up, I will go on ahead of you to Galilee.”
But Peter said to Him, “Although everyone else will have doubts <sup>[about you]</sup>, I never will.”
And Jesus said to him, “Truly I tell you, today, in fact this very night, before the rooster crows twice, you will deny <sup>[knowing]</sup> me three times.”
But Peter kept insisting emphatically, “Even if I have to die with you, still I will not deny <sup>[knowing]</sup> you.” And all the rest of the disciples spoke the same way.

Jesus and His disciples came to a place called Gethsemene <sup>[Note: This was an olive orchard on a hillside just east of Jerusalem]</sup>. He said to His disciples, “You sit here while I <sup>[go away and]</sup> pray.”
And He took Peter, James and John with Him and began to be troubled and deeply distressed.
He said to them, “My soul is extremely grieved, even to the point where I could die. Stay here and be alert <sup>[i.e., in prayer]</sup>.”
And He went on ahead a short distance and fell to the ground and prayed for that hour <sup>[i.e., that time of terrible grief]</sup> to pass away from Him, if it were at all possible.

So, He said, “Abba, <sup>[which means]</sup> Father, everything is possible with you. Take this cup <sup>[i.e., of suffering]</sup> away from me; however, let it not be what I want but what you want for me.”
And when He came back and found His disciples sleeping He said to Peter, “Simon, are you sleeping? Could you not stay alert <sup>[in prayer]</sup> for one hour?

Be alert and pray, so that you do not give in to temptation. The spirit of a person is truly willing <sup>[i.e., to do a certain thing]</sup>, but his physical nature is <sup>[often]</sup> too weak <sup>[to carry it out]</sup>.”
And He went away again and prayed, saying the same words.
Then He came back again and found His disciples still asleep, because they had not been able to keep their eyes open at all.

And He came back a third time and said to them, “<sup>[You might as well]</sup> Go ahead and sleep now and get your rest. That is enough; the time has come. See, the Son of man is about to be turned over into the hands of sinners.
Get up, let us be going. Look, the one who will turn me over <sup>[i.e., to the Jewish leaders]</sup> is nearby.”

And immediately, as Jesus was still speaking, Judas, one of the twelve apostles came with a crowd who were carrying swords and clubs. <sup>[They had come]</sup> from the leading priests, the experts in the Law of Moses and the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders.

Now the one who turned Him over to the Jewish leaders <sup>[i.e., Judas]</sup> had given them a signal, saying, “Whoever I give a kiss <sup>[of greeting]</sup> to, he is the one; arrest him and lead him away under guard.”
And when Judas came, immediately he went to Jesus and said, “Rabbi,” and then kissed Him <sup>[enthusiastically]</sup>.
Then the soldiers and officers arrested Him and began taking Him away.

But a certain disciple who was standing nearby <sup>[i.e., Peter. See John 18:10]</sup> drew his sword and struck the head priest’s servant <sup>[i.e., Malchus. See John 18:10]</sup>, shearing off his ear.
Then Jesus said to them, “Have you come out to arrest me with swords and clubs like you would a thief?
I was with you every day, teaching in the Temple, and you did not <sup>[come to]</sup> take me. But this has happened so that the Scriptures would be fulfilled.”
And all of His disciples left Him and ran away.

And a certain young man <sup>[Note: Some think this is a reference to Mark himself]</sup>, who had a linen outer garment thrown over his scantily clad body, was following Jesus until they <sup>[i.e., the soldiers and officers]</sup> took hold of him. <sup>[Note: The wording here does not indicate total nakedness, but rather the wearing of underclothing only]</sup>
Then he ran away in his underclothing, leaving his linen outer garment behind.
Then they led Jesus away to the head priest, and all the leading priests, <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders and experts in the Law of Moses came together with Him.
But Peter had followed Him from a distance, right into the courtyard of the head priest. He was sitting there with the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> officers, warming himself by the light of the fire.

Now the leading priests and the entire Council <sup>[called the “Sanhedrin”]</sup> were looking for <sup>[false]</sup> witnesses to testify against Jesus in order to put Him to death, but did not find any.
For many persons gave false testimony against Him, but their stories did not harmonize.
Then two people <sup>[See Matt. 26:60]</sup> stood up and gave false testimony against Him, saying,
“We heard him say, ‘I will destroy this Temple, which was constructed by hand, and in three days I will build another one, not made by hand.’”
And not even <sup>[in this attempt did]</sup> their testimony harmonize.
The head priest stood up in front of them and asked Jesus, “Do you not have anything to answer? What about this charge being made against you by these people?”
But Jesus kept quiet and did not give them any answer. Again the head priest asked Him, “Are you the Christ <sup>[i.e., God’s specially chosen one]</sup>?”
And Jesus answered, “Yes, I am; and you will see the Son of man sitting at the right side of Power <sup>[i.e., God, Himself]</sup>, and coming on the clouds of the sky.”
And the head priest tore at his clothing <sup>[i.e., as an expression of frustration]</sup> and said, “What additional need do we have for witnesses?
You <sup>[yourselves]</sup> have heard him speaking against God. What do you people think <sup>[i.e., about such statements]</sup>?” And they all condemned Him as deserving the death penalty.

And some of them began to spit on Him, then to cover His face <sup>[with a blindfold]</sup> and hit Him <sup>[with their fists]</sup>, saying, “Prophesy <sup>[i.e., tell who hit you]</sup>.” And the officers in charge of Him also struck Him with their hands.
Now when Peter was in the courtyard downstairs <sup>[i.e., from where the Sanhedrin was having its meeting. See verse 55]</sup>, one of the head priest’s servant girls came in,
and seeing Peter warming himself by the fire, she looked at him and said, “You were with Jesus from Nazareth, too.”
But he denied it, saying, “I do not know or understand what you are talking about.” Then he went out onto the porch and the rooster crowed.
When the servant girl saw him <sup>[there]</sup>, she again began saying to those nearby, “This man is one of them <sup>[i.e., a disciple of Jesus]</sup>.”

But again he denied it. Then after a little while those standing nearby said to Peter again, “We know for sure that you are one of them because you are from Galilee.” <sup>[Note: Peter’s accent gave him away. See Matt. 26:73]</sup>.
Then Peter began to curse and swear, saying, “I do not know this man you are talking about.”

Immediately the rooster crowed for the second time and Peter was reminded of the words of Jesus, who had said to him, “You will deny <sup>[knowing]</sup> me three times before the rooster crows twice.” And as he thought about this, he cried.

As soon as it was morning the leading priests, along with the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders and experts in the Law of Moses and the entire Council, held a conference. Then they tied Jesus up, led Him away and turned Him over to Pilate <sup>[the Roman governor]</sup>.
Pilate asked Him, “Are you the king of the Jews?” Jesus answered him, “You have said so.”
<sup>[Then]</sup> the leading priests accused Him of many things.
So, Pilate again asked Him, “Do you not have any answer? Look at how many charges they are bringing against you.”
But still Jesus gave no answer to anything, so that Pilate was amazed.
Now at the Passover Festival Pilate customarily released whatever prisoner the people asked him to.
One prisoner, named Barabbas, was kept chained up in prison along with men who had committed murder during a rebellion.
The crowd <sup>[then]</sup> went to Pilate and asked him to do what he was accustomed to doing for them <sup>[i.e., release a prisoner of their choosing during the Passover Festival]</sup>.
Pilate answered them; “Do you want me to release to you the king of the Jews <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup>?”
For he perceived that the leading priests had turned Jesus over to him out of envy.
But the leading priests stirred up the crowd to insist that Pilate release Barabbas to them instead.
<sup>[So]</sup>, Pilate again asked them, “Then what shall I do to this one you call the king of the Jews?”
And they shouted out again, “Crucify him!”
And Pilate said to them, “Why <sup>[should we]</sup>; what wrong has he done?” But they shouted out even louder, “Crucify him.”
So Pilate, wanting to satisfy the crowd, released Barabbas to them, and after he had Jesus flogged, he turned Him over to be crucified.
Then the soldiers led Jesus away to the courtyard of the governor’s headquarters, where they assembled the entire battalion.

They placed a purple robe on Him <sup>[Note: Matt. 27:28 calls this a “scarlet” robe. In that day, any color with a mixture of “red” in it was often called “purple”]</sup>, and making a wreath out of thorns, they placed it on His head.
Then they began “greeting” Him with “Hey, king of the Jews!”
They beat His head with a stick, spat on Him, knelt down before Him and <sup>[mockingly]</sup> worshiped Him.
And when they had mocked Him, they took the purple robe off of Him and put His own clothing <sup>[back]</sup> on Him. Then they led Him out to crucify Him.

They forced a passerby, named Simon of Cyrene <sup>[Note: This was a city in the northern African country of Libya]</sup>, the father of Alexander and Rufus, who was coming in from the countryside, to go with them so he could carry Jesus’ cross <sup>[i.e., probably only the cross-beam portion]</sup>.
Then they brought Jesus to the place called Golgotha which, being interpreted, means “the place of the skull” <sup>[i.e., probably because it was a skull-shaped knoll in appearance]</sup>.
They offered Him wine, mixed with aromatic spices, but He refused to accept it. <sup>[Note: Matt. 27:34 calls this “gall,” a bitter drug for the purpose of dulling the pain of crucifixion]</sup>.
So, they crucified Him and divided His clothing by gambling for them to decide what each soldier would get.

It was nine o’clock in the morning and they crucified Him. <sup>[Note: John 19:14 indicates that the crucifixion took place about noon. This can be harmonized if we take Mark’s account to mean when the entire process began]</sup>.
The inscription, stating the charge against Him, was attached above His head <sup>[i.e., to the upright portion of the cross]</sup>. It read, “The king of the Jews.”
And they also crucified two thieves with Him, one at His right side and one at His left.
Some ancient manuscripts add verse “And the Scripture was fulfilled, which said, ‘He was counted with the lawbreakers.’”
And those who passed by the cross shouted abuse at Him and shook their heads <sup>[in derision]</sup>, saying, “Ha, you who said you would destroy the Temple and rebuild it in three days,
save yourself <sup>[from dying]</sup> and come down from the cross.”
In the same way, the leading priests also mocked Him among themselves, along with the experts in the Law of Moses, saying, “He saved other people, now he cannot even save himself.
This ‘Christ, the king of Israel,’ let him now come down from the cross so we can see it and believe in him.” And the thieves who were being crucified with Him also spoke abusively to Him.
And when it became noon there was darkness over the entire land until three o’clock in the afternoon.

Then at three o’clock Jesus shouted in a loud voice, “Eloi, Eloi, lama, sabachthani” <sup>[Note: These words were in the commonly spoken Aramaic language]</sup>, which being interpreted, means “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?”
And when some of those standing there heard this, they said, “Look, he is calling for Elijah.”

Then one of the soldiers ran and filled a sponge full of sour wine, put it on a stick and gave it to Him to drink, saying, “Let him alone; let us see if Elijah comes to take him down <sup>[from the cross]</sup>.”
Then Jesus spoke in a loud voice and gave up His spirit <sup>[to God. See Luke 23:46]</sup>.
Then the Temple curtain was torn in two from top to bottom.
And when the military officer in charge of one hundred men, who was standing in front of Jesus, saw Him die like that, he said, “Truly this man was the Son of God.”

And there were also some women watching all this from a distance. Among them were Mary from Magdala; Mary the mother of the younger James and <sup>[his brother]</sup> Joseph <sup>[i.e., probably the wife of Clopas. See John 19:25]</sup>; and Salome <sup>[i.e., the mother of James and John and wife of Zebedee. See Matt. 27:56]</sup>.
These women had ministered to Jesus’ needs when they followed Him from Galilee. Others watching were many women who had come up to Jerusalem with Him.
When evening had come, and since it was the day of Preparation <sup>[for the Passover Festival]</sup>, being the day before the <sup>[special]</sup> Sabbath Day,

Joseph, from Arimathea, a highly respected member of the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> Council, who was expecting the kingdom of God, went boldly to Pilate <sup>[i.e., the Roman appointed governor]</sup> and asked <sup>[him]</sup> for the body of Jesus.
Pilate was amazed that Jesus had already died, and calling the military officer to him, asked how long He had been dead.
And when he found out from the military officer, he granted the body to Joseph.

<sup>[Joseph then]</sup> brought a linen cloth, and after taking Jesus down from the cross, wrapped the linen cloth around His body and placed it in a grave site that had been cut out of <sup>[a ledge of]</sup> rock. Then he rolled a huge stone across the entrance to the cave.
<sup>[Now]</sup> Mary from Magdala and Mary the mother of Joseph <sup>[See verse 40]</sup> saw where the body was laid.

Now when the Sabbath day was over <sup>[i.e., after sunset on Saturday evening]</sup>, Mary from Magdala; Mary the mother of James <sup>[i.e., the “other Mary” of Matt. 28:1, who was probably Jesus’ mother. See Matt. 27:56]</sup>; and Salome <sup>[i.e., the mother of James and John and wife of Zebedee. See Matt. 27:56]</sup>, bought spices <sup>[i.e., consisting of aloes and other aromatic perfumes]</sup> in order to apply them to His body <sup>[i.e., as a means of embalming]</sup>.
And very early on the first day of the week, after sunrise <sup>[i.e., on Sunday morning]</sup>, they went to the grave site.
They were questioning among themselves, “Who will roll away the stone from the entrance of the cave for us?”
And when they looked up, they saw that the stone had <sup>[already been]</sup> rolled away, and it was very large.
As they were entering the cave, they were amazed when they saw a young man, dressed in a white robe, sitting at the right side of the entrance.

He said to them, “Do not be amazed. You are looking for Jesus from Nazareth, who was crucified, <sup>[are you not]</sup>? He has risen from the dead. He is not here. Look at the place where they laid Him.
So, go and tell His disciples and Peter, ‘He is going on ahead of you to Galilee. You will see Him there, just as He told you.’”
So, they left, running out of the cave, <sup>[and]</sup> trembling with amazement. And they said nothing to anyone about this for they were afraid.
Now when Jesus arose from the dead early on the first day of the week <sup>[i.e., Sunday morning]</sup>, He appeared first to Mary from Magdala, from whom He had driven out seven evil spirits.
Then she went and told those disciples who had been with Him what had happened. They were sorrowful and began to cry.
And when they heard that Jesus was alive and had been seen by Mary from Magdala, they refused to believe it.

After these things happened, Jesus showed Himself in a different form to two disciples <sup>[Note: One was named Cleopas. See Luke 24:13-18]</sup>, as they were walking out into the countryside <sup>[i.e., to the village of Emmaus, about seven miles northwest of Jerusalem. See Luke 24:13]</sup>.
And these two men went and told about their encounter with Jesus to the rest of the disciples, but they did not believe them either.

Later on Jesus showed Himself to the eleven apostles as they sat <sup>[i.e., reclined. See Matt. 23:6]</sup>, eating a meal. He scolded them for their lack of faith and stubbornness because they refused to believe those who had seen Him <sup>[alive]</sup> after His resurrection.
Then He said to them, “You <sup>[men]</sup> go into the entire world and preach the good news to every person.
The person who believes <sup>[i.e., the Gospel]</sup> and is immersed will be saved <sup>[i.e., from condemnation]</sup>, but whoever does not believe it will be condemned.

And these <sup>[miraculous]</sup> signs will attend <sup>[the conversion of]</sup> those who believe. <sup>[Note: This refers to the supernatural miracles performed by the apostles and their associates during the apostolic age. See verse 20 and Acts 2:43]</sup>. In my name <sup>[i.e., by my authority]</sup>, they will drive out evil spirits; they will speak in languages <sup>[supernaturally]</sup>;

they will pick up snakes <sup>[i.e., without being harmed]</sup>; and if they happen to drink anything poisonous, it will not harm them; they will place hands on sick people <sup>[i.e., with prayer]</sup> and they will be healed.”
So then, after the Lord Jesus had spoken to them He was taken up to heaven, where He sat down at the right side of God.

And the apostles <sup>[See verses 14-15]</sup> went out and preached everywhere. The Lord worked with them and confirmed the message <sup>[i.e., verified that it was true]</sup> by means of the <sup>[miraculous]</sup> signs which accompanied their ministry. May it be so.
Your Excellency Theophilus: Since so many people have undertaken the task of compiling a narrative of events that have happened among us,
<sup>[that is, people]</sup> who were ministers of the word and original eyewitnesses <sup>[of these events]</sup>, and who passed them on to us; <sup>[and since]</sup>
I have thoroughly investigated the entire series of events <sup>[myself]</sup>, I felt it would be good for me also to write you an orderly account of them.
Then you could know how reliable the information is that you have been told.
In the days of King Herod, of Judea, there was a certain priest named Zacharias of the “Abijah” division. He had a wife named Elizabeth who was a descendant of Aaron.
They both did what was right in God’s sight <sup>[by]</sup> living blamelessly according to all the commandments and requirements of the Lord.
But they had no children, because Elizabeth was unable to bear offspring due to their both being very old.
Now it happened that when Zacharias’ turn came in his division,

to perform his customary duties as a priest before God, he drew the responsibility <sup>[that day]</sup> of entering the Temple of the Lord to burn the incense offering. <sup>[Note: This was done on a golden Altar in the Temple by a priest twice a day]</sup>.
There was a whole crowd of people praying outside because it was the time for burning the incense offering.
Just then an angel from the Lord appeared to him, standing at the right side of the incense altar.
Zacharias became disturbed when he saw the angel and was gripped by fear.
But the angel said to him, “Do not be afraid Zacharias, for your earnest prayer has been heard. Your wife Elizabeth will give birth to a son and you will name him John.
And you will have joy and happiness and many people will rejoice when he is born.

For he will be great in the sight of the Lord and he will not drink any wine or intoxicating drinks <sup>[Note: This restriction suggests that John may have taken the special vow of consecration making him a “Nazarite”]</sup>. And he will be filled with the Holy Spirit from the time he is born.
And he will influence many Israelites to turn to the Lord their God.

He will go on ahead of the Messiah <sup>[See verse 76]</sup>, in the spirit and power of Elijah, to rekindle the affections of fathers for their children, and <sup>[to influence]</sup> disobedient people to live wisely like righteous people. And he will prepare the people to be ready for <sup>[the coming of]</sup> the Lord.”
And Zacharias said to the angel, “How will I know this? For I am an old man and my wife is <sup>[also]</sup> very old.”
The angel answered him, “I am Gabriel, who stands in the presence of God, and I was sent to speak to you and to bring you this good news.

And consider this, you will <sup>[have to]</sup> remain a mute and be unable to speak until the day when these things happen because you did not believe my words, which will be fulfilled in due time.”
Now the people who waited <sup>[outside]</sup> for Zacharias were very surprised that he was staying in the Temple so long.

And when he <sup>[finally]</sup> came out, he was not able to talk to them. Then they realized he had seen a vision while in the Temple. So, he remained a mute and had to make <sup>[hand]</sup> signs to them <sup>[i.e., in order to be understood]</sup>.
And so it happened, when he had completed the <sup>[required]</sup> days of his ministry, he went home.
After those days, his wife Elizabeth became pregnant and she stayed in seclusion for five months, saying,
“The Lord has done this for me, and at this time <sup>[in my life]</sup> He has favored me by removing the stigma placed on me by people <sup>[i.e., for not having children]</sup>.”
Now in the sixth month <sup>[of Elizabeth’s pregnancy]</sup> the angel Gabriel was sent from God to the Galilean town of Nazareth,
to a virgin engaged to a man named Joseph, a descendant of King David. The virgin’s name was Mary.
The angel came to her and said, “Greetings, you are someone greatly favored <sup>[i.e., by God]</sup>; the Lord is with you.”
But she became very troubled by what he said and wondered what such a greeting could mean.
Then the angel said to her, “Do not be afraid Mary, for you have received favor from God.
And consider this, you will become pregnant and give birth to a son and you will name Him Jesus.
He will be great and will be called the Son of the Highest <sup>[i.e., God]</sup>, and the Lord will give Him the throne of His forefather King David.
And He will rule over the <sup>[spiritual]</sup> descendants of Jacob forever and there will be no end to His kingdom.”
And Mary said to the angel, “How can this be, since I have never had sex with a man?”
The angel answered, “The Holy Spirit will come upon you and the power of the Highest <sup>[i.e., God]</sup> will overshadow you. Therefore, the child you carry will be called the holy Son of God.
And consider this, your relative Elizabeth is also pregnant with a son in spite of her old age, and <sup>[even though]</sup> she was said to be unable to have children, she is now six months along.
For nothing that God says <sup>[will happen]</sup> is impossible.”
And Mary said, “Here I am, the Lord’s slave-girl; let it happen to me according to what you say.” Then the angel left her.
About that time Mary made a hurried trip to a Judean town in the hill country. <sup>[Note: This was the same province in which Jerusalem was located]</sup>.
<sup>[Upon arriving]</sup> she entered the home of Zacharias and greeted <sup>[his wife]</sup> Elizabeth.
And it happened when Elizabeth heard Mary greet her, the baby in Elizabeth’s womb “kicked,” and she was filled with the Holy Spirit.

Then she spoke out in a loud voice and said, “<sup>[Mary]</sup>, you are <sup>[truly]</sup> blessed <sup>[from]</sup> among <sup>[all]</sup> women, and the child you will bear <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup> is blessed <sup>[as well]</sup>.
But why has this happened to me, that the mother of my Lord should come to <sup>[visit]</sup> me?
For listen, when I heard your voice greeting me, the baby in my womb “kicked” for joy.

And she who <sup>[has]</sup> believed <sup>[the promises made by the Lord]</sup> is <sup>[truly]</sup> blessed, for the things which have been spoken to her <sup>[i.e., to Mary]</sup> by the Lord will <sup>[indeed]</sup> be fulfilled.”
Then Mary said, “My soul lifts up the Lord,
and my spirit has rejoiced in God, my Savior.
For He has looked favorably on the humble circumstances of His servant-girl. And consider this, all generations of people will call me ‘blessed’ from now on.
For the Mighty One has done great things for me, and His name is holy.
He shows mercy to generation after generation of people who have reverence forHim.
He has demonstrated power with His arm. He hasscattered those who are proud in the thoughts of their heart.
He has dethroned rulers and has exalted humble people.
He has filled the hungry with good things and has sent the rich away empty-handed.
(As He said to our forefathers), He has helped His servants, the Israelites, <sup>[showing]</sup> that He remembered to be merciful
to Abraham and his descendants forever.”
So, Mary stayed with <sup>[her relative]</sup> Elizabeth for about three months <sup>[i.e., until about the time John was born. See 1:26-31]</sup>, and then returned to her home.
Now when Elizabeth was due to deliver, she gave birth to a son.
When her neighbors and relatives heard that the Lord had extended great mercy toward her <sup>[i.e., in giving her a child]</sup>, they rejoiced with her.

And it happened on the eighth day <sup>[i.e., since the baby was born]</sup> that they came to circumcise the child <sup>[i.e., to perform the Jewish rite of identity, signifying the responsibility to observe the Law of Moses]</sup>. And they <sup>[i.e., probably the neighbors and/or relatives of Elizabeth and Zacharias]</sup> suggested that they name him Zacharias, after his father.
But his mother answered, “No, he will be named John.”
And they replied to her, “<sup>[But]</sup> there are not any of your relatives named that.”
And <sup>[so]</sup> they made <sup>[hand]</sup> signs to the <sup>[baby’s]</sup> father to find out what he wanted to name him.
Then Zacharias asked for a writing tablet and wrote <sup>[the words]</sup> “His name is John.” And they were all amazed.
Just then his mouth was capable of uttering speech, and his tongue could form words, and he praised God.
Then all those who lived in the area became afraid; and people were talking about all these things throughout the entire hill country of Judea.
And everyone who heard about this incident remembered it <sup>[i.e., for some time]</sup>. They were saying, “What will this child become? For the hand of the Lord was <sup>[surely]</sup> with him.”
Then his father Zacharias was filled with the Holy Spirit and prophesied, saying,
“May the Lord be praised, <sup>[who is]</sup> the God of the Israelites, for He has come to <sup>[the aid of]</sup> His people and bought them back <sup>[i.e., from bondage]</sup>.
He has provided a horn of salvation <sup>[i.e., His saving strength]</sup> for us from a descendant of His servant King David <sup>[i.e., Jesus, born of Mary]</sup>
(as He spoke through the message of the holy prophets of long ago).
<sup>[He has provided]</sup> salvation from our enemies, and from the <sup>[destructive]</sup> hand of all those who hate us,
in order to show mercy to our forefathers and to remember His holy Agreement.
<sup>[This Agreement was]</sup> the oath by which He swore to Abraham, our forefather,
that we would be delivered from the <sup>[destructive]</sup> hand of our enemies and <sup>[then]</sup> serve Him without fear
by living holy and righteous lives before Him all of our days.
Yes, you <sup>[my]</sup> child <sup>[i.e., John]</sup>, will be called the prophet of the Highest <sup>[i.e., God]</sup>, for you will go ahead of the Lord’s presence to prepare His ways.
And <sup>[you will]</sup> provide the knowledge of salvation to His people, by <sup>[granting them]</sup> the forgiveness of their sins
because of God’s merciful compassion. Therefore, the morning sun from above will arise
to shine on those who are sitting in darkness and <sup>[are under]</sup> the shadow of death, and to direct our feet into the way of peace.”
So, the child <sup>[i.e., John]</sup> grew and became strong in <sup>[his]</sup> spirit and stayed in the desert until the day when he appeared publicly to the Israelites.

Now it happened in those days that a decree was sent out from Caesar Augustus <sup>[the Emperor of the Roman Empire]</sup> requiring <sup>[people from]</sup> the whole empire to be registered <sup>[i.e., for taxation purposes]</sup>.
This was the first registration made while Quirinius was governor of Syria.
And everyone went to register in his own home town.
So, Joseph also went from the Galilean town of Nazareth to David’s town, which was the Judean town of Bethlehem, because he was among the descendants and family of David.
<sup>[He went]</sup> to register, along with Mary, who was engaged to him and who was pregnant <sup>[at the time]</sup>.
And it happened while they were there that the due date came for her to have the baby.
So, she bore her firstborn Son and wrapped Him in <sup>[linen]</sup> cloths and laid Him in an animal’s feeding trough because there was no room for them in the lodge.
Now there were <sup>[some]</sup> shepherds in that region who were living in the fields and guarding their flocks <sup>[of sheep]</sup> at night.
<sup>[Suddenly]</sup> an angel from the Lord <sup>[was seen]</sup> standing near them and the glorious presence of the Lord shone around them, and they were very afraid.
And the angel said to them, “Do not be afraid; for look, I am bringing good news that will cause great joy to all people.
For a Savior has been born today in David’s city <sup>[i.e., Bethlehem]</sup>, who is Christ the Lord <sup>[i.e., God’s specially chosen one]</sup>.
And this is the sign <sup>[that will verify it]</sup> to you: You will find a baby wrapped in <sup>[linen]</sup> cloths lying in an animal’s feeding trough.”
Then suddenly there appeared with the angel a large number of the heavenly “army” <sup>[i.e., of angels]</sup>, who were praising God and saying,
“May there be glory to God in the highest <sup>[heavens]</sup>, and may there be peace on earth among men who are pleasing to God.”
And it happened when the angels left them and returned to heaven, that the shepherds said to one another, “Let us go to Bethlehem now and see what all happened that the Lord told us about.”
So, they went quickly and found both Mary and Joseph with the baby, lying in the animal’s feeding trough.
And when they saw this, they told <sup>[everyone]</sup> what the angel had said about this baby.
And all who heard it marveled at the things the shepherds told them.
But Mary treasured all these things and wondered about them <sup>[often]</sup>.
Then the shepherds returned <sup>[to their fields]</sup>, honoring and praising God for everything they had heard and seen, for it all happened just as they had been told.

Eight days later it was time to circumcise Jesus <sup>[i.e., to perform the Jewish rite of identity, signifying the responsibility to observe the Law of Moses]</sup>, and they named Him Jesus, which was the name given by the angel before He was <sup>[even]</sup> conceived.

Then when the days of their purification according to the Law of Moses were completed <sup>[Note: This was a set procedure required for a Jewish mother on the birth of a child. See Lev. 12:1-8]</sup>, Joseph and Mary brought Jesus up to Jerusalem to present Him to the Lord.
(It is recorded in the law of the Lord <sup>[Ex. 13:2, 12]</sup> that every firstborn male child be considered holy <sup>[i.e., specially dedicated]</sup> to the Lord.)
They came to offer a sacrifice of two doves or two young pigeons, in accordance with what is written in the Law of Moses.

Now there was a righteous and devoted man named Simeon at Jerusalem, who was expecting the consolation of the Israelites <sup>[i.e., the coming of the Messiah to save them]</sup>, and the Holy Spirit was upon him.
Now it had been revealed to him by the Holy Spirit that he would not die until he had seen the Lord’s Christ <sup>[i.e., God’s, specially chosen one]</sup>.
And he was led by the Holy Spirit into the Temple, and when Joseph and Mary brought the baby Jesus in, so they could do to Him what was customary under the Law of Moses,
Simeon took Jesus in his arms, praised God and said,
“Master, you may now allow your servant to be released <sup>[i.e., die]</sup> in peace, according to what you said.
For my eyes have seen your salvation <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup>,
which <sup>[i.e., whom]</sup> you have prepared before all people <sup>[i.e., for their benefit]</sup>,
<sup>[to be]</sup> a light for revealing <sup>[God’s way of salvation]</sup> to the <sup>[unconverted]</sup> Gentiles, and a <sup>[source of]</sup> glory to your people, the Israelites.”
And Jesus’ <sup>[step-]</sup> father and His mother were amazed at the things said about Him.

Then Simeon <sup>[asked God to]</sup> bless them and said to Jesus’ mother Mary, “Look, many Israelites will be caused to fall <sup>[i.e., into spiritual ruin]</sup> or rise <sup>[i.e., to spiritual renewal]</sup> because of Him. And <sup>[He will be]</sup> a ‘sign’ <sup>[for people]</sup> to speak against,
and thus reveal the thoughts of many hearts. Yes, and a sword will cut you <sup>[Mary]</sup> to the heart <sup>[i.e., causing you inward pain]</sup>.”

And there was a woman prophet named Anna, the daughter of Phanuel, from the tribe of Asher <sup>[i.e., she was a descendant of one of Jacob’s son]</sup>. (She was very old, having lived with her husband for <sup>[only]</sup> seven years after being married,

and has been a widow <sup>[all these years]</sup>, and is now eighty-four years old.) She has never left the Temple, but worships <sup>[God]</sup> by fasting <sup>[i.e., going without food and/or water for religious reasons]</sup> and praying night and day.

She came forward at that very time and thanked God <sup>[in prayer]</sup> and spoke about the child <sup>[Jesus]</sup> to everyone <sup>[in the Temple area]</sup> who was expecting the redemption of the people of Jerusalem <sup>[i.e., their liberation from sin]</sup>.
So, when Joseph and Mary had completed everything required by the law of the Lord, they returned to their <sup>[current home]</sup> town of Nazareth, in Galilee.
And the child <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup> grew and became strong. He was filled with wisdom and God’s favor was upon Him.

Now Jesus’ parents went to Jerusalem every year to attend the Passover Festival. <sup>[Note: This was the annual Jewish festival commemorating Israel’s deliverance from Egyptian bondage under Moses’ leadership]</sup>.
When Jesus was twelve years old, they <sup>[all]</sup> went up to the Festival as usual.
And when they had completed <sup>[all]</sup> the days <sup>[of festivities]</sup>, and were returning <sup>[home]</sup>, the boy Jesus stayed behind in Jerusalem. Now His parents did not know this,
so thinking He was in the caravan, they continued traveling for an entire day <sup>[before]</sup> they began looking for Him among their relatives and friends.
And when they could not find Him, they returned to Jerusalem to look for Him.
And it happened after three days that they found Him in the Temple, sitting in the middle of the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> teachers, listening to them and asking questions.
And all of them who heard Him were amazed at His understanding <sup>[i.e., of spiritual matters]</sup> and His answers <sup>[to questions they would ask Him]</sup>.

When His parents <sup>[finally]</sup> saw Him, they were very surprised and His mother asked Him, “Son, why have you treated us this way? Look, your <sup>[step-]</sup> father and I have been anxiously searching for you.”
And He replied to them, “Why were you looking for me? Did you not know that I had to be in my Father’s house <sup>[i.e., the Temple, or, “about my Father’s affairs”]</sup>?”
But they did not understand what He meant when He said that to them.
So, Jesus went down to Nazareth with them and <sup>[continued]</sup> to obey them. And His mother kept all these things <sup>[stored up]</sup> in her heart.
And Jesus advanced in wisdom; He grew up and <sup>[increased]</sup> in favor with God and the people.

Now it was the fifteenth year of the reign of Tiberius Caesar. Pontius Pilate was governor of Judea, Herod was ruler of Galilee, his brother Philip was ruler of the regions of Ituraea and Trachonitus <sup>[Note: These two men were sons of Herod the Great (See Matt. 2:1) and ruled over provinces east of the Jordan River]</sup> and Lysanias was ruler of Abilene <sup>[Note: This was a province just north of the two previously mentioned ones]</sup>.

<sup>[During this time]</sup>, while Annas and Caiaphas <sup>[Note: This man was Annas’ son-in-law. See John 18:13]</sup> were head priests, God’s message came to John <sup>[the Immerser]</sup>, son of Zacharias, <sup>[when he was]</sup> in the desert <sup>[of Judea]</sup>.

He went into the whole region around the Jordan River proclaiming that those immersed on the basis of their repentance <sup>[i.e., determining to change their hearts and lives]</sup> would receive the forgiveness of sins <sup>[from God]</sup>.

This was according to what is written in the book containing the words of Isaiah, the prophet <sup>[Isa. 40:3ff]</sup>, “The voice of a man who speaks out in the desert <sup>[urging people]</sup> to make the road for the Lord ready <sup>[for Him to travel]</sup>; to make a straight pathway for Him <sup>[to walk on]</sup>.
Every valley will be filled up and every mountain and hill will be leveled. And crooked <sup>[roads]</sup> will become straight and rough ones smoothed out.
And all mankind will see the salvation of God <sup>[i.e., become available to them]</sup>.”
He said to the crowds of people that went out <sup>[to the desert]</sup> to be immersed by him, “You children of snakes, who warned you to run away from the coming wrath <sup>[of God]</sup>?

Demonstrate by your lives that you have <sup>[really]</sup> repented, and quit saying to yourselves, ‘We claim Abraham as our forefather,’ because I tell you, God is able to make Abraham’s children out of these stones.

And even now the axe is also ready to chop down the trees at their roots <sup>[i.e., you Jews]</sup>. Every tree <sup>[i.e., person]</sup> that does not produce wholesome fruit <sup>[i.e., a godly life]</sup> will be cut down and thrown into the fire <sup>[i.e., the punishment of hell]</sup>.”
Then the crowds asked him, “What then must we do <sup>[i.e., to demonstrate our repentance]</sup>?”
And he answered them, “The person who has two coats should give <sup>[one of them]</sup> to the person who does not have any; and the person who has food should do the same thing.”
Then some tax collectors came to him to be immersed and asked him, “Teacher, what must we do <sup>[i.e., to demonstrate our repentance]</sup>?”
And he answered them; “Do not collect any more <sup>[tax money]</sup> than you are required to.”

Some soldiers on duty also asked him, “And what must we do <sup>[i.e., to demonstrate our repentance]</sup>?” And he answered them, “Do not force people to give you money or accuse them falsely, and be content with your pay.”
Now as people were anticipating <sup>[what would happen]</sup> and everyone was wondering in their hearts if perhaps John were the Christ <sup>[i.e., God’s specially chosen one]</sup>,

John answered <sup>[their concerns]</sup> by saying to all of them, “I am <sup>[the one]</sup> immersing you in water, but One who is more powerful than I will follow my <sup>[ministry]</sup>. I am not <sup>[even]</sup> worthy to loosen the straps of His sandals. He will immerse you people with the Holy Spirit and with fire <sup>[i.e., the punishment of hell. See Matt. 3:10-12]</sup>.

His separating shovel is in His hand and He will completely clear off His threshing floor <sup>[i.e., by removing all of the husks]</sup>. And He will gather the wheat into His barn, but will burn up the husks with a fire that will never go out.”
So, John preached the good news to the people with many different appeals.

But because he had rebuked Herod, the tetrarch <sup>[i.e., the governor of the fourth part of a region]</sup>, for <sup>[marrying]</sup> his brother’s wife Herodias, and for all the other bad things he had done,
to top it off, Herod put John in prison.
Now it happened when all the people had been immersed <sup>[by John]</sup>, that Jesus was also immersed <sup>[by him]</sup>. And while Jesus was praying, heaven was opened
and the Holy Spirit came down on Him in a physical form, as a dove, and a voice spoke out of heaven <sup>[saying]</sup>, “You are my dearly loved Son. I am very pleased with you.”

And Jesus was about thirty years old when He began to teach. (It was assumed that) He was the son of Joseph, who was the <sup>[legal]</sup> son of Heli <sup>[Note: A lineage through Jesus’ mother Mary follows:]</sup>
<sup>[Heli was]</sup> the son of Matthat, who was the son of Levi, who was the son of Melchi, who was the son of Jannai, who was the son of Joseph,
who was the son of Mattathias, who was the son of Amos, who was the son of Nahum, who was the son of Esli, who was the son of Naggai,
who was the son of Maath, who was the son of Mattathias, who was the son of Semein, who was the son of Josech, who was the son of Joda,
who was the son of Joanan, who was the son of Rhesa, who was the son of Zerubbabel, who was the son of Shealtiel, who was the son of Neri,
who was the son of Melchi, who was the son of Addi, who was the son of Cosam, who was the son of Elmadam, who was the son of Er,
who was the son of Jesus, who was the son of Eliezer, who was the son of Jorim, who was the son of Matthat, who was the son of Levi,
who was the son of Symeon, who was the son of Judas, who was the son of Joseph, who was the son of Jonam, who was the son of Eliakim,
who was the son of Melea, who was the son of Menna, who was the son of Mattatha, who was the son of Nathan, who was the son of David.
<sup>[And David]</sup> was the son of Jesse, who was the son of Obed, who was the son of Boaz, who was the son of Salmon, who was the son of Nahshan,
who was the son of Amminadab Some ancient manuscripts insert “who was the son of Admim,” who was the son of Arni, who was the son of Hezron, who was the son of Perez, who was the son of Judah,
who was the son of Jacob, who was the son of Isaac, who was the son of Abraham. <sup>[And Abraham]</sup> was the son of Terah, who was the son of Nahor,
who was the son of Serug, who was the son of Reu, who was the son of Peleg, who was the son of Eber, who was the son of Shelah,
who was the son of Cainan, who was the son of Arphaxad, who was the son of Shem, who was the son of Noah, who was the son of Lamech,
who was the son of Methuselah, who was the son of Enoch, who was the son of Jared, who was the son of Mahalaleel, who was the son of Cainan,
who was the son of Enos, who was the son of Seth, who was the son of Adam. <sup>[And Adam]</sup> was the son of God.
Then Jesus, full of the Holy Spirit, returned from the Jordan River <sup>[i.e., where He had been immersed. See 3:21]</sup> and was led by the Holy Spirit <sup>[See Matt. 4:1]</sup> into the desert.
During the forty days He was being put to the test by the devil, He did not eat anything; then after it was over He was <sup>[very]</sup> hungry.
Then the devil said to Him, “If you are the Son of God, command this stone to turn into bread.”
But Jesus answered him, “It is written <sup>[Deut. 8:3]</sup>, ‘A person is not to live by <sup>[eating]</sup> bread only.’”
Then he led Him up <sup>[i.e., to a high mountain. See Matt. 4:8]</sup> and showed Him all the world’s kingdoms in a fleeting instant.
And the devil said to Him, “I will give you full authority <sup>[over them]</sup> and their splendor, for it has <sup>[all]</sup> been granted to me and to whom I want to give it.
Therefore, if you will worship in front of me it will all be yours.”
And Jesus answered him, “It is written <sup>[Deut. 6:13]</sup>, ‘You are supposed to worship the Lord, your God, and serve only Him.’”
Then the devil led Jesus to Jerusalem and placed Him on the elevated wing of the Temple and said to Him, “If you are the Son of God, throw yourself down from here,
for it is written <sup>[Psa. 91:11-12]</sup>, ‘He will put his angels in charge of you, to protect you,’
and ‘They will lift you up with their hands so you do not trip over a stone.’”
And Jesus answered him, “It is said <sup>[Deut. 6:16]</sup>, ‘You shall not put the Lord your God to the test.’”
And when the devil had finished every test, he left Jesus until a <sup>[later]</sup> time.
Then Jesus returned to Galilee with power from the Holy Spirit; and a report about Him spread through the entire surrounding region.
And He was honored by everyone <sup>[as]</sup> He taught in their <sup>[Jewish]</sup> synagogues.
Jesus then went to Nazareth where He had been brought up. He entered the synagogue on the Sabbath day, according to His custom, and stood up to read <sup>[the Scriptures]</sup>.
The book <sup>[i.e., actually a scroll]</sup> of the prophet Isaiah was handed to Him. He opened the book and found the passage where it was written <sup>[Isa. 61:1f]</sup>,

“The Holy Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because He anointed me <sup>[i.e., specially chose me]</sup> to preach good news to poor people. He has sent me to proclaim freedom to those who are captives <sup>[i.e., to sin]</sup>; recovery of sight to the <sup>[spiritually as well as physically]</sup> blind; to set free those who are oppressed <sup>[i.e., by Satan]</sup> and
to proclaim the year of the Lord’s acceptance <sup>[i.e., the time when people would become His obedient followers]</sup>.”
