“And why do you not judge for yourselves what is right?

And while you are on your way to court with your opponent, make every effort to be reconciled <sup>[to him]</sup> so that he does not drag you in front of the judge, and then the judge turns you over to the officer, and the officer throws you into prison.

I tell you, you will not be released from there until you have paid the last coin <sup>[Note: This coin amounted to about five minutes of a farm laborer’s pay, or about seventy-five cents in 2005]</sup>.”

Now there were some there at that very time who told Jesus about some people from Galilee whose blood Pilate <sup>[the Roman governor]</sup> had mixed with their sacrifices <sup>[i.e., he had them murdered while they were offering their sacrifices]</sup>.
And He answered them, “Do you think those Galileans were worse sinners than all <sup>[other]</sup> Galileans because they suffered such a fate?
No, I tell you; but unless you repent <sup>[i.e., change your hearts and lives]</sup>, you <sup>[too]</sup> will all be destroyed just as they were.
Or do you think that those eighteen persons who were killed when the tower of Siloam fell on them were worse offenders than all the <sup>[other]</sup> men who lived in Jerusalem?
No, I tell you; but unless you repent, you <sup>[too]</sup> will all be destroyed just as they were.”
Then He told them this parable: “A certain man had a fig tree <sup>[which had been]</sup> planted in his orchard. When he came looking for figs on it, he did not find any.

So, he said to the orchard worker, ‘Look, I have been coming <sup>[here]</sup> looking for figs for three years now, but have not found any. Cut down the tree, <sup>[for]</sup> why should it waste the space <sup>[i.e., by not producing]</sup>?’
And the orchard worker answered him, ‘Leave it alone for <sup>[just]</sup> this year, sir, until I cultivate it and fertilize it.
And <sup>[then]</sup> if it begins to produce, fine; but if it does not, <sup>[then]</sup> you should cut it down.’”
Now Jesus was teaching in one of the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> synagogues on the Sabbath day.

And just then <sup>[He met]</sup> a woman who had an <sup>[evil]</sup> spirit that had caused her to be deformed for eighteen years. She was bent over and could not raise herself up. <sup>[Note: This was probably osteomyelitis or osteoporosis]</sup>.
And when Jesus saw her, He called and said to her, “Woman, you are freed from your deformed condition.”
Then He placed His hands on her and immediately she straightened up and praised God.

But the official of the synagogue became angry because Jesus had healed <sup>[someone]</sup> on the Sabbath day. <sup>[Note: This was viewed as a violation of proper Sabbath day observance by certain Jews who interpreted the Law of Moses with narrow legalism]</sup>. So, the official said to the crowd, “There are six days <sup>[in the week]</sup> for people to work. Therefore, you should come to be healed on one of them, and not on the Sabbath day.”
But the Lord answered them, “You hypocrites! Does not every one of you untie his ox or his donkey from its stall and lead it to water on the Sabbath day?

So, should not this woman, being a daughter <sup>[i.e., descendant]</sup> of Abraham, whom Satan has bound <sup>[with this disease]</sup> for eighteen years, have been released from this bondage on the Sabbath day?”
And as He said these things, all of His enemies felt ashamed <sup>[i.e., for objecting to such a noble act]</sup>, but the whole crowd rejoiced over all the wonderful things He was doing.
Then Jesus asked, “What is the kingdom of God like? And what shall I compare it to?
It is similar to a seed from the mustard tree, which a man took and planted in his garden. And it grew and became a <sup>[large]</sup> tree, and the birds of the sky roosted in its branches.”
And again He asked, “What shall I compare the kingdom of God to?
It is similar to yeast, which a woman worked into three batches of dough, until the dough was completely permeated <sup>[by it]</sup>.”
Then Jesus went through <sup>[various]</sup> towns and villages, teaching as He traveled on to Jerusalem.
<sup>[Along the way]</sup> someone asked Him, “Lord, will <sup>[only]</sup> a few people be saved?” And He answered them,
“You should try hard to enter <sup>[God’s kingdom]</sup> by means of the narrow door, for I tell you, many people will try to enter it but will not be able to.

Once the owner of the house gets up and closes the door, leaving you standing outside knocking on the door, <sup>[and]</sup> saying, ‘Sir, <sup>[please]</sup> open <sup>[the door]</sup> for us,’ and he answers you, ‘I do not know you or where you came from,’
then you will begin to say, ‘We used to eat and drink with you, and you taught in our streets.’

And He will say <sup>[i.e., the Lord is represented as saying this in the judgment. See Matt. 7:23; 8:11- 12]</sup>, ‘I tell you, I do not know where you came from, <sup>[so]</sup> go away from me, all you people who have done sinful things.’

<sup>[Then]</sup> there will be crying and excruciating pain, when you will see Abraham, Isaac and Jacob and all the prophets in the kingdom of God and <sup>[you will see]</sup> yourselves thrown outside.
And people will come from the east and west and from the north and south to sit down in the kingdom of God.

And certainly, those <sup>[who appear to be]</sup> last will be first <sup>[i.e., in importance and blessings]</sup>, and those <sup>[who appear to be]</sup> first will <sup>[end up]</sup> being last.”
At that very time certain Pharisees came to Jesus saying, “Get out of here and go someplace else, for King Herod wants to kill you.”

And He said to them, “Go and tell that fox <sup>[Note: This is a metaphor signifying the sly, cunning nature of King Herod]</sup>, ‘Look, I am driving out evil spirits and performing <sup>[miraculous]</sup> healings today and tomorrow, but on the third day I will be finished.’
Nevertheless, I must continue to travel today and tomorrow and the next day, for it is not possible for a prophet to be killed outside of Jerusalem.

“O, Jerusalem, Jerusalem, you people who murdered the prophets and stoned those who were sent to you! How often I have wanted to gather you people together, even as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, but you were not willing!

Look, your house <sup>[i.e., the Jewish Temple]</sup> is being abandoned <sup>[by me (?)]</sup>. You will not see me until <sup>[the time when]</sup> you will be saying, ‘Praise the One who is coming in the name of the Lord.’”

And it happened on a Sabbath day, when Jesus went to the house of one of the leading Pharisees for a meal, that they <sup>[i.e., probably other Pharisees who were also guests]</sup> were watching Him closely.
And there He saw a certain man suffering from dropsy <sup>[Note: This is a condition in which the tissues of the body retain too much fluid]</sup>.
So, Jesus asked the teachers of the Law of Moses and the Pharisees, saying, “Is it permissible by the Law of Moses to heal people on the Sabbath day, or not?”
But they did not say anything. So, Jesus took hold of the <sup>[sick]</sup> man, and <sup>[after]</sup> healing him, sent him away.
Then He said to them, “Which one of you, who has a son or an ox that falls into a well, would not immediately pull him out on a Sabbath day?”
And they were not able to give an answer to these things.

Then Jesus told a parable to those who had been invited <sup>[i.e., the dinner guests at the Pharisee’s house. See verse 1]</sup> because He noticed how they were choosing places of honor <sup>[at the dinner table]</sup>. <sup>[So]</sup>, He said to them,
“When somebody invites you to a wedding reception, do not recline at the head of the table <sup>[Note: See Matt. 23:6]</sup>, for maybe someone more honorable than you has been invited by the host.

Then the one who invited both of you will come <sup>[over]</sup> and say to you, ‘Give this man your seat <sup>[at the table]</sup>,’ and then you will be embarrassed and have to move to the least important seat.

But when you are invited <sup>[i.e., to a wedding reception]</sup>, go and recline at the least important seat, so that when the host comes <sup>[over to you]</sup>, he will say, ‘Friend, move up to a better seat.’ Then you will be shown honor in front of everyone at the dinner table.
For every person who exalts himself <sup>[as important]</sup> will be humbled, but the person who humbles himself will be exalted <sup>[as important]</sup>.”

Then Jesus also said to the Pharisee who had invited Him <sup>[to dinner]</sup>, “When you prepare a breakfast or a supper, do not invite your friends, or brothers, or relatives or rich neighbors <sup>[only]</sup>, for maybe they will invite you back, and <sup>[then]</sup> you will be repaid <sup>[for your kindness]</sup>.
But when you prepare a banquet, invite people who are poor, handicapped, crippled and blind <sup>[as well]</sup>.
Then you will receive a blessing, because these people do not have anything to pay you back with, for you will be paid back when righteous people are raised from the dead.”

And when one of the people who were reclining at the <sup>[supper]</sup> table with Jesus heard this, he said to Him, “The person who will eat bread in the <sup>[coming]</sup> kingdom of God is <sup>[certainly]</sup> blessed.”
But Jesus replied to him, “A certain man prepared a lavish supper and invited many <sup>[guests]</sup>.
When the supper was about ready, he sent his slave out to tell those who were invited, ‘Come, for everything is <sup>[now]</sup> ready.’

Then all of them, in the same way, began to excuse themselves <sup>[from attending]</sup>. The first one said to the host, ‘I just bought some land and I need to go out and look it over, so please excuse me <sup>[from attending the supper]</sup>.’
And another person said, ‘I just bought five teams of oxen and I need to go and try them out, so please excuse me <sup>[from attending the supper]</sup>.’
And the third person said, ‘I just got married, so I cannot attend <sup>[the supper]</sup>.’

Then the slave returned and told his master about these things. Then the master of the house became angry and said to his slave, ‘Go out quickly into the streets and alleys of the town and bring people who are poor, and handicapped, and blind and crippled in here <sup>[i.e., to the supper]</sup>.’
And the slave said, ‘Master, I did what you ordered me to do, and still there is room <sup>[at the dinner table]</sup>.’
Then the master said to the slave, ‘Go out into the roads and along the brushy trails and urge people to attend <sup>[the supper]</sup> so that my house will be full <sup>[of guests]</sup>.’
For I tell you, not one of those who were invited will <sup>[even]</sup> taste my supper.”
Now large crowds were following Jesus and He turned <sup>[around]</sup> and said to them,

“If anyone comes to me <sup>[i.e., to be my disciple]</sup>, and does not hate his father, and mother, and wife, and children, and brothers and sisters and, yes, even his own life, he cannot be my disciple.
<sup>[And]</sup> whoever does not carry his own cross <sup>[i.e., his responsibilities with all their difficulties]</sup> and follow me cannot be my disciple.
For which one of you who wants to build a <sup>[lookout]</sup> tower will not first sit down and calculate its cost, to see whether he has enough <sup>[money and materiel]</sup> to complete it?
For if he does not, maybe after he has laid the foundation and <sup>[then]</sup> is not able to complete <sup>[the tower]</sup>, everyone who sees it will ridicule him,
saying, ‘This man began to build <sup>[a tower]</sup> but was not able to finish it.’

Or what king, planning to engage another king in war, will not first sit down and evaluate whether he will be able, with ten thousand <sup>[soldiers]</sup>, to defeat someone who attacks him with twenty thousand <sup>[soldiers]</sup>?
Or maybe, while the enemy is still a long way off, he will send representatives to ask for peace terms.
So therefore, every one of you who does not say ‘goodbye’ to everything he owns cannot be my disciple.
Therefore salt is good, but if it loses its flavoring <sup>[ability]</sup>, how will it ever get it back again?

It is not suitable for the soil <sup>[i.e., for growing crops]</sup> or for fertilizer, but <sup>[only]</sup> for people to throw out. <sup>[See note on Matt. 5:13]</sup>. The person who has ears to hear with ought to listen <sup>[to this]</sup> carefully.”
Now the tax collectors and worldly people were all gathering around Jesus to listen to Him.
And the Pharisees and experts in the Law of Moses both complained, saying, “This man welcomes worldly people and <sup>[even]</sup> eats with them.”
So, Jesus told them this parable, saying,
“Which one of you who has a hundred sheep, and loses one of them, would not leave the ninety-nine in a deserted place and go looking for the lost one until he finds it?
And when he finds it, he places it on his shoulders <sup>[i.e., to return it]</sup>, and is so glad.
And when he gets home, he calls his friends and neighbors together and says to them, ‘Rejoice with me, for I found my sheep that was lost.’

I tell you that, in the same way, there will be more joy in heaven over one sinful person who repents <sup>[i.e., changes his heart and life]</sup>, than over ninety-nine persons who do not need to repent.

“Or what woman, who has ten silver coins <sup>[Note: These coins each amounted to about one twelve-hour day of a farm laborer’s pay, or about $108 in 2005]</sup>, and if she loses one of them, would not light a lamp, sweep the house and search for it diligently until she finds it?
And when she finds it, she calls her friends and neighbors together, saying, ‘Rejoice with me, for I found the coin I had lost.’
In the same way, there is joy in the presence of God’s angels <sup>[i.e., in heaven (?)]</sup> over one sinful person who repents.”
Then Jesus said, “A certain man had two sons.

The younger one said to his father, ‘Father, <sup>[please]</sup> give me my share of your property that I have coming.’ So, the father divided up what he had to live on <sup>[with his two sons]</sup>.
And a few days later, this younger son gathered everything together and traveled to a distant country where he squandered his possessions in wild living.
And when he had spent everything <sup>[he had]</sup>, a severe famine occurred in that country and he became desperate for food.
So, he went to work for one of the citizens of that country who sent him to his fields to feed his hogs.
Now he wanted to fill his stomach with the <sup>[carob]</sup> pods the hogs were eating, but no one gave him any <sup>[or perhaps, anything else]</sup>.
But when he came to his senses, he said, ‘How many of my father’s hired servants have more than enough bread to eat, and here I am, dying from hunger!
I will get up and go to my father and say to him, Father, I have sinned against <sup>[the God of]</sup> heaven and against you.
I do not deserve to be called your son <sup>[anymore]</sup>. Consider me as <sup>[just]</sup> one of your hired servants.’

So, he got up and went to his father. But while he was still some distance <sup>[from the house]</sup>, his father saw him and felt deep pity <sup>[for him]</sup>, so ran and embraced him and kissed him <sup>[enthusiastically]</sup>.
Then the son said to him, ‘Father, I have sinned against <sup>[the God of]</sup> heaven and against you. I do not deserve to be called your son.’
But the father said to his slaves, ‘Quickly, bring out the best robe and put it on him. And put a ring on his finger and sandals on his feet.
And bring the fattened calf, kill it and let us eat and enjoy ourselves.
For my son here was dead <sup>[spiritually]</sup>, but is <sup>[now]</sup> alive again. He was lost <sup>[from me]</sup>, but <sup>[now]</sup> is found.’ And they began to celebrate.
Now the father’s older son was <sup>[out]</sup> in the field. And when he came in and got close to the house he heard music and dancing.
So, he called one of the servants to him and asked what was going on.
And the servant said to him, ‘Your brother has come <sup>[home]</sup> and your father has killed the fattened calf, because he welcomed him back safe <sup>[from harm]</sup>.’
But the older son got angry and refused to go into <sup>[the house]</sup>. So, his father came out and pleaded with him <sup>[i.e., to come in and join the celebration]</sup>.

But he replied to his father, ‘Look, I have served you <sup>[as a slave]</sup> for all these years and have never disobeyed any of your orders, yet you never gave me <sup>[even]</sup> a young goat, so I could enjoy myself with my friends.
But when this son of yours came <sup>[home]</sup>, after having squandered all you gave him on prostitutes, you killed the fattened calf for him.’
And the father replied, ‘Son, you are always <sup>[here]</sup> with me, and everything I have is yours.

But it was <sup>[only]</sup> proper that we celebrate and have a good time, for this brother of yours was dead <sup>[spiritually]</sup>, but is <sup>[now]</sup> alive again; he was lost <sup>[from me]</sup> but <sup>[now]</sup> is found.’”
Then Jesus said to His disciples, “There was a certain rich man who had a property manager who was reported to be squandering his possessions.

So, he called his property manager in and said to him, ‘What is this that I hear about you? Turn in a <sup>[full]</sup> report of <sup>[all]</sup> your financial transactions, for you are not working for me anymore.’

Then the property manager said to himself, ‘What am I going to do since my master is taking away my job? I am not strong enough to dig <sup>[i.e., as a farm laborer]</sup>, and I am too ashamed to beg.
I know what I will do, so when I am fired they <sup>[i.e., his master’s customers]</sup> will welcome me into their homes.’
So, he called on each one of his master’s customers and said to the first one, ‘How much do you owe my master?’
And he replied, ‘Eight hundred gallons of <sup>[olive]</sup> oil.’ And he said to him, ‘Take your contract and quickly sit down and write in four hundred <sup>[gallons]</sup>.’

Then he said to another <sup>[customer]</sup>, ‘And how much do you owe <sup>[him]</sup>?’ And he answered, ‘One thousand bushels of wheat.’ The property manager said to him, ‘Take your contract and write eight hundred <sup>[bushels]</sup>.’

So, his master commended the dishonest property manager for his shrewdness. For worldly people are more shrewd in dealing with their own kind than are the people of light <sup>[i.e., God’s people]</sup>.

Therefore, I tell you, make friends for yourselves by using worldly wealth, so that when it fails <sup>[i.e., when you run out of money]</sup> they <sup>[i.e., the friends you made with its wise and benevolent use]</sup> may welcome you into the eternal <sup>[i.e., heavenly]</sup> dwelling places. <sup>[Note: The idea here seems to be that by your shrewd use of material possessions for the benefit of others, you are actually storing up treasures for yourself in heaven]</sup>.

“The person who is trustworthy in regard to very small matters, <sup>[such as material possessions]</sup> is also <sup>[likely to be]</sup> trustworthy in regard to large matters, <sup>[such as spiritual values]</sup>, and the person who does not do what is right in very small matters is also not <sup>[likely]</sup> to do what is right in large ones.
If therefore you have not been trustworthy in worldly wealth, who will put you in charge of the true <sup>[spiritual]</sup> riches?
And if you have not been trustworthy with what belongs to someone else, who will give you something of your own?

No person can serve two masters, for he will either hate the one and love the other, or else he will cling to the one <sup>[i.e., as a close friend]</sup> and look down on the other. You cannot serve God and material things <sup>[at the same time]</sup>.”
Then the Pharisees, who loved money, heard these things and sneered at Jesus.

And He said to them, “You are people who claim to be just in the eyes of <sup>[other]</sup> people, but God knows <sup>[what is in]</sup> your hearts. For that which is held up as most important among people <sup>[i.e., fame or fortune (?)]</sup> is disgusting in the eyes of God.

The Law of Moses and the prophets prophesied <sup>[See Matt. 11:13]</sup> until <sup>[the time of]</sup> John <sup>[the Immerser]</sup>. From that time onward the good news of the <sup>[coming]</sup> kingdom of God is <sup>[being]</sup> preached, and everyone is <sup>[attempting to]</sup> enter it forcibly <sup>[i.e., before its appointed time. See Acts 1:6-7]</sup>.
But it is easier for heaven and earth to pass away <sup>[i.e., to be destroyed. See II Pet. 3:3]</sup>, than for one portion of a letter of the Law of Moses to be dropped.

“Every person who divorces his wife and marries another woman becomes sexually unfaithful to her <sup>[i.e., because he is still regarded by God as married to his first wife. See Matt. 19:9]</sup>. And the person who marries someone who has been divorced by her husband commits sexual sin with her <sup>[i.e., because she is still rightfully married to her first husband]</sup>.
“Now there was a certain rich man who was dressed in purple <sup>[clothing]</sup> and soft linen <sup>[garments]</sup> and who lived every day in pleasure and luxury.
And a certain beggar named Lazarus, who was full of sores, was laid at the rich man’s doorway.
He longed to be fed with scraps that fell from the rich man’s table and even dogs came and licked his sores.
And it happened that the beggar died and <sup>[his spirit]</sup> was carried away by angels to a place close to Abraham. And the rich man also died and <sup>[his body]</sup> was buried.
And in the unseen place of departed spirits the rich man, who was being tormented, looked up and saw Abraham far away, with Lazarus close beside him.

And he shouted out, ‘Forefather Abraham, take pity on me and send Lazarus <sup>[to me]</sup>, so he can dip the tip of his finger in water <sup>[and touch]</sup> my tongue to cool it, because I am in agony in these flames.’

But Abraham said <sup>[to him]</sup>, ‘Son, remember how you enjoyed good things when you were alive <sup>[on earth]</sup> and how Lazarus had to put up with bad things? But now here <sup>[i.e., in the unseen place of departed spirits]</sup> he is being comforted and you are in agony.

And besides all this, there is a huge chasm situated between us and you so that those who wanted to go from here to there <sup>[where you are]</sup> cannot do it. And no one can cross over from there to us <sup>[over here]</sup>.’
Then the rich man said, ‘I beg you therefore, forefather <sup>[Abraham]</sup>, that you would send Lazarus to my father’s house,
where I have five brothers. Then he could warn them so that they will not <sup>[have to]</sup> come to this <sup>[terrible]</sup> place of torment also.’
But Abraham said, ‘They have <sup>[the writings of]</sup> Moses and the prophets; let them pay attention to them.’
Then the rich man said, ‘No, forefather Abraham, but if someone goes to them from the dead, <sup>[then]</sup> they will repent <sup>[i.e., change their hearts and lives]</sup>.’

And Abraham replied, ‘If they will not listen to Moses and the prophets, neither will they be persuaded <sup>[even]</sup> if someone rises from the dead <sup>[i.e., to come back and warn them]</sup>.’”

Then Jesus said to His disciples, “It is inevitable that occasions which cause people to be led astray <sup>[from God]</sup> will come, but it is too bad for that person who is responsible for it happening.

It would be good <sup>[i.e., better]</sup> for him to have had a huge millstone tied around his neck and be thrown into the ocean <sup>[Note: This “millstone” was a huge, circular stone rolled over grain to crush it and was moved by an animal walking in a circle]</sup>, rather than having him cause one of these little ones <sup>[i.e., humble followers of the Lord]</sup> to be led astray <sup>[from God]</sup>.

Pay attention to yourselves! “If your brother sins, rebuke him <sup>[i.e., show him where he is wrong in hope of effecting a change]</sup>; and if he repents <sup>[i.e., changes his heart and life]</sup>, forgive him.
And if he sins against you seven times a day and <sup>[then]</sup> comes to you seven times and says, ‘I repent,’ you should forgive him.”
Then the apostles said to the Lord, “Give us more faith.”
And the Lord said, “If you had faith as <sup>[small as]</sup> a mustard tree seed, you could say to this mulberry tree, ‘Be uprooted and replanted in the ocean,’ and it would obey you.
“But which of you, whose slave is plowing or tending sheep, will say to him when he comes in from the field, ‘Come and sit right down to eat’?

Would you not say to him instead, ‘Prepare the food for my meal <sup>[first]</sup> and get ready to serve me until I have had enough to eat and drink and <sup>[then]</sup> afterward you can eat and drink <sup>[yourself]</sup>’?
Would he thank his slave for doing the things that he was ordered to do?
So, you too, after you have done everything you were told to, should say, ‘We are unworthy slaves. We have done <sup>[only]</sup> what we were supposed to do.’”
And it happened as Jesus was on His way to Jerusalem that He passed along the border between Samaria and Galilee. <sup>[Note: These were the next two provinces north of Judea]</sup>.

And as He entered a certain village, He was met by ten men with infectious skin diseases, who were standing some distance away <sup>[Note: Restrictions in the Jewish law prevented contact with persons having infectious diseases. See Lev. 13:45ff]</sup>,
and shouting, “Jesus, Master, have pity on us.”
And when Jesus saw them, He said to them, “Go and show yourselves to the priests.” <sup>[See Lev. 13:49; 14:2ff]</sup>. And it happened, as they went <sup>[along]</sup>, that they were healed.
Then one of them, seeing he had been healed, returned and praised God in a loud voice.
And he fell on his face at Jesus’ feet and thanked Him. And he was a Samaritan. <sup>[Note: Samaritans were despised by the Jews and regarded as a ‘half-breeds’]</sup>.
Jesus asked <sup>[those standing nearby]</sup>, “Were there not ten men <sup>[who were]</sup> healed? Where are the <sup>[other]</sup> nine?
Did not anyone <sup>[else]</sup> come back to give praise to God, except this foreigner?”
Then Jesus said to the one <sup>[who had returned]</sup>, “Get up and go on your way. Your faith has made you well.”
And when He was asked by the Pharisees when the kingdom of God would come, Jesus answered them; “The kingdom of God will not come the way you are looking for it.

People will not be saying, ‘Look, here <sup>[it is]</sup>!’ or ‘There <sup>[it is]</sup>!’ For in fact, the kingdom of God is <sup>[now]</sup> among you <sup>[i.e., as represented by the presence and ministry of Jesus]</sup>.”

Then He said to the disciples, “The time is coming when you will long to see one of the days of the Son of man <sup>[i.e., the time when the Messiah would deliver His people from tribulation]</sup>, but you will not see it <sup>[i.e., such deliverance would not occur during their lifetimes]</sup>.
And people will be saying to you, ‘Look, there <sup>[He is]</sup>! Look, here <sup>[He is]</sup>! Do not leave or follow after them.
For the Son of man <sup>[will come]</sup> in His day just like lightning which flashes from one part of the sky and lights up the other part.
But first He must experience much suffering and be rejected by the people of this generation.
“And just as it happened in the days of Noah, so it will be in the days of the Son of man.

People were eating and drinking and <sup>[men were]</sup> marrying and <sup>[women]</sup> were being given away in marriage <sup>[right up]</sup> until the day that Noah entered the ship and the Flood came and drowned them all.
“It happened in a similar way in the days of Lot <sup>[i.e., Abraham’s nephew]</sup>. People were eating and drinking and buying and selling and planting and building.
But on the day that Lot left Sodom, fire and sulfur rained down from the sky and consumed them all.
“It will be the same way on the day the Son of man is revealed <sup>[i.e., at His second coming]</sup>.

On that day, the person who is on a housetop <sup>[Note: This was a flat area where people retired for rest, prayer, etc., with its stairway on the outside]</sup>, whose belongings are <sup>[still]</sup> in his house, is not to go down and <sup>[attempt to]</sup> take any of them away. And the person who is in a field <sup>[i.e., doing farm work]</sup>, also is not to return <sup>[to his house]</sup>.
Remember <sup>[what happened to]</sup> Lot’s wife!

Whatever person tries to gain <sup>[everything to satisfy him in]</sup> life <sup>[here on earth]</sup> will lose it <sup>[i.e., never ending life]</sup>. But whatever person <sup>[is willing to]</sup> lose <sup>[the comforts and security of]</sup> his life <sup>[here on earth]</sup> will preserve it <sup>[for eternity]</sup>.

I tell you, on that night <sup>[i.e., when Jesus returns. See verse 30]</sup>, there will be two men in one bed; one will be taken <sup>[i.e., to be with the Lord]</sup> while the other one will be left <sup>[i.e., to be punished by the Lord]</sup>.

There will be two women grinding grain together <sup>[Note: This was done with a hand-operated millstone where two persons sat across from each other and rotated a circular stone over kernels of grain]</sup>; one will be taken while the other one will be left.

Some ancient manuscripts include this verse “There will be two men in a field <sup>[i.e., doing farm work]</sup>; one will be taken <sup>[i.e., to be with the Lord]</sup>, while the other one will be left <sup>[i.e., to be punished by the Lord]</sup>.” <sup>[See Matt. 24:40]</sup>
And they asked Jesus, “Where [will this happen], Lord?” And He answered them, “Wherever there is a <sup>[dead]</sup> body <sup>[you will see]</sup> vultures gathered there also.”
Then Jesus told His disciples a parable for the purpose of showing them that they should pray continually and not become discouraged.
He said, “There was a certain judge in this town who did not revere God nor respect man.
There was <sup>[also]</sup> a widow in that <sup>[same]</sup> town who kept coming to the judge saying, ‘Grant me a <sup>[favorable]</sup> judgment against my opponent.’
And for some time the judge refused to do it. But finally he said to himself, ‘Even though I do not revere God or respect man,
I will grant her a <sup>[favorable]</sup> judgment because she keeps bothering me <sup>[about the matter]</sup> and is about to exhaust my patience by her persistent visits.’”
And the Lord said, “Listen to what <sup>[even]</sup> this unjust judge said <sup>[about the widow]</sup>.
So, will God not grant justice to His elect <sup>[i.e., His people]</sup> who call out to Him <sup>[i.e., in prayer]</sup> day and night? Will He be slow in helping them?
I tell you, He will grant justice to them quickly. However, will the Son of man find <sup>[very many]</sup> believing people on the earth when He returns?”
Then Jesus told this parable to certain ones who were relying on their own goodness and putting everyone else down:
“Two men went up to the Temple <sup>[in Jerusalem]</sup> to pray. One of them was a Pharisee and the other one was a tax collector.

The Pharisee stood up <sup>[conspicuously]</sup> and prayed about himself this way <sup>[i.e., bragging to God about how good he was]</sup>, ‘God, I thank you that I am not like other people: swindlers, unjust, sexually unfaithful to their mates, or even like that tax collector <sup>[over there]</sup>.
I fast <sup>[i.e., going without food and/or drink for religious reasons]</sup> twice a week and I give <sup>[to God’s work]</sup> a tenth of everything I get.’

But the tax collector stood at a distance <sup>[i.e., inconspicuously]</sup> and would not even look up to heaven, but struck his chest <sup>[i.e., in remorse]</sup>, saying, ‘God, <sup>[please]</sup> have mercy on a sinner like me <sup>[i.e., “cover over” or forgive my sins]</sup>.’

I tell you, this man went back home right with God instead of the other man. For every person who exalts himself <sup>[as important]</sup> will be humbled, but whoever humbles himself will be exalted <sup>[as important]</sup>.”
People were bringing babies to Jesus so He could touch them <sup>[i.e., to bestow a blessing on them]</sup>, but when His disciples saw this, they spoke harshly to these people.

But Jesus called His disciples to Him and said, “Allow these little children to come to me and stop trying to prevent them, for the kingdom of God belongs to such as these <sup>[i.e., humble ones. See Matt. 19:14]</sup>.
Truly I tell you, whoever does not welcome the kingdom of God the way a little child does, that person will by no means enter it.”
Then a certain ruler <sup>[Note: This man was young and rich. See Matt. 19:16-22]</sup> asked Jesus, “Good Teacher, what should I do in order to inherit never ending life?”
Jesus answered him, “Why do you call me ‘good’? No one is good except God only.
You know the commandments: Do not be sexually unfaithful to your mate. Do not murder. Do not steal. Do not give false testimony. Show honor to your father and mother.”
And the ruler replied, “I have <sup>[already]</sup> been observing all of these commandments since I was <sup>[very]</sup> young.”

When Jesus heard this He said to him, “<sup>[There is]</sup> one thing you still lack. Sell all your possessions and distribute <sup>[the money]</sup> to poor people; become my follower, <sup>[and then]</sup> you will have treasure in heaven.”
But when the ruler heard these words, he became very sad for he was extremely rich.
Now Jesus looked at him and said, “How difficult <sup>[it is]</sup> for those who are wealthy to enter the kingdom of God!
For it is <sup>[actually]</sup> easier for a camel to pass through the eye of a needle than for a wealthy person to enter the kingdom of God.”
And those who heard this said, “Then who can <sup>[possibly]</sup> be saved?”
Jesus answered, “The things that are impossible with people are <sup>[certainly]</sup> possible with God.”
Then Peter said, “Look, we have left our homes to follow you.”

And Jesus said to the disciples, “Truly I tell you, there is no person who has left his house, or wife, or brothers, or parents, or children for the sake of the kingdom of God <sup>[i.e., to become a servant of the Lord]</sup>,
who will not receive <sup>[back]</sup> many times as much <sup>[as he left in this life]</sup>, and never ending life in the coming age.”

Then Jesus took the twelve apostles with Him, saying to them, “Look, we are going up to Jerusalem and <sup>[while there]</sup> everything written in <sup>[the writings of]</sup> the prophets will be fulfilled concerning the Son of man.
For He will be turned over to the <sup>[unconverted]</sup> Gentiles, and will be mocked, and insulted and spit on.
<sup>[The Romans]</sup> will flog Him and kill Him, but on the third day He will rise again <sup>[from the dead]</sup>.”
But His disciples did not understand <sup>[what He meant by]</sup> any of these things, for this message was hidden from them so they did not comprehend what He was saying.

And it happened as Jesus was approaching Jericho <sup>[Note: This was a town about eighteen miles northeast of Jerusalem]</sup>, that a certain blind man was <sup>[seen]</sup> sitting along side of the road, begging.
As he heard a crowd of people passing by, he asked <sup>[someone]</sup> what was happening.
And he was told that Jesus from Nazareth was passing by <sup>[that way]</sup>.
So, he shouted out, “Jesus, you son of David, take pity on me.”
But those walking in front <sup>[of Jesus]</sup> spoke harshly to the blind beggar, telling him to be quiet. But he continued shouting all the more, “You son of David, take pity on me.”
So, Jesus stopped and ordered the beggar to be brought to Him. And when he came near, Jesus asked him,
“What do you want me to do for you?” And he replied, “Lord, I want to receive my sight.”
And Jesus said to him, “Receive your sight, your faith has made you well.”
And immediately his sight was restored and he followed Jesus, giving honor to God. And when all the people saw this, they <sup>[too]</sup> praised God.
So, Jesus <sup>[finally]</sup> entered Jericho and was traveling through it.
Just then, there appeared a man named Zacchaeus, who was a wealthy, chief tax collector.
He was trying to see which one was Jesus, but could not due to the large crowd and <sup>[because]</sup> he was so short.

So, he ran on ahead <sup>[of the crowd]</sup> and climbed up a mulberry tree <sup>[Note: This was a tree that produced a fig-like fruit]</sup> in order to see Jesus, who was supposed to pass that way <sup>[soon]</sup>.
And when Jesus came to the tree, He said to him, “Zacchaeus, hurry and come down <sup>[from that tree]</sup>, for I need to stay at your house today.”
So, he came down immediately and welcomed Jesus gladly.
And when the crowd saw this, they began complaining <sup>[i.e., because Zacchaeus was a despised tax collector]</sup> and said, “He is going to lodge with a sinful man.”

Then Zacchaeus stood there and said to the Lord, “Look, Lord, I <sup>[plan to]</sup> give half of my possessions to poor people. And if I have cheated anyone out of something, <sup>[I promise]</sup> to repay them four times as much.”
Then Jesus said to him, “Salvation has come to this house today, since he too <sup>[i.e., Zacchaeus]</sup> is a descendant of Abraham.
For the Son of man came to search for and save those who were lost.”
And as the crowd listened to these things, Jesus told them another parable, because He was close to Jerusalem and they assumed the kingdom of God was going to appear right away.

So, He said, “A certain distinguished gentleman went to a distant country to receive a royal appointment <sup>[i.e., as king over a territory]</sup> and then return. <sup>[Note: This is the only parable of Jesus’ that correlates with a known incident; that of Herod’s son Archelaus going to Rome to receive from Caesar Augustus an appointment to rule over Judea, Samaria and Idumaea in 4 B.C. See Matt. 2:22]</sup>.

So, he called ten of his slaves and gave each of them a sum of money <sup>[Note: The amount of each sum was equivalent to one hundred twelve-hour days of a farm laborer’s pay, or about $10,800 in 2005]</sup>. <sup>[Then]</sup> he said to them, ‘Invest this money until I return.’

But the citizens <sup>[of his newly acquired kingdom]</sup> hated him and sent a delegation after him <sup>[i.e., to the person who appointed the gentleman king over them]</sup>, saying, ‘We do not want this man to rule over us.’

And it happened when the gentleman returned, having received his appointment as king, that he ordered the slaves to whom he had given the <sup>[sums of]</sup> money, to report to him. He wanted to find out how much <sup>[money]</sup> they had made on their investments.
So, the first one appeared in front of him, and said, ‘Master, your sum of money has earned ten times more.’

And the gentleman replied, ‘Well done, you are a good slave. Because you have proven trustworthy over a <sup>[relatively]</sup> very small matter, you will <sup>[now]</sup> have charge over ten towns.’
Then the second slave appeared, and said, ‘Master, your sum of money has earned five times more.’
And the gentleman said to him also, ‘You <sup>[will]</sup> have charge over five towns.’
Then another slave appeared and said, ‘Look, master, here is your sum of money. I kept it <sup>[safely]</sup> wrapped up in a cloth,

for I was afraid of you, knowing you are a hard man to deal with and that you pick up <sup>[and keep]</sup> something you did not lay down <sup>[i.e., lose]</sup>, and you harvest a crop that you did not plant.’

The gentleman replied to him, ‘I will judge you <sup>[based]</sup> on what you <sup>[yourself]</sup> have <sup>[just]</sup> said, you evil slave. <sup>[Since]</sup> you knew that I am a hard man to deal with, picking up <sup>[and keeping]</sup> something I had not laid down, and harvesting a crop I had not planted,
then why did you not deposit my money in the bank? <sup>[Then]</sup> when I returned I could have withdrawn <sup>[the original sum of money]</sup> plus interest.’
Then he said to those who were standing around, ‘Take the sum of money away from him and give it to the one who made ten times as much <sup>[with the original sum]</sup>.’
But the people said to him, ‘Master, he <sup>[already]</sup> has ten times as much money <sup>[as you gave him]</sup>.’

<sup>[So the gentleman concluded]</sup>, ‘I tell you, to every person who <sup>[already]</sup> has something, <sup>[more]</sup> will be given, but from that person who has <sup>[almost]</sup> nothing, even what <sup>[little]</sup> he has will be taken away from him.
But bring those enemies of mine here <sup>[i.e., the citizens. See verse 14]</sup>, who did not want me to rule over them, and kill them in front of me.’'
And when Jesus had said this, He went on ahead <sup>[of His disciples. See Matt. 21:1]</sup> up to Jerusalem.

And it happened when Jesus got close to Bethphage and Bethany <sup>[Note: These were two small towns about two miles east of Jerusalem]</sup>, at a hill called Olivet, that He sent two of His disciples,

saying <sup>[to them]</sup>, “Go into the village just ahead of you, and upon entering <sup>[it]</sup>, you will find tied up <sup>[there]</sup> a colt that no one has ever ridden. Untie it and bring it <sup>[here]</sup>.
And if anyone asks you, ‘Why are you untying it?’ you should say, ‘The Lord needs it.’'
So, those who were sent <sup>[i.e., Jesus’ disciples]</sup>, went away and found <sup>[things]</sup> just as Jesus had told them.
And as they were untying the colt, its owners said to them, “Why are you untying the colt?”
And they replied, “The Lord needs it.”

So, they brought the colt to Jesus and spread their clothing on it, <sup>[then]</sup> placed Jesus on it <sup>[i.e., held the animal still as Jesus mounted it, since it had never been ridden before. See verse 30]</sup>.
And as He rode <sup>[along]</sup>, people spread their clothing on the roadway.

Then, as He descended the Mount of Olives and got close to the city <sup>[i.e., Jerusalem]</sup>, the entire crowd of disciples began joyfully praising God in loud voices because of all the powerful miracles they had seen <sup>[Him perform]</sup>.

They were saying, “May the King who comes in the name <sup>[i.e., by the authority]</sup> of the Lord, be blessed. May there be peace in heaven and praise <sup>[to God]</sup> in the highest <sup>[i.e., may this praise reach to the highest heaven]</sup>.”
But some of the Pharisees from the crowd said to Jesus, “Teacher, speak sternly to your disciples <sup>[i.e., to prevent them from honoring Jesus]</sup>.”
But He answered them, “I tell you, if these people remain quiet <sup>[about me]</sup>, <sup>[then]</sup> the stones will shout <sup>[about it]</sup>.”
And when Jesus got close and saw the city <sup>[of Jerusalem]</sup>, He cried over it,
saying, “If <sup>[only]</sup> you people realized today those things which <sup>[could]</sup> lead <sup>[you]</sup> to peace! But now they are <sup>[being]</sup> hidden from your sight.
For the days will come upon you when your enemies will build a dirt wall around you, surrounding you, and hemming you in on every side.

They will throw you and your children, <sup>[who are]</sup> within your walls, to the ground and they will not allow one stone to remain on top of another in your city because you did not recognize that <sup>[God was]</sup> visiting you.” <sup>[Note: This “visitation” refers either to the redemption which they had rejected or to the punishment of the siege and destruction of Jerusalem in A.D. 70]</sup>.
Then Jesus entered the Temple and began to drive out those who sold <sup>[things there]</sup>,
saying to them, “It is written <sup>[Isa. 56:7]</sup>, ‘And my house <sup>[i.e., the Temple]</sup> will be a house for prayer, but <sup>[Jer. 7:11]</sup> ‘You have made it a hideout for thieves.’”
And Jesus was teaching in the Temple every day. But the leading priests and the experts in the Law of Moses and the leading men of the people were looking <sup>[for a way]</sup> to kill Him,
but they could not find a way to do it, because all the people were listening to Him attentively.

And it happened on one of the days that Jesus was teaching people in the Temple and preaching the good news, that the leading priests and the experts in the Law of Moses, <sup>[along]</sup> with the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders, came to Him.
They said to Him, “Tell us, by what authority are you doing these things <sup>[i.e., miracles and teaching]</sup>? Or, who is the one who gave you this authority?”
And Jesus answered them; “Let me also ask you a question. Tell me,
was the immersion of John <sup>[authorized]</sup> from heaven or from men?”
And they discussed this among themselves, saying, “If we say ‘from heaven,’ He will say ‘Why <sup>[then]</sup> did you not believe him <sup>[i.e., believe his message]</sup>?’
But if we say ‘from men,’ all the people will stone us, for they are convinced that John was a prophet.”
Then they answered Jesus that they did not know <sup>[who authorized John’s immersion]</sup>.
So, Jesus said to them, “<sup>[Then]</sup> neither will I tell you by what authority I am doing these things.”

Then Jesus began speaking to the people. <sup>[Note: And perhaps to the religious leaders as well. See Mark 11:18 with 12:1]</sup>. He told them this parable: “A man planted a vineyard, then leased it out to tenant farmers and went to another country for a long time.

And when the <sup>[grape harvest]</sup> season came, the owner of the farm sent a slave to the tenant farmers, asking them for the grape harvest <sup>[to be delivered to him]</sup>. But the tenant farmers beat the slave and sent him away empty handed.
Then he sent out another slave <sup>[i.e., to arrange for receiving the crop]</sup>, but the tenant farmers beat him also, shamefully abused him and sent him away empty handed <sup>[as well]</sup>.
So, he sent a third <sup>[slave]</sup> and they wounded him also and threw him out <sup>[of the vineyard]</sup>.
Then the owner of the vineyard said, ‘What shall I do <sup>[about this]</sup>? I will send my dearly loved son. Hopefully, they will treat him with respect.’
But when the tenant farmers saw him, they began reasoning with one another, saying, ‘This is the heir <sup>[to the vineyard]</sup>; let us kill him so the inheritance will be ours.’
So, they threw him out of the vineyard and killed him. Therefore, what will the owner of the vineyard do to them? <sup>[Jesus asked]</sup>.
He will come and kill those tenant farmers and will give the vineyard to other people.” And when the people heard <sup>[Him say]</sup> this, they said, “May that never happen.”

But Jesus looked at them and said, “Then what does it mean that is written <sup>[Psa. 118:22]</sup>, ‘The building block rejected by the builders <sup>[is]</sup> the same <sup>[one]</sup> that was made the principal stone by which the entire building was aligned?’
Every person who falls on that stone will be broken to pieces, but whoever it falls on will be scattered like dust.”

Then the experts in the Law of Moses and the leading priests attempted to arrest Jesus right away, <sup>[but hesitated]</sup> because they feared <sup>[what]</sup> the people <sup>[might do]</sup>, since they perceived that He had spoken this parable against them.

And they kept watching Him, <sup>[even]</sup> sending informants who pretended to be good <sup>[men]</sup>, in order to get hold of something He said so they could turn Him over to the ruling authority of the governor.
And they asked Him, “Teacher, we know that you speak and teach correctly and that you do not show partiality to anyone, but <sup>[rather]</sup> teach the truth about God’s way.
Is it lawful to pay taxes to Caesar, or not?”
But Jesus perceived their trickery, and said to them,

“Show me the coin <sup>[used for paying the taxes]</sup>. <sup>[Note: This coin was equivalent to one twelve-hour day of a farm laborer’s pay, or about $108 in 2005]</sup>. Whose image and inscription are on this coin?” And they answered Him, “Caesar’s.”
He replied, “Then pay to Caesar the things that belong to Caesar and pay to God the things that belong to God.”
And they were not able to get hold of anything Jesus said in front of the people. And they marveled at His answer and did not say anything <sup>[further]</sup>.
<sup>[Then]</sup> certain Sadducees came to Jesus (they are the ones who say there is no resurrection <sup>[of the dead]</sup>).

They said to Him, “Teacher, Moses wrote to us <sup>[Deut 25:5]</sup> that if a man’s brother dies, leaving his widow <sup>[behind]</sup> without having had any children, his brother should marry his widow and father children <sup>[by her]</sup>. These children would then be considered his <sup>[dead]</sup> brother’s.
<sup>[Now]</sup> there were these seven brothers; the first one got married, but when he died he did not leave any children behind.
Then the second one did the same thing.

Then the third one married the <sup>[surviving]</sup> widow <sup>[of the first two brothers]</sup>. And in the same way, all seven <sup>[brothers]</sup> married her, but left no children <sup>[behind when they died]</sup>.
Finally, the woman also passed away.
<sup>[Now]</sup> whose wife will she be in the resurrected state, for all seven <sup>[brothers]</sup> were married to her?”
And Jesus said to them, “The people of this age <sup>[i.e., who live on earth]</sup> marry and are given away in marriage,
but those who are considered worthy of gaining that age <sup>[i.e., the future, never ending state]</sup> and the resurrection from the dead, neither marry nor are given away in marriage.

For they cannot die anymore either, because they are equal to the angels and are children of God, as well as children of the resurrection <sup>[i.e., people who are assured of being resurrected]</sup>.
But even Moses showed that dead people would be raised in the incident about the bush <sup>[Ex. 3:6]</sup>, where he called the Lord, ‘Abraham’s God, and Isaac’s God, and Jacob’s God.’
So, He is not God to those who are dead, but <sup>[He is]</sup> God to those who are alive, for to God, all people are alive.”
Then certain experts in the Law of Moses replied to Jesus, “Teacher, you have spoken well.”
For they did not dare ask Him any more questions.

And Jesus said to them <sup>[i.e., the Pharisees. See Matt. 22:41]</sup>, “How is it that they <sup>[i.e., the experts in the Law of Moses. See Mark 12:35]</sup> can say that the Christ <sup>[i.e., God’s specially chosen one]</sup> is the son of David?
For David himself said in the book of Psalms <sup>[Psa. 110:1]</sup>, ‘The Lord <sup>[i.e., God]</sup> said to my <sup>[i.e., David’s]</sup> Lord <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup>, sit at my right side
until I make your enemies the footrest <sup>[i.e., in subjection]</sup> under your feet.’
Since David called Him <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup> Lord, how could He be his son?”
Then Jesus said to His disciples in the hearing of all the people,

“Look out for the experts in the Law of Moses, who like to walk <sup>[around]</sup> in long <sup>[flowing]</sup> robes and love the special greetings they receive at the open shopping markets, and the principal seats in the synagogues, and the head places at dinner tables.
They consume widows’ houses <sup>[i.e., by foreclosing on them]</sup> and <sup>[then]</sup> cover it up by offering lengthy prayers. These people will receive a heavier judgment.”

Then Jesus looked up <sup>[i.e., from his seated position at the entrance to the Temple]</sup>, and saw people throwing their gifts <sup>[of money]</sup> into the treasury <sup>[Note: This was a large open­mouthed container used for receiving offerings for meeting expenses of Temple upkeep]</sup>.
And He saw a certain poor widow throwing two small copper coins into it <sup>[Note: These two coins amounted to about ten minutes of a farm laborer’s pay, or about $1.50 in 2005]</sup>.
So, He said, “Truly I tell you, this poor widow has thrown in more than all the rest <sup>[i.e., referring to the many rich people who were also throwing in money]</sup>.
For all of them threw in from their abundance, but she threw in from her poverty all she had to live on.”
Then, as someone <sup>[i.e., of His disciples. See Mark 13:1]</sup>, spoke of how the Temple was decorated with beautiful stones and objects dedicated as gifts, Jesus said,
“As for these things you are looking at, the time will come when there will not be one stone left on another here that will not be thrown down.”
So, they asked Him, saying, “Teacher, when will these things <sup>[you just spoke of]</sup> happen? And what will be the sign <sup>[that indicates]</sup> when these things are about to be fulfilled?”

And He replied, “Pay attention, so that you are not led astray <sup>[from the truth]</sup>. For many <sup>[false teachers]</sup> will come, claiming to be me, and saying, ‘I am <sup>[the Christ]</sup>,’ and ‘The time is near.’ <sup>[But]</sup> do not go after them.

And when you hear of wars and uprisings <sup>[going on]</sup>, do not be afraid, for such things must necessarily happen first. But the end will not come right away.” <sup>[Note: By “the end” here Jesus probably alludes to the downfall of Jerusalem, in AD 70, with its attending destruction of the Temple buildings]</sup>.
Then Jesus said to His disciples, “Nations will wage war against one another and kingdoms will attack one another.
And great earthquakes will happen, and at various places there will be famines and disease epidemics. And there will be fearful events and great <sup>[supernatural]</sup> signs from heaven.

But before all these things happen, the authorities will grab hold of you, and persecute you, and turn you over to the synagogues and prisons <sup>[i.e., for punishment and confinement]</sup>. They will take you in front of kings and governors for being loyal to me.
It will result in <sup>[an opportunity for]</sup> witnessing to them.
Determine in your hearts, therefore, not to plan ahead how you will answer them <sup>[when interrogated]</sup>,

for I will give you a mouth <sup>[i.e., the ability to speak properly]</sup> and wisdom <sup>[i.e., the knowledge of what to say]</sup>, which none of your opponents will be able to resist or speak against.

But you will be turned over <sup>[i.e., to the authorities for punishment]</sup> even by <sup>[your own]</sup> parents and brothers and relatives and friends. And they will <sup>[even]</sup> put some of you to death.
And you will be hated by all people for being loyal to me.
However, not one hair of your head will be lost.
<sup>[But]</sup>, by holding out steadfastly, you will gain your life. <sup>[Note: This means either “preserve your earthly life,” or “attain never ending life”]</sup>.
“So, when you see Jerusalem surrounded by <sup>[Roman]</sup> armies, then you will know that her total destruction is near.

Then those <sup>[of you]</sup> in Judea are to run away into the <sup>[nearby]</sup> mountains. And those who are inside of the city are to leave it <sup>[immediately]</sup>. And those who are in the countryside should not enter the city.

For these are times for punishment <sup>[i.e., to be inflicted on Jerusalem]</sup>, so that everything written <sup>[i.e., by the prophets]</sup> may be fulfilled <sup>[i.e., about Jerusalem’s destruction. See Dan. 9:26-27]</sup>.

It will be too bad for pregnant women and those who are nursing <sup>[babies]</sup> in those days! For there will be great distress in this land <sup>[i.e., Judea, and especially Jerusalem]</sup>, and raging anger <sup>[will be shown]</sup> against these people <sup>[i.e., by the Roman armies]</sup>.

And people will die by the sword and be led captive into all nations. And Jerusalem will be trampled on by the <sup>[unconverted]</sup> Gentiles <sup>[i.e., by the Romans and later by others]</sup> until the times of <sup>[domination by]</sup> the Gentiles are fulfilled.

“Then <sup>[Note: This event answers to the “immediately” of Matt. 24:29, and suggests the next major event on God’s calendar]</sup> there will be <sup>[supernatural]</sup> signs <sup>[evident]</sup> in the sun and moon and stars. And on earth <sup>[there will be]</sup> distress among nations; they will be perplexed by the roaring of the ocean’s churning waves. <sup>[Note: Probably this is figurative language for highly agitated conditions on earth]</sup>.
Men will become despondent from fear, and from dreading the <sup>[terrible]</sup> things which will come upon the earth, for the forces of the heavens will be shaken.

And then they will see the Son of man coming in a cloud with power and great splendor <sup>[Note: This no doubt refers to the second coming of Christ. See II Pet. 3:9-10; Matt. 24:30-31; Rev. 1:7]</sup>.

But when these things begin to happen, stand up and lift up your heads <sup>[i.e., in anticipation]</sup>, for your redemption <sup>[i.e., deliverance, either from destruction or condemnation]</sup> is getting closer.”
Then Jesus told them a parable: “Look at the fig tree, as well as all <sup>[other]</sup> trees:
When they begin to sprout their leaves you know when you look at them, without being told, that summer is near.

In the same way also, when you see these things happening <sup>[i.e., the above mentioned “signs”]</sup>, you can know that the kingdom of God <sup>[i.e., either its heavenly phase or the destruction of Jerusalem]</sup> is near.
Truly I tell you, the people of this generation will not <sup>[all]</sup> die off before all these things <sup>[i.e., mentioned above]</sup> happen.
Although the sky and the earth will pass away <sup>[See II Pet. 3:10]</sup>, my words will not pass away <sup>[i.e., they will certainly come true]</sup>.

“But pay attention to yourselves so that your hearts do not become overburdened by carousing, and drunkenness, and the worries of life. <sup>[If you do]</sup>, that ‘day’ <sup>[i.e., Jesus’ second coming and judgment]</sup> will arrive suddenly, like a trap <sup>[i.e., which catches an animal by surprise]</sup>.
For that is the way this ‘day’ will arrive for everyone <sup>[else]</sup> living on the entire earth.

But you should be alert at all times, offering earnest prayers, so that you will be strong enough to escape all these things that will happen, and to appear <sup>[acceptable]</sup> in front of the Son of man <sup>[on that day]</sup>.”

Now Jesus was teaching in the Temple every day, and He went out and lodged on the hill called Olivet every night <sup>[Note: Possibly this meant He lodged in a house in Bethany, which was situated on the side of this hill]</sup>.
And all the people came to hear Him early <sup>[each]</sup> morning.

Now the Festival of Unleavened Bread, which is called the Passover, was approaching. <sup>[Note: This was the annual Jewish festival commemorating Israel’s deliverance from Egyptian bondage under Moses’ leadership]</sup>.

And the leading priests and experts in the Law of Moses were looking for a way to kill Jesus because they were afraid of the people <sup>[i.e., that people would riot if it were done during the Festival. See Matt. 26:5]</sup>.
Then Satan entered the heart of Judas, called Iscariot, who was one of the twelve apostles.

So, he left <sup>[probably the house of Simon, the man healed of an infectious skindisease. See Matt. 26:6-13]</sup> and went and discussed with the leading priests and captains <sup>[i.e., of the Temple guard]</sup> how he could turn Jesus over to them.
And they were very pleased, so agreed to give him money.
So Judas consented <sup>[to their offer]</sup> and looked for a <sup>[good]</sup> opportunity to turn Jesus over to them <sup>[i.e., the Jewish leaders]</sup> when the crowd was not present.
Then the day came for the Festival of Unleavened Bread <sup>[to be held]</sup>, on which the Passover <sup>[lamb]</sup> was to be sacrificed.
So, Jesus sent Peter and John <sup>[to Jerusalem]</sup>, saying, “Go and prepare for us to eat the Passover <sup>[meal]</sup>.”
And they said to Him, “Where do you want us to prepare <sup>[for it]</sup>?”
And He replied to them, “Look, when you have entered the city, a man carrying a pitcher of water will meet you; follow him into whatever house he goes.
Then you should say to the owner of the house, ‘The Teacher says to you, where is the guest room where I can eat the Passover meal with my disciples?’
And he will show you a large, completely furnished upstairs room. Make preparations <sup>[for us]</sup> there <sup>[i.e., to observe the Passover Festival]</sup>.”
So, they went and found <sup>[everything]</sup> just as Jesus told them <sup>[it would be]</sup>, and they prepared for the Passover meal.
And when the time came <sup>[for the Passover meal]</sup>, Jesus reclined with His apostles <sup>[at the table]</sup>. <sup>[Note: See Matt. 23:6]</sup>.
And He said to them, “I have earnestly desired to eat this Passover meal with you <sup>[men]</sup> before I suffer <sup>[i.e., torture and death]</sup>.
For I tell you, I will not eat it again until <sup>[I do so when]</sup> it is fulfilled in the kingdom of God <sup>[See Matt. 26:29]</sup>.”

Then Jesus took a cup <sup>[Note: This was one of the cups partaken of during the Passover meal]</sup>, and after giving thanks to God <sup>[for it]</sup>, He said, “Take this and share it among yourselves,
for I tell you, I will not drink this fruit of the vine <sup>[i.e., grape juice]</sup> from now on until <sup>[I do, figuratively, when]</sup> the kingdom of God has come.”

Then He took a <sup>[small]</sup> loaf of bread, and after He had given thanks to God, He broke it and gave <sup>[pieces]</sup> to His apostles, and said, “This is <sup>[i.e., represents]</sup> my <sup>[physical]</sup> body which is <sup>[to be]</sup> given for you; continue to do this <sup>[i.e., eat it regularly]</sup> to remember me by.”

And in the same way He took <sup>[another]</sup> cup, after the <sup>[Passover]</sup> meal, and said, “This cup is <sup>[i.e., represents]</sup> the New Agreement <sup>[i.e., between God and mankind]</sup> made by my blood, which is <sup>[to be]</sup> poured out for you.
But look, the hand of the one who is turning me over <sup>[i.e., to the Jewish leaders]</sup> is with me on the table <sup>[i.e., dipping his hand in the sauce bowl. See Matt. 26:23]</sup>.
For the Son of man is certainly going <sup>[to die]</sup>, just as it has been <sup>[pre-]</sup> determined, but it is too bad for that person through whom He is <sup>[to be]</sup> betrayed!”
So, the apostles began discussing among themselves which one of them would do such a thing.
Then a dispute arose among the apostles as to which one of them should be considered the greatest.

And Jesus said to them, “The kings of the <sup>[unconverted]</sup> Gentiles lord it over their own people, and those who domineer over them are called ‘Benefactors’ <sup>[Note: This was a title used by Greek kings in Egypt and Syria]</sup>.

But it will not be that way among you, for the person who is the most important among you should become <sup>[like]</sup> the youngest <sup>[i.e., least important]</sup>. And the person who is the leader should become like the servant.

For who is most important, the person who reclines at the dinner table or the person who serves <sup>[him]</sup>? Is it not the person who reclines at the dinner table? But I am among you as the person who serves.
Now you apostles are the ones who have remained with me throughout my trials,
and just as my Father assigned a kingdom to me, so I am assigning a kingdom to you,

so you can eat and drink at my table in my <sup>[coming]</sup> kingdom. <sup>[Also]</sup> you will sit on <sup>[twelve]</sup> thrones <sup>[See Matt. 19:28]</sup>, judging the twelve tribes of Israel <sup>[i.e., judging God’s people through their writings]</sup>.

“Simon, Simon <sup>[i.e., Peter]</sup>, Satan has earnestly requested the right to sift you like wheat. <sup>[Note: Grain was sifted by using a sieve-like device in which grain and particles of dirt and straw were shaken together, allowing the grain to fall through while the dirt and straw remained. This was an allusion to the agitating trials Peter was soon to endure, and survive]</sup>.

But I prayed earnestly for you, that your faith would not fail <sup>[to be strong]</sup>. And when you have turned back <sup>[i.e., from going astray briefly]</sup>, provide <sup>[spiritual]</sup> support to your brothers.”
And Peter replied, “Lord, I am ready to go to prison and to death with you.”
And Jesus said, “I tell you, Peter, you will deny <sup>[even]</sup> knowing me three times before the rooster crows today.”

Then Jesus said to them, “When I sent you out without a money belt, or traveling bag <sup>[i.e., for personal belongings]</sup>, or sandals, did you lack anything?” And the apostles answered, “<sup>[No]</sup>, nothing.”

Then He said to them, “But this time the person who has a money belt should take it, and also <sup>[he should take]</sup> a traveling bag. And if he does not have a sword, he should sell his coat and buy one.

For I tell you that this <sup>[passage of Scripture]</sup>, which was written about me, must be fulfilled <sup>[Isa. 53:12]</sup>: ‘And He <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup> was counted with <sup>[i.e., as though He were one of]</sup> the criminals.’ For the part <sup>[of that passage]</sup> that refers to me is being fulfilled.”
And the apostles said, “Look, Lord, we have two swords <sup>[here]</sup>.” And Jesus replied, “That is enough.” <sup>[Note: Possibly Jesus means, “enough talk about weapons”]</sup>.
Then Jesus went out to the Mount of Olives, as He was accustomed to doing <sup>[each evening during His final week]</sup>, and His disciples followed Him.
And when He got there He said to them, “Pray that you do not give in <sup>[i.e., to the devil]</sup> under trials.”
Then Jesus went away from them about the distance of a stone’s throw and knelt down and prayed,
saying, “Father, if you are willing, take this cup <sup>[i.e., of suffering]</sup> away from me; nevertheless, let it not be what I want, but what you want <sup>[for me]</sup>.”
Now an angel from heaven appeared to Him and strengthened Him. <sup>[See Matt. 4:11]</sup>.
And He became anguished as He prayed more fervently; even His sweat was like drops of blood falling to the ground.
And when He got up from praying He went to His disciples and found them asleep, exhausted from grief.
So, He said to them, “Why were you sleeping? Get up and pray so you do not give in <sup>[to the devil]</sup> under trials.”
While He was still speaking suddenly one of the twelve apostles, named Judas, <sup>[who was]</sup> leading a crowd, came near Jesus to kiss Him.
But Jesus said to him, “Judas, are you betraying the Son of man with a kiss?”
And when <sup>[the rest of]</sup> the disciples, who were gathered around Jesus, saw what was about to happen, they said, “Lord, do you want us to strike them with a sword?”
Then a certain one of them struck the head priest’s slave and sheared off his right ear. <sup>[Note: It was Peter who struck this man, whose name was Malchus. See John 18:10]</sup>.
But Jesus replied, “Stop it; <sup>[that is]</sup> enough!” Then He touched the man’s <sup>[partially severed]</sup> ear and healed him.

And Jesus said to the leading priests and captains of the Temple <sup>[guard]</sup> and the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders that came to take Him, “Have you come out <sup>[to arrest me]</sup> with swords and clubs as you would <sup>[apprehend]</sup> a thief?

When I was with you every day in the Temple you did not raise a hand against me. But this is your hour <sup>[i.e., the time for you to act against me]</sup> and the power of darkness <sup>[has now taken over]</sup>.”

Then they arrested Jesus and led Him away to the head priest’s house <sup>[i.e., Caiaphas. See Matt. 26:57]</sup>. But Peter followed Him from a distance <sup>[i.e., as they went to the head priest’s courtyard]</sup>.

And when they <sup>[i.e., the priest’s servants]</sup> had built a fire in the middle of the courtyard, they sat down together <sup>[i.e., to warm themselves. See Mark 14:67]</sup>, with Peter <sup>[sitting]</sup> among the group.
Then a certain <sup>[servant]</sup> girl saw Peter sitting there in the light of the fire. As she stared at him she said, “This man was with Jesus, too.”
But Peter denied it, saying, “Woman, I do not <sup>[even]</sup> know Him.”
Then a little while later, someone else saw Peter and said, “You are one of them also <sup>[i.e., one of Jesus’ disciples]</sup>.” But Peter replied, “Man, I am not.”

And about an hour later someone else stated emphatically, “I know for sure that this man was also with him <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup>, for he is from Galilee <sup>[too]</sup>.” <sup>[Note: Peter’s accent gave him away. See Matt. 26:73]</sup>.
But then Peter said, “Man, I do not know what you are talking about.” Just then, as he was speaking, the rooster crowed.

Then the Lord turned and gave Peter a <sup>[penetrating]</sup> glance. And Peter remembered the words of the Lord, how He had said to him, “You will deny <sup>[knowing]</sup> me three times before the rooster crows today.”
Then Peter went out and cried bitterly.
Now the men who were holding Jesus <sup>[in custody]</sup> began mocking Him and beating Him.
And they blindfolded Him and asked Him, “Prophesy, who is it that hit you?”
And they said many other derogatory things against Him.

And when it became daylight, the body of <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders of the people, <sup>[consisting of]</sup> both leading priests and experts in the Law of Moses, was gathered together. Then they led Jesus away to their Council <sup>[called the “Sanhedrin”]</sup>, and asked Him,
“Tell us if you are the Christ <sup>[i.e., God’s specially chosen one]</sup>.” But He replied, “You would not believe me if I <sup>[did]</sup> tell you <sup>[who I am]</sup>.
And if I asked you <sup>[who I am]</sup> you would not answer.
But from now on the Son of man will be seated at the right side of the powerful God.”
So, they all asked <sup>[Him]</sup>, “Then are you the Son of God?” And He answered them, “You <sup>[are right to]</sup> say that I am <sup>[the Christ]</sup>.”
And they replied, “Why do we need any more evidence? For we have heard <sup>[enough]</sup> from his own mouth, ourselves.”
Then the entire group of them <sup>[i.e., of the Jewish elders, priests and law experts]</sup> got up <sup>[from their Council meeting]</sup> and took Jesus to <sup>[governor]</sup> Pilate.

They began bringing accusations against Him, saying, “We discovered this man misleading the people of our nation and telling them not to pay taxes to Caesar, and claiming that he himself was the Messiah, a king!”
So, Pilate asked Him, “Are you the king of the Jews?” And Jesus answered him, “You have said so.”
Then Pilate said to the leading priests and to the crowds <sup>[that had gathered]</sup>, “I do not find anything wrong with this man.”

But the group became more insistent, saying, “He is stirring up the people and teaching <sup>[these things]</sup> throughout all Judea. He began <sup>[doing this]</sup> in Galilee and <sup>[has continued it]</sup> even to this place <sup>[i.e., Jerusalem]</sup>.”
When Pilate heard this, he asked if the man were a Galilean.
And when he realized that Jesus was under Herod’s <sup>[political]</sup> jurisdiction, he sent Him to Herod, who was also in Jerusalem at that time.
Now Herod was very pleased when he saw Jesus for he had wanted to see Him for a long time. He had heard <sup>[things]</sup> about Jesus and <sup>[now]</sup> hoped to see Him perform a miracle.
So, he asked Him many questions, but Jesus did not respond to any <sup>[of them]</sup>.
Then the leading priests and experts in the Law of Moses stood up and viciously charged Him <sup>[i.e., with false accusations]</sup>.
Then Herod and his soldiers treated Jesus with contempt and mocked Him. They dressed Him in a luxurious robe and then sent Him back to Pilate.
So, Herod and Pilate became mutual friends that very day. (Now before this they had been enemies).
Then Pilate called together the leading priests, the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> leaders and the people,

and said to them, “You brought this man to me, claiming he was inciting people to disloyalty <sup>[i.e., to the government]</sup>. And look, I questioned him in front of you people but did not find him guilty of the charges you
or Herod have brought <sup>[against him]</sup>. For Herod sent him back to us and now I find that he has not done anything deserving of death.
Therefore, I will have him punished and <sup>[then]</sup> release him.”
Some ancient manuscripts include this verse “For Pilate was required to release one prisoner to the people at the <sup>[Passover]</sup> Festival”.
But the people all joined together shouting, “Take this man <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup> and release Barabbas to us <sup>[instead]</sup>.”
<sup>[Now]</sup> Barabbas was a person who had been thrown into prison for <sup>[inciting]</sup> a rebellion in the city <sup>[of Jerusalem]</sup> and for committing murder.
Then Pilate spoke to them again <sup>[because]</sup> he wanted to release Jesus,
but they continued shouting, “Crucify him, crucify him.”

So, Pilate called out to them a third time, “Why <sup>[should he be crucified]</sup>; what wrong has this man done? I have not found any reason for putting him to death, so I will punish him and <sup>[then]</sup> release him.”
But the people loudly demanded that Jesus be crucified. And their shouting won out.
So, Pilate issued the sentence the people were demanding

and released the man they had asked for <sup>[i.e., Barabbas]</sup>, who had been thrown into prison for <sup>[inciting]</sup> a rebellion and committing murder. But he turned Jesus over to the people do with Him whatever they wanted.

Then, as they were leading Jesus away, the soldiers <sup>[See Mark 15:16-21]</sup> grabbed hold of a man named Simon, from Cyrene <sup>[Note: This was a city in the northern African country of Libya]</sup>, who was coming in from the countryside, and placed the cross <sup>[i.e., probably only the cross-beam portion]</sup> on him, so he could carry it while following Jesus.
A large crowd of people followed Jesus, including some women who wailed <sup>[in grief]</sup> and mourned over His plight.
But Jesus turned and said to them, “Daughters of Jerusalem <sup>[i.e., women citizens]</sup>, do not cry over me, but cry over yourselves and your children.
For look, the time will come when people will be saying, ‘Those <sup>[women]</sup> who are without children and who have never given birth or nursed babies are <sup>[truly]</sup> happy!’
Then they will begin to say to the mountains <sup>[Hosea 10:8]</sup>, ‘Fall on us,’ and to the hills, ‘Cover us <sup>[up]</sup>.’

For if people are doing these things <sup>[to me]</sup> while the <sup>[leaves of the]</sup> tree are green <sup>[i.e., to someone innocent of wrongdoing]</sup>, what will they do when <sup>[the leaves]</sup> are dried up <sup>[i.e., to the sinful Jews of Jerusalem]</sup>?”
And two other men, who were criminals, were also led out with Jesus to be put to death.

And when they came to the place called “The Skull” <sup>[Note: The Latin word for this place is “Calvary”]</sup>, they crucified Him there, along with the criminals, one at His right side and one at His left.

Then Jesus called out, “Father, forgive these people <sup>[i.e., the ones responsible for crucifying Him]</sup>, for they do not know <sup>[i.e., realize]</sup> what they are doing.” Then the soldiers <sup>[See John 19:23]</sup> divided Jesus’ clothing among them by gambling for them.

People stood <sup>[around]</sup> watching <sup>[all this]</sup>, while the leaders even sneered at Him, saying, “He saved other people; <sup>[now]</sup> let him save himself <sup>[i.e., from dying]</sup> if he <sup>[really]</sup> is the Christ, God’s specially chosen one.”
The soldiers also mocked Him; they came to Him, offering Him sour wine <sup>[to drink]</sup>,
saying, “If you <sup>[really]</sup> are the King of the Jews, save yourself.”
There was an inscription above Him <sup>[i.e., on the upright portion of the cross]</sup>, stating, “This is the king of the Jews.”

Then one of the criminals, who was hanging <sup>[on a cross also]</sup> spoke abusively to Him, saying, “Are you not the Christ <sup>[i.e., God’s specially chosen one]</sup>? <sup>[If you are]</sup>, then save yourself and us.”
But the other criminal spoke harshly to the first one, saying, “Do you not even have any fear of God <sup>[left]</sup>, since you <sup>[too]</sup> are experiencing the same sentence of condemnation?
And for us it is truly a just sentence, for we are getting back what we deserve for our actions, but this man has not done anything wrong.”
Then he said, “Jesus, remember <sup>[to be merciful to]</sup> me when you come into your kingdom.”

Then Jesus said to him, “Truly I tell you, today you will be with me in Paradise <sup>[Note: This is a place in God’s presence, referred to as “the third heaven” (II Cor. 12:2-4), and where overcomers will be able to eat from “the tree of life” (Rev. 2:7)]</sup>.”
Now when it was about twelve o’clock noon, darkness came over all the land until three o’clock in the afternoon, for
the sun’s light failed to shine. Then the Temple curtain became torn down the middle.
Jesus called out in a loud voice, “Father, I commit my spirit into your hands.” And after He said this, He gave up His spirit <sup>[i.e., to God in death]</sup>.
And when the military officer in charge of one hundred men saw what had happened, he honored God <sup>[by]</sup> saying, “Certainly this man had <sup>[always]</sup> done what was right.”
And after the crowds that had gathered to witness this sight saw what had happened they left, beating their chests <sup>[i.e., as an expression of anguish over witnessing Jesus’ death]</sup>.
And all of those who knew Him, as well as the women who had followed Him from Galilee <sup>[See Matt. 27:55- 56]</sup>, watched all this from a distance.
And just then <sup>[there appeared]</sup> a member of the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> Council named Joseph from the city of Arimathea. He was a good man who tried to do what was right,
(and who had not agreed with the decision and action of the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> Council). He was expecting the kingdom of God <sup>[to appear soon]</sup>.
<sup>[So]</sup>, Joseph went to <sup>[governor]</sup> Pilate and asked for the body of Jesus.

<sup>[Upon receiving permission]</sup> he took it down <sup>[from the cross]</sup> and wrapped it in a linen cloth, and placed it in a grave site, cut out of <sup>[a ledge of]</sup> rock, where no one had ever been buried.
This happened on the Day of Preparation <sup>[for the Passover Festival]</sup>, as the Sabbath day was about to begin <sup>[i.e., shortly before 6 PM on Friday]</sup>.
The women who had accompanied Jesus from Galilee followed behind <sup>[those carrying the body]</sup> and saw where the grave site was and how His body was placed in it.

So, they returned <sup>[home]</sup> and prepared spices and <sup>[aromatic]</sup> oils <sup>[i.e., for embalming Jesus’ body]</sup>. Then they rested on the Sabbath day according to the <sup>[fourth]</sup> commandment. <sup>[See Ex. 20:10]</sup>.
