“I am the good shepherd; and a good shepherd <sup>[willingly]</sup> dies for the sheep.

The person who is <sup>[only]</sup> a hired hand, and not the shepherd, <sup>[and]</sup> who does not own the sheep <sup>[himself]</sup>, sees the wolf coming and leaves the sheep and runs away. So, the wolf attacks the flock and scatters <sup>[the sheep]</sup>.
<sup>[The reason]</sup> he runs away is because he is a hired hand and does not <sup>[really]</sup> care about the sheep.
I am the good shepherd and I know my sheep <sup>[intimately]</sup>, and they know me <sup>[that way]</sup>,
just like the Father knows me <sup>[intimately]</sup> and I know the Father <sup>[that way]</sup>. And I <sup>[am about to]</sup> give up my life for the sheep.

I have other sheep <sup>[also]</sup> which are not <sup>[presently]</sup> in this corral; I must lead them, too. They will hear my voice and become one flock <sup>[i.e., with the others]</sup>, having one shepherd. <sup>[Note: This probably refers to believing Gentiles becoming God’s people, along with the Jews]</sup>.
The Father loves me because I <sup>[am about to]</sup> give up my life, so that I can take it back again.
No one is taking it away from me, but I am giving it up voluntarily. I have the right to give it up and I have the right to take it back again. I received the commandment to do this from my Father.”
The Jews who heard these words became divided again <sup>[i.e., over their meaning]</sup>.
Many of them said, “This man is dominated by an evil spirit; he is crazy, why listen to him?”
Others said, “These are not the words of someone dominated by an evil spirit. Can an evil spirit restore sight to a blind person?”

During the winter, when the Festival of Dedication was being held in Jerusalem <sup>[Note: This was the Jewish festival commemorating the rededication of the Temple in 164 BC, after its pagan desecration by Greeks. It is still observed today by Jews as “Hanukkah”]</sup>,
Jesus was walking in the Temple <sup>[area]</sup> in “Solomon’s Portico” <sup>[Note: This was a large covered area, with rows of columns, on the outside of the Temple enclosure]</sup>.
Some Jewish <sup>[authorities]</sup> gathered around Him and asked, “How long will you hold us in suspense? If you <sup>[really]</sup> are the Christ, tell us plainly.”

Jesus answered them, “I did tell you, but you would not believe me. The <sup>[miraculous]</sup> deeds that I am doing in my Father’s name <sup>[i.e., by the Father’s authority]</sup> testify about me.
But you do not believe me, because you are not <sup>[part]</sup> of my sheep.
My sheep listen to my voice, and I know them, and they follow me.
I give them never ending life and they will never be destroyed, and no one will <sup>[be able to]</sup> steal them out of my hand.
What my Father has given me <sup>[i.e., my sheep]</sup> are greater than everything <sup>[else]</sup>, and no one is able to steal them from the Father’s hand.
I and the Father are one.”
Then the Jews again picked up stones <sup>[intending]</sup> to stone Him.
Jesus said to them, “I have shown you many good deeds <sup>[i.e., miracles]</sup>, performed through the Father. Which one of them are you stoning me for?”

The Jews answered Him, “We are not stoning you for a good deed, but for your abusive speech <sup>[i.e., about God]</sup>, and because you are claiming to be God, even though you are <sup>[only]</sup> a man.”
Jesus replied to them, “Is it not written in your Law <sup>[Psa. 82:6]</sup>, ‘I said, you people are gods’?
If God called those people, to whom His message came, “gods” (and Scripture cannot be set aside),

how can you people say to Him, whom the Father set apart and sent into the world, ‘You are speaking abusively <sup>[i.e., about God]</sup>,’ because I said, ‘I am the Son of God’? He is the One whom the Father set apart <sup>[i.e., for His service]</sup> and sent into the world.
If I am not doing my Father’s deeds <sup>[i.e., miracles]</sup>, do not believe me.
But if I am doing them, even though you do not believe me, believe the deeds. Then you will know and continue to know that the Father is in me, and that I am in the Father.”
<sup>[Then]</sup> the Jews again tried to arrest Him, but He escaped from their hands.
Then Jesus again went across <sup>[to the east side of]</sup> the Jordan River, to the place where John had first immersed people, and stayed there.
Many people came to Him and were saying, “It is true that John did not perform any <sup>[miraculous]</sup> signs, but everything he said about this man was true.”
So, many people there believed in Jesus.
Now a certain man named Lazarus, from Bethany, became sick. Bethany was the town where Mary and her sister Martha lived. <sup>[See 11:18]</sup>.
This was the Mary who had poured the perfume on the Lord and wiped His feet with her hair <sup>[See Matt. 26:6-13]</sup>, whose brother Lazarus was sick.
So, the sisters sent <sup>[someone]</sup> to Him, saying, “Look, Lord, the person you love <sup>[i.e., your dear friend, Lazarus]</sup> is sick.”
But when Jesus heard this, He said, “This illness will not result in <sup>[his]</sup> death, but was intended to bring honor to God, so that the Son of God would be honored by it.”
Now Jesus loved Martha and her sister <sup>[Mary]</sup> and <sup>[her brother]</sup> Lazarus.
So, when He heard that Lazarus was sick, He stayed where He was <sup>[i.e., on the east side of the Jordan River. See 10:40]</sup> for two <sup>[more]</sup> days.
Then, after this, He said to His disciples, “Let us go <sup>[back]</sup> to Judea again.”
The disciples replied to Him, “The Jews <sup>[there]</sup> have just been trying to stone you, and you are going back again?”
Jesus answered, “Are there not twelve hours in a day? <sup>[i.e., hours of daylight]</sup>. If a person walks during daylight, he will not stumble because he can see <sup>[by]</sup> the sun’s light.

But if he walks at night he <sup>[might]</sup> stumble, because there is no light <sup>[to see by]</sup>. <sup>[Note: These words have a figurative meaning as Jesus is speaking about His mission in life]</sup>.
After saying these things He said to His disciples, “Our friend Lazarus has fallen asleep, but I am going to wake him up.”
But the disciples said to Him, “Lord, if he is <sup>[only]</sup> asleep, he will get well.”
Now Jesus had been talking about Lazarus’ death, but the disciples thought He was referring to normal sleep.
So, Jesus said plainly, “Lazarus is dead.

And I am glad for your sakes that I was not there <sup>[when he died]</sup>, so that <sup>[now]</sup> you will believe <sup>[i.e., when you see him miraculously raised up]</sup>. But let us go to him <sup>[now]</sup>.”
Then Thomas, which means, “The Twin,” said to his fellow disciples, “Let us go too, so we can die with Him.” <sup>[i.e., by being stoned to death with Jesus. See 11:8]</sup>.
So, when Jesus arrived, He found out that Lazarus had already been in his grave <sup>[i.e., a cave-like tomb]</sup> for four days.
Now Bethany was close to Jerusalem, <sup>[being]</sup> fewer than two miles away,
and many of Martha and Mary’s Jewish friends had come to comfort them over <sup>[the loss of]</sup> their brother.

So, when Martha heard that Jesus was coming, she went <sup>[out to the edge of town. See verse 30]</sup> to meet Him, but Mary <sup>[just]</sup> sat at home <sup>[i.e., probably grief-stricken]</sup>.
Then Martha said to Jesus, “Lord, if <sup>[only]</sup> you had been here, my brother would not have died.
And I know that even now God will give you whatever you ask Him for.”
Jesus said to her, “Your brother will rise again <sup>[from the dead]</sup>.”
Martha replied to Him, “I know that he will rise again at the resurrection on the last day <sup>[i.e., the judgment day]</sup>.”
Jesus said to her, “I am the resurrection and the life; the person who believes in me will live on, even though he dies<sup> [physically]</sup>.
And whoever goes on living and believing in me, will never die <sup>[spiritually]</sup>. Do you believe this?”
Martha answered Him, “Yes, Lord, I have <sup>[already]</sup> believed that you are the Christ, the Son of God, who was to come into the world.”
After saying this, she left and called her sister Mary and said to her privately, “The Teacher is here and is asking for you.”
And when Mary heard this, she got up quickly and went to Jesus.
(Now Jesus had not yet arrived in town, but was still at the place where Martha had met Him <sup>[i.e., probably on the road near the edge of town]</sup>).
When Martha’s Jewish friends, who had been comforting her at her house, saw Mary getting up quickly and leaving, they followed her, thinking she was going to the grave site to mourn.
So, when Mary arrived at where Jesus was and saw Him, she fell down at His feet and said, “Lord, if <sup>[only]</sup> you had been here, my brother would not have died.”

When Jesus saw her and her Jewish friends, who had come with her, crying, He felt distressed in His spirit and <sup>[visibly]</sup> troubled <sup>[Note: This last word means to shake with emotion, and seems to have been caused by His deep sympathy for these grieving people]</sup>,
and said, “Where have they laid his body?” They answered Him, “Lord, come and see.”
Jesus shed tears.
<sup>[Some of]</sup> the Jewish friends then said, “Look how <sup>[much]</sup> he loved him!” <sup>[See verse 3]</sup>.
But others of them said, “Could not this man, who restored sight to a blind person, have kept Lazarus from dying?”
So, Jesus again felt distressed within Himself <sup>[as He]</sup> went to the grave site, which was a cave with a stone <sup>[slab]</sup> across its entrance.
Jesus said, “You people, take the stone away.” <sup>[But]</sup> Martha, the dead man’s sister, said to Jesus, “Lord, his body is decomposing by now, for he has been dead four days.”
Jesus replied to her, “Did I not tell you that you would see God’s glory <sup>[i.e., a miraculous resurrection. See verses 22-27]</sup> if you believed?”
So, they removed the stone. Then Jesus looked up and said, “Father, thank you for hearing me. <sup>[Note: Jesus had already prayed for and been assured of Lazarus’ resurrection]</sup>.
I know that you always hear me, but I said this for the sake of the crowd standing here, so they would believe that you sent me.”
And after He had said this, He called out in a loud voice, “Lazarus, come out!”

<sup>[Immediately]</sup> the man who had died came out <sup>[of the cave]</sup> with his hands and feet <sup>[still]</sup> wrapped in bandages, and a cloth around his face. Jesus said to his Jewish friends, “Unwrap him and let him go.”
When many of Mary’s Jewish friends, who had come to console her, saw what Jesus had done, they believed in Him.
But some of them went to the Pharisees and told them what Jesus had done.

So, the leading priests and the Pharisees assembled the Council <sup>[Note: This was the Jewish governing body called “the Sanhedrin”]</sup> and said, “What should we do, for this man is performing many <sup>[miraculous]</sup> signs?
If we allow him to go on <sup>[doing this]</sup>, everyone will believe in him, and the Romans will come and destroy our Temple and our nation <sup>[i.e., its people]</sup>.”
But a certain member of the Sanhedrin, <sup>[named]</sup> Caiaphas, who was head priest that year, said to <sup>[the rest of]</sup> them, “You do not know what you are talking about.
And you are not taking into consideration that it would be profitable to you for one man to die for the people, so that the entire nation would not be destroyed.”

Now he did not say this on his own <sup>[i.e., he was not aware of the significance of his words]</sup>, but since he was head priest that year, he was prophesying <sup>[i.e., predicting]</sup> that Jesus should die for the nation.
And not just <sup>[die]</sup> for the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> nation, but also that He would assemble together into one body the children of God who were scattered <sup>[throughout the world]</sup>.
So, from that day onward, the Jewish authorities began plotting <sup>[how]</sup> to kill Jesus.

So, Jesus stopped traveling publicly among the Jews <sup>[in Judea]</sup>, but left there and went to a district near the desert, to a town called Ephraim, where He remained with His disciples. <sup>[Note: Ephraim was a small town about 14 miles northeast of Jerusalem, near Samaria]</sup>.

Now the Jewish Passover Festival was to be held soon and many people went up to Jerusalem from the countryside before the <sup>[actual]</sup> Festival in order to perform the ceremonial purification rituals.
So, they began looking for Jesus and saying among themselves, as they gathered in the Temple, “What do you think? He will not attend the Festival, will he?”
Now the leading priests and the Pharisees had given orders that if anyone knew where Jesus was, he was to report it, so they could arrest Him.
So, six days before the Passover Festival <sup>[began]</sup>, Jesus went to Bethany, where Lazarus lived. He was the man Jesus <sup>[had]</sup> raised from the dead.
There Mary and Martha prepared a meal for Jesus. Martha served it while Lazarus was among those who reclined at the table with Him. <sup>[Note: See Matt. 23:6]</sup>.

Then Mary took about a pint of expensive, aromatic perfume; <sup>[it was]</sup> genuine nard, <sup>[Note: This was a sweet-smelling herb]</sup> and she poured <sup>[some of]</sup> it on Jesus’ feet and wiped them with her hair. The <sup>[whole]</sup> house was filled with the fragrant scent of the perfume.
But one of Jesus’ disciples, named Judas Iscariot, who later turned Him over <sup>[i.e., to the Jewish authorities]</sup>, said

“Why was this perfume not sold for a large sum of money <sup>[Note: The amount here was equivalent to three hundred twelve-hour days of a farm laborer’s pay, or over $32,000 in 2005]</sup> and <sup>[then]</sup> the money given to poor people?”
Now he did not say this because he cared about poor people, but because he was in charge of the money bag, and being a thief, he used to steal from it.
So, Jesus said, “Leave her alone. Let her keep <sup>[the rest of]</sup> it for <sup>[use on]</sup> the day of my burial.
For you will always have poor people among you, but you will not always have me.”
When a large crowd of Jews learned that Jesus was there <sup>[in Bethany]</sup>, they came not only because of Him, but also to see Lazarus whom He had raised from the dead.
But the leading priests made plans to kill Lazarus also,
for because of him <sup>[i.e., his miraculous resurrection]</sup>, many of the Jews rejected the priests’ leadership and believed in Jesus.
On the next day a large crowd that had come <sup>[to Jerusalem]</sup> for the Passover Festival heard that Jesus <sup>[also]</sup> was coming to Jerusalem.

<sup>[So]</sup>, they took branches from palm trees and went out to meet Him, shouting, “Hosanna <sup>[Note: See Matt. 21:9 for the meaning of this word]</sup>, may He, the King of Israel, be praised, for He comes in the name <sup>[i.e., by the authority]</sup> of the Lord!”
Jesus found a young donkey and sat on it, as it is written <sup>[Zech. 9:9]</sup>,
“Do not be afraid, daughter of Zion <sup>[i.e., people of Jerusalem]</sup>. Look, the King is coming <sup>[to you]</sup>; He <sup>[will be]</sup> riding on a donkey’s colt.”

His disciples did not understand these things at first, but after Jesus received splendor <sup>[in heaven]</sup>, then they remembered that these things had been written about Him <sup>[in Scripture]</sup> and that people had done such things to Him.
The crowd of people that was with Jesus <sup>[and saw Him]</sup> when He called Lazarus out of the grave and raised him from the dead, gave testimony <sup>[about all this]</sup>.

But the crowd that went out to meet Jesus <sup>[i.e., as He entered Jerusalem]</sup> did so because they had heard about Him performing this <sup>[miraculous]</sup> sign <sup>[i.e., of raising Lazarus]</sup>.
So, the Pharisees were saying among themselves, “Look, you men are getting nowhere <sup>[in this situation]</sup>. See, the <sup>[whole]</sup> world has gone after him.”

Now there were certain Greeks among those going up to Jerusalem to worship <sup>[God]</sup> at the <sup>[Passover]</sup> Festival <sup>[Note: These people were God-fearing Gentiles like Cornelius. See Acts 10]</sup>.
So, these people approached <sup>[the apostle]</sup> Philip, who was from Bethsaida, in Galilee with the request, ‘Sir, we would like to see Jesus.”
Philip went and told <sup>[this to]</sup> Andrew <sup>[his brother]</sup>; <sup>[then]</sup> Andrew and Philip told <sup>[it to]</sup> Jesus.
Then Jesus said to <sup>[all of]</sup> them, “The time has come for the Son of man to be glorified <sup>[i.e., by His crucifixion, resurrection and ascension]</sup>.
Truly, truly, I tell you, unless a grain of wheat drops into the soil and dies, it remains a single grain, but if it dies, <sup>[then]</sup> it will produce many grains.
The person who loves his <sup>[earthly]</sup> life will <sup>[eventually]</sup> lose it <sup>[forever]</sup>, but the person who hates his life in this world will retain never ending life.
If any person wants to serve me, he should become my follower; and wherever I am, my servant will be there also. If any person serves me, the Father will honor him.

“Now my heart is distressed; and what shall I say <sup>[i.e., to God]</sup>? ‘Father, bring me safely through this time.’ But, this is the reason I came <sup>[to earth]</sup>; to <sup>[go through]</sup> such a time.
‘Father, honor your name <sup>[in all this]</sup>.’” Then a voice came out of heaven, saying, “I have honored it, and I will honor it again.”
The crowd that was standing around heard this <sup>[i.e., the sound of the Father’s voice]</sup>, but said it was thunder. Others said, “An angel spoke to Him <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup>.”
Jesus said to them, “This voice did not speak for my sake, but for yours.
Judgment is now coming to this world; the prince of this world <sup>[i.e., Satan]</sup> will now be driven out.
And if I am lifted up from the earth <sup>[i.e., on the cross]</sup>, I will draw <sup>[i.e., attract]</sup> all people to me.”
Now He said this to indicate the kind of death He was about to experience <sup>[i.e., of being lifted up on a cross]</sup>.

Then the crowd replied to Him, “We have heard from <sup>[the writings of]</sup> the Law of Moses that the Christ will live forever; so how can you say, ‘The son of man must be lifted up <sup>[i.e., to die]</sup>?’ Who is this son of man?”

So, Jesus said to them, “The Light will be among you for only a little while <sup>[longer]</sup>. Walk around while you <sup>[still]</sup> have the Light <sup>[with you]</sup>, before darkness surrounds you. For the person who walks around in darkness does not know where he is going.

<sup>[So]</sup>, believe in the Light <sup>[i.e., Jesus is speaking of Himself]</sup> while you <sup>[still]</sup> have the Light <sup>[with you]</sup>, so that you can become enlightened people.” After Jesus said these things, He left there and hid Himself from them.
But <sup>[even]</sup> though Jesus had performed so many <sup>[miraculous]</sup> signs in front of the people, still they did not believe in Him.
This was so that these words, which Isaiah the prophet had spoken, would be fulfilled <sup>[Isa. 53:1]</sup>, “Lord, who has believed our message? And who has the Lord’s power been shown to?”
They were not able to believe because Isaiah also said <sup>[Isa. 6:10]</sup>,

“God has blinded their eyes <sup>[of understanding]</sup> and closed their minds. If He had not done so, they would <sup>[be able to]</sup> see with their eyes and understand with their minds and turn <sup>[to God]</sup> so He could heal them <sup>[spiritually]</sup>.”
Isaiah had said these things because he saw the <sup>[coming]</sup> glory of Jesus, and <sup>[so]</sup> was speaking about Him.
However, even many <sup>[Jewish]</sup> leaders believed in Him, but did not admit it, for fear of being expelled from the synagogue.
For they loved the honor they received from people more than the honor they <sup>[could]</sup> receive from God.
Then Jesus spoke out in a loud voice, “The person who believes in me, does not <sup>[only]</sup> believe in me, but <sup>[also]</sup> in Him who sent me <sup>[i.e., God]</sup>.
And the person who sees me, sees Him who sent me <sup>[also]</sup>.
I have come into the world to be a light <sup>[to it]</sup>, so that whoever believes in me would not have to remain in <sup>[spiritual]</sup> darkness.
And if any person hears my message and does not obey it, I will not judge him <sup>[now]</sup>, for I did not come into the world to judge it, but to save it.
The person who rejects me and does not accept my message has One who <sup>[will]</sup> judge him. The message that I have spoken will judge him on the last day <sup>[i.e., the judgment day]</sup>.
For I did not speak from myself <sup>[i.e., on my own authority]</sup>, but the Father Himself, who sent me, has ordered me what to say and speak.
And I know that <sup>[obeying]</sup> His commandments <sup>[leads to]</sup> never ending life. So, the things which I say are what the Father has told me <sup>[to say]</sup>.”

Now it was before the Passover Festival <sup>[was to begin]</sup> and Jesus knew that the time had come for Him to leave this world and return to the Father. Those <sup>[living]</sup> in the world, whom He had loved, and who were His own <sup>[disciples]</sup>, He continued to love <sup>[dearly]</sup> to the very end <sup>[i.e., of His life on earth]</sup>.
The devil had already filled the heart of Judas Iscariot, Simon’s son, with the desire to turn Jesus over <sup>[i.e., to the Jewish authorities]</sup>. During the <sup>[Passover]</sup> meal,
J, who knew that the Father had given Him complete authority, <sup>[also]</sup> knew that He had come from God and was <sup>[about]</sup> to return to God.
So, He got up from the dinner table, laid aside His <sup>[outer]</sup> clothing and took a towel and wrapped it around Himself.
Then He poured water into a basin and began washing His disciples’ feet and drying them with the towel around His waist.
He came to Simon Peter, who said to Him, “Lord, are you going to wash my feet, <sup>[too]</sup>?”
Jesus answered him, “You do not understand what I am doing, but you will later on.”
Peter said to Him, “<sup>[No]</sup>, you will never wash my feet.” Jesus replied to him, “If I do not wash you, you will not be able to participate with me <sup>[i.e., as a disciple]</sup>.”
Simon Peter <sup>[then]</sup> said to Him, “Lord, do not <sup>[just]</sup> wash my feet only, but my hands and head, too.”

Jesus replied to him, “The person who has already had a bath is completely clean and does not need to wash anything but his feet. And you disciples are <sup>[already]</sup> clean <sup>[i.e., spiritually]</sup>, but not every one of you.”
For Jesus knew who would betray Him; that is why He said, “Not every one of you is <sup>[spiritually]</sup> clean.”

So, after Jesus had washed His disciples’ feet and put His <sup>[outer]</sup> clothing back on, He reclined again <sup>[at the dinner table]</sup> and said to them, “Do you understand what I have done to you?
You call me ‘Teacher,’ and ‘Lord,’ and you are right in doing so because that is who I am.
So if I, then, being your Lord and Teacher have washed your feet, you should wash one another’s feet also.
For I have set an example for you, so that you should do as I have done for you.
Truly, truly, I tell you, a slave is not greater <sup>[in importance]</sup> than his master, nor is a messenger greater than the person who sent him.
If you know these things, you will be happy if you practice them.

“I am not talking about all of you; for I know the ones I have chosen <sup>[i.e., which of the twelve apostles would be faithful]</sup>. But let the Scripture <sup>[Psa. 41:9]</sup> be fulfilled <sup>[that says]</sup>, ‘The person who eats with me has <sup>[viciously]</sup> turned against me.’
I am telling you this now, <sup>[even]</sup> before it happens, so that when it does happen, you will believe that I am <sup>[the Messiah]</sup>.
Truly, truly, I tell you, the person who welcomes whoever I send welcomes me; and the one who welcomes me, welcomes God, who sent me.”
After Jesus said this, He felt disturbed in His spirit and declared, “Truly, truly, I tell you, one of you will turn me over <sup>[to the Jewish authorities]</sup>.”
The disciples looked at one another, puzzled over who He was talking about.
One of Jesus’ disciples, <sup>[the one]</sup> He loved <sup>[Note: Probably this was the apostle John]</sup> was reclining at the dinner table close to Jesus.
So, Simon Peter motioned <sup>[to John]</sup> and said, “Ask Jesus which one <sup>[of us]</sup> He is talking about.”
So, John leaned over close to Jesus and asked Him, “Lord, who is it?”

Jesus answered; “It is the person to whom I give a piece of bread after dipping it <sup>[in the sauce bowl]</sup>.” So, when He had dipped the piece of bread, He took it <sup>[out]</sup> and gave it to Judas, the son of Simon Iscariot.
Then after <sup>[he ate]</sup> the piece of bread, Satan entered Judas’ heart. So, Jesus said to him, “Do what you are going to do, and do it quickly.”
Now, no one at the table knew why He had said this to him.

For some of them thought, since Judas was in charge of the <sup>[money]</sup> bag, that Jesus was telling him, “Buy whatever we need for the <sup>[Passover]</sup> meal,” or that he was <sup>[being told]</sup> to give <sup>[money]</sup> to poor people <sup>[from it]</sup>.
So, after eating the piece of bread, Judas went out immediately. And it was nighttime.

Then after Judas left, Jesus said, “Now the Son of man is <sup>[being]</sup> glorified <sup>[Note: Being “glorified” involved Jesus’ crucifixion, resurrection and ascension. The first step of this had now begun]</sup>. And God is <sup>[being]</sup> glorified through the Son’s life.

And if God is <sup>[so]</sup> glorified through Him <sup>[now]</sup>, God will <sup>[also]</sup> glorify the Son in Himself very soon. <sup>[Note: Possibly this was a reference to Jesus returning to the Father’s presence]</sup>.

“I will be with you, <sup>[my dear]</sup> children, for only a little while <sup>[longer]</sup>. You people will look for me, but I am now telling you what I told the Jewish authorities, ‘You will not be able to go where I will be.’
I am giving you a new commandment: love one another. Also you should love one another in the same way that I have loved you.
If you love one another, then everyone will know by <sup>[observing]</sup> this that you are my disciples.”
Simon Peter said to Jesus, “Lord, where are you going?” Jesus answered, “You cannot follow me where I am going now, but you will <sup>[be able to]</sup> follow later.”
Peter <sup>[then]</sup> said to Him, “Lord, why can I not follow you <sup>[there]</sup> even now? I am willing to die for you.”
Jesus answered him; “Will you <sup>[really]</sup> die for me? Truly, truly, I tell you, before the rooster crows, you will deny <sup>[knowing]</sup> me three times.”
<sup>[Jesus continued]</sup>, “Do not allow your hearts to be upset. You should believe in God, and believe in me also.
There are many rooms in my Father’s house, and I am going to prepare a place for each one you <sup>[in them]</sup>. I would not have told you this if it were not so.
And if I go to prepare a place for each of you, I will return to take all you to <sup>[be with]</sup> me, so that you also will be where I am.
And you all know how to get where I am going.”
Thomas said to Jesus, “Lord, we do not know where you are going; how could we <sup>[possibly]</sup> know the way <sup>[to get there]</sup>?”

Jesus answered him, “I am the way <sup>[to God]</sup>, and the truth <sup>[to believe]</sup>, and the life <sup>[to live]</sup>. No one can go to the Father, except through <sup>[believing in]</sup> me.
If you have come to know me, you will know my Father also. From now on you do know Him, and have seen Him.”
Philip said to Him, “Lord, show us the Father and it <sup>[will]</sup> be enough for us.”

Jesus replied to him, “Have I been with you men all this time, and <sup>[still]</sup> you do not know me, Philip? The person who has seen me, has <sup>[also]</sup> seen the Father. How can you say, ‘Show us the Father’?

Do you not believe that I am in <sup>[fellowship with]</sup> the Father, and the Father is in <sup>[fellowship with]</sup> me? The words which I say to you do not originate with me, but it is the Father, who lives in me, who does His work <sup>[in me]</sup>.

You should believe me <sup>[when I say]</sup> that I am in <sup>[fellowship with]</sup> the Father, and the Father is in <sup>[fellowship with]</sup> me. Or else believe me for the sake of the deeds themselves that I perform.

Truly, truly, I tell you, the person who believes in me will perform the deeds that I do also; and he will perform even greater deeds than these, because I am going to the Father. <sup>[Note: The “greater deeds” are probably evangelizing the world]</sup>.
And I will do <sup>[for you]</sup> whatever you ask in my name <sup>[i.e., by my authority]</sup>, so that the Father may be honored through the Son.
I will do anything <sup>[for you]</sup>, if you ask <sup>[for it]</sup> in my name.
If you love me, you will obey my commandments.
“And I will ask the Father and He will give you another Helper <sup>[i.e., besides me]</sup>, who will be with you forever.

He is the Holy Spirit of truth <sup>[i.e., who reveals truth]</sup>, whom the world cannot receive, for it does not see Him or know Him. <sup>[But]</sup> you know Him, for He lives with you, and will be within you.
I will not abandon you <sup>[Note: The Greek says, “leave you orphans”]</sup>; I will come <sup>[back]</sup> to you.
After a little while <sup>[longer]</sup> the world will not see me anymore, but you men will see me. Because I <sup>[will continue to]</sup> live, you will live also.

When that day comes <sup>[i.e., after His ascension (?)]</sup>, you will know that I am in <sup>[fellowship with]</sup> the Father, and that you are in <sup>[fellowship with]</sup> me, and that I am in you <sup>[i.e., through the Holy Spirit]</sup>.

The person who knows <sup>[what]</sup> my commandments <sup>[are]</sup> and obeys them is the one who <sup>[truly]</sup> loves me. And the person who loves me will be loved by my Father, and I will love him and reveal myself to him.”

<sup>[Then]</sup> Judas (not the betrayer) <sup>[Note: This was the son of James, See Luke 6:16. He is also called Thaddaeus, See Matt. 10:3]</sup>, said to Jesus, “Lord, what has happened <sup>[i.e., to your original plans to be seen by everyone when you came. See Luke 21:27]</sup>, that you will reveal yourself to us apostles <sup>[only]</sup> and not to the <sup>[whole]</sup> world?”

Jesus answered him, “If a person loves me, he will obey my teaching, and my Father will love him and we will come to him and make our home with him <sup>[i.e., in the person of the indwelling Holy Spirit]</sup>.
The person who does not love me will not obey my teaching. And the message you are hearing is not mine, but belongs to the Father, who sent me.
“I have told you these things while I am still with you.

But the Helper, that is, the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name <sup>[i.e., by my authority]</sup> will teach you everything. And He will remind you of everything that I <sup>[have]</sup> said to you.
I leave you with peace; I give you my peace. I am not giving you peace like the world gives. Do not allow your hearts to be upset or afraid.

You heard me say to you, ‘I am going away and I am returning to you.’ If you <sup>[really]</sup> loved me, you would have been happy that I am going to the Father, because the Father is greater than I am.
Now I have told you this, even before it happens, so that when it does happen you will believe <sup>[in me]</sup>.
I will not speak with you very much anymore because the ruler of the world <sup>[i.e., Satan]</sup> is coming, and he has no control over me.

But, in order for the world to know that I love the Father, I am <sup>[always]</sup> doing what the Father commands me to. Let us get up, and leave here.” <sup>[i.e., leave the room where they had eaten the Passover meal. See 13:1 with 18:1]</sup>.
<sup>[Jesus continued]</sup>, “I am the real vine, and my Father is the owner of the vineyard.

He will cut off <sup>[See verse 6]</sup> every branch <sup>[i.e., follower of Christ]</sup> who is in <sup>[fellowship with]</sup> me that does not bear fruit. And He will prune every branch that does bear fruit, so it will bear more fruit.
You people are already ‘clean’ <sup>[i.e., have been pruned of undesirable traits]</sup> because of <sup>[obedience to]</sup> the teaching I have given you.

Remain united to me, and I will remain united to you. Just as a branch cannot bear fruit by itself, unless it remains united to the vine, so neither can you people <sup>[bear fruit]</sup> unless you remain united to me.
“I am the vine; you people are the branches. The person who remains united to me, and I to him, will bear much fruit. For you cannot do anything if <sup>[you are]</sup> separated from me.

If a person does not remain united to me, he will be thrown out as a <sup>[fruitless]</sup> branch and dried up. People gather up such branches and throw them into the fire to be burned <sup>[as fuel (?)]</sup>.
If you remain in <sup>[fellowship with]</sup> me and my teaching remains in your hearts, <sup>[you can]</sup> ask for whatever you want, and it will be done for you.
My Father is honored by your bearing much fruit and <sup>[thus]</sup> showing that you are my disciples.

“Just as the Father has loved me, <sup>[so]</sup> I have also loved you. You should continue to receive <sup>[or, respond to]</sup> my love. <sup>[Note: The next verse tells how this can be done]</sup>.
If you obey my commandments, <sup>[then]</sup> you will continue receiving my love; just as I have obeyed my Father’s commandments and <sup>[so]</sup> continue receiving His love.
I have said these things to you so that my joy may be in your hearts, and that your joy may be complete.
My commandment <sup>[to you]</sup> is this: You should love one another just as I have loved you.
No one has <sup>[any]</sup> greater love than to give up his life for his friends.John 15:14 You people are my friends if you do what I command you to.
I do not call you slaves anymore, for a slave does not know what his master is doing. But I have called you friends, because I have told you everything that I heard from myFather.

You <sup>[apostles]</sup> did not choose me, but I chose you, and appointed you to go and bear much fruit <sup>[i.e., through their evangelizing]</sup>, and that your fruit should last. Then the Father will give you whatever you ask Him for in my name <sup>[i.e. by my authority]</sup>.
I command you to do these things so that you will <sup>[demonstrate your]</sup> love for one another. <sup>[Note: Or this may mean that His command was to love one another, as in verse 12]</sup>.
“If the world hates you, then know that it has <sup>[already]</sup> hated me, <sup>[even]</sup> before it hated you.
If you belonged to the world, the world would love <sup>[you as]</sup> its own people. But since you do not belong to the world, for I chose you out of it, this is why the world hates you.

Remember what I said to you, ‘A slave is not greater <sup>[in importance]</sup> than his master.’ If they persecuted me, they will <sup>[also]</sup> persecute you, too; if they obeyed my teaching, they will obey yours also.
But they <sup>[i.e., the world]</sup> will do all these things to you for my name’s sake <sup>[i.e., because you are my disciples]</sup>, for they do not know Him <sup>[i.e., God]</sup>, who sent me.
They would not be guilty of sinning if I had not come and spoken to them. But now <sup>[that I have]</sup>, they have no excuse for their sin.
The person who hates me hates my Father also.
If I had not performed the <sup>[miraculous]</sup> deeds among them which no one else had done, they would not be guilty of sinning. But now they have both seen and hated both me and my Father.
This all happened so the Scripture, written in their Law <sup>[Psa. 35:19]</sup>, would be fulfilled, ‘They hated me for no reason.’

“But the Helper will come, that is, the Holy Spirit of truth <sup>[i.e., who reveals truth]</sup>. He comes from the Father and I will send Him to you from the Father. <sup>[When He comes]</sup> He will give testimony about me.
And you will give testimony about me <sup>[also]</sup>, because you men have been with me from the beginning <sup>[of my ministry]</sup>.”
<sup>[Jesus continued]</sup>, “I have told you these things so that you men would not fall away <sup>[i.e., from the faith]</sup>.
The Jewish authorities will expel you from their synagogues. In fact, the time will come when whoever kills you will think he is doing a service to God.
And they will do these things because they have not known <sup>[either]</sup> the Father or me.

But I have told you these things so that, when the time comes <sup>[for people to do such things]</sup>, you will remember that I had warned you. I did not tell you them at the beginning <sup>[of my ministry]</sup> because I was with you <sup>[i.e., to protect you from persecution]</sup>.
“But now I am going to Him who sent me; yet none of you is asking me, ‘Where are you going?’
And now that I have told you these things, your hearts are full of grief.
Yet I am telling you the truth: It will be helpful to you for me to go away. For unless I go, the Helper <sup>[i.e., the Holy Spirit]</sup> will not come to you. But if I go, I will send Him to you.

And when He does come, He will bring conviction to the world regarding sin <sup>[i.e., exposing it and producing guilt]</sup>, regarding what is right <sup>[i.e., upholding godly standards]</sup>, and regarding judgment <sup>[i.e., showing that sin condemns]</sup>.
<sup>[The world will be proven guilty]</sup> of sin for not believing in me.
<sup>[It will be shown]</sup> what is right because I am going <sup>[back]</sup> to the Father and you will not see me anymore.
<sup>[And it will be shown]</sup> judgment, because the ruler of this world <sup>[i.e., Satan. See 12:31]</sup> has been condemned <sup>[i.e., by Jesus’ death and resurrection]</sup>.
“I still have many things to tell you, but you cannot bear <sup>[to hear]</sup> them at present.

However, when the Holy Spirit of truth comes, He will lead you <sup>[apostles]</sup> into all the truth. For He will not speak on His own authority, but will speak <sup>[only]</sup> what He hears <sup>[from the Father]</sup>. And He will tell you about the things that are to come.
He will honor me, for He will receive what belongs to me and will declare it to you.
Everything the Father has belongs to me. Therefore, I said that the Holy Spirit will receive what belongs to me and will declare it to you.
“After a little while <sup>[longer]</sup> you will not see me anymore. Then a little while <sup>[later]</sup> you will see me again.”

Then some of Jesus’ disciples said to one another, “What is this He is telling us: ‘After a little while <sup>[longer]</sup> you will not see me. Then, a little while <sup>[later]</sup> you will see me again,’ and ‘Because I am going <sup>[back]</sup> to the Father’?” <sup>[See verse 10]</sup>.
So, they <sup>[again]</sup> said, “What does He mean by saying, ‘A little while’? We do not know what He is talking about.”

Jesus sensed that the disciples wanted to ask Him a question, so He said to them, “Are you discussing among yourselves my saying ‘After a little while <sup>[longer]</sup> you will not see me. Then, a little while <sup>[later]</sup> you will see me again’?

Truly, truly, I tell you, you will cry and mourn <sup>[i.e., over my suffering and death]</sup>, but the world will be glad <sup>[about it]</sup>. You will be sad, but your sadness will turn to joy <sup>[i.e., when learning of my resurrection]</sup>.
A woman has pain when giving birth because her time has come. But when the child is born, she does not remember the intense suffering because she is so happy over the child being born into the world.
So, you men also will experience grief <sup>[i.e., over my suffering and death]</sup>, but I will see you again and your hearts will be glad. And no one will take away your joy.

At that time <sup>[i.e., after my resurrection]</sup>, you will not be asking me <sup>[for]</sup> anything. Truly, truly, I tell you, if you will ask the Father for anything <sup>[then]</sup>, in my name <sup>[i.e., by my authority]</sup>, He will give it to you.
Up until now you have not asked for anything in my name. <sup>[So now]</sup> ask, and you will receive, that your joy may be complete.
“I have spoken these things to you in figurative language. <sup>[However]</sup>, the time will come when I will not speak to you in figurative language, but will tell you plainly about the Father.
At that time you will ask Him <sup>[i.e., for things in prayer]</sup>, in my name; I do not say that I will ask the Father for you,
for the Father Himself loves you <sup>[i.e., and will grant your requests without my persuasion]</sup>, because you have loved me and have believed that I came from God.
I came from the Father into this world and now I am leaving the world and returning to the Father.”
<sup>[Then]</sup> Jesus’ disciples said <sup>[to Him]</sup>, “Look, you are speaking plainly now, and not in figurative language.
Now we realize that you know everything and do not need anyone to ask you <sup>[questions]</sup>. This makes us believe that you came from God.”
Jesus replied to them, “Do you believe <sup>[in me]</sup> now?

Look, the time is coming --- yes, it is already here --- when each of you will be scattered to his own home, and will leave me <sup>[all]</sup> alone. And yet I am not alone, because the Father is with me.
I have said these things to you so that you can have peace in <sup>[fellowship with]</sup> me. You will have trouble in the world, but cheer up, for I have conquered the world.”
After saying these things, Jesus looked up to heaven and said, “Father, the time has come; honor your Son so that your Son may honor you.
Just as you gave Him authority over all mankind, He will give never ending life to all those people <sup>[Note: The Greek word here is neuter gender, “all those things”]</sup> you have given Him.
And this is never ending life; that people may know you <sup>[as]</sup> the only true God, and Jesus Christ, the One whom you have sent.
I am honoring you <sup>[while]</sup> on earth by completing the work you gave me to do.
And now, Father, honor me in your presence with the honor I had with you before the world was <sup>[created]</sup>.

I have revealed your name <sup>[i.e., your power and character]</sup> to the men you gave me out of the world <sup>[i.e., the apostles]</sup>. They belonged to you, but you gave them to me, and they have obeyed your word.
Now they know that everything you gave me came from you,
because I have given them the message you gave me, and they accepted it. They <sup>[also]</sup> knew for certain that I came from you, and they believed that you sent me.
I am praying for them <sup>[i.e., the apostles]</sup>; I am not praying for the world, but for those whom you gave me, who belong to you.
And everything I have belongs to you, and what you have belongs to me; and I am honored through these men.

And <sup>[now]</sup> I am coming to you, for I will not be in the world any longer, yet these men are <sup>[still]</sup> in the world. Holy Father, protect them by <sup>[the power of]</sup> your name which you have given me. May these men be one, as we are <sup>[one]</sup>.

I protected them while I was with them by <sup>[the power of]</sup> your name which you gave me. <sup>[See verse 11]</sup>. I guarded them so that not one of them was lost except the “son of destruction” <sup>[i.e., Judas]</sup>. <sup>[This was]</sup> so that the Scripture would be fulfilled <sup>[Psa. 41:9 (?)]</sup>.
But now I am coming to you, and I am speaking these things <sup>[while still]</sup> in the world, so that they <sup>[i.e., the apostles]</sup> may have my joy within them in all its fullness.
I have given them your word, and the world hated them because they do not belong to the world just as I do not belong to the world.
I am not praying that you should take them out of the world, but that you should protect them from the evil one <sup>[i.e., the devil]</sup>.
They do not belong to the world, just as I do not belong to the world.
Set them apart <sup>[i.e., for your service]</sup> through the truth; your word is truth.
Just as you have sent me into the world, so I have sent <sup>[i.e., will send]</sup> them into the world.
And for their sakes I dedicate myself <sup>[to you]</sup> so that they themselves also may be truly dedicated <sup>[to you]</sup>.
“I am not praying for these <sup>[apostles]</sup> only, but also for <sup>[all of]</sup> those who believe in me through their message.

<sup>[I pray]</sup> that all of them may be one, just as you, Father, are <sup>[one]</sup> in <sup>[fellowship with]</sup> me, and I <sup>[am one]</sup> in <sup>[fellowship with]</sup> you, so that they also may be <sup>[one]</sup> in <sup>[fellowship with]</sup> us in order that the world may <sup>[see it and]</sup> believe that you sent me.
And I have given them <sup>[i.e., all believers]</sup> the honor which you gave me, so that they may be one, just as we are one:
I in them, and you in me, so that they may be completely one. <sup>[This is]</sup> so the world will know that you sent me and that you love them just as you love me.

“Father, I want those you gave me <sup>[i.e., the apostles, see verse 6]</sup> to be with me where I am <sup>[going]</sup>, so they will see my splendor <sup>[i.e., in heaven]</sup>, which you have given me, for you loved me before the creation of the world.
Righteous Father, the world does not know you but I know you, and these men know that you sent me.
And I declared your name to them and I will continue to declare it, so that the love you have for me will be in them <sup>[also]</sup>, and that I <sup>[will be]</sup> in them.”

After Jesus had said these things He went out with His disciples across the Kidron valley. <sup>[Note: This was a “wadi,” or dry creek bed which carried a torrent of water during the rainy season. It ran parallel with the east wall of Jerusalem, between the city and the Mount of Olives]</sup>. There was an <sup>[olive]</sup> orchard there and Jesus and His disciples went into it.
Now Judas, who <sup>[was soon to]</sup> turn Jesus over <sup>[to the Jewish authorities]</sup> also knew where this place was, for Jesus had frequently met there with His disciples.

So, Judas took a detachment of <sup>[Roman]</sup> soldiers and some <sup>[Temple]</sup> guards sent from the leading priests and Pharisees, and carrying lanterns, torches and weapons, they went to the orchard.
Since Jesus knew everything that was going to happen to Him, He went out and said to them, “Who are you men looking for?”
They answered Him, “Jesus, from Nazareth.” Jesus replied to them, “I am <sup>[He]</sup>.” Judas, who was turning Him over <sup>[to the Jewish authorities]</sup> was standing there with them also.
Now when Jesus had said to them, “I am <sup>[He]</sup>,” they <sup>[all]</sup> moved backward and fell to the ground. <sup>[Note: This appears to have been done by some miraculous power]</sup>.
So, He asked them again, “Who are you looking for?” And they answered, “Jesus, from Nazareth.”
Jeshu saith to them, “I <sup>[already]</sup> told you that I am <sup>[He]</sup>; so if you are looking for me, let these men <sup>[i.e., the apostles]</sup> go.”
<sup>[He said this]</sup> so the words He had spoken <sup>[previously]</sup> would be fulfilled <sup>[See 17:12]</sup>, “I did not lose any of those men you <sup>[i.e., God]</sup> gave me.”
So, Simon Peter, who had a sword, drew it out and struck the head priest’s slave, cutting off his right ear. Now the slave’s name was Malchus.
Then Jesus said to Peter, “Put your sword back in its sheath. Should I not drink the cup <sup>[i.e., of suffering]</sup> the Father has given me?”
So, the detachment of <sup>[Roman]</sup> soldiers, their commanding officer and the Jewish <sup>[Temple]</sup> guards arrested Jesus and tied Him up.
They led Him to Annas first <sup>[Note: This man was a former head priest. See Luke 3:2]</sup>, for he was the father-in-law of Caiaphas, who was head priest that year.
Now Caiaphas was the one who had advised the Jews that it was profitable for one man to die for the people <sup>[See 11:50]</sup>.

Simon Peter followed along with another disciple. <sup>[Note: Some think this refers to the apostle John himself, but there is no strong evidence in favor of that conclusion]</sup>. Now that disciple was <sup>[well-]</sup> known to the head priest, so was able to enter his courtyard with Jesus

while Peter stood outside at the gate. So, the other disciple, who was <sup>[well-]</sup> known to the head priest, went outside and spoke to the girl gatekeeper, <sup>[who]</sup> then brought Peter inside <sup>[too]</sup>.
So, the slave-girl gatekeeper asked Peter, “Are you not one of this man’s disciples, too?” “<sup>[No]</sup>, I am not,” Peter replied.

Now the <sup>[head priest’s]</sup> slaves and the <sup>[Temple]</sup> guards were standing around a charcoal fire they had made to warm themselves by, because it was cold. And Peter was also warming himself with them.

Meanwhile, the head priest asked Jesus about His disciples and His teaching. <sup>[Note: This was probably Annas, the former “head priest” who later sent Jesus to the current head priest, Caiaphas. See verse 24]</sup>.
Jesus answered him, “I have spoken publicly to the world; I always taught in the synagogues and in the Temple, where all the Jews assemble. I have never said anything in secret.
Why are you asking me? Ask the people who have heard me about what I said to them. Look, these people know what I said.”
When Jesus said this, one of the guards standing there struck Him <sup>[in the face]</sup>, saying, “Is that any way to talk to the head priest?”
Jesus answered him, “If I have said anything wrong, tell me what it was. But if <sup>[I spoke]</sup> properly, why did you hit me?”
So, Annas had Him tied up and sent Him <sup>[on]</sup> to Caiaphas, the head priest.

Now Simon Peter had been standing and warming himself <sup>[by the outdoor charcoal fire]</sup>. So, someone asked him, “Are you not one of His disciples, too?” Peter denied it, saying, “<sup>[No]</sup>, I am not.”
One of the head priest’s slaves, who was a relative of the man whose ear Peter had cut off, said, “Did I not see you in the orchard with him?”
So, Peter denied it again, and immediately the rooster crowed.

Then, early in the morning they led Jesus from Caiaphas to <sup>[Governor Pilate’s]</sup> headquarters. But the Jewish authorities would not enter it, because <sup>[if they had]</sup> they would have become ceremonially unclean, and could not eat the Passover meal. <sup>[Note: This was because they regarded a Gentile house as defiling]</sup>.
So, Pilate went outside to them and asked, “What charge do you bring against this man?”
The Jewish authorities answered Pilate, “If this man were not guilty of wrongdoing, we would not have turned him over to you.”
So, Pilate said to them, “Take him yourselves and judge him according to your <sup>[own]</sup> law.” <sup>[But]</sup> the Jews replied to him, “It is not permissible for us to put anyone to death.”

<sup>[They said this]</sup> so that the words Jesus had spoken would be fulfilled, regarding the kind of death He would die. <sup>[Note: Jesus had predicted he would be “lifted up,” indicating a Roman crucifixion. See 12:32-33]</sup>.
So, Pilate went back into his headquarters and called for Jesus. He said to Him, “Are you the King of the Jews?”
Jesus answered, “Are you saying this of your own accord, or did other people tell you about me?”
Pilate answered, “Am I a Jew? Your own people and the leading priests turned you over to me. What have you done?”

Jesus answered him; “My kingdom does not belong to this world. If it did, then my servants <sup>[i.e., disciples]</sup> would fight to keep me from being turned over to the Jews. But now my kingdom does not belong here.”. <sup>[i.e., Jesus’ kingdom was not based on physical, but spiritual principles]</sup>

So, Pilate said to Him, “Are you a king, then?” Jesus answered, “You are <sup>[correct in]</sup> calling me a ‘king.’ This was the <sup>[very]</sup> purpose for which I was born and came into the world, that I should testify about the truth. Every person who wants <sup>[to know and obey]</sup> the truth listens to me.”
Pilate replied to Him, “What is truth?” After saying this, Pilate again went outside to the Jews and said to them, “I do not find him guilty of any crime.
But since you <sup>[Jews]</sup> have a custom that I should release to you one <sup>[prisoner]</sup> during the Passover Festival, do you want me to release to you the King of the Jews?”
They shouted out again, “<sup>[No]</sup>, not this man. <sup>[We want]</sup> Barabbas!” <sup>[Note: Ironically, Barabbas’ name means “son of the father”]</sup>. Now Barabbas was a robber.
So, Pilate then took Jesus and had Him flogged.
The soldiers wove thorny twigs into a crown and placed it on His head and dressed Him in a purple robe.
Then they went <sup>[up]</sup> to Him and said, “Hey! King of the Jews!” and struck Him <sup>[in the face with their hands]</sup>.
Now Pilate went outside again and said to the Jewish authorities, “Look, I am bringing him out here to you, so you can know that I do not find him guilty of any crime.”
So, Jesus came outside wearing the crown of thorns and the purple robe. Then Pilate said to the <sup>[assembled]</sup> Jewish authorities, “Look, <sup>[here is]</sup> the man!”

Then, when the leading priests and the <sup>[Temple]</sup> guards saw Him, they shouted out, “Crucify him, crucify him!” Pilate responded by saying, “Take him yourselves and crucify him, because I do not find him guilty of any crime.”
The Jews replied, “We have a law which requires that he ought to die because he claimed to be the Son of God.”
So, when Pilate heard this statement, he was <sup>[even]</sup> more afraid
and went back into the headquarters again and said to Jesus, “Where did you come from?” But Jesus did not answer him.
Then Pilate said to Him, “Are you not going to speak to me? Do you not know that I have the authority to release you or to crucify you?”
Jesus answered him, “You would not have any authority over me unless it were given to you from above <sup>[i.e., from God]</sup>. So, the person who turned me over to you is guilty of a worse sin.”

After <sup>[hearing]</sup> this, Pilate tried to release Jesus, but the Jews shouted out, “If you release this man, you are no friend of Caesar’s. Everyone who claims to be a king <sup>[i.e., as they had accused Jesus of doing. See 18:37]</sup> is in opposition to Caesar.”

Then when Pilate heard <sup>[them say]</sup> this, he brought Jesus outside <sup>[again]</sup> and sat down on the judge’s bench at a place called The Stone Pavement, but in the Hebrew language is called Gabbatha.

Now it was about six o’clock in the morning <sup>[Note: This was according to Roman time, but if Jewish time were meant, it would have been 12 noon]</sup> on the Day of Preparation for the Passover Festival. <sup>[Note: This would be the day before the Passover Festival]</sup>. And Pilate said to the Jews, “Look, <sup>[here is]</sup> your king!”

Then they shouted out, “Take him away; take him away and crucify him!” Pilate replied to them, “Do you want me to crucify your king?” The leading priests answered, “We do not have any <sup>[other]</sup> king, except Caesar.”
Finally, Pilate turned Jesus over to them to be crucified.

So, they took Jesus <sup>[away]</sup> and He went out <sup>[of the city]</sup> carrying the cross by Himself to a place called The Place of the Skull, <sup>[also]</sup> called Golgotha in the Hebrew language <sup>[and Calvary in Latin]</sup>.
There they crucified Him, along with two other men, one on each side with Jesus in the middle.
Then Pilate had an inscription written and placed on the cross <sup>[i.e., on the upright portion, above His head]</sup>. It read, “Jesus from Nazareth, the King of the Jews.”
Many of the Jews read this notice, for the place where Jesus was crucified was near the city <sup>[of Jerusalem]</sup>. It was written in the Hebrew, Latin and Greek languages.
But the Jewish leading priests said to Pilate, “Do not write ‘The King of the Jews,’ but ‘He said, I am the King of the Jews.’”
Pilate replied, “What I have written I have written.” <sup>[i.e., “I am not going to change it.”]</sup>

So, when the <sup>[Roman]</sup> soldiers had crucified Jesus, they took His clothing and divided them into four parts, one for each soldier. They also took His seamless coat <sup>[i.e., which was left over]</sup>, woven from top <sup>[to bottom]</sup> in one piece,

and said to one another, “Let us not tear it <sup>[i.e., because of its great value]</sup>, but gamble to see who gets it.” <sup>[This was]</sup> so that the Scripture would be fulfilled, which said <sup>[Psa. 22:18]</sup>, “They divided my clothing between themselves, and they gambled for my clothing.” So, this is what the soldiers did.

Now standing near Jesus’ cross were His mother; His mother’s sister <sup>[i.e., Salome, the mother of James and John, the sons of Zebedee. See Matt. 27:55; Mark 15:40]</sup>; Mary the wife of Clopas <sup>[i.e., the mother of James and Joseph. See Matt. 27:55]</sup>; and Mary, from Magdala.
So, when Jesus saw His mother standing beside the disciple He loved <sup>[i.e., probably the apostle John]</sup>, He said to her, “<sup>[My dear]</sup> woman, look, <sup>[here is]</sup> your son!”
Then He said to the disciple, “Look, <sup>[here is]</sup> your mother!” And from that time onward, this disciple took Jesus’ mother to <sup>[live in]</sup> his own home.
Now Jesus knew that everything had been completed <sup>[i.e., relating to His crucifixion]</sup>, so then He said, in order to fulfill Scripture <sup>[Psa. 69:21]</sup>, “I am thirsty.”
A bottle full of sour wine was sitting nearby so someone put a sponge full of the wine on a hyssop plant stalk and lifted it up to Jesus’ mouth.
After drinking some of the sour wine, Jesus said, “It is completed,” <sup>[i.e., His work of dying for the sins of mankind]</sup> and gave up His spirit <sup>[i.e., to God. See Luke 23:46]</sup>.

Now it was the Day of Preparation <sup>[Note: This was the day before the Passover Festival began, and fell on a Friday that year]</sup>, and the Jews did not permit bodies to remain on a cross over the Sabbath day. (Besides, that Sabbath day was a special day <sup>[i.e., since it was both the Passover and the regular weekly Sabbath, as well]</sup>). So, they asked Pilate to have Jesus’ and the criminals’ legs broken <sup>[i.e., to speed up their deaths]</sup> and then have their bodies taken away.
So, the soldiers went and broke the legs of the first <sup>[criminal]</sup> and then of the other man crucified with Jesus.
But when they came to Jesus and saw that He was already dead, they did not break His legs.
However, one of the soldiers pierced Jesus’ side with a spear, and immediately blood and water flowed out.
And the person who saw this happen is giving a true testimony about it. He knows it is true <sup>[and is giving it]</sup> so that you will believe <sup>[it, also]</sup>.
For these things happened so that the Scripture would be fulfilled <sup>[Ex. 12:46]</sup>, “Not a <sup>[single]</sup> bone of his will be broken.”
And another Scripture says <sup>[Zech. 12:10]</sup>, “People will look at him whom they <sup>[had]</sup> pierced.”

After this happened, one of Jesus’ disciples, named Joseph from Arimathaea, asked Pilate (but secretly, for fear of the Jews) if he could remove Jesus’ body <sup>[from the cross]</sup>. Pilate granted him permission, so he went and removed the body from the cross.

Nicodemus, the man who first came to Jesus one night, also went with him <sup>[i.e., to claim Jesus’ body]</sup>. He brought about a seventy-five pound mixture of myrrh and aloes <sup>[i.e., aromatic spices used for embalming]</sup>.
So, they took Jesus’ body and wrapped it in linen cloths, with spices, according to customary Jewish burial practices.

Now there was an <sup>[olive]</sup> orchard at the place where Jesus was crucified. And in the orchard there was a new grave site <sup>[i.e., a cave-like tomb]</sup> where no one had ever been buried.

So, they placed Jesus’ body there, for it was the Day of Preparation and that was the closest grave site. <sup>[Note: Since this was the day before the Passover Festival, and the next day was also the regular weekly Sabbath, they had to bury the body before sundown to comply with Jewish restrictions against working on a Sabbath day]</sup>.

Now early <sup>[in the morning]</sup>, while it was still dark on the first day of the week <sup>[i.e., Sunday]</sup>, Mary from Magdala went to the <sup>[cave-like]</sup> tomb and saw the stone <sup>[had been]</sup> removed from <sup>[the entrance of]</sup> the tomb.

So, she ran and came to Simon Peter and to the other disciple, whom Jesus loved <sup>[i.e., probably the apostle John]</sup>, and said to them, “They have taken the Lord out of the tomb and we do not know where they have put Him.”
So, Peter and the other disciple left <sup>[hurriedly]</sup> and went to the grave site.
The two of them ran together, but the other disciple outran Peter and arrived at the grave site first.

He stooped down and looked inside <sup>[the cave-like tomb]</sup> and saw the linen cloths lying there <sup>[i.e., these were wrappings used to hold embalming spices against the body]</sup>, but he did not go in.
Then Simon Peter, who had been following him, also arrived and went <sup>[straight]</sup> into the cave-like tomb and saw the linen cloths lying there.
Now the cloth that had been around Jesus’ head was not lying with the <sup>[other]</sup> linen cloths, but was rolled up by itself.

Finally, the other disciple, who had arrived at the cave-like tomb first, went on into it. And <sup>[when]</sup> he saw <sup>[the grave clothes, but no body]</sup>, he believed <sup>[in Jesus’ resurrection]</sup>.
But these disciples still did not understand the Scripture that <sup>[predicted]</sup> Jesus must rise again from the dead.
So, the disciples went back home again,
but Mary <sup>[from Magdala]</sup> was standing outside of the cave-like tomb crying. While she was <sup>[still]</sup> crying she stooped down to look in the tomb,
and there she saw two angels <sup>[dressed]</sup> in white <sup>[clothing]</sup> sitting where Jesus’ body had lain, one at His head and one at His feet.
They said to Mary, “<sup>[My dear]</sup> woman, why are you crying?” She answered, “They have taken my Lord away, and I do not know where they have put Him.”
After she said this, she turned around and saw Jesus standing there, but did not know it was Him.

<sup>[Then]</sup> Jesus said to her, “<sup>[My dear]</sup> woman, why are you crying? Who are you looking for?” Assuming He was the grounds keeper, she said to Him, “Sir, if you have carried Him away from here, tell me where you laid Him, and I will go and get Him.”
<sup>[Then]</sup> Jesus said to her, “Mary!” She turned toward Him and replied in the Hebrew language, “Rabboni” which means “Teacher.”

Jesus said to her, “Do not hold on to me, for I have not ascended to the Father yet <sup>[Note: Apparently Jesus was discouraging Mary from embracing Him in worship as was done in Matt. 28:9, and thereby causing a delay of when the rest of the disciples would learn of His resurrection]</sup>, but go to my brothers and tell them <sup>[I said]</sup> ‘I am <sup>[about]</sup> to ascend to my Father and to your Father, and to my God and your God.’”
So, Mary from Magdala went and told the disciples, “I have seen the Lord,” and that He had said these things to her. <sup>[See Matt. 28:7-10]</sup>.

So, when it was evening on that day, <sup>[which was]</sup> the first day of the week <sup>[i.e., Sunday]</sup> and the doors <sup>[of someone’s house]</sup>, where the disciples were gathered, were closed <sup>[i.e., probably locked]</sup> for fear of the Jewish authorities, Jesus came and stood among them. <sup>[Note: Probably Jesus passed through the closed door miraculously]</sup>. He said to them, “May there be peace in your hearts.”
After saying this, Jesus showed them His hands and His side, so they rejoiced when they saw the Lord <sup>[i.e., now convinced that He had indeed risen from the dead]</sup>.
Jesus said to them again, “May there be peace in your hearts. Just as the Father has sent me, so I am sending you.”

Upon saying this, Jesus breathed on the disciples and said to them, “<sup>[Be willing to]</sup> receive the Holy Spirit.” <sup>[Note: This “breathing” served as a pledge of the Holy Spirit which actually came on these apostles a few weeks later. See Acts 1:5-8; 2:4]</sup>.

Whosever sins you forgive, they will be forgiven <sup>[by God]</sup>. And whosever sins you do not forgive, they will not be forgiven <sup>[by God]</sup>.” <sup>[Note: Actual forgiveness would result only when people personally responded, by faith, to the Gospel message. See Acts 2:37-38]</sup>.
But one of the twelve apostles, named Thomas, <sup>[and also]</sup> called “The Twin,” was not with them when Jesus came.

So, the other disciples told him, “We have seen the Lord.” But he replied to them, “I will not believe <sup>[it]</sup> unless I see the scars of the nails in His hands, and put my finger on the nail scars, and put my hand in His side.”

Eight days later <sup>[i.e., the following Sunday]</sup>, Jesus’ disciples were inside <sup>[the house]</sup> again, <sup>[but this time]</sup> Thomas was with them. The doors were <sup>[still]</sup> closed when Jesus <sup>[suddenly]</sup> stood among them <sup>[See note on verse 19]</sup> and said, “May there be peace in your hearts.”

Then He said to Thomas, “Put your finger here <sup>[i.e., to touch the nail scars]</sup> and look at my hands. Reach out your hand and put it into my side <sup>[i.e., where the spear had pierced Him]</sup>. Stop doubting and believe <sup>[that it is really I]</sup>.”
Thomas replied to Him, “My Lord and my God.”
Jesus said to him, “Have you believed <sup>[that I am alive]</sup> because you have seen me? Those who have not seen me and yet believe are <sup>[even more]</sup> blessed.”
Jesus performed many other <sup>[miraculous]</sup> signs in front of His disciples which are not recorded in this book <sup>[i.e., in the Gospel of John]</sup>.

But these <sup>[signs]</sup> are written down so that you will believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that when you believe <sup>[this]</sup>, you will have <sup>[never ending]</sup> life in His name <sup>[i.e., through His person and work]</sup>.
After these things happened Jesus showed Himself to His disciples again at Lake Tiberias. <sup>[Note: This was another name for Lake Galilee]</sup>. Here is the way He did it.

Simon Peter; Thomas, called “The Twin;” Nathaniel from Cana in Galilee; the sons of Zebedee <sup>[i.e., James and John]</sup> and two other disciples of Jesus were <sup>[all]</sup> together <sup>[at the lake]</sup>.

Simon Peter said to <sup>[the rest of]</sup> them, “I am going fishing.” The others replied to him, “We will go with you, too.” <sup>[So]</sup>, they went out and got into their boat but did not catch anything that night.
Now as the day was dawning Jesus was <sup>[seen]</sup> standing on the shore, but the disciples did not know it was Jesus. <sup>[Note: Probably because it was not light enough yet]</sup>.
So, Jesus said to them, “Young fellows, you have not <sup>[caught]</sup> any fish yet, have you?” They answered Him, “No, <sup>[we have not]</sup>.”

Then He said to them, “Throw your net over the right side of the boat and you will catch some <sup>[fish]</sup>.” So, they threw <sup>[the net out]</sup> but they were not able to pull it in because of so many fish.

So, that disciple whom Jesus loved <sup>[i.e., probably the apostle John]</sup> said to Peter, “It is the Lord!” When Simon Peter heard it was the Lord, he wrapped his outer robe around him, (for he <sup>[had taken it off and]</sup> was wearing only his underclothing) and jumped into the lake.
But the other disciples came <sup>[to shore]</sup> in their little boat, dragging their net full of fish (for they were only about a hundred yards from land).
So, when they got to shore, they saw a charcoal fire burning there with fish <sup>[cooking]</sup> on it and <sup>[some]</sup> bread.
Jesus said to them, “Bring <sup>[me]</sup> some of the fish you just caught.”

Then Simon Peter boarded <sup>[the boat]</sup> and dragged the net full of large fish to shore. And even though there were one hundred fifty-three <sup>[fish]</sup>, the net did not tear from such a large number.
Jesus said to them, “Come and have breakfast.” <sup>[But]</sup> none of the disciples dared to ask Him, “Who are you?” because they <sup>[already]</sup> knew it was the Lord.
<sup>[So]</sup>, Jesus went <sup>[over to them]</sup> and took the bread and fish and gave it to them.
This was then the third time that Jesus was made known to His disciples after He arose from the dead.

So, after they had eaten, Jesus said to Simon Peter, “Simon, son of John, do you really love me more than these <sup>[other disciples do]</sup>?” Peter answered Him, “Yes, Lord, you know that I love you.” <sup>[Note: Since two different Greek words for “love” are used in this conversation, some scholars see a distinction in their meaning. See Butler, Vol. II, pp. 453-457 for a discussion of these views]</sup>. Jesus replied to him, “<sup>[Then]</sup> feed my lambs.”

Jesus asked him a second time, “Simon, son of John; do you <sup>[really]</sup> love me?” Peter answered Him, “Yes, Lord, you know that I love you.” Jesus replied to him, “<sup>[Then]</sup> take care of my sheep.”

Then Jesus asked him a third time, “Simon, son of John, do you <sup>[truly]</sup> love me?” Peter was sad because Jesus had asked him a third time, “Do you <sup>[even]</sup> love me?” So, he said to Jesus, “Lord, you know all things. You <sup>[surely]</sup> know that I love you.” Jesus replied to him, “<sup>[Then]</sup> feed my sheep.

Truly, truly, I tell you <sup>[Peter]</sup>, when you were young you used to fasten your belt around you <sup>[i.e., to get ready]</sup> and travel wherever you wanted to go. But when you are old, you will stretch out your hands and someone else will tie you up and take you <sup>[away]</sup> to where you do not want to go.”

Now Jesus said this to indicate the kind of death Peter would experience in honoring God. <sup>[Note: Some see the “stretching out your hands,” verse 18, as a reference to crucifixion]</sup>. And after saying this, Jesus said to him, “Be my follower.”

Peter turned around and saw the disciple whom Jesus loved <sup>[i.e., probably the apostle John]</sup> following <sup>[along behind him]</sup>. This was the same disciple who had leaned over close to Jesus at the <sup>[Passover]</sup> meal and asked Him, “Lord, who is it that will turn you over <sup>[to the Jewish authorities]</sup>?”
<sup>[When]</sup> Peter saw this disciple, he said to Jesus, “Lord, what will happen to this man?”
Jesus said to him, “If I want him to remain <sup>[alive]</sup> until I return, what <sup>[business]</sup> is that of yours? You be my follower.”

So, a rumor began circulating among the brothers that this disciple would not die. Yet Jesus did not tell him he would not die, but <sup>[He said]</sup>, “If I want him to remain <sup>[alive]</sup> until I return, what <sup>[business]</sup> is that of yours?”
This is the disciple who is giving testimony about these things and has written them down, and we know his testimony is true.
And Jesus did many other things; if every one of them were written down, I do not think the world would hold <sup>[all]</sup> the books that would be written.
<sup>[Dear]</sup> Theophilus, in my former letter <sup>[i.e., the Gospel of Luke]</sup> I wrote to you concerning what all Jesus did and taught since the beginning <sup>[of His ministry]</sup>,

up until the day He returned to heaven. <sup>[Just before that time]</sup> He had given <sup>[certain]</sup> instructions to His <sup>[specially]</sup> selected apostles through <sup>[the direction of]</sup> the Holy Spirit.

Now, following His death on the cross <sup>[and His resurrection]</sup>, Jesus appeared alive <sup>[to His apostles]</sup> for forty days, and demonstrated by many convincing proofs <sup>[that He had been raised bodily from the dead]</sup>. <sup>[During that time]</sup> He also spoke to them about the <sup>[coming]</sup> kingdom of God.

<sup>[One day, as He was]</sup> gathered with these apostles, He urged them not to leave Jerusalem, but to wait there until they received what the Father had promised them. (Jesus had previously discussed this with them). <sup>[See Luke 24:49]</sup>.

<sup>[Jesus then said,]</sup> “For John immersed you <sup>[men]</sup> in water but <sup>[this time]</sup> you will be immersed in the Holy Spirit, and <sup>[it will happen]</sup> in just a few more days.” <sup>[See Matt. 3:11]</sup>.
When the apostles had come together, they asked <sup>[Jesus]</sup>, “Lord, are you ready to restore the kingdom <sup>[of God]</sup> to <sup>[the nation of]</sup> Israel <sup>[at this time]</sup>?”

Jesus replied, “You are not permitted to know the <sup>[specific]</sup> time or date <sup>[when this will happen]</sup>. For the Father has appointed it <sup>[to happen]</sup> when He determines <sup>[the right time]</sup>.

But, you apostles will receive power when the Holy Spirit descends on you and <sup>[then]</sup> you will become My witnesses <sup>[i.e., to tell what you know]</sup> in Jerusalem, in <sup>[the countries of]</sup> Judea and Samaria, and <sup>[even]</sup> to the distant regions of the earth.”
And when Jesus had said these things, just as the apostles were watching <sup>[Him]</sup>, He was taken up by a cloud <sup>[and disappeared]</sup> out of their sight.
And while they gazed up into the sky as He ascended, suddenly two men wearing white clothing appeared beside them
and said, “You men from Galilee, why are you standing there looking up at the sky? This Jesus who was received up from your presence into the sky will return in the same way you saw Him go there.”
Then the apostles returned to Jerusalem from the place called “Mount of Olives,” which was about three-fourths of a mile from Jerusalem.

Upon arriving at the house where they were staying, they went upstairs. <sup>[Those present were]</sup>: Peter, John, James, Andrew, Philip, Thomas, Bartholomew, Matthew, James, the son of Alpheus, Simon the Zealous <sup>[one]</sup> and Judas, the son of James. <sup>[Note: Judas Iscariot, the twelfth apostle, had already committed suicide]</sup>.
These men continued in united prayer <sup>[for the kingdom to come (?); See 1:6; Matt. 6:10]</sup> along with <sup>[certain]</sup> women, Mary the mother of Jesus and His <sup>[half-]</sup> brothers.
And during this time Peter stood up to speak to a gathering of about one hundred twenty persons.
<sup>[He said]</sup>, “Brothers, it was necessary that the Scriptures be fulfilled which the Holy Spirit spoke through King David about Judas, who guided the soldiers who came to arrest Jesus.
For he was one of our number and shared with us <sup>[in the responsibility of]</sup> this ministry.”

(Now this man <sup>[Judas]</sup> paid for <sup>[in a sense]</sup> a <sup>[burial]</sup> field with the reward money he had received for his sinful act <sup>[of betraying Jesus, See Matt. 27:3-10]</sup>. <sup>[Then, some time after Judas hanged himself, See Matt. 27:5]</sup> he fell down headlong, <sup>[his swollen body]</sup> bursting so that his intestines gushed out.

This incident became known to everyone who lived in Jerusalem so that the <sup>[burial]</sup> field purchased with the reward money became known, in their own <sup>[i.e., the Jewish]</sup> language, as “Akeldama” <sup>[an Aramaic word]</sup>, which means “Field of Blood.”)

“For it was written <sup>[about Judas]</sup> in the book of Psalms <sup>[69:25]</sup>: ‘Let the place where he lives become deserted, with no one living there’ and <sup>[Psa. 109:8]</sup> ‘Let someone else fill his ministry.’
The person <sup>[to replace Judas]</sup> would have to be one of those who accompanied us <sup>[disciples]</sup> during the time the Lord traveled around with us,

from His immersion by John <sup>[the Immerser]</sup> until He was received up from us <sup>[in a cloud]</sup>. Of these people <sup>[we must choose]</sup> someone to become a witness with us <sup>[i.e., to tell people]</sup> of Jesus' resurrection <sup>[from the dead]</sup>.”
And so they presented two men <sup>[for consideration]</sup>: Joseph, called Barsabbas (and also called Justus) and Matthias.
Then they prayed, “Lord, you know the inner thoughts of all men, so show us which one of these two men should be selected
to replace Judas in this ministry and this group of apostles, from which he fell away to go to where he belonged” <sup>[i.e., to the place of punishment for unrepentant sinners]</sup>.
So, they drew lots <sup>[i.e., devices used for making random choices]</sup> and the lot <sup>[i.e., the choice]</sup> favored Matthias, so he was added to the other eleven apostles.

And when the day of Pentecost came <sup>[Note: This was a Jewish festival held fifty days following the Passover Festival. Lev. 23:15-21]</sup>, they <sup>[i.e., the twelve apostles]</sup> were all gathered together in one place <sup>[i.e., probably the Temple area]</sup>.
Suddenly a rushing sound, like a fierce wind blowing, came from the sky above them, and <sup>[its noise]</sup> filled the entire house where they were sitting.
Then they saw what appeared to be flames of fire shooting down and landing on each one of them.

And they <sup>[i.e., the apostles]</sup> were all filled with <sup>[the power of]</sup> the Holy Spirit and began to speak <sup>[“the mighty accomplishments of God,” See verse 11]</sup> in different languages, as the Holy Spirit gave them the ability.
Now some devoted Jews from every nation of the world were visiting Jerusalem <sup>[for the Pentecost festival]</sup>.

And when they heard the noise <sup>[of the blowing wind]</sup> a large number of them assembled <sup>[around the apostles]</sup> and were puzzled over how they could hear them speaking in their native languages.
Being so amazed and astounded <sup>[at some­thing so unusual happening]</sup>, they exclaimed, “Look! are not all these men who are speaking from <sup>[the country of]</sup> Galilee?
How is it then, that we are hearing them speak in our own native languages:
Parthians, Medes, Elamites, people from Mesopotamia, Judea, Cappodocia, Pontus, Asia,
Phrygia, Pamphylia, Egypt and areas of Libya around Cyrene, Jewish and proselyte travelers from Rome,
Cretans and Arabians. How can it be that we hear <sup>[these apostles]</sup> speaking about the mighty accomplishments of God in the language of our national origin?”
And they were amazed and perplexed <sup>[over this]</sup> and began saying to one another, “What does all this mean?”
But others made fun of them, saying, “Oh, they are just drunk <sup>[i.e., full of new wine]</sup>.”
