But since we are being judged, we are <sup>[also]</sup> being disciplined by the Lord, so that we do not have to be condemned along with the world.
So, my brothers, when you assemble for a meal, wait for each other <sup>[i.e., so some will not be finished before others. See verse 21]</sup>.

If anyone is <sup>[too]</sup> hungry <sup>[i.e., to wait]</sup>, he should eat at home, so that your assembly does not result in judgment <sup>[falling on some of you. See verse 30]</sup>. And I will tend to the remaining matters when I come <sup>[to you]</sup>.
Now concerning spiritual <sup>[i.e., supernatural]</sup> gifts, brothers, I want you to be <sup>[fully]</sup> knowledgeable.

You people know that when you were <sup>[still unconverted]</sup> Gentiles, you were led astray to <sup>[worship]</sup> lifeless idols, however you were led <sup>[i.e., wherever you turned, you were being led into idolatrous practices]</sup>

Therefore, I am telling you that no one who speaks under the influence of the Holy Spirit of God says, “Jesus is cursed!” And no one can say, “Jesus is Lord,” except under the influence of the Holy Spirit.
Now there are a variety of <sup>[spiritual]</sup> gifts, but <sup>[they are given by]</sup> the same Holy Spirit.
And there are a variety of services, but <sup>[they are rendered to]</sup> the same Lord.
And there are a variety of <sup>[Christian]</sup> activities, but the same God provides the power for everyone to do all of them <sup>[i.e., it is God who empowers every person who has a gift]</sup>.
But the evidence of the Holy Spirit’s <sup>[power]</sup> is given to each person for the good of everyone.

For one person is given the <sup>[supernatural]</sup> message of wisdom through the Holy Spirit; another person is given the <sup>[supernatural]</sup> message of knowledge by means of the same Holy Spirit.

Still another person is given <sup>[supernatural]</sup> faith <sup>[See 13:2]</sup> by the same Holy Spirit; yet another person is given gifts <sup>[providing the ability]</sup> to perform <sup>[supernatural]</sup> healings by that one Holy Spirit.

Another <sup>[is given]</sup> the ability to perform miracles; and another <sup>[is given]</sup> the ability to prophesy <sup>[supernaturally]</sup>; and another <sup>[is given]</sup> the ability to speak in different languages <sup>[supernaturally]</sup>; and still another <sup>[is given]</sup> the ability to interpret those languages <sup>[supernaturally]</sup>.
Now all of these <sup>[supernatural gifts]</sup> are the work of one and the same Holy Spirit, who distributes them to each person individually as He desires.

For <sup>[just]</sup> as the human body is one <sup>[unit]</sup>, made up of many parts, and all the many parts make up a single body, so it is with Christ. <sup>[Note: Just as a human body is a single unit and Christ is a single being, so the members of His body, the church, constitute one body. See verses 27-28]</sup>.

For we were all immersed by means of the one Holy Spirit into one body <sup>[i.e., the church]</sup>, whether <sup>[we were]</sup> Jews or Greeks <sup>[i.e., Gentiles]</sup>, whether slaves or free people, and were all given the one <sup>[and the same]</sup> Holy Spirit to drink. <sup>[Note: This probably refers to the indwelling of the Holy Spirit, given to all immersed believers. See John 7:37-39; Acts 2:38]</sup>.
For the human body is not made up of <sup>[only]</sup> one part, but of many parts.
If the foot should say, “I am not part of the body because I am not the hand,” that does not mean it is not part of the body <sup>[does it?]</sup>.
And if the ear should say, “I am not part of the body because I am not the eye,” that does not mean it is not part of the body <sup>[does it?]</sup>
If the whole body were an eye, how could it hear? If the whole body were an ear, how could it smell?
But the way it is, God has placed each individual part in the human body just as it pleased Him.
And if all the parts were one part, how could there be a <sup>[whole]</sup> body?
But the way it is, there are many parts, but one body.
And the eye cannot say to the hand, “I do not need you,” or again, the head <sup>[cannot say]</sup> to the feet, “I do not need you.”
On the contrary, those parts of the body which seem to be weaker <sup>[i.e., delicate organs like the heart, lungs, kidneys, etc.]</sup> are necessary <sup>[for life]</sup>.

And those parts of the body which we consider less honorable <sup>[i.e., probably female breasts, buttocks, etc.]</sup>, we clothe with greater honor. <sup>[Note: The Greek word for “clothe” here refers to clothing elsewhere in the New Testament]</sup>. And our unpresentable parts <sup>[i.e., probably sex organs]</sup> need to be more presentable <sup>[i.e., by being covered up with modest clothing]</sup>,

whereas, our presentable parts <sup>[i.e., probably face and hands]</sup> do not need <sup>[covering up]</sup>. But God has put the body together <sup>[in such a way as]</sup> to give greater honor to that part which lacked it,

so that there would not be any division in the body <sup>[Note: Paul here leaves the analogy of the physical body and makes application to the spiritual body, the church]</sup>, but that its parts should have equal concern for each other.

So, if one part <sup>[i.e., one Christian]</sup> suffers, then all the parts <sup>[i.e., the other Christians]</sup> should suffer with it. Or, if one part is honored, all the <sup>[other]</sup> parts should rejoice with it.
Now you people are the <sup>[spiritual]</sup> body of Christ and each one of you is an individual part of it.

Now God has placed some <sup>[ministries]</sup> in the church <sup>[as follows]</sup>: First apostles, secondly prophets, thirdly teachers, then <sup>[those able to perform]</sup> miracles, then gifts <sup>[enabling people]</sup> to perform <sup>[supernatural]</sup> healings, <sup>[those able]</sup> to help the needy, <sup>[wise]</sup> administrators, <sup>[and]</sup> those able to speak in different languages.
Are all <sup>[ministers in the church]</sup> apostles? Are all prophets? Are all teachers? Do all perform miracles?
Do all have gifts <sup>[enabling them]</sup> to perform <sup>[supernatural]</sup> healings? Do all speak in languages? Do all interpret <sup>[those languages]</sup>?

<sup>[Obviously not]</sup>, but you should eagerly desire the more important gifts. And now I will show you a much better way <sup>[i.e., a better way to serve one another in the church than using supernatural gifts]</sup>:

If I have the ability to speak in the languages of human beings and angels <sup>[Note: Perhaps the idea is supernaturally and eloquently]</sup>, but do not have love <sup>[for people]</sup>, I have become <sup>[no more than]</sup> a noisy gong or a clanging cymbal.

And if I have the <sup>[spiritual]</sup> gift of prophecy, and can understand all <sup>[of God’s]</sup> secret purposes, and have all knowledge; and if I have all <sup>[the supernatural]</sup> faith necessary to relocate mountains <sup>[See Matt. 17:20]</sup>, but do not have love <sup>[for people]</sup>, I am nothing.

And if I donate all my possessions to feed poor people, and if I surrender my body to be burned <sup>[Note: This probably refers to becoming a martyr, but some manuscripts read, “that I may be able to boast”]</sup>, but do not have love <sup>[for people]</sup>, it is no benefit to me.

<sup>[Note: The following traits should be understood in the context of love for people]</sup>. Love is patient <sup>[with people]</sup>, and is kind <sup>[to them]</sup>; love does not envy <sup>[what people are or have]</sup>; love does not boast <sup>[of being superior to others]</sup>, it is not arrogant <sup>[in dealing with people]</sup>.

<sup>[Love]</sup> does not act improperly <sup>[toward people]</sup>; it does not insist on having its own way <sup>[at the expense of others]</sup>; it is not easily irritated <sup>[by others]</sup>; it does not keep track of the wrongs <sup>[done by others]</sup>.
<sup>[Love]</sup> is not glad about the evil <sup>[that people do]</sup>, but is glad about the truth <sup>[i.e., the good in their lives]</sup>.

<sup>[Love]</sup> conceals all <sup>[people’s]</sup> faults; it believes <sup>[the best about]</sup> all people; it hopes <sup>[for the best in]</sup> all people; it endures <sup>[ill treatment from]</sup> all people.

Love will never cease. But though there are <sup>[now supernatural gifts of]</sup> prophecies, <sup>[someday]</sup> they will be abolished <sup>[i.e., such gifts will cease to be exercised]</sup>. Though there are <sup>[now supernatural gifts of]</sup> languages, <sup>[someday]</sup> they will stop <sup>[i.e., such gifts will cease to be exercised]</sup>. Though there is <sup>[now the supernatural gift of]</sup> knowledge, <sup>[someday]</sup> it will be abolished <sup>[i.e., this gift will cease to be exercised]</sup>
For <sup>[now]</sup> we have partial knowledge and partial prophetic messages,

but when that which is complete comes <sup>[i.e., the full revelation of God’s will, contained in the New Testament Scriptures]</sup>, then the partial will be abolished <sup>[i.e., the supernatural gifts, which provided only part of the revealed will of God, will cease to be exercised]</sup>.

When I was a child I spoke, thought and reasoned like a child. But now that I have become a man, I have put away childish things. <sup>[Note: Paul uses an analogy to illustrate that the church made use of the supernatural gifts in its infancy, but that these would no longer be needed when it matured]</sup>.

For now <sup>[i.e., during the church’s infant state]</sup> we see in a mirror <sup>[Note: God’s word is elsewhere likened to a mirror. See James 1:22-25]</sup> indistinctly <sup>[i.e., because of having only partial knowledge and prophecy]</sup>, but then <sup>[i.e., when the church matures and has the completed Scriptures]</sup>, we will see face to face <sup>[i.e., clearly]</sup>. Now I have <sup>[only]</sup> partial knowledge, but then I will have full knowledge, just as <sup>[God has]</sup> full knowledge of me.
But now <sup>[i.e., since the temporary gifts are to pass away]</sup>, these three things remain: faith, hope and love; and the most important of them is love.
You should make loving <sup>[others]</sup> your aim, yet eagerly desire <sup>[to possess]</sup> spiritual gifts, and especially <sup>[the gift of]</sup> prophecy.

For the person who speaks in a language <sup>[supernaturally]</sup> does not speak to people, but to God, for no one can understand him <sup>[i.e., unless there is an interpreter or it is the person’s native language]</sup>; he speaks <sup>[previously]</sup> unrevealed truths under the influence of the Holy Spirit <sup>[or “in his spirit”]</sup>. <sup>[Note: For a correct understanding of this chapter, it must be kept in mind that Paul is addressing a misuse of the gift of speaking in languages supernaturally. The Corinthians were doing this in the wrong way and for the wrong purpose]</sup>.
But the person who prophesies speaks <sup>[God’s message]</sup> to people to build them up <sup>[spiritually]</sup>, encourage them and comfort them.
The person who speaks in a language <sup>[supernaturally]</sup>, builds up <sup>[only]</sup> himself, but the one who prophesies builds up the church.

Now I would like all of you to speak in languages <sup>[supernaturally]</sup>, but I would rather have you prophecy. For the person who prophesies is more important <sup>[i.e., to the church]</sup> than those who speak in languages <sup>[supernaturally]</sup>, unless that person interprets <sup>[the languages]</sup>, so the church can be built up <sup>[spiritually]</sup>.

But now, brothers, if I come to you speaking in languages <sup>[supernaturally]</sup>, how will I benefit you unless my speaking consists of a revelation <sup>[from God]</sup>, or a <sup>[message of]</sup> knowledge <sup>[See 12:8]</sup>, or a prophecy, or a teaching? <sup>[i.e., the message must be intelligible to be beneficial]</sup>.

<sup>[This is true]</sup> even of inanimate objects which make sounds, such as a flute or harp. If they do not make distinguishable sounds, how will anyone know what tune <sup>[or signal]</sup> is being played?
For if the trumpet gives an indistinct sound, who will get ready for a battle?
So, unless you people also speak clearly with your tongue, how will your speaking be understood? For it will be as though you were speaking into the air.
There are, no doubt, many kinds of sounds <sup>[i.e., languages]</sup> in the world, and none of them is meaningless.

So, if I do not know the meaning of the sound <sup>[i.e., language]</sup>, I will <sup>[appear]</sup> as a foreigner to the person speaking <sup>[that language to me]</sup>. And the person speaking <sup>[to me]</sup> will <sup>[appear]</sup> as a foreigner to me.
So, you people also, since you are eager for <sup>[supernatural]</sup> spiritual gifts, should look for ways to excel in building up the church.
Therefore, the person who speaks in a language <sup>[supernaturally]</sup> should pray <sup>[i.e., in advance]</sup> for the ability to interpret it <sup>[as well]</sup>.

For if I pray in a language <sup>[supernaturally]</sup>, <sup>[it is]</sup> my spirit that is praying, but my mind does not understand <sup>[what I am saying]</sup> <sup>[Note: Here Paul raises an objection to the use of a language without the person knowing what he is saying]</sup>.

What should I do then? I will pray with my spirit <sup>[i.e. from within]</sup>, and I will pray with my understanding also <sup>[i.e., with a knowledge of what I am saying]</sup> I will sing with my spirit, and I will sing with my understanding also.

For if you <sup>[ask God to]</sup> bless with your spirit <sup>[i.e., in a language supernaturally]</sup>, how will the person there, who does not have the gift <sup>[i.e., of interpreting the language]</sup> be able to say, “May it be so,” when you offer <sup>[a prayer of]</sup> thanksgiving, since he does not know what you are saying?
For <sup>[though]</sup> you truly offer thanksgiving acceptably, the other person is not built up.
I thank God that I speak in languages <sup>[i.e., supernaturally]</sup> more than all of you.

However, in the assembly I would rather speak five words with my understanding, so that I can instruct other people also, than to speak ten thousand words in a language <sup>[that people do not understand]</sup>.
Brothers, do not be children in the way you think, but be babies in wrongdoing; yet be mature in your thinking.

It is written in the law <sup>[Isa. 28:1 1f]</sup>, “I <sup>[i.e., God]</sup> will speak to these people <sup>[i.e., the Israelites]</sup> by men with foreign languages and through the lips of strangers, and even then they will not listen to me, says the Lord.”

Therefore, languages <sup>[i.e., spoken supernaturally]</sup> are for a <sup>[miraculous]</sup> sign to unbelievers, not to believers. But prophesying is for a <sup>[miraculous]</sup> sign to believers, not to unbelievers.

Therefore, if the whole church assembles together and everyone speaks in languages <sup>[i.e., supernaturally]</sup>, and outsiders <sup>[i.e., people who do not understand the language being spoken]</sup> or unbelievers come <sup>[into the assembly]</sup>, will they not say that you people are crazy?

But if everyone prophesies and someone comes <sup>[into the assembly]</sup> who is an outsider or an unbeliever, he will be convicted <sup>[of his sins]</sup> by everyone <sup>[i.e., who speaks the message in an intelligible language]</sup>, and judged by everyone.
The secrets of his heart will be revealed so that he will fall <sup>[to the ground]</sup> on his face and worship God, declaring that God is truly among you.

So, what is the conclusion, brothers? When you people assemble together, each one of you has a psalm <sup>[to sing]</sup>, or a lesson <sup>[to teach]</sup>, or a <sup>[supernatural]</sup> revelation <sup>[to give]</sup>, or a language <sup>[to speak supernaturally]</sup>, or an interpretation <sup>[of that language]</sup>. Everything that is done should be for the building up <sup>[of the church]</sup>.

If any person speaks in a language <sup>[supernaturally]</sup>, there should be <sup>[only]</sup> two, or at most three people <sup>[doing it in one meeting]</sup>, and they should take turns. And <sup>[only]</sup> one person should interpret <sup>[the language spoken]</sup>

But if there is no interpreter present <sup>[at the meeting]</sup>, the person <sup>[i.e., with the ability to speak a language supernaturally]</sup> should remain quiet in the assembly. <sup>[Note: This implies that the speaker would know before he begins speaking whether or not an interpreter is present]</sup>. <sup>[In that case]</sup>, he should speak <sup>[only]</sup> to himself and to God. <sup>[See verse 2]</sup>.
And <sup>[only]</sup> two or three prophets should speak <sup>[in one meeting]</sup>, and <sup>[then]</sup> other people should determine the significance <sup>[of their messages]</sup>

But if something is revealed <sup>[supernaturally, at about the same time]</sup> to another person sitting nearby, the first one should finish speaking <sup>[i.e., before the second one begins]</sup>.
For all of you <sup>[i.e., who have the gifts]</sup>, can prophesy, one at a time, so that all of you may learn and be encouraged.
And the prophets’ spirits are <sup>[to be]</sup> under the prophets’ control <sup>[i.e., a prophet was to determine if or when he exercised his gift]</sup>,
because God is not a God of disorder but of peace <sup>[i.e., harmony]</sup>. As is the practice in all of the churches of the saints <sup>[i.e., God’s holy people]</sup>,

the women should remain quiet in the assemblies. For they are not permitted to speak <sup>[i.e., in a language supernaturally, or to prophesy]</sup>, but they are to be in subjection <sup>[i.e., to male leaders. See I Tim. 2:11-12]</sup>, as the law also says. <sup>[See Gen. 3:16 (?)]</sup>.

And if they want to find out about something, they should ask their own husbands at home <sup>[i.e., instead of asking someone else in the assembly and thereby suggesting insubordination to their husbands]</sup>. For it is a shame for a woman to speak in the assembly. <sup>[See verse 34]</sup>.
Did the message of God originate from you <sup>[i.e., the church in Corinth]</sup>? Or, were you people the only ones who received it?
If any man thinks he is a prophet or has a spiritual gift, he should realize that the things I am writing to you are the Lord’s commandment.
But if any man does not know this, he should remain ignorant. <sup>[Note: The Greek may mean, “If any man does not recognize this (truth), he will not be recognized (in the Judgment)”]</sup>.
Therefore, my brothers, desire eagerly to prophesy and <sup>[yet]</sup> do not prohibit people from speaking in a language <sup>[supernaturally]</sup>.
But everything <sup>[in the assembly]</sup> should be done properly and orderly.
Now, brothers, I want to remind you of the good news which I preached to you. You received it; you are standing firm in it;

<sup>[and]</sup> you have been saved by it also, if you hold on to it firmly <sup>[i.e., continue believing and practicing it]</sup>. For if you do not <sup>[hold on to it firmly]</sup>, you will have believed <sup>[it]</sup> all for nothing.
For I passed on to you the <sup>[following]</sup> main principles that I received <sup>[i.e., by revelation]</sup>: Christ died for our sins, according to the Scriptures;
He was buried; He was raised <sup>[from the dead]</sup> on the third day, according to the Scriptures;
and He appeared to Cephas <sup>[i.e., Peter. See Luke 24:34]</sup>, and then to the twelve apostles. <sup>[See Luke 24:33]</sup>.
After that He appeared to over five hundred brothers at one time, most of whom are still alive, but some have died.
Then He appeared to James <sup>[Note: This was probably Jesus’ half-brother. See Acts 12:17; Gal. 1:19]</sup>, and then to all of the apostles. <sup>[See John 20:26]</sup>.
Then last of all, He appeared to me also, as someone born abnormally. <sup>[Note: This probably refers to Paul being converted later than the rest of the apostles]</sup>.
For I am the least <sup>[significant]</sup> of the apostles, and am not worthy to be called an apostle, because I persecuted the church of God.

But I am whatever I am because of God’s unearned favor, and the favor He showed me was not for nothing <sup>[i.e., Paul made the most of his conversion by being extra diligent in his service to God]</sup>. But I worked harder than all the other apostles, yet it was not I, but God’s favor on me <sup>[that accomplished it]</sup>.
So, whether it was I, or the other apostles <sup>[who did the work]</sup>, this is <sup>[the message]</sup> that we have preached, and that you people have received.
Now if it is preached that Christ was raised from the dead, how can some people among you say that people will not be raised from the dead?
But if there is no resurrection from the dead, <sup>[then]</sup> Christ has not been raised up either.
And if Christ has not been raised up, then our preaching is for nothing, and your faith is for nothing too.

And also, we are found to be false witnesses about God because we testified that God raised up Christ <sup>[from the dead]</sup>, when <sup>[in reality]</sup> He did not raise Him up if, in fact, the dead are not raised up.
For if dead people are not raised up, <sup>[then]</sup> neither has Christ been raised.
And if Christ has not been raised up, <sup>[then]</sup> your faith is for nothing; you are still condemned by your sins.
Then <sup>[also]</sup>, those who have died in <sup>[fellowship with]</sup> Christ are lost.
If we have placed our hope in Christ while in this life only, we are to be pitied more than all <sup>[other]</sup> people.

But now Christ has been raised up from the dead; <sup>[He is]</sup> the first fruits of those who have died. <sup>[Note: By “first fruits” is meant the first installment of the crop which promises the entire harvest eventually]</sup>.
For since death happened to a man <sup>[i.e., Adam]</sup>, the resurrection from the dead also happened to a man <sup>[i.e., Christ]</sup>.

For as all people die <sup>[physically]</sup> in <sup>[their relationship to]</sup> Adam, so also will all people be made alive <sup>[physically]</sup> in <sup>[their relationship to]</sup> Christ. <sup>[Note: However, some view “made alive” as referring to the new spiritual life given to believers]</sup>.
But each one <sup>[is raised up]</sup> in his own turn: Christ as the first fruits <sup>[See verse 20]</sup>; then those who are Christ’s <sup>[i.e., believers]</sup>, when He returns.

Then <sup>[i.e., at Christ’s return]</sup> the end <sup>[of the world]</sup> will occur, when He will turn over the kingdom to God, the Father, after He has abolished all <sup>[hostile]</sup> dominion, authority and power.
For Christ must <sup>[continue to]</sup> rule <sup>[over His kingdom, the church]</sup> until God has put all of His enemies under Christ’s feet.
The last enemy that will be abolished is death.

For He <sup>[i.e., God]</sup> placed everything in subjection under His <sup>[i.e., Christ’s]</sup> feet. But when God said, “Everything is in subjection <sup>[to Him]</sup>,” it is clear that this did not include God <sup>[Himself]</sup>, who placed everything in subjection to Him <sup>[i.e., Christ]</sup>.

And after everything <sup>[else]</sup> has been subjected to Christ, then the Son Himself will also be subjected <sup>[i.e., He will subject Himself]</sup> to God, who subjected everything to Christ, so that God may rule over everything.

Otherwise <sup>[i.e., if there were no resurrection]</sup>, what do people accomplish by being immersed for those who have <sup>[already]</sup> died? If dead people are not raised up at all, why then are people immersed on their behalf? <sup>[Note: The foregoing rendition is the most commonly held one of over 30 interpretations given to this very difficult verse. Other possibilities are: a) “immersed in view of (being raised from) the dead,” b) “immersed in view of being dead (to sin),” c) “immersed in view of (having to suffer) death,” d) “immersed in view of (someday joining) the dead”]</sup>.
Why do we also risk danger every hour <sup>[i.e., if there were no resurrection]</sup>?
I face death every day, I assure you brothers, as surely as I boast of <sup>[fellowship with]</sup> you in Christ Jesus our Lord.

What benefit was it to me, from a human standpoint, if I fought wild animals in Ephesus, and <sup>[yet]</sup> the dead are not raised? <sup>[Note: This probably refers to Paul facing ferocious opposition from people there in Ephesus]</sup>. We should <sup>[just]</sup> eat and drink, for tomorrow we <sup>[may]</sup> die.
Do not be fooled; bad company ruins good moral behavior.
Come to your senses; do what is right and stop sinning. Some of you do not know God <sup>[i.e., in a personal way]</sup> I am saying this to make you ashamed.
But someone will ask, “How are dead people raised up?” and “What kind of a body will they have?”
You foolish person. The seed you plant does not come to life <sup>[i.e., grow]</sup> unless it dies.
And what you yourself sow is not the plant that will <sup>[eventually]</sup> come up, but simply a seed. It may be a grain of wheat or something else.
But God gives it the <sup>[kind of]</sup> body He wants <sup>[it to have]</sup>, so each seed gets its own plant.

All flesh is not the same <sup>[Note: The word “flesh” here refers to the material composition of matter, and in no way suggests anything sinful, as it does in many other places in Paul’s writings]</sup>. But mankind has one <sup>[kind of]</sup> flesh; animals have another kind; birds have another; and fish <sup>[still]</sup> another.

Also, there are heavenly bodies <sup>[i.e., stars and planets]</sup> and earthly bodies <sup>[i.e., men and animals]</sup> But the splendor of the heavenly bodies is one <sup>[kind]</sup>, and the splendor of the earthly bodies is another <sup>[kind]</sup>.

The sun has one <sup>[kind]</sup> of splendor, and the moon has another <sup>[kind]</sup>, and the stars <sup>[still]</sup> another. For <sup>[even]</sup> one star’s splendor is different from another’s.
It is the same way with the resurrection of dead people. <sup>[A person’s body]</sup> is buried <sup>[only]</sup> to decay; it is raised up never to decay again.
It is buried in dishonor; it is raised up in splendor. It is buried in weakness; it is raised up in power.
It is buried as a physical body; it is raised up as a spiritual body. If there is <sup>[such a thing as]</sup> a physical body, <sup>[then]</sup> there is also a spiritual body.

So, it is also written <sup>[Gen. 2:7]</sup>, “The first man, Adam, became a living being.” The last Adam <sup>[i.e., Christ]</sup>, became the Spirit who gives <sup>[never ending]</sup> life. <sup>[See John 5:21]</sup>.
However, the spiritual <sup>[body]</sup> does not come first, but the physical <sup>[body does]</sup>; then the spiritual <sup>[body comes]</sup>.
The first man <sup>[i.e., Adam]</sup> came from the dust of the ground <sup>[See Gen. 2:7]</sup>; the second man <sup>[i.e., Christ]</sup> came from heaven.
Just as earthly people are like the man who came from dust; so heavenly people are like the man who came from heaven.

And just as we <sup>[now]</sup> appear <sup>[in both a physical and spiritual body]</sup> like the man who came from dust <sup>[i.e., Adam]</sup>; we will also <sup>[someday]</sup> appear <sup>[in both a physical (?) and a spiritual body]</sup> like the man who came from heaven <sup>[i.e., Christ]</sup>.

Now this is what I am saying, brothers; flesh and blood <sup>[i.e., physical bodies]</sup> cannot possess the kingdom of God <sup>[i.e., the heavenly realm]</sup>; neither can <sup>[bodies that]</sup> decay possess <sup>[a place]</sup> which does not decay.
Look, I am telling you a secret truth: We will not all die, but we will all be changed <sup>[i.e., physically and spiritually]</sup>

in a split second, as quickly as the twinkle of an eye, when the last trumpet blows. For the trumpet will blow and dead people <sup>[i.e., believers]</sup> will be raised up, never to decay again, and <sup>[the living]</sup> will be changed <sup>[i.e., physically and spiritually]</sup>.

For this <sup>[body]</sup> that can decay must be clothed with <sup>[a body]</sup> which cannot decay. And this <sup>[body]</sup> that can die must be clothed with <sup>[a body]</sup> which cannot die.

But when this <sup>[body]</sup> that can decay has been clothed with one which cannot decay, and this <sup>[body]</sup> that can die has been clothed with one which cannot die, then the Scriptural saying will be fulfilled <sup>[Isa. 25:8]</sup>, “Death has been swallowed up <sup>[i.e., eliminated]</sup> by victory.” <sup>[Note: The reference here is to the time when believers will experience victory over death by being resurrected or taken directly to be with God]</sup>.
<sup>[Hos. 13:14 says]</sup>, “Death, where is your victory <sup>[over people]</sup>? Death, where is your <sup>[harmful]</sup> sting?”
The <sup>[harmful]</sup> sting that causes <sup>[physical]</sup> death is sin <sup>[See Rom. 5:12]</sup>; and the power of sin <sup>[to do this]</sup> comes from the law. <sup>[See Rom. 8:2]</sup>.
But I thank God for giving us victory <sup>[i.e., over sin and death]</sup> through our Lord Jesus Christ.

Therefore, my dearly loved brothers, stand firm <sup>[in the faith]</sup>; do not be moved <sup>[from your commitment]</sup> Always do your best to work for the Lord, since you know that your labor in the Lord’s <sup>[service]</sup> is not for nothing.
Now concerning the collection for the <sup>[needy]</sup> saints <sup>[whom you wrote me about]</sup>: You people also should do what I directed the churches in Galatia to do.

On every first day of the week <sup>[i.e., at the regular Sunday assembly]</sup> each one of you should set aside and store up <sup>[money]</sup> in proportion to your income, so that no collections will have to be taken when I come.
Then when I arrive, I will send letters with the men you appoint to take the gift to Jerusalem.
And if it seems suitable for me to go also, they can go with me.
But I will come to you after I have gone through Macedonia, for I am planning to go through Macedonia <sup>[Note: This was the Roman province of northern Greece]</sup>
And perhaps I will stay with you, even for the winter, so that you can help me on my journey, wherever I go.
But I do not plan to see you yet, as I will <sup>[only]</sup> be passing through. For I am hoping to stay with you for some time <sup>[i.e., in the future]</sup>, if the Lord permits.
But I will wait <sup>[here]</sup> in Ephesus until the Day of Pentecost <sup>[Note: This was an annual Jewish festival, held at spring harvest time]</sup>;

because a great door <sup>[of opportunity]</sup> has opened up to me <sup>[here]</sup> for doing an effective work <sup>[for God]</sup>, but there are <sup>[also]</sup> many enemies who oppose me. <sup>[See Acts 19]</sup>.

Now if <sup>[or, probably, “when”]</sup> Timothy comes <sup>[i.e., there to Corinth]</sup>, make sure he feels at ease <sup>[Note: Due to his youthful age and inexperience, Timothy was probably inclined to be easily intimidated]</sup>, for he is doing the Lord’s work, just as I am.
So, do not let anyone look down on him, but help him on his journey in peace so that he can come to me, for I am expecting him <sup>[to come]</sup> with the brothers.
Now concerning our brother Apollos: I strongly urged him to go to you with the other brothers, but he was not at all willing to do so at this time. But he will go when he has the opportunity.
You people should be alert; stand firm in the faith; act like <sup>[godly]</sup> men; be <sup>[spiritually]</sup> strong.
Everything you do should be done lovingly.

You know that <sup>[members of]</sup> the family of Stephanas are the first fruits of Achaia <sup>[i.e., the first converts in that province of southern Greece]</sup>, and that they have committed themselves to serving God’s people <sup>[i.e., the saints]</sup>.
Now I urge you, brothers, that you should also submit to <sup>[the leadership of]</sup> such people and to everyone <sup>[else]</sup> who works and labors <sup>[with them]</sup>
And I was glad when Stephanas, Fortunatus and Achaicus arrived <sup>[here in Ephesus]</sup>, for they have provided <sup>[me]</sup> with what you were not able to.

For they cheered me up, as they have cheered <sup>[or, will cheer]</sup> you up <sup>[also]</sup>. <sup>[Note: How these three men did this for Paul and the Corinthians is not known]</sup>. You should give recognition to such men.

The churches in Asia send you their greetings <sup>[Note: This was the westernmost province in present-day Turkey]</sup>. Aquila and Prisca <sup>[Note: This married couple were close friends and fellow-tentmakers of Paul]</sup>, along with the church that meets in their house, send you their warm greetings in <sup>[fellowship with]</sup> Christ.
All of the brothers <sup>[and sisters]</sup> send you their greetings <sup>[also]</sup>. Greet one another with a holy kiss <sup>[i.e., to signify affection, yet without sensuality]</sup>.
These greetings are being written with my own hand. <sup>[See II Thess. 3:17]</sup>.
If anyone <sup>[there]</sup> does not love the Lord, he deserves to be cut off from God. O Lord, come <sup>[back soon]</sup>
May the unearned favor of the Lord Jesus Christ <sup>[continue to]</sup> be with you.
<sup>[I send]</sup> my love to all of you in <sup>[fellowship with]</sup> Christ Jesus. May it be so.

<sup>[This letter is from]</sup> Paul, an apostle of Christ Jesus by God’s will, and Timothy, our brother. <sup>[It is being sent]</sup> to the church of God at Corinth, with all the saints <sup>[i.e., God’s holy people]</sup> who are in the entire province of Achaia <sup>[Note: This was the southern province of Greece]</sup>
May <sup>[God’s]</sup> unearned favor be yours, <sup>[along with]</sup> peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
May the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who is the Father of compassionate feelings and the God of all comfort, be praised.

He comforts us <sup>[Note: Most commentators view the editorial “us,” “we,” and “our” in this passage as a reference to a tragic personal experience of Paul. See verses 8-11]</sup> in all our troubles so that we will be able to comfort those who are in any <sup>[kind of]</sup> trouble, through the comfort we ourselves have received from Him.
For just as we experience an overflow of Christ’s sufferings, even so we also experience an overflow of comfort through Christ.

But if we experience trouble, it is in order <sup>[to provide]</sup> for your comfort and salvation. Or, if we are comforted, it is in order <sup>[to provide]</sup> for your comfort <sup>[also]</sup>, which enables you to endure patiently the same sufferings we experience.
And our hope for you is firm, for we know that just as you share our sufferings, so you will also <sup>[share]</sup> our comfort.

For we want you to know, brothers, about the trouble that we experienced in Asia. <sup>[See I Cor. 16:19]</sup>. We were under a heavy burden <sup>[which was]</sup> more than we could stand, even to the point of despairing of life.
Actually, we felt within us that we had <sup>[already]</sup> received a death sentence, so that we could <sup>[learn]</sup> not to trust in ourselves but in God, who raises the dead.
He has rescued us from a serious 217 threat of death and He will do it again. We have placed our hope in Him, that He will also continue to rescue us,
as you cooperate by praying for us. Then, many people will give thanks <sup>[to God]</sup> on our behalf for the gift He has given to us through <sup>[your]</sup> many <sup>[prayers]</sup>.

For this is what we boast of: Our conscience testifies that we have conducted ourselves in the world, and especially toward you, in holiness and godly sincerity, not with human wisdom, but with the favor of God.
For we are not writing anything to you than what you can read and understand. And I hope that, on the day of our Lord Jesus <sup>[i.e., the judgment day]</sup>, you will understand us fully,

as you have understood us partially <sup>[or “only a part of you have understood us”]</sup>, that we are <sup>[the occasion of]</sup> your boasting, even as you are <sup>[the occasion of]</sup> our boasting.

And because of this confidence <sup>[i.e., that you people have such a favorable opinion of me]</sup>, I had determined to visit you first <sup>[i.e., before going to Macedonia. See I Cor. 16:5-7]</sup>, so that you could have an additional blessing. <sup>[Note: The “blessing” here would be Paul getting to minister to them both before and after visiting Macedonia]</sup>.
<sup>[I had planned]</sup> to visit you on my way to Macedonia and then again on my way back from there, and to have you help me on my journey to Judea. <sup>[See I Cor. 16:6]</sup>.

So, when I made this decision, was I just vacillating <sup>[in uncertainty]</sup>? Or, do I make plans in a worldly way <sup>[i.e., just to suit my own fancy]</sup>, so that my “yes” and “no” <sup>[do not really mean anything]</sup>?
But just as certainly as God is dependable, our message to you is not <sup>[both]</sup> “yes” and “no” <sup>[at the same time]</sup>.

For the Son of God, Jesus Christ, who was proclaimed among you by me, <sup>[and]</sup> Silvanus <sup>[i.e., the same as Silas]</sup> and Timothy, was not “yes” and “no” <sup>[i.e., both our message and Jesus Himself were not contradictory]</sup>, but with Him, it was “yes” <sup>[i.e., Jesus consistently spoke the truth]</sup>.

For <sup>[no matter]</sup> how many promises God has made, in Christ <sup>[the answer]</sup> is “yes” <sup>[i.e., Christ has fulfilled all of God’s promises to mankind]</sup> Also, through Christ, the “amen” <sup>[Note: This word is always translated elsewhere as “May it be so”]</sup> can be said by us in order to honor God.

Now God is the One who makes us stand firm with you in <sup>[fellowship with]</sup> Christ. He has anointed us <sup>[Note: “Anointed” here probably refers to selecting, appointing and commissioning people for some form of Christian service. See Luke 4:18]</sup>;
He has placed His seal of ownership upon us by giving us the down payment of the Holy Spirit <sup>[to live]</sup> in our hearts.

But I appeal to God as my witness <sup>[i.e., to verify that I am telling the truth]</sup>; it was in order to spare you <sup>[i.e., from a disciplinary action]</sup> that I decided not to return to Corinth.

For we do not have <sup>[the right to]</sup> rule over your faith <sup>[i.e., to dictate concerning your personal relationship with God]</sup>, but we want to work with you for your happiness. For you are <sup>[already]</sup> standing firm in your faith. <sup>[Note: This whole narrative alludes to some unresolved problems still existing there in Corinth]</sup>
So, I decided for myself not to make another painful visit to you.
For if I caused you sorrow, who can make me glad except the one I have grieved? <sup>[See verse 6]</sup>.

And I wrote you this very thing so that when I did come I might not be grieved by those who should make me happy. I had confidence in all of you, that whatever makes me happy <sup>[also]</sup> makes you happy.
For I wrote to you out of much distress and anguish of heart and with many tears, not in order to grieve you, but that you people might know how very much I love you.
But if anyone has caused grief, he has not done it to me <sup>[only]</sup>, but to almost all of you <sup>[or, “only partially to all of you”]</sup> for I do not want to overstate the case.

The punishment inflicted <sup>[on him]</sup> by the majority of you was sufficient. <sup>[Note: If this is a reference to the incestuous man of I Cor. 5:4ff, then the punishment was removing him from their fellowship]</sup>.
So instead, you people should <sup>[now]</sup> forgive and comfort him, in order that such a person does not become overwhelmed with extreme grief.
So, I urge you to reassure him of your love for him.
For this also was my purpose in writing you, that I might test your willingness to obey everything <sup>[I required of you]</sup>.

Now whomever you forgive <sup>[for doing]</sup> anything <sup>[wrong]</sup>, I will forgive also. For indeed, whatever I have forgiven, if I have <sup>[actually]</sup> forgiven anything, has been for your sakes in the sight of Christ. <sup>[Note: Paul seems to be saying “Since my forgiveness depends on your forgiveness of the man, I am not sure of my forgiveness because I am not sure of yours”]</sup>.
<sup>[I do this]</sup> so that Satan cannot take advantage of us, for we are not unaware of his schemes.

Now when I came to Troas <sup>[See Acts 16:8]</sup>, in order to preach the good news about Christ, and when a door <sup>[of opportunity]</sup> was opened to me in <sup>[the service of]</sup> the Lord <sup>[there]</sup>,
I had no peace of mind because I did not find my brother Titus <sup>[there]</sup>. So, I said “good-bye” to them and went on to Macedonia.
But I offer thanks to God, who always leads us <sup>[preachers]</sup> to victory in <sup>[the service of]</sup> Christ, and through us makes known the “fragrant odor” of His knowledge everywhere.
For we <sup>[i.e., through our preaching]</sup> are like a fragrant incense which goes up to God, <sup>[and spreads the knowledge]</sup> of Christ among both saved and lost people.

To lost people <sup>[we become]</sup> a smell of death <sup>[i.e., we bring condemnation to people who reject our message]</sup>; to saved people <sup>[we become]</sup> a fragrance of life <sup>[i.e., we bring salvation to people who accept our message]</sup>. And who is adequate to <sup>[accomplish]</sup> such tasks?

For we <sup>[apostles]</sup> are not like so many people, who “peddle” God’s message merely for profit <sup>[or, “who corrupt God’s message”]</sup>, but in Christ’s <sup>[service]</sup> we proclaim the message from God with sincerity, <sup>[realizing we are]</sup> in the presence of God.
Are we beginning to commend ourselves again <sup>[i.e., by saying that we are not like other people]</sup>? Or, do we need letters of recommendation to you or from you, as others do?
You people are our letters <sup>[of recommendation]</sup>, written on our hearts, known and read by everyone.

You people show <sup>[i.e., by your conversions]</sup> that you are a letter from Christ, delivered by us <sup>[i.e., the result of our ministry]</sup>, not written with ink but with the Holy Spirit of the living God; not on stone tablets <sup>[i.e., as in the case with Moses’ ministry]</sup>, but on tablets of the human heart. <sup>[See Jer. 31:33; Heb. 8:10]</sup>.
<sup>[We say this]</sup> because we have such confidence in God through<sup>[our relationship with]</sup> Christ.
It is not that we are <sup>[so]</sup> competent as to consider anything <sup>[we do]</sup> as coming from ourselves, but our competence comes from God.

He has also made us competent as ministers of a New Agreement <sup>[i.e., between Himself and mankind]</sup>. It is not <sup>[an agreement]</sup> based <sup>[merely]</sup> on a written document <sup>[i.e., such as the Law of Moses]</sup>, but on the Holy Spirit, for the written document brings <sup>[spiritual]</sup> death <sup>[See Rom. 8:2]</sup>, but the Holy Spirit brings <sup>[never ending]</sup> life.

But if the ministry <sup>[of the law]</sup> that brings <sup>[spiritual]</sup> death, being carved in letters on stones, came with <sup>[such]</sup> splendor that the Israelites could not look at Moses’ face because of its radiance <sup>[See Ex. 34:29-35]</sup>, (even though such radiance <sup>[eventually]</sup> faded away),
will not the ministry of the Holy Spirit <sup>[i.e., the New Agreement]</sup> have <sup>[greater]</sup> splendor?
For if the ministry <sup>[of the law]</sup> that brings condemnation came with splendor, will not the ministry that brings righteousness <sup>[i.e., the New Agreement]</sup> have greater splendor?
For truly, what once came with splendor <sup>[i.e., the Old Agreement]</sup> has now lost it in comparison to that which has surpassing splendor <sup>[i.e., the New Agreement]</sup>.

For if that which was fading away <sup>[i.e., the Old Agreement, represented by the Law of Moses]</sup> had splendor, how much greater is the splendor of that which remains <sup>[i.e., the New Agreement represented by the Gospel]</sup>? <sup>[Note: In this section Paul uses the analogy of the fading splendor on Moses’ face to the fading splendor of the Old Agreement]</sup>.
Therefore, since we have such a hope <sup>[i.e., that the Gospel is much superior to the law]</sup>, we speak with great boldness,

and are not like Moses, who had to put a veil over his face to keep the Israelites from staring at it as <sup>[its]</sup> radiance was completely fading away. <sup>[Note: The analogy continues by suggesting that the Old Agreement was fading away like the splendor on Moses’ face did]</sup>.

But the minds of the Israelites became dull for, to this very day, the same veil remains on them <sup>[i.e., figuratively]</sup> when the Old Agreement <sup>[Scriptures]</sup> are read. It is not being revealed to them that the Old Agreement is being abolished by Christ <sup>[i.e., by His death on the cross. See Col. 2:14]</sup>
But to this day, a veil remains on their minds whenever <sup>[the writings of]</sup> Moses are read.
But whenever a person <sup>[i.e., a Jew]</sup> turns to the Lord, the veil is removed <sup>[i.e., so they can understand the meaning of God’s promises to them through Christ]</sup>.
Now the Lord <sup>[i.e., Christ]</sup> is the Holy Spirit, and there is freedom <sup>[from the bondage of sin and fear]</sup> wherever the Spirit of the Lord is.

But all of us <sup>[Christians]</sup>, with unveiled faces, see the Lord’s splendor reflected, as <sup>[though we were]</sup> looking in a mirror, and <sup>[so]</sup> are being transformed into His likeness in ever-increasing splendor. This is <sup>[the work]</sup> of the Lord, <sup>[who is]</sup> the Holy Spirit.
So, having obtained this ministry through <sup>[God’s]</sup> mercy, we do not become discouraged.

Instead, we have renounced <sup>[the practice of]</sup> secret, disgraceful behavior. We do not practice deception, nor resort to distorting God’s message. But, by making known the truth <sup>[i.e., the true message]</sup>, we commend ourselves to everyone’s conscience in the presence of God.
For even if our Gospel message is veiled, it is veiled <sup>[only]</sup> from those who are lost.
For the god of this world <sup>[i.e., Satan]</sup> has blinded the minds of the unbelievers, so that they would not see the light of the Gospel of the splendor of Christ, who is the image of God.
For we do not preach ourselves, but <sup>[we preach]</sup> Christ Jesus as Lord, and ourselves as your slaves for Jesus’ sake.

For God, who said <sup>[Gen. 1:3]</sup>, “Light will shine out of darkness,” has <sup>[also]</sup> shone in our hearts to provide <sup>[us with]</sup> the light of the knowledge of God’s splendor <sup>[as it shines]</sup> on the face of Christ. <sup>[Note: This allusion to “splendor shining on Christ’s face” may be a continuation of the analogy used in 3:13]</sup>.

But we have this treasure <sup>[i.e., the Gospel message]</sup> in clay pots <sup>[i.e., the mortal bodies of the apostles]</sup> to show that the <sup>[i.e., its]</sup> extraordinary power comes from God and not from us.
We are afflicted in every way, but are not crushed; <sup>[we are]</sup> perplexed, but not to the point of despair;
<sup>[we are]</sup> persecuted, but are not deserted <sup>[by God]</sup>; <sup>[we are]</sup> struck down, but are not destroyed.

We always carry around in our <sup>[physical]</sup> bodies the <sup>[threat of]</sup> dying, as Jesus did <sup>[i.e., the apostles were constantly exposed to impending danger and death]</sup>, so that the life of Jesus <sup>[i.e., His virtue, courage, faithfulness, etc.]</sup> may be demonstrated in our bodies <sup>[i.e., in the way we live]</sup> also.

For while we live, we are always exposed to death for Jesus’ sake <sup>[See Rom. 8:36]</sup>, so that the life of Jesus may be demonstrated in our mortal bodies <sup>[i.e., in our weak and frail human nature]</sup>.
So then, <sup>[the possibility of physical]</sup> death is at work in us, while <sup>[spiritual]</sup> life is at work in you.

But, we have the same spirit <sup>[i.e., type]</sup> of faith as that written about <sup>[Psa. 116:10]</sup>, “I believed <sup>[God’s message]</sup>, so I spoke.” We also believe <sup>[it]</sup>, and so we also speak <sup>[about it]</sup>

We know that God, who raised up the Lord Jesus <sup>[from the dead]</sup>, will also raise us <sup>[apostles]</sup> up with Jesus and present us with you people <sup>[i.e., in the presence of the Father in heaven]</sup>.

For everything <sup>[i.e., that we have experienced. See verses 8-12]</sup> is for your sakes so that <sup>[God’s]</sup> unearned favor, being extended to so many, <sup>[in bringing them salvation through Paul’s ministry]</sup> may result in much thanksgiving in praise to God.
Therefore, we do not get discouraged, for though our physical body is decaying, yet our inner person is being renewed daily.

For our insignificant troubles <sup>[here on earth]</sup>, which last only a short time, are producing for us an immeasurable, never ending <sup>[counter-]</sup> weight of splendor <sup>[i.e., in heaven. See Rom. 8:18]</sup>
So, we do not look at the things which can be seen, but at what cannot be seen, for the things which can be seen are <sup>[only]</sup> temporary, but the things which cannot be seen are never ending.

For we know that if our earthly “house” <sup>[i.e., our physical body]</sup> which is like a tent, is torn down, we have <sup>[the promise of]</sup> a building from God, a never ending “house” in heaven that is not made by human hands.
For indeed, we groan while in this “house,” longing to be covered over by our heavenly dwelling place,
because when we are covered by it, we will not be found naked <sup>[i.e., without a suitable dwelling for our body]</sup>.

For indeed, we who are in this “tent” groan under its burden, not <sup>[wanting]</sup> to be uncovered, but to be covered over. Then our mortal body will be swallowed up <sup>[i.e., replaced]</sup> by <sup>[never ending]</sup> life.

Now God is the One who has prepared us for this very thing <sup>[i.e., the receiving of a new body to live in]</sup>. He has <sup>[also]</sup> given us the Holy Spirit as a down payment. <sup>[Note: This refers to the gift of the indwelling Holy Spirit as being a guarantee that we will receive the balance of our inheritance in the form of a new body]</sup>.
So, we are always encouraged and know that, while we are at home in our <sup>[physical]</sup> body, we are away from the Lord,
for we live by faith, not by what we see.
We are encouraged, I say, and would rather be away from our body and at home with the Lord.
So, we are also eager to please God very much, whether we are at home <sup>[in our body]</sup> or away from it.

For we must all appear in front of the judgment bar of Christ, so that each one of us may receive what is due him for what he had done while in his <sup>[physical]</sup> body <sup>[i.e., on earth]</sup>, whether it be good or bad.

Since we <sup>[apostles]</sup> know <sup>[what it means]</sup> to revere the Lord, we try to persuade people <sup>[i.e., to accept the Gospel, or to acknowledge our integrity]</sup>. But we are <sup>[already]</sup> known to God, and I hope that you also know us in your hearts <sup>[i.e., to have integrity]</sup>.

We are not trying to commend ourselves to you again, but we speak <sup>[in such a way as]</sup> to give you an occasion to be proud of us, so that you can have an answer for those who take pride in <sup>[outward]</sup> appearances instead of what is in the heart.
For if we <sup>[seem to]</sup> be crazy, it is for the sake <sup>[of the work]</sup> of God, or if we <sup>[seem to]</sup> be sensible, it is for your benefit.

For Christ’s love for us motivates us, because we have concluded that, <sup>[since]</sup> one person <sup>[i.e., Christ]</sup> died for all people, so then all people have died. <sup>[Note: This may mean simply that Christ died for all people, who were dead in their sins]</sup>.
And Christ died for all people, so that those who are alive should not live for themselves anymore, but for Him who died and rose again for their sakes.

So, from now on we will not regard any person by worldly standards, even though we <sup>[once]</sup> regarded Christ that way. But we will not regard Him <sup>[that way]</sup> anymore. <sup>[Note: Paul seems to be saying that before his conversion he viewed Christ on the basis of His family, His education, His position, etc., but since his conversion he does not view Him that way anymore]</sup>.
Therefore, if any person is in <sup>[fellowship with]</sup> Christ, he is a new person. His old ways have passed away; now his ways have become new.
All <sup>[these]</sup> things are from God, who restored us to fellowship with Himself through Christ, and gave us the ministry of restoring <sup>[other]</sup> people to <sup>[such]</sup> fellowship.

That is, God was in Christ restoring the world to fellowship with Himself, not counting people’s sins against them. And He entrusted to us the message of restoring people to fellowship <sup>[with Him]</sup>.

So, we <sup>[apostles]</sup> are ambassadors, <sup>[speaking]</sup> on behalf of Christ. <sup>[It is]</sup> as though God were appealing <sup>[to people]</sup> through us: “We urge you people, on behalf of Christ, to be restored to fellowship with God.”
God considered Christ to be sinful on our behalf, even though He never sinned, so that we could be considered right with God through Him.
As workers together with God we urge you also, who have received God’s unearned favor, do not allow it to be for nothing,

for God said <sup>[Isa. 49:8]</sup>, “I listened to you at the right time <sup>[i.e., when you needed my favor]</sup>, and I helped you on the day of your salvation.” Look, now is the right time <sup>[to accept God’s favor]</sup>; now is the day of salvation.
<sup>[We try]</sup> not to place an obstacle in anyone’s way <sup>[i.e., to keep them from accepting the Gospel message]</sup>, so that our ministry would not be blamed.

But, as ministers of God, we <sup>[try to]</sup> commend ourselves <sup>[to others]</sup> in every way. <sup>[We do this]</sup> by <sup>[our]</sup> great steadfastness in troubles, hardships and difficulties.
<sup>[We have been]</sup> beaten, jailed, and mobbed. <sup>[We have]</sup> worked hard, missed sleep and gone without food.
<sup>[We have ministered]</sup> with purity, knowledge, patience and kindness. <sup>[We have done this]</sup> by <sup>[the power of]</sup> the Holy Spirit; out of genuine love;

by preaching the truth; through the power of God <sup>[i.e., possibly including miracles]</sup>; and by <sup>[using]</sup> weapons of righteousness in <sup>[our]</sup> right and left hands. <sup>[Note: This could allude to offensive and defensive weapons and means that Paul was fully equipped to do whatever was right]</sup>.
<sup>[We have experienced]</sup> honor and dishonor; a bad reputation and a good one. <sup>[We have been viewed]</sup> as deceivers and yet are genuine;

as <sup>[both]</sup> unknown <sup>[to people]</sup> and yet well-known; <sup>[we have almost]</sup> died, and yet as you see, we are still alive; <sup>[we have been]</sup> punished and yet not killed;
sad and yet always joyous; poor and yet making many people <sup>[spiritually]</sup> rich; destitute and yet possessing everything.
We have spoken freely to you, O Corinthians, and our hearts have been wide open to you.
We are not holding back <sup>[our affections]</sup> from you, but you are holding back your affections <sup>[from us]</sup>.
I am speaking to you as my <sup>[spiritual]</sup> children; open up <sup>[your hearts]</sup> to me in return.

Do not team up with unbelievers <sup>[i.e., in close relationships, such as marriage, etc.]</sup> For what do righteousness and wickedness have in common? Or, what fellowship can light have with darkness?
And what harmony can Christ have with Belial? <sup>[Note: “Belial” means “worthlessness” and was used for referring to the devil]</sup>. Or, what does a believer have in common with an unbeliever?

And what agreement is there between a temple of God and idols? For we are a temple of the living God, just as God said <sup>[Lev. 26:12]</sup>, “I will live in them and walk among them <sup>[i.e., among His people]</sup>; and I will be their God and they will be my people.”

Therefore, the Lord says <sup>[Isa. 52:11]</sup>, “You people should leave their company <sup>[i.e., unbelievers, verse 14]</sup> and separate yourselves from them, and do not touch anything unclean <sup>[i.e., their evil practices]</sup>, and I will welcome you.”
The Lord Almighty <sup>[also]</sup> said <sup>[Hos. 1:10]</sup>, “I will be a Father to you, and you will be sons and daughters to me.”

<sup>[So]</sup>, since we have these promises <sup>[See 6:16-18]</sup>, dear ones, we should purify ourselves from everything that pollutes <sup>[our]</sup> body and spirit while living lives completely separated <sup>[from sin]</sup>, out of reverence for God.
<sup>[Note: The following section seems to pick up where 6:13 left off]</sup>. Make room for us in your hearts. We have not wronged or injured anyone, nor have we taken advantage of anyone.
I am not saying this to condemn you, for <sup>[as]</sup> I said before, you have such a place in our hearts that we would live or die with you.
I have great confidence in talking to you; I have great pride in you and I am full of encouragement <sup>[over you]</sup> In all of our troubles I am overflowing with joy.
For even when we came to Macedonia we had no rest for our bodies. But we met with trouble on every hand. <sup>[There were]</sup> conflicts on the outside and fears within us.
But God, who comforts downhearted people, comforted us <sup>[also]</sup> by the arrival of Titus.

<sup>[We were comforted]</sup> not only by his presence, but also <sup>[by learning]</sup> that you comforted him. He <sup>[also]</sup> told us of your longing and grieving and concern for me, so that made me all the happier.

For even though I caused you sorrow by my letter <sup>[Note: Paul’s writing of I Corinthians to correct abuses at Corinth apparently produced sorrow for their wrongdoing]</sup>, I do not regret it, though I did regret it. <sup>[For]</sup> I see that my letter hurt you, though only for a short time.
Now I am glad, not because you were made sorry, but because your sorrow led you to repent. For you were made sorry from a godly motive, so were not harmed by us in any way.

For the sorrow that God desires produces repentance <sup>[i.e., a change of heart and life]</sup> that leads to salvation, and which brings no regrets. But the sorrow of the world <sup>[i.e., sorrow for wrongdoing for reasons unrelated to God or moral principles]</sup> produces <sup>[spiritual]</sup> death.

See how the very fact that you were made sorry from a godly motive has produced earnestness in you! <sup>[It produced]</sup> a desire to vindicate yourselves <sup>[i.e., by making amends for your wrongdoing]</sup>; an attitude of just anger <sup>[i.e., toward the wrongdoers]</sup>; a sense of fear <sup>[i.e., a fear of God for doing wrong]</sup>; an eager desire <sup>[i.e., to do the right thing]</sup>; a zeal <sup>[i.e., to correct problems]</sup>; and an attitude of avenging <sup>[i.e., that punished the wrongdoer]</sup>. You demonstrated in everything <sup>[you did]</sup> that you were pure <sup>[i.e., innocent]</sup> in this matter. <sup>[Note: This whole description of their conduct appears to be a reference to the incident mentioned in 2:1- 11]</sup>.
So, although I wrote to you, it was not for the sake of the person who did the wrong, nor for the one who was wronged, but so that you could realize before God how devoted to us you are.
So, we have been encouraged, and in addition to <sup>[our]</sup> encouragement, we were very glad that Titus was so happy <sup>[also]</sup>, because you had refreshed his spirit.

For if there is anything I have boasted to him about you, you did not cause me to be ashamed of it. But as everything we said to you was true, so our boasting to Titus <sup>[about you]</sup> proved to be true also.

And he has great affection for you when he remembers how obedient all of you were <sup>[i.e., obedient to his or Paul’s instructions to the church]</sup>, and how you welcomed him with fear and trembling <sup>[i.e., with a respectful, submissive spirit]</sup>
I am glad I can have so much confidence in you about everything.
Now, brothers, we want you to know about the favor God has given to the churches in Macedonia.
<sup>[It is]</sup> how, in spite of their suffering severe trials, their overflowing joy and extreme poverty produced an overwhelming expression of generosity.
For I testify that they gave <sup>[financial help]</sup> to the full limit of their ability, yes and even beyond it. <sup>[And they did this]</sup> entirely on their own,

<sup>[even]</sup> pleading with us and requesting the privilege of taking part in this service to the saints <sup>[i.e., God’s holy people]</sup> in Jerusalem. <sup>[See also 9:1-15; I Cor. 16:1-2; Rom. 15:25-28]</sup>.
And they did not <sup>[just]</sup> do as we had hoped, but they gave themselves first to the Lord and <sup>[then]</sup> to us through the will of God.

So, <sup>[i.e., because of your enthusiastic response to this need. See verse 10]</sup>, we urged Titus, who had previously begun <sup>[taking up this collection]</sup>, to help you also complete this favorable work <sup>[i.e., by finalizing it]</sup>.

But just as you excel in everything <sup>[else]</sup>; in your faith, in your speaking; in your knowledge <sup>[Note: Possibly these refer to spiritual gifts. See I Cor. 12:8-10]</sup>, in your eagerness <sup>[to serve]</sup>, and in your love for us, now you people should also excel in this favorable work <sup>[i.e., of giving financial help to needy people]</sup>.
I am not saying this <sup>[to you]</sup> as a command, but by telling you how eager others are <sup>[to help]</sup>, to find out how genuine your love is also.

For you know the unearned favor of our Lord Jesus Christ, <sup>[how]</sup> that even though He was rich, He became poor for your sakes, so that through His poverty you people could become <sup>[spiritually]</sup> rich.

Now here is my advice on what is best to do in this matter: Since you were the first ones to begin <sup>[taking up a collection]</sup> a year ago, <sup>[now you should]</sup> not only be doing something <sup>[about it]</sup>, but <sup>[you should continue]</sup> wanting to do something <sup>[as well]</sup>.
So, now finish doing it also, so that just as you were eager to want to, you will also <sup>[be eager to]</sup> finish it, according to your ability.
For if the eagerness is there, <sup>[then]</sup> whatever a person has <sup>[to give]</sup> is acceptable, and not what he does not have.
I am not saying this to make it easy for others <sup>[to give]</sup> and difficult for you people,

but that there will be equality <sup>[i.e., in everyone’s giving]</sup>. Your abundance can now supply their need, so that their abundance can <sup>[later]</sup> supply your need. <sup>[That way]</sup> there can be equality,

as it is written <sup>[Ex. 16:18]</sup>, “The person who gathered much <sup>[i.e., manna]</sup> did not have any left over, and the one who gathered <sup>[only]</sup> a little <sup>[still]</sup> had plenty.”
But I thank God for putting the same concern <sup>[that I have]</sup> for you in the heart of Titus.
For he not only accepted our urging, but being very diligent himself, he went to you of his own accord <sup>[i.e. Titus had wanted to go to Corinth even before being urged to]</sup>.

And we have sent <sup>[i.e., are sending]</sup> with him the brother who is praised throughout all the churches for <sup>[his work in]</sup> preaching the Gospel <sup>[Note: This “brother” cannot be identified with any certainty]</sup>.

And not only this, but he was also appointed by the churches to travel with us in <sup>[taking]</sup> this gift <sup>[to Jerusalem]</sup> as we minister in order to honor the Lord Himself, and to demonstrate our eagerness <sup>[to help poor people]</sup>.
<sup>[We made this arrangement]</sup> to avoid anyone blaming us for the way we handled this generous gift.
For we take measures to do what is honorable, not only in the sight of the Lord, but also in the sight of people.

And we have sent <sup>[i.e., are sending]</sup> with them our brother <sup>[Note: This is a different person from the one mentioned in verse 18]</sup>, who has often proven his diligence to us in many ways, and is now proving to be even more diligent because of the great confidence he has in you <sup>[i.e., confidence in the Corinthians completing their collection for the poor]</sup>.
As for Titus, he is my partner and fellow-worker on your behalf. As for our brothers <sup>[i.e., those who will accompany him]</sup>, they are the messengers of the churches and an honor to Christ.
<sup>[So]</sup>, demonstrate in front of the churches evidence of your love <sup>[for them]</sup> and our reason for boasting about you.
Now it is unnecessary for me to write you about this service to God’s holy people <sup>[in Jerusalem, who were in need]</sup>.

For I know how eager you <sup>[already]</sup> are, and I have boasted about you to the Macedonians, that Achaia <sup>[Note: This was the southernmost province of Greece, where Corinth was located]</sup> has been prepared <sup>[to give]</sup> since last year. And your enthusiasm has stirred up most of them <sup>[to give also]</sup>.
But I have sent the brothers <sup>[See 8:18, 22]</sup>, so that our boasting about you being prepared will not prove to be empty words.
For if any Macedonians come with me and learn that you are not prepared, we, not to mention you, will be ashamed for having been so over confident.

So, I thought it necessary to urge the brothers <sup>[See verse 3]</sup> to visit you ahead of time and arrange beforehand for your previously promised generous gift. Then it will be ready <sup>[when I arrive]</sup> as a generously given gift and not as something given grudgingly.

But <sup>[remember]</sup> this: The person who plants only a few seeds will harvest a meager crop. And the one who plants many seeds <sup>[Note: The Greek says, “plants with blessings”]</sup> will harvest a bumper crop.
Each person should give what he has decided to in his heart, not grudgingly, nor out of a feeling that he has to, for God loves a cheerful giver.
And God is able to provide you with abundant gifts, so that you will always have plenty of everything, and that you will also have plenty for doing every good deed.
As it is written <sup>[Psa. 112:9]</sup>, “He has distributed <sup>[gifts]</sup> widely; he has given to poor people; his generosity will last forever.”
Now God, who provides seeds to the planter and bread for food, will <sup>[also]</sup> supply, and <sup>[even]</sup> multiply your seeds for planting, and will increase the harvest of your generosity.
You will be made rich in everything so you can be generous in every way. This will result in people thanking God <sup>[for what you have done]</sup> through us.

For the rendering of this service not only <sup>[fully]</sup> meets the needs of the saints <sup>[i.e., God’s holy people]</sup>, but also produces overflowing expressions of thanksgiving to God from many people.

Because you people have proven <sup>[yourselves]</sup> by this service, the poor Christians in Jerusalem will praise God for your obedient <sup>[spirit]</sup> in professing <sup>[commitment to]</sup> the Gospel of Christ, and for your generous contribution to them and to everyone else.
And when they see the outstanding <sup>[evidence of]</sup> God’s favor in you, they will pray for you with deep affection <sup>[or, “will long to see you”]</sup>.
I thank God for His indescribable gift <sup>[i.e., Christ. See Rom. 8:32]</sup>.

Now I Paul, who am humble <sup>[when speaking]</sup> face to face with you, but bold when I am away from you, urge you by the meekness and gentleness of Christ <sup>[Note: This means either Paul was imitating Christ’s behavior or that the Corinthians were to do so]</sup>:

I make this request so that I do not <sup>[have to]</sup> be bold when I am present <sup>[with you]</sup>, and feel compelled to speak in an authoritative tone to some people <sup>[there]</sup> who think we are living by worldly standards.

For although we live in the world <sup>[i.e., in a human body]</sup>, we do not wage war <sup>[i.e., for God and against evil]</sup> on a worldly level <sup>[i.e., by using the tactics of unsaved people]</sup>.

For the weapons of our warfare are not like the world’s <sup>[i.e., which rely on human ingenuity and unspiritual principles]</sup>, but they are empowered by God to overthrow <sup>[Satanic]</sup> fortifications.

We overthrow <sup>[vain]</sup> reasonings and every lofty idea raised against the knowledge of God <sup>[i.e., as revealed through the Gospel]</sup>, and take captive <sup>[our]</sup> every thought to <sup>[make it]</sup> obey Christ.

<sup>[And we are]</sup> prepared to punish all disobedience when your obedience is complete. <sup>[Note: This probably refers to the Corinthian church dealing with its unrepentant member. See I Cor. 5:3-5]</sup>.

You people are looking <sup>[merely]</sup> at the appearance of things <sup>[or, “You should look at what is obvious!” In either case, it suggests they were misjudging Paul’s ministry]</sup>. If any person is confident within himself that he belongs to Christ, he should reflect on this again: We are just as much Christ’s <sup>[servants]</sup> as he is.
For even if I should boast too much about the authority the Lord gave us for building you people up <sup>[spiritually]</sup>, and not tearing you down, I will not be ashamed of it.
<sup>[Now]</sup> I do not want to appear to be trying to scare you by my letters.
For some people are saying, “Paul’s letters are heavy and severe, but in person he is weak <sup>[physically (?)]</sup> and his speaking ability is unimpressive.”
Such people should consider this: What we say in our letters when we are away is what we will do when we are there.

For we do not dare to classify or compare ourselves with certain people who commend themselves. People who measure their<sup>[spiritual]</sup> stature by what <sup>[they think]</sup> they are, and compare their <sup>[spiritual]</sup> status with their own set of standards, are foolish.

But we will not boast beyond our proper boundaries <sup>[Note: This may mean that Paul was not attempting to exercise apostolic authority outside the parameters assigned to him by God]</sup>, but will restrict our boasting to the area assigned to us by God as a field of service, which includes you people <sup>[there in Corinth]</sup>
Now we are not going too far in claiming you as part of our outreach, since we were the first ones to come to you with the Gospel of Christ.

So, we will not go beyond our proper limits by boasting of work <sup>[already]</sup> done by others, but we hope that, as your faith increases, our field of service among you will <sup>[also]</sup> greatly expand.

<sup>[This is]</sup> so we can proclaim the good news <sup>[about Christ]</sup> even to areas beyond you <sup>[Note: Perhaps Paul here alludes to such places as Italy and Spain]</sup>, and <sup>[thereby]</sup> not boast over what has <sup>[already]</sup> been done in someone else’s field of service.
<sup>[Jer. 9:24 says]</sup>, “But the person who boasts should boast about the Lord.”
For it is not the person who commends himself that is approved <sup>[by God]</sup>, but the one whom the Lord commends.
I wish that you could put up with a little “foolishness” from me, but you really are doing that already.
For I am jealous for you with a godly jealousy, because I promised you in marriage to one husband, that I might present you to Christ as a pure virgin.
But I am afraid that somehow, just as the snake tricked Eve by his cunning, your minds might<sup>[also]</sup> be led astray from your sincere and pure devotion to Christ.

For if someone comes <sup>[to you]</sup> proclaiming another “Jesus,” whom we <sup>[apostles]</sup> had not preached, or if you people receive a different “spirit” from the one you have <sup>[already]</sup> received, or a different “gospel” from the one you have <sup>[already]</sup> accepted, you put up with him quite well.
For I consider myself not to be one bit inferior to those “super apostles.” <sup>[Note: Paul is referring to his opponents there in Corinth who claimed apostolic authority]</sup>.
But even though I am an unpolished speaker, I am not lacking in knowledge. We have made this clear to you in every way.
Or, did I sin by putting myself down in order to build you people up, because I preached the Gospel of God to you without taking <sup>[financial]</sup> support?
I <sup>[virtually]</sup> robbed other churches by accepting <sup>[financial]</sup> support from them in order to serve you.

And when I was there with you and in need, I was not a <sup>[financial]</sup> burden to anyone, for when the brothers came from Macedonia they provided me with what I lacked, so I kept myself from being a burden to you in every way and will continue to do so.

As sure as the truth of Christ is in my heart, no one will stop me from boasting about this <sup>[arrangement]</sup> throughout the regions of Achaia. <sup>[Note: This was the province where Corinth was located]</sup>.
Why <sup>[am I doing this]</sup>? Is it because I do not love you? God knows <sup>[I do]</sup>

But what I am doing, I will continue to do, so that I can cut off the opportunity from those who want an occasion <sup>[to receive financial support for themselves because of my example of receiving it]</sup>. They want to boast <sup>[that they are preaching]</sup> on the same terms as I do. <sup>[Note: Paul’s argument seems to be that one reason for his foregoing financial support was so those “super apostles” in Corinth (see verse 5) would not be able to use his example to wrongfully exact financial support from the church]</sup>.
For such men are false apostles; they are deceitful workers, who disguise themselves as <sup>[true]</sup> apostles of Christ.
And it is no wonder, for even Satan disguises himself as an angel of light <sup>[i.e., someone pure and righteous]</sup>.
Therefore, it should not be surprising for Satan’s ministers to disguise themselves as ministers of righteousness. Their <sup>[final]</sup> end will be what they deserve for such actions.
Again I say, no one should think I am foolish, but if you do, <sup>[then]</sup> accept me that way, so that I too can boast a little bit.
What I am saying in this boastful <sup>[show of]</sup> confidence is not what the Lord would <sup>[say]</sup>, but is <sup>[just plain]</sup> “foolishness.”
Since many <sup>[other]</sup> people boast about worldly matters, I will boast about such things also.
For, since you people are so smart, you gladly put up with fools.

For you put up with someone, <sup>[even]</sup> if he enslaves you <sup>[i.e., exercises control over your life]</sup>; <sup>[even]</sup> if he takes <sup>[unfair]</sup> advantage of you; <sup>[even]</sup> if he acts superior toward you; and <sup>[even]</sup> if he slaps you in the face.

I am ashamed to say that we were <sup>[too]</sup> weak to do such things. <sup>[Note: Paul is probably saying, sarcastically, “We are too ‘weak’ to take financial advantage of people and mistreat them the way those ‘super apostles’ there in Corinth were doing”]</sup>. Yet, in whatever <sup>[matters]</sup> anyone <sup>[there]</sup> dares to boast about, (now I am really speaking foolishly), I will dare to boast also.
Are they <sup>[i.e., Paul’s opponents there in Corinth]</sup> Hebrews? So am I. Are they Israelites? So am I. Are they descendants of Abraham? So am I.

Are they servants of Christ? (I am really talking like a crazy person now). I am more <sup>[of a servant, than they are]</sup>. I have worked much harder. I have been in prison more often. I have been flogged more severely. I have been in danger of death more frequently.
I received thirty-nine lashes from the Jews five times.
I was beaten with rods three times. I was stoned once. I suffered shipwreck three times and spent a night and a day in the open water.

I have been on frequent journeys; in danger from rivers; in danger from thieves; in danger from my own people; in danger from the Gentiles; in danger in the city; in danger in the desert; in danger in the ocean; in danger among false brothers.
I have worked and suffered. I have often gone without sleep, been hungry and thirsty, often gone without food <sup>[i.e., for spiritual reasons]</sup>, been cold and had inadequate clothing.
Besides these <sup>[kinds of]</sup> things <sup>[or, “besides these outward things”]</sup>, there is the daily pressure on me of concern for all the churches.
Who is weak, and I do not feel their weakness? Who is led astray <sup>[from God]</sup>, and I do not burn <sup>[i.e., with righteous anger over the one who caused it]</sup>?
If I must boast, I will boast about the things that show my weakness.
The God and Father of the Lord Jesus, who should be praised forever, knows that I am not lying.

<sup>[When I was]</sup> in Damascus <sup>[Note: Paul here refers to his conversion. See Acts 9:1ff]</sup>, the governor <sup>[who served]</sup> under King Aretas, was guarding the city of the Damascenes in order to capture me,
but I escaped from his hands by being lowered in a basket through a window in the <sup>[city]</sup> wall.

Although I must boast about myself <sup>[See 11:21-27]</sup>, it <sup>[really]</sup> is not profitable. So, I will go on to <sup>[tell about]</sup> visions and <sup>[inspired]</sup> revelations given <sup>[to me]</sup> from the Lord.

I know a man in <sup>[fellowship with]</sup> Christ who, fourteen years ago, was taken <sup>[literally, “snatched”]</sup> up to the third heaven. <sup>[Note: The first heaven would be where birds fly; the second where the stars are; and the third where God is]</sup>. (I do not know whether he was in his physical body or whether it was an “out­of-the-body” experience, but God knows). <sup>[Note: Paul is obviously telling about a personal experience. See verse 7]</sup>.
I know that this man --- whether in his body or out of it, I do not know, but God does ---

was <sup>[actually]</sup> taken up into Paradise. <sup>[Note: This is the same as the “third heaven” of the previous verse, and was a place in the presence of God. See Luke 23:43; Rev. 2:7]</sup>. There he heard inexpressible words which are not permitted for people to repeat.
I will boast on behalf of this person, but I will not boast about myself <sup>[anymore]</sup>, except about my weaknesses.
However, if I wanted to boast, I would not be a fool, because I would be telling the truth. But I will hold back, so that no one will think more of me than what he sees or hears from me.
